  • 2.1 Point
  • 2.2 Line Segment
  • 2.3 Line
  • 2.4 Ray
  • 2.5 Angle
  • 2.6 Comparing Angles
  • 2.7 Making Rotating Arms
  • 2.8 Special Types of Angles
  • 2.9 Measuring Angles
  • 2.10 Drawing Angles
  • 2.11 Types of Angles and their Measures

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 2.4 — Points, Lines, Rays
  • Section 2.5 — Angles Basics
  • Section 2.6 — Comparing Angles
  • Section 2.8 — Special Types of Angles (Straight, Right, Perpendicular, Classifying)
  • Section 2.9 — Measuring Angles (Degrees & Protractor)
  • Page 45 — Where are the Angles? (Real-World)
  • Section 2.10 — Drawing Angles
  • Section 2.11 — Types of Angles and their Measures (Acute, Obtuse, Reflex, Complete)

Chapter 2: Lines and Angles

Topics Explained — Chapter 2Each subtopic explained in depth with diagrams, real-life examples and activities

🎯 Why study Lines and Angles?

Look around you right now! The edges of your notebook, the corner of a wall, the hands of a clock, the slope of a ladder, the petals of a flower, the rails of a railway track — all of geometry begins with just two ideas: straightness and turning. Lines describe straightness; angles describe turning. Together they let us describe almost every shape and design that engineers, artists, architects, sportspeople and even nature use every single day.

In this chapter you will learn the “alphabet of geometry” — the smallest building blocks (point, line segment, line, ray) and how two such blocks meet to form an angle. You will then learn how to compare, measure (in degrees), classify and draw angles. Everything else in geometry that you learn later (triangles, quadrilaterals, circles, polygons, constructions, symmetry) is built on top of what you learn here. So take your time, draw a lot, and play with a protractor while reading!

§2.1 — Point

A point is the most basic object in geometry. It marks an exact location in space — nothing more. A point has no length, no width and no thickness. It has zero size in every direction. We say a point is zero-dimensional (0-D).

Because a real point has no size, we cannot actually see one! What you draw on paper with a sharp pencil is only a representation of a point. The smaller the dot, the closer it is to a true mathematical point. We label points with capital letters: A, B, P, X, etc.

Key properties of a point

  • A point shows position only. It has no length, breadth or height.
  • A point is named with a single capital letter (A, B, P, Q…). The name is written close to the dot, never on top of it.
  • Two different points are always at two different positions. If they were at the same position they would be the same point.
  • Through a single point, you can draw an infinite (uncountable) number of lines. Imagine spinning a stick around a pin — every position of the stick is a different line through that pin.

🔍 A tiny puzzle

How many points are there on a 5 cm long line segment?
Answer: an uncountable infinity! Between any two points on the segment, you can always squeeze another one. Geometry is full of infinity even in tiny spaces.

Common mistakes to avoid

  • Drawing a huge dot. A true point is as small as your pencil can make it.
  • Writing the name on top of the dot so it hides the dot. Always write the label beside the dot.
  • Using a lower-case letter (a, b, p). Points get capital letters; lines often get lower-case letters (l, m, n).

§2.2 — Line Segment

Take two points A and B. The shortest straight path connecting them is called the line segment AB. It has two clear endpoints, a fixed length, and it does not extend beyond either endpoint.

We write a line segment as AB or BA (the order does not matter — both names describe the same segment) and often draw a small bar over it: ̅AB.

How to measure a line segment

The length of a line segment is the distance between its two endpoints. We measure it with a ruler (scale) in centimetres (cm) or millimetres (mm).

  1. Place the 0 mark of the ruler exactly at one endpoint.
  2. Lay the ruler along the segment, edge touching it.
  3. Read the mark on the ruler at the other endpoint.

Real-life examples of line segments

  • The edge of your ruler
  • One side of a book, table or notebook
  • A pencil lying still on the desk
  • A piece of chalk
  • The bars of a window grill
  • The shortest path you walk from your bench to the door (if no obstacles!)

Worked example

Example 1. Three points L, M, N are placed in a line in that order. LM = 4 cm and MN = 3 cm. What is the length of segment LN?

Solution: Since L, M, N are on the same straight line in the order L → M → N, the segment LN is made of LM and MN joined end to end.

LN = LM + MN = 4 cm + 3 cm = 7 cm.

Things to remember

  • A line segment is finite — it has a measurable length.
  • It has exactly 2 endpoints.
  • AB and BA name the same segment.
  • Length is always positive: AB = BA > 0.

§2.3 — Line

A line is a straight path that extends endlessly in both directions. It has no thickness and no endpoints. Because it goes on for ever, we cannot measure its length — a line is infinite.

When we draw a line on paper we only draw a small part of it and put little arrows on both ends to show that “it keeps going.” Lines are named in two ways:

  • By any two points on the line: line AB, written as ↔AB (with a double-headed arrow on top).
  • By a single lower-case letter: line l, line m, line n.
l l

Two important facts about lines

  1. One point → infinitely many lines. Through a single point you can draw as many different lines as you wish, in every possible direction.
  2. Two points → exactly one line. If you fix two points, there is only one straight line that passes through both of them. (This is one of the most important truths in geometry!)
straight line that passes through both A and B — you can't!

Special relationships between two lines

When you draw two lines on a flat page, only three things can happen:

  • Intersecting lines meet at exactly one point. That point is called the point of intersection.
  • Parallel lines always stay the same distance apart and never meet, no matter how far you extend them. Examples: railway tracks, opposite edges of a notebook, lines on ruled paper.
  • Perpendicular lines are a special case of intersecting lines — they meet at a right angle (90°). Examples: the two arms of a +, edges of a window frame.

Comparing point, segment and line

ObjectEndpointsLengthHow we draw it
Point—none (just a position)a small dot
Line segment2finite, measurablea straight stroke with two dots
Line0infinitea straight stroke with arrows on both ends

§2.4 — Ray

A ray is part of a line that has one starting point (called the initial point or vertex) and goes on endlessly in one direction. It is like a line segment that has been “cut” at one end and stretched to infinity at the other.

A ray is written using two letters: the first letter is the starting point, and the second letter is any other point that lies on the ray. We draw a small arrow over them to show direction: →OA.

Real-life examples of a ray

  • A beam of sunlight coming out of the sun
  • The light of a torch flashed in a dark room
  • A laser pointer beam
  • The path of an arrow once it leaves the bow
  • A rocket leaving Earth in a straight direction

Rules for naming rays

  1. First letter = starting point. Always. Ray OA starts at O. Ray AO starts at A. They are different!
  2. The second letter is just any other point lying on the ray — it tells direction.
  3. If two points A and B both lie on the same ray starting at O, then ray OA = ray OB (same starting point, same direction).

Worked example

Example 2. Points P, Q, R, S lie on the same straight line in that order. How many different rays are there with starting point Q?

Solution: From Q we can go either left or right.

  • Going right: ray QR (which is the same as ray QS, since S also lies in that direction). So that is 1 ray.
  • Going left: ray QP. That is 1 more ray.

Total = 2 rays starting at Q (in opposite directions). These two together actually form the whole line!

Difference between line, line segment and ray

FeatureLine segmentRayLine
Endpoints21 (start only)0
Lengthfiniteinfiniteinfinite
Directionboth, but limitedone direction, endlessboth, endless
SymbolAB→AB↔AB
AB = BA?YesNoYes

§2.5 — Angle

An angle is the figure formed when two rays share the same starting point. The shared starting point is called the vertex, and the two rays are called the arms (or sides) of the angle. The angle itself is the amount of opening between the two arms.

Three ways to name an angle

  1. Using three letters — one point on the first arm, then the vertex in the middle, then a point on the second arm. Example: ∠DBE or ∠EBD. The vertex letter must be in the middle.
  2. Using just the vertex letter — only when there is no confusion (i.e. only one angle at that vertex). Example: ∠B.
  3. Using a number or small Greek letter — sometimes we mark an angle with ∠1, ∠2 or ∠α, ∠β to refer to it quickly.

⚠️ Why the vertex must be in the middle

If you write ∠BDE instead of ∠DBE, the letter D is in the middle — so D becomes the vertex! That is a different angle altogether. The middle letter always tells us the vertex.

When does a single letter cause trouble?

If only one angle is formed at a vertex, ∠B is fine. But if many rays meet at the same vertex B (say BA, BC and BD), then ∠B is ambiguous — which two arms? In that case you must use three letters: ∠ABC, ∠ABD or ∠CBD.

Real-life examples of angles

  • The two hands of a clock meeting at the centre
  • The two blades of an open pair of scissors
  • A partly open book (the spine is the vertex, the covers are the arms)
  • An open laptop (hinge = vertex)
  • The corner of a slice of pizza
  • The angle a kicker's leg makes with the ground

Interior, exterior and the angle itself

An angle divides the page into three regions:

  • The interior — the region inside the opening
  • The exterior — the region outside the opening
  • The angle itself — the two arms together with the vertex

§2.6 — Comparing Angles

Suppose you have two angles ∠P and ∠Q drawn on different pages. How can you tell which one is larger? Here is the most important idea: the length of the arms does NOT decide the size of the angle. What matters is the opening between the arms.

Both angles above are equal even though the arms have very different lengths. The opening is what counts.

Three ways to compare two angles

  1. By observation. Sometimes one angle is clearly larger (a wide-open door vs. a slightly-open door). This is quick but not always reliable.
  2. By superimposition (tracing). Trace one angle on tracing paper, then place the vertex on the vertex of the other angle and align one arm with one arm. If the second arm of the traced angle lies inside the other, the traced angle is smaller; if it lies outside, it is larger.
  3. By measurement. Use a protractor to find each angle in degrees and then compare numbers. This is the most precise method.

Worked example

Example 3. Without measuring, decide which is larger: the angle made by a clock at 2 o'clock or the angle at 4 o'clock?

Solution: At 2 o'clock the gap between the hands covers 2 hour-divisions; at 4 o'clock it covers 4 hour-divisions. More hour-divisions = larger opening. So the angle at 4 o'clock is larger.

§2.7 — Making Rotating Arms

An angle is not just a static picture; it can also be thought of as the amount of turning from one arm to the other. To feel this, you can build a simple “rotating-arm angle maker” with two paper strips.

Activity: make your own angle maker

  1. Cut two thin paper (or cardboard) strips, each about 10–12 cm long.
  2. Place one strip on top of the other so that their ends overlap at one point.
  3. Push a paper-pin (or a paper fastener) through both strips at the overlapping end.
  4. Now one strip can rotate around the pin while the other strip stays fixed.

As you rotate the top strip:

  • A tiny rotation gives a tiny angle (acute).
  • A quarter turn gives a right angle (90°).
  • A half turn gives a straight angle (180°) — both strips line up on opposite sides.
  • A full turn brings the rotating arm back to its starting position (360°).

💡 Why this activity matters

It shows you that an angle = amount of rotation. This is the same idea behind clocks, steering wheels, opening doors, swinging your arms, and even the rotation of the Earth. Once you understand “turning,” the degree system becomes very natural.

§2.8 — Special Types of Angles

Even before we learn to measure angles in numbers, some angles are so important and so common that mathematicians have given them special names: the straight angle, the right angle, and the complete angle. From these we also get the idea of perpendicular lines and the first family-names for “sharp” (acute) and “wide” (obtuse) angles.

The straight angle

Open a book completely flat on a table. The two covers together form a single straight line through the spine. The angle at the spine is called a straight angle because its two arms together make a straight line.

A straight angle is formed by half a full turn of the rotating arm.

The right angle

If you take a straight angle and divide it into two equal parts (by folding the paper so one arm lies exactly on the other), each of the two equal angles formed is called a right angle. So a straight angle is made up of two right angles, and a right angle is exactly half of a straight angle (or one-quarter of a full turn).

A right angle has the shape of the letter L. To show that an angle is a right angle, we mark it with a small square at the vertex (instead of the usual arc).

Perpendicular lines

When two lines (or rays, or segments) meet at a right angle, we say they are perpendicular to each other. The symbol for “perpendicular” is ⊥. For example, “line l ⊥ line m” reads as “line l is perpendicular to line m”.

The complete angle (full turn)

When the rotating arm makes a complete turn and comes back to its original position, it has formed a complete angle — one full rotation. A complete angle is made up of two straight angles, or four right angles.

Acute and obtuse angles (the first classification)

Now compare any angle with a right angle:

  • If the opening is less than a right angle (less than a quarter turn), it is called an acute angle. The word acute means “sharp” — the opening is narrow and sharp.
  • If the opening is exactly a right angle, it is a right angle.
  • If the opening is more than a right angle but less than a straight angle (between a quarter turn and a half turn), it is called an obtuse angle. The word obtuse means “blunt” — the opening is wide and blunt.

Right angles are all around you

Look at any window, door, picture frame, table top, page of a book, the letter T, or the corner where two walls meet a floor — you will see right angles everywhere.

💡 Activity (NCERT “Math Talk”)

Take a rectangular sheet of paper. Mark a straight angle ∡AOB on one of its edges. Fold the paper through O so that ray OB falls exactly on top of ray OA. The crease produced through O divides the straight angle into two equal parts — two right angles!

If a straight angle is half of a full turn, then a right angle must be one-quarter of a full turn. Try it and convince yourself!

§2.9 — Measuring Angles

So far we have compared angles — we know one is bigger or smaller than another, or which family it belongs to. But can we put an actual number on the size of an angle, just like we use 5 cm for length or 200 g for weight?

To measure an angle we need a unit. Just as length has the metre and weight has the kilogram, an angle has the degree, written with a small circle: 30°, 90°, 180°.

How was the degree invented?

Mathematicians divided one full turn into 360 equal parts and called each part one degree. Why 360? Because 360 has a huge number of divisors (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 18, 20, 24, 30, 36, …). That makes it easy to divide a full turn into many equal pieces — halves, thirds, quarters, sixths, eighths, twelfths — without using fractions.

🌚 A pinch of history

The choice of 360 is very old. The Rigveda speaks of a wheel with 360 spokes (verse 1.164.48); ancient calendars in India, Persia, Babylonia and Egypt often used 360-day years; and Babylonian astronomers liked to count in sixties and three-hundred-sixties. Above all, 360 is the smallest number that can be divided evenly by every counting number from 1 to 10 (except 7) — very convenient!

The big idea: angle = fraction × 360°

If an angle is n equal parts out of a full turn, then its measure is

angle = (n / total parts) × 360°

Example 4. A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. What is the angle at the tip of each slice?

Solution: Each slice is 1/8 of a full turn, so each angle = (1/8) × 360° = 45°.

Example 5. The Ashoka Chakra on the Indian flag has 24 equally-spaced spokes. What is the angle between two adjacent spokes?

Solution: 360° ÷ 24 = 15°.

Measures of the special angles in degrees

Now that we have a number for every angle, we can finally quantify the special angles we met in §2.8:

  • A complete angle (a full turn) measures 360°.
  • A straight angle is half of a full turn, so it measures 180°.
  • A right angle is half of a straight angle, so it measures 90°.
  • An angle of 0° is a zero angle — both arms overlap, no opening at all.

The protractor — our measuring instrument

A protractor is the instrument we use to measure angles in degrees. It is shaped like a half-circle (semi-circle) and goes from 0° to 180°. Most protractors have two scales — an outer scale and an inner scale — reading in opposite directions. We use whichever scale begins at 0° on the arm of our angle.

Step-by-step: how to measure an angle

  1. Place the centre of the protractor exactly on the vertex of the angle.
  2. Align the base line (the straight 0°–180° edge) of the protractor along one arm of the angle. Make sure that arm passes through the 0° mark.
  3. Decide which scale to use: the scale where the chosen arm reads 0° is the right one.
  4. Follow the other arm outward and read the number where it crosses the curve. That number, in degrees, is the measure of the angle.

Which scale do I use?

The trick is simple: start from 0° on the arm that you aligned. Then move along the curve to the other arm. Whichever scale starts at 0° on the chosen arm and counts up smoothly to the second arm is the correct one. Never mix the two scales!

Worked examples

Example 6. An arm of an angle aligns with the 0° mark on the inner scale, and the other arm crosses the protractor at 120° on the inner scale. What is the angle?

Solution: Both readings come from the inner scale, so the answer is simply 120°.

Example 7. While measuring, a student lined up one arm with the 0° of the outer scale and read 70° on the inner scale (by mistake). What should the correct reading be?

Solution: Since the two scales add to 180° at every position, the correct outer-scale reading = 180° − 70° = 110°.

Measuring a reflex angle

A protractor only goes up to 180°. If the angle to be measured is bigger than 180° (a reflex angle), measure the smaller angle (the “other side”) and subtract:

Reflex angle = 360° − (measured smaller angle)

§2.10 — Drawing Angles

Drawing an angle of a chosen measure is the reverse of measuring one. We use the protractor again, but this time we place a mark instead of reading one.

Steps to draw an angle (say, 50°)

  1. Draw a ray, say ray BA, using a ruler. This will be the base arm of the angle. Point B is the vertex.
  2. Place the centre of the protractor on vertex B and align the 0° line with ray BA.
  3. Starting from the 0° mark, count along the correct scale up to 50° and put a small dot. Call this point C.
  4. Remove the protractor and join B and C with a ruler to get ray BC.
  5. The angle ∠ABC = 50°. Mark the angle with a small arc and label it.

Drawing a reflex angle (e.g. 250°)

A protractor only goes up to 180°, so we can't mark 250° directly. We use a small trick: draw the “other side” instead.

  1. 250° + 110° = 360°. So the “short side” of a 250° angle is 110°.
  2. Draw a 110° angle the normal way.
  3. The other opening (the one on the outside) is the 250° reflex angle.

Worked examples on drawing

Example 8. Draw an angle of 75°.

Solution: Draw a ray, place the protractor centre on its endpoint, align with 0°, find 75° on the correct scale, mark a point, join with the vertex. Done!

Example 9. Draw an angle of 300°.

Solution: 360° − 300° = 60°. Draw a 60° angle and the reflex side is 300°.

Drawing right angles without a protractor

Two quick ways:

  • Using a set-square. A set-square already contains a 90° corner; trace around it.
  • By paper folding. Fold a sheet anywhere to get a straight crease. Fold the sheet again so the crease falls exactly on top of itself; the new crease is perpendicular to the first, so you get four perfect right angles!

✅ Self-check while drawing

After drawing, always re-measure your angle with the protractor. If you wanted 50° and get something else, the most common slip is using the wrong scale.

§2.11 — Types of Angles and their Measures

Once we can measure angles, we can sort them into groups by their size:

Type-by-type details

1. Zero angle (0°). Both arms lie on top of each other. There is no opening at all. Example: the hands of a clock at exactly 12 o'clock.

2. Acute angle (more than 0° but less than 90°). A sharp, narrow opening. Examples: the tip of a slice of cake, the angle at 1 o'clock (30°), the angle made by your fingers when you give a small “V” sign.

3. Right angle (exactly 90°). A perfect corner. Examples: corners of a notebook, the letter T, the angle at 3 o'clock or 9 o'clock, the angle between a wall and the floor.

4. Obtuse angle (more than 90° but less than 180°). A wide, blunt opening. Examples: an open laptop screen, a partly reclined chair, the angle at 4 o'clock (120°).

5. Straight angle (exactly 180°). The two arms together form a straight line. Example: the hands of a clock at 6 o'clock.

6. Reflex angle (more than 180° but less than 360°). If you take the “long way round” instead of the short way, you get a reflex angle. Example: the angle at 8 o'clock measured from 12 going clockwise the long way.

7. Complete angle (exactly 360°). A full rotation that brings the arm back to its starting position.

Quick test: classify these angles

AngleType
30°Acute
90°Right
120°Obtuse
180°Straight
250°Reflex
360°Complete

📐 Clock formula

At exactly n o'clock (with the minute hand on 12), the angle between the hour and minute hands is

angle = n × 30°  (taking the smaller side).

Examples: 1 o'clock → 30° (acute), 3 o'clock → 90° (right), 5 o'clock → 150° (obtuse), 6 o'clock → 180° (straight), 7 o'clock → 210°… but we usually take the smaller side, so 360° − 210° = 150° (obtuse).

🎯 Putting it all together

Here is the “big picture” you have built in this chapter:

  • You started with a point — pure position.
  • Two points gave you a line segment; extending it both ways gave a line; cutting it gave a ray.
  • Two rays sharing a vertex made an angle.
  • Angles can be compared (by sight, tracing, or measuring) and measured in degrees, where one full turn = 360°.
  • The protractor lets us read and draw angles precisely.
  • By size, angles are acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex or complete.

Every shape you will study later — triangle, square, polygon, circle — is just lines and angles put together. So this chapter is the foundation of everything.

Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved with explanations

📝 Quick Recap Before Exercises

  • Line segment = fixed length, 2 endpoints
  • Ray = 1 start point, extends infinitely in one direction
  • Line = extends infinitely in both directions
  • Angle = two rays sharing a common vertex
  • Right angle = 90°, Straight angle = 180°, Full turn = 360°
  • Clock formula: Angle between hands = |hour − other| × 30°

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Ray direction: Ray OA ≠ Ray AO! The FIRST letter is always the starting point
  • Naming angles: When 2+ angles share a vertex, DON'T use single letter (∠P). Use 3 letters (∠APB)
  • Protractor reading: Use the correct scale! If angle opens from 0° mark, read that scale
  • Reflex angles: Protractor only reads up to 180°. For reflex: 360° − measured angle

🔥 Warm-Up (Try before reading solutions!)

  1. How many lines can pass through 2 points? [Ans: Exactly 1]
  2. A clock shows 3 o'clock. What angle? [Ans: 90°]
  3. Is 200° acute, obtuse, or reflex? [Ans: Reflex]

Figure it Out — Section 2.4 (Points, Lines, Segments, Rays)

Q1. Can you help Rihan and Sheetal find their answers?

Rihan: How many lines can pass through one point?
Sheetal: How many lines can pass through two points?

Solution:

Rihan: Through a single point, infinitely many (uncountable) lines can pass. Imagine rotating a line around the point — every position is a different line!

Sheetal: Through two distinct points, exactly one line can pass. Two points determine a unique line.

Q2. Name the line segments in Fig. 2.4. Which of the five marked points are on exactly one of the line segments? Which are on two?

Solution:

Line segments: LM, MP, PQ, QR

Points on exactly one segment: L (only on LM) and R (only on QR)

Points on two segments: M (on LM and MP), P (on MP and PQ), Q (on PQ and QR)

💡 The endpoints of the chain are on only one segment; middle points are shared by two.

Q3. Name the rays shown in Fig. 2.5. Is T the starting point of each of these rays?

Solution:

Rays: TA, TB, TN and NB

Is T the starting point of each? No! T is the starting point of TA, TB, and TN. But NB has N as its starting point (it starts at N and goes through B).

💡 Remember: the first letter in a ray's name is ALWAYS its starting point!

Q4. Draw a rough figure and write labels appropriately to illustrate each of the following:

a. OP and OQ meet at O
b. XY and PQ intersect at point M
c. Line l contains points E and F but not point D
d. Point P lies on AB

Solution:

(a) Draw two rays starting from the SAME point O, going in different directions. Label one arm P and the other Q.

(b) Draw two line segments crossing each other. Mark the crossing point as M. Label the endpoints X, Y and P, Q.

(c) Draw a line and mark two points on it as E and F. Mark point D somewhere NOT on the line.

(d) Draw line segment AB and mark point P somewhere between A and B on the line.

Q5. In Fig. 2.6, name: (a) Five points (b) A line (c) Four rays (d) Five line segments

Solution:

(a) Five points: D, E, O, B and C

(b) A line: DE (or equivalently: DO, DB, EO, EB, OB — all name the same line!)

(c) Four rays: OC, OB, OE, OD

(d) Five line segments: DE, DO, DB, EO, EB (also OB and OC are possible)

Q6. Ray OA starts at O and passes through A and B.

a. Can you also name it as OB? Why?
b. Can we write OA as AO? Why or why not?

Solution:

(a) Yes! We can name it OB because O is the starting point and B lies on the ray going in the direction of A. OA is just the extension of OB (or vice versa) — same ray!

(b) No! OA is a ray with starting point O, going through A. AO would be a ray with starting point A, going through O — that’s a completely different ray going in the opposite direction!

💡 The first letter = starting point. Changing the order changes the ray!

Figure it Out — Section 2.5 (Angles)

Q1. Can you find the angles in the given pictures? Draw the rays forming any one of the angles and name the vertex.

Solution:

Yes! In the spectacles, wallet, and other objects shown, angles are formed where two edges meet.

Example: One angle is ∠BDC. Its vertex is D. One arm is ray DC and the other arm is ray DB.

💡 Look for any two lines meeting at a point — that’s an angle! They’re everywhere: scissors, clock hands, book covers...

Q2. Draw and label an angle with arms ST and SR.

Solution:

The vertex must be S (the common letter in both arms). Draw point S, then draw ray ST going in one direction and ray SR in another direction. The angle formed is ∠TSR (or ∠RST).

Q4. Name the angles marked in the given figure.

Solution:

The marked angles are: ∠RTQ and ∠RTP

Q5. Mark any three points A, B, C (not on one line). Draw all possible lines through pairs of these points. How many lines? How many angles can you name?

Solution:

Lines: We get 3 lines — AB, BC, CA

Angles: We can name 3 angles:

  • ∠ABC (or ∠CBA) — at vertex B
  • ∠BCA (or ∠ACB) — at vertex C
  • ∠CAB (or ∠BAC) — at vertex A

💡 These three points form a triangle with exactly 3 angles!

Q6. Now mark any four points A, B, C, D (no three on one line). Draw all possible lines. How many? How many angles?

Solution:

Lines: We get 6 lines — AB, BC, CD, DA, AC, BD

Angles: We can name 12 angles:

∠BAC, ∠CAD, ∠BAD, ∠ADB, ∠BDC, ∠ADC, ∠DCA, ∠ACB, ∠DCB, ∠CBD, ∠DBA, ∠CBA

💡 Each vertex has 3 angles (formed by choosing 2 lines out of 3 passing through it). 4 vertices × 3 = 12 angles.

Figure it Out — Section 2.6 (Comparing Angles)

Q1. Fold a rectangular sheet of paper, then draw a line along the fold created. Name and compare the angles formed.

Solution:

When we fold and draw a line, we create angles at the intersection points. The angles formed are:

∠AEF, ∠BEF, ∠DFE, ∠CFE

Comparing: ∠AEF and ∠CFE are larger than ∠BEF and ∠DFE (or vice versa, depending on the fold).

💡 Try different folds — the angles change each time!

Q2. In each case, determine which angle is greater and why:

a. ∠AOB or ∠XOY
b. ∠AOB or ∠XOB
c. ∠XOB or ∠XOC

Solution:

(a) ∠AOB is greater. ∠XOY is just a part inside ∠AOB. In fact, ∠AOB = ∠AOX + ∠XOY + ∠YOB.

(b) ∠AOB is greater. Ray OX is between OA and OB, so ∠AOB contains ∠XOB plus more.

(c) Neither — they are equal! ∠XOB = ∠XOC (the diagram shows OB and OC in the same direction).

Q3. Which angle is greater: ∠XOY or ∠AOB? Give reasons.

Solution:

We cannot tell just by looking! The arms' lengths are misleading — a longer arm doesn’t mean a bigger angle. We need to either superimpose them or measure with a protractor.

💡 An angle measures the OPENING between rays, not the length of the rays!

Figure it Out — Section 2.8 (Special Types of Angles — Right Angles & Perpendiculars)

Q1. How many right angles do the windows of your classroom contain? Do you see other right angles in your classroom?

Solution:

A typical rectangular window contains 4 right angles (one at each corner).

If your classroom has 3 windows, that’s 3 × 4 = 12 right angles from windows alone.

Other right angles in your classroom:

  • Corners of the blackboard / whiteboard (4 each)
  • Corners of the door (4)
  • Corners of every notebook, textbook (4 each)
  • Corners of the desk top (4 per desk)
  • Where two walls meet, or where the wall meets the floor / ceiling (every corner of the room)
  • Tiles on the floor — every tile’s corners

💡 Right angles are EVERYWHERE in a man-made room!

Q2. Join A to other grid points in the figure by a straight line to get a straight angle. What are the different ways of doing it?

Solution:

To make a straight angle (180°) at A, we need a line that passes through A and continues on both sides of A. This means we need to find pairs of grid points such that A lies exactly between them in a straight line.

On a typical NCERT dot grid (with the line through A and B as one arm), the different ways are:

  • Join two grid points horizontally on either side of A (along the row containing A).
  • Join two grid points vertically above and below A (along the column containing A).
  • Join two grid points along each diagonal direction through A (45° lines).
  • Any other slant for which a grid point exists symmetrically on the opposite side of A.

For each direction, the two arms together form a straight line, so the angle at A is exactly 180°.

Q3. Join A to other grid points in the figure by a straight line to get a right angle. What are the different ways of doing it?

Solution:

A right angle at A is exactly half of a straight angle. So we need to draw two lines through A whose arms make a 90° opening.

Two simple recipes:

  1. One arm is the horizontal grid line through A; the other arm is the vertical grid line through A. (Two perpendicular grid lines → 4 right angles at A.)
  2. One arm goes up-right at 45°; the other goes up-left at 45°. (Two diagonal grid lines → 4 right angles at A.)

Hint from NCERT: Extend the line CAB through A into a straight angle. To get a right angle at A, draw a line through A that divides the straight angle CAB into two equal parts — that line is perpendicular to CAB at A.

Q4. Get a slanting crease on the paper. Now, try to get another crease that is perpendicular to the slanting crease.

a. How many right angles do you have now? Justify why they are exact right angles.
b. Describe how you folded the paper.

Solution:

(a) Four right angles. When two perpendicular lines cross, they create 4 angles. Since the total around a point = 360° and all 4 are equal, each = 360° ÷ 4 = 90°.

(b) To get perpendicular creases: Fold the paper along any slant. Then fold again so the first crease lies exactly on itself — this second fold will be perpendicular to the first!

Q5 (NCERT). Identify acute, right, obtuse and straight angles in the previous figures.

Solution:

This question asks us to look back at the figures we have already met in §2.5–§2.8 (the cranes, the door, the swing, the clock, the windows, the paper folds, the dot-grid angles at A, the angle ∠XOY vs ∠AOB, etc.) and classify each angle we can see.

How to classify an angle by sight:

  • Right angle (= 90°): looks like the corner of a book or a wall. Examples: corners of a window, corners of the blackboard, corners of a notebook, the “L”-shape between two grid lines, two perpendicular paper folds.
  • Acute angle (< 90°): looks smaller / sharper than a corner of a book. Examples: the angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 1 o’clock or 2 o’clock, a slightly opened scissors, the “V” of a slim crane’s beak, the slope of a low ladder.
  • Obtuse angle (between 90° and 180°): looks wider than a right angle but is not yet a straight line. Examples: a wide-open door, the angle of a deck-chair’s back, the angle at 4 o’clock or 5 o’clock between the clock hands, the open beak of a hungry crane in NCERT’s Fig. 2.10.
  • Straight angle (= 180°): the two arms make a single straight line. Examples: a fully opened book lying flat, the horizon line, a clock at 6 o’clock (hour and minute hands point exactly opposite), two grid points joined through A on the same row / column / diagonal.

Sample classification (cranes, door, clock, swing pictures from earlier sections):

Where the angle appearsType
Window / classroom cornerRight angle
Clock hands at 1 o’clock (30°)Acute
Clock hands at 4 o’clock (120°)Obtuse
Clock hands at 6 o’clock (180°)Straight angle
Crane with mouth slightly openAcute
Crane with mouth wide openObtuse
Door pushed half-openRight (≈ 90°)
Door pushed almost fully openObtuse
Door fully open against the wallStraight angle

💡 The trick is to mentally compare each angle with the corner of a book (right angle) and with a flat line (straight angle).

Q6. Make a few acute angles and a few obtuse angles. Draw them in different orientations.

Solution:

Acute angles (less than 90°): Draw two rays from a point with a small opening — like 30°, 45°, 60°, 75°. These look “sharp.”

Obtuse angles (between 90° and 180°): Draw two rays with a wide opening — like 100°, 120°, 150°. These look “wide.”

💡 The orientation doesn’t matter! A 45° angle is still acute whether it points up, down, left, or right.

Q7. Do you know what acute and obtuse mean? Why do you think these words were chosen?

Solution:

Acute means “sharp” (like a needle). Acute angles have a SHARP, pointy opening.

Obtuse means “blunt” (like a butter knife). Obtuse angles have a WIDE, blunt opening.

The words describe how the angles “feel” — sharp vs wide!

Figure it Out — Section 2.9 (Measuring Angles with a Protractor)

Q1. Write the measures of the following angles: (a) ∠KAL (b) ∠WAL (c) ∠TAK

Solution:

(a) ∠KAL = 30°

(b) ∠WAL = 50°

(c) ∠TAK = 120°

💡 Yes, it is possible to count the number of units in 5s or 10s on the protractor markings!

Q (Page 36). Name the different angles in the figure and write their measures.

Solution:

Reading from the protractor:

  • ∠POQ = 35°
  • ∠POR = 95°
  • ∠POS = 125°
  • ∠POT = 160°
  • ∠QOR = 60°
  • ∠QOS = 90° (right angle!)
  • ∠QOT = 125°
  • ∠QOU = 145°
  • ∠ROS = 30°
  • ∠ROT = 65°
  • ∠ROU = 85°
  • ∠SOT = 35°
  • ∠SOU = 55°

Q (Page 40 — Think). In Fig. 2.20, ∠AOB = ∠BOC = ∠COD = ∠DOE = ∠EOF = ∠FOG = ∠GOH = ∠HOI = _____. Why?

Solution:

Each angle = 22.5°

Why: A straight angle (180°) is divided into 8 equal parts. So each part = 180° ÷ 8 = 22.5°

Q1 (Page 40). Find the degree measures of the angles using your protractor.

Solution:

Using a protractor carefully:

  • First angle: 47°
  • Second angle: 23°
  • Third angle: 108°

Q3. Find the degree measures for the angles given below. Check if your paper protractor can be used here!

Solution:

Angles: 42° and 116°

No, the paper protractor cannot be used directly here because the angles are oriented in a way that doesn’t align with the paper protractor easily. You need a real protractor!

Q4. How can you find the degree measure of the marked angle (greater than 180°)?

Solution:

Method: Measure the smaller (unmarked) angle with the protractor, then subtract from 360°.

Measure of marked angle = 360° − 100° = 260° (reflex angle)

💡 A protractor only measures up to 180°. For reflex angles, always use: Reflex angle = 360° − measured angle

Q5. Measure and write the degree measures for each of the following angles.

Solution:

(a) 80°   (b) 120°   (c) 60°

(d) 130°   (e) 130°   (f) 60°

Q6. Find the degree measures of ∠BXE, ∠CXE, ∠AXB and ∠BXC.

Solution:

∠BXE = 115°,   ∠CXE = 85°,   ∠AXB = 65°,   ∠BXC = 30°

Q7. Find the degree measures of ∠PQR, ∠PQS and ∠PQT.

Solution:

∠PQR = 45°,   ∠PQS = 100°,   ∠PQT = 150°

Figure it Out — Page 45: Where are the Angles?

🔍 Real-World Angle Hunt

Angles are not just in geometry drawings — they're all around us! This section helps you discover angles in everyday objects and situations. Look carefully at each scenario and identify where the angle is hiding, what its vertex is, and what its arms are.

Q1. Angles in a clock:

a. The hands of a clock make different angles at different times. At 1 o'clock, the angle between the hands is 30°. Why?
b. What will be the angle at 2 o'clock? And at 4 o'clock? 6 o'clock?
c. Explore other angles made by the hands of a clock.

Solution:

(a) Why 30° at 1 o'clock?

A clock face is a full circle = 360°. It is divided into 12 equal parts (the hours).

Angle between consecutive numbers = 360° ÷ 12 = 30°

At 1 o'clock, the hour hand is at 1 and the minute hand is at 12 — they are separated by exactly 1 gap = 30°.

(b) Angles at other times:

  • At 2 o'clock: hands span 2 gaps = 2 × 30° = 60°
  • At 4 o'clock: hands span 4 gaps = 4 × 30° = 120°
  • At 6 o'clock: hands span 6 gaps = 6 × 30° = 180° (straight angle!)

(c) Other interesting clock angles:

  • 3 o'clock = 3 × 30° = 90° (perfect right angle!)
  • 5 o'clock = 5 × 30° = 150° (obtuse angle)
  • 9 o'clock = 9 × 30° = 270° (reflex angle!)
  • 12 o'clock = 0° or 360° (both hands together)

💡 Quick formula for whole hours: Angle = (number of hours) × 30°

Challenge: What about half-past times? At 1:30, the hour hand moves halfway between 1 and 2! The angle becomes smaller...

Q2. The angle of a door:

Is it possible to express the amount by which a door is opened using an angle? What will be the vertex of the angle and what will be the arms of the angle?

Solution:

Yes! The opening of a door can definitely be expressed as an angle.

The vertex: The point where the door hinges meet the wall (the hinge point on the floor or ceiling)

The arms:

  • First arm: The edge of the wall (or the door's closed position)
  • Second arm: The edge of the door in its current position

Examples of door angles:

  • Door completely closed = 0°
  • Door half-open (perpendicular to wall) = 90°
  • Door wide open (flat against wall) = 180°
  • Door slightly ajar = 15°–30° (acute angle)

💡 Next time you open a door, imagine you're drawing an angle! The wider you open it, the larger the angle.

Q3. Vidya on the swing:

Vidya is enjoying her time on the swing. She notices that the greater the angle with which she starts the swinging, the greater is the speed she achieves on her swing. But where is the angle? Are you able to see any angle?

Solution:

Yes, there is an angle! It's at the top of the swing where the chains/ropes are attached.

The vertex: The point where the swing's rope/chain is attached to the overhead bar

The arms:

  • First arm: The vertical line from the attachment point straight down (the rest position)
  • Second arm: The rope of the swing in its current pulled-back position

Why does a bigger starting angle mean more speed?

When Vidya pulls the swing back at a bigger angle (say 45° instead of 15°), she starts higher off the ground. As she swings down, gravity pulls her through a longer arc, giving her more speed at the bottom!

💡 This is physics in action: gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy. The bigger the starting angle, the more energy!

Try it yourself: On a swing, start with different angles and feel the difference in speed. (Always be safe!)

Q4. Toy with slanting slabs:

Here is a toy with slanting slabs attached to its sides; the greater the angles or slopes of the slabs, the faster the balls roll. Can angles be used to describe the slopes of the slabs? What are the arms of each angle? Which arm is visible and which is not?

Solution:

Yes! Angles can describe the slope (steepness) of the slabs.

The vertex: The point where the slab meets the base of the toy

The arms:

  • Visible arm: The slanting surface of the slab
  • Invisible arm: The horizontal line (the flat base or ground level) — this is often imaginary/invisible but forms the reference

How it works:

  • A small angle (say 15°) means a gentle slope → the ball rolls slowly
  • A large angle (say 60°) means a steep slope → the ball rolls very fast!
  • At 90° (vertical), the ball would fall straight down!

💡 This is why steep hills are harder to climb — the angle of the slope with the horizontal is larger!

Real-world examples: Road slopes, wheelchair ramps, slides in playgrounds — all use angles to control steepness.

Q5. Insect and rotation:

Observe the images below where there is an insect and its rotated version. Can angles be used to describe the amount of rotation? How? What will be the arms of the angle and the vertex?

Hint: Observe the horizontal line touching the insects.

Solution:

Yes! Rotation can be described using angles. When an object rotates, the amount of turn is measured in degrees.

The vertex: The center point of rotation (usually the center of the insect's body)

The arms:

  • First arm: A line from the rotation center to a reference point on the original insect (e.g., along the horizontal)
  • Second arm: A line from the rotation center to the same point on the rotated insect

Example rotations:

  • 90° rotation: The insect appears to have turned a quarter-circle (like turning right)
  • 180° rotation: The insect is upside-down (half-circle turn)
  • 270° rotation: Three-quarter turn
  • 360° rotation: Full circle — back to starting position!

💡 This is how computer graphics work! Every time you rotate an image on your phone, software calculates the rotation angle.

Try it: Draw a simple arrow on paper. Put a pin through its center. Now rotate it and measure the angle of rotation with a protractor!

🎯 Key Insight — Angles in Daily Life

Angles help us describe and measure many everyday phenomena:

  • Direction & Navigation: "Turn 90° left" or "The sun is at a 45° angle"
  • Opening & Closing: Doors, laptop screens, umbrellas
  • Slopes & Inclines: Roads, ramps, roofs
  • Rotation & Spinning: Wheels, clock hands, ceiling fans
  • Measurement of Time: Clock faces divide time into angular segments

Once you start looking for angles, you'll find them everywhere! Mathematics is not separate from life — it is life.

Figure it Out — Section 2.10 (Drawing Angles)

Q1. Use a protractor to draw angles having the following measures: 80°, 120°, 27°, 165°, 45°, 90°.

Solution:

For every angle: (i) draw a ray with a ruler, (ii) place the protractor centre on the endpoint and align 0° with the ray, (iii) find the required measure on the correct scale, mark a small dot, (iv) join the vertex to the dot with the ruler, (v) mark a small arc and label the angle.

  • 80° — acute
  • 120° — obtuse
  • 27° — acute (use the smaller divisions on the protractor for accuracy)
  • 165° — obtuse, almost a straight angle
  • 45° — acute (half of a right angle)
  • 90° — right angle (mark with a small square at the vertex, not an arc)

✅ After drawing, always re-measure with the protractor as a self-check.

Q2. Draw an angle of measure 110° and bisect it (divide it into two equal angles using a fold or a ruler).

Solution:

Step 1: Draw ∡AOB = 110° with the protractor.

Step 2: Place the protractor again on O. Mark the point at 55° (= 110° ÷ 2). Call it C.

Step 3: Join OC. Now ∡AOC = ∡COB = 55°.

Alternative (paper folding): trace the angle, then fold so that OB falls exactly on OA — the crease is the bisector.

Q3. Draw a reflex angle of 240° using a protractor (which only reads up to 180°).

Solution:

Trick: 360° − 240° = 120°.

So draw an ordinary angle of 120°. The opening on the other side (the “long way round”) is the reflex angle of 240°. Mark it with a dashed arc on the outside and label it 240°.

Q4. Draw a right angle without using a protractor (using only paper folding).

Solution:

Take a sheet of paper. Make any fold to get a straight crease. Now make a second fold so that the straight crease falls exactly on itself. The second crease will be perpendicular to the first — you have made four perfect right angles!

Q5. In Fig. 2.23, list all the angles possible. Did you find them all? Now, guess the measures of all the angles. Then, measure the angles with a protractor. Record all your numbers in a table. See how close your guesses are to the actual measures.

Solution:

All possible angles: ∠CAP, ∠ACD, ∠APL, ∠DLP, ∠RPL, ∠SLP, ∠PRS, ∠LSR, ∠BRS, ∠CLP (and possibly more depending on the figure)

Steps:

  1. List all angles: Systematically identify every angle by looking at all pairs of rays meeting at each vertex
  2. Estimate first: Without measuring, guess each angle's size. This trains your "angle sense"
  3. Measure accurately: Use your protractor to find the actual measure of each angle
  4. Compare: Record both estimates and actual measures in a table

Sample Table:

AngleEstimateActualDifference
∠CAP___°___°___°
∠ACD___°___°___°
∠APL___°___°___°
... (continue for all angles)

💡 The closer your estimates, the better your spatial sense! With practice, you can estimate angles without a protractor.

Q6. Use a protractor to find the measure of each angle shown in the figure. Then classify each angle as acute, obtuse, right or reflex.

Solution:

For angles shown in the textbook figure:

(a) ∠PTR = 30° — Acute angle (less than 90°)

(b) ∠PTQ = 60° — Acute angle

(c) ∠PTW = 102° — Obtuse angle (between 90° and 180°)

(d) ∠WTP = 258° — Reflex angle (greater than 180°)

Classification Reminder:

  • Acute: 0° < angle < 90°
  • Right: angle = 90°
  • Obtuse: 90° < angle < 180°
  • Straight: angle = 180°
  • Reflex: 180° < angle < 360°
  • Complete: angle = 360°

Let's Explore

In this figure, ∠TER = 80°. What is the measure of ∠BET? What is the measure of ∠SET?

Solution:

∠BET = 100°

Why? Observe that ∠REB is a straight angle. Hence, the degree measure of ∠REB = 180°, of which 80° is covered by ∠TER. Therefore:

∠BET = 180° − 80° = 100°

∠SET = 10°

Why? A similar argument can be applied. ∠SER appears to be a right angle = 90°. Since ∠TER = 80°, we have:

∠SET = 90° − 80° = 10°

💡 Key concept: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°. Angles in a right angle add up to 90°.

Figure it Out — Section 2.11 (Types of Angles and their Measures — Classifying Acute/Right/Obtuse/Reflex)

📐 Drawing & Classifying Angles Practice

This section focuses on creating angles with specific measurements and classifying angles by their type. You'll practice using your protractor to both create precise angles AND identify whether they are acute, right, obtuse, or reflex.

Q1. Draw angles with the following degree measures:

a. 140°   b. 82°   c. 195°   d. 70°   e. 35°

Solution:

For each angle, follow these steps:

  1. Draw a base ray with a ruler
  2. Place the protractor's center on the ray's endpoint
  3. Align 0° with the base ray
  4. Mark the required degree measure
  5. Join the endpoint to the mark to form the second ray

(a) 140° — Obtuse angle (between 90° and 180°)

(b) 82° — Acute angle (less than 90°)

(c) 195° — Reflex angle (greater than 180°). To draw: First draw 360° − 195° = 165° in the normal direction, then the larger opening on the other side is 195°. Mark with a dashed arc.

(d) 70° — Acute angle

(e) 35° — Acute angle

💡 Remember: For reflex angles (>180°), standard protractors only go up to 180°, so subtract from 360° and draw the complementary angle.

Q2. Estimate the size of each angle and then measure it with a protractor:

(There are 6 angles shown in the textbook: a, b, c, d, e, f)

Classify these angles as acute, right, obtuse or reflex angles.

Solution:

Steps:

  1. First estimate by comparing to known angles (45°, 90°, 180°)
  2. Then measure accurately with your protractor
  3. Classify the angle based on its measure
  4. Compare your estimation with actual measurement

Classification Guide:

  • Acute: 0° < angle < 90° (sharp, pointy)
  • Right: angle = 90° exactly (perfect L-shape)
  • Obtuse: 90° < angle < 180° (wide, blunt)
  • Straight: angle = 180° (flat line)
  • Reflex: 180° < angle < 360° (the "long way around")

💡 The better you get at estimation, the faster you'll identify angles in real life! Practice makes perfect.

For the six angles in the textbook figure:

Measure each carefully and record your results in a table:

AngleEstimateActualType
a.___°___°________
b.___°___°________
c.___°___°________
d.___°___°________
e.___°___°________
f.___°___°________

Q3. Make any figure with three acute angles, one right angle and two obtuse angles.

Solution:

Draw a hexagon (or any 6-sided figure) where:

  • ∠A, ∠B, ∠C are acute (e.g., 60°, 70°, 80°)
  • ∠D, ∠F are obtuse (e.g., 120°, 130°)
  • ∠E is a right angle (90°)

Total should = (6−2) × 180° = 720°. Check: 60+70+80+120+130+90 = 550° — adjust as needed for a proper closed figure!

Q4. Draw the letter 'M' such that the angles on the sides are 40° each and the angle in the middle is 60°.

Solution:

Draw two vertical lines. From the top of each, draw slanting lines that meet in the middle forming a 'V'. Ensure:

  • The two side angles (where verticals meet the slants) = 40° each
  • The middle angle (where the two slants meet) = 60°

Use a protractor to draw these angles precisely!

Q5. Draw the letter 'Y' such that the three angles formed are 150°, 60° and 150°.

Solution:

Draw a vertical line going down (the stem of Y). At the top, draw two arms going up-left and up-right. The angles between the arms and stem should be 150° (on each side) and the angle between the two arms should be 60°.

Check: 150° + 150° + 60° = 360° ✓ (angles around the junction point must total 360°)

Q6. The Ashoka Chakra has 24 spokes. What is the angle between two adjacent spokes? What is the largest acute angle between two spokes?

Solution:

Angle between adjacent spokes: 360° ÷ 24 = 15°

Largest acute angle between any two spokes: We need the largest multiple of 15° that is still less than 90°.

15° × 5 = 75° (acute ✓), 15° × 6 = 90° (NOT acute, it’s a right angle)

So the largest acute angle = 75° (spanning 5 gaps between spokes)

Q7 (Puzzle). I am an acute angle. Double me = acute. Triple me = acute. Quadruple me = acute. But 5 times me = obtuse. What are my possible measures?

Solution:

Let the angle = x°. We need:

  • x < 90° (acute) ✓
  • 2x < 90° → x < 45°
  • 3x < 90° → x < 30°
  • 4x < 90° → x < 22.5°
  • 5x > 90° → x > 18°

So: 18° < x < 22.5°

Since angle measures are in whole degrees: x can be 19°, 20°, 21° or 22°

💡 Check: for x = 19°: 2×19=38✓, 3×19=57✓, 4×19=76✓, 5×19=95 (obtuse!) ✓

💭 Strategy Tip

For angle problems: (1) Identify the vertex and arms, (2) Decide the type (acute/right/obtuse/straight/reflex), (3) Remember: angles on a straight line add to 180°, angles around a point add to 360°, (4) For reflex angles: subtract from 360°.

🧠 Self-Check Quiz

  1. What angle do clock hands make at 5 o'clock?
  2. Is 91° acute or obtuse?
  3. How many lines pass through 2 distinct points?
  4. What is the reflex angle when the measured angle is 70°?
  5. Ray AB — which is the starting point?
  1. 150° → 5 × 30° = 150°
  2. Obtuse → just over 90°
  3. Exactly 1
  4. 290° → 360° − 70° = 290°
  5. A → First letter = starting point
NCERT Deep Dive — Chapter 2A guided walk through the NCERT chapter “Lines and Angles”

A guided tour of NCERT "Lines and Angles"

NCERT begins this chapter with a simple observation: everything around us has shape, and every shape is made of points, lines and angles. A book, a window, a road, a building — all of them are described by these three simple ideas.

The chapter develops the ideas in this order:

  1. Point — the smallest geometric idea.
  2. Line segment — a part of a straight path with two endpoints.
  3. Line — an unending straight path in two directions.
  4. Ray — a straight path with one endpoint, extending to infinity in the other direction.
  5. Angle — the figure formed when two rays meet at a common endpoint.
  6. Comparing angles — without measuring, by superposition.
  7. Measuring angles — using degrees and a protractor.
  8. Types of angles — acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex, complete.
  9. Drawing angles — with ruler and protractor.

Each section is full of hands-on activities so that you can see and feel the geometry, not just read it.

Hands-on activity bank from the chapter

NCERT suggests many activities. Here are some you can try at home with simple materials.

Activity 1 — The dotted street. Mark several dots on a sheet of paper. Try drawing straight lines through any two of them. How many different lines can you draw if you have 3 dots? 4 dots? 5 dots?

Hint: for n dots no three of which are collinear, the number of lines is n(n − 1) / 2.

Activity 2 — The angle hunter. Walk around your home and identify objects that show acute angles, right angles, obtuse angles, straight angles and reflex angles. Make a list with 3 examples of each type.

Activity 3 — The folding paper. Take a rectangular sheet and fold it so that the fold makes an angle with the bottom edge. Estimate the angle by eye, then measure with a protractor. How close was your estimate?

Activity 4 — The rubber-band ray. Loop a rubber band around two thumbtacks. The rubber band stretches into a line segment. Now release one end: it “flies away” in one direction, just like a ray going to infinity from one endpoint.

Activity 5 — The clock-angle game. At what time will the hour hand and the minute hand form a right angle? A straight angle? Try to find at least three positions for each.

Sample answer: right angles occur 22 times in a 12-hour day at intervals of about 32 minutes 43 seconds.

Activity 6 — The angle-of-a-page. Open a notebook to different angles. Estimate the angle of opening. Measure with a protractor placed along the spine.

Activity 7 — Reflex hunt. Find five real-life examples of reflex angles in your home (e.g. the outside angle at a corner of a chair).

The protractor — full anatomy

The protractor is the most useful angle-measuring instrument. Let's study every part.

1. A protractor is a semicircular plastic ruler with degree markings from 0° to 180°.

2. The flat base is the diameter; the curved part is half a circle.

3. The centre point (often marked with a small cross or a hole) sits at the midpoint of the base. This is the point you place over the vertex of the angle.

4. Two scales run around the curve: the inner scale reads 0° on the right and 180° on the left; the outer scale reads the opposite. Always start counting from 0° along the arm of the angle.

5. Each small mark is 1°. Every 10th mark is longer and labelled. The bold marks at 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150° help in quick reading.

6. A larger protractor (360°, full circle) is sometimes used for reflex angles.

Steps to measure an angle:

  1. Place the centre point of the protractor on the vertex.
  2. Align the base of the protractor with one arm of the angle.
  3. Read the scale that begins with 0° along that arm.
  4. Read the number where the other arm crosses the curve.

Steps to draw an angle of a given measure (say 65°):

  1. Draw a ray AB.
  2. Place the centre of the protractor at A, with the base along AB.
  3. Mark a point at 65° on the appropriate scale.
  4. Remove the protractor and draw a ray from A through the mark.
  5. The angle between the two rays is 65°.

Extra worked angle problems (NCERT-style)

Problem 1. The hour and minute hands of a clock at 3:00 form an angle of ____.

Solution. 3 × 30° = 90° (right angle).

Problem 2. The angle between the hands at 6:00 is ____.

Solution. 180° (straight angle).

Problem 3. The angle between the hands at 9:00 is ____.

Solution. 90° (but on the other side from 3:00).

Problem 4. Classify the following as acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex: 32°, 90°, 119°, 180°, 240°, 1°, 350°.

Solution. 32° acute; 90° right; 119° obtuse; 180° straight; 240° reflex; 1° acute; 350° reflex.

Problem 5. If you turn by 90° four times in the same direction, you face ____.

Solution. The original direction (4 × 90° = 360° = a full turn).

Problem 6. A ray makes an angle of 60° with a horizontal line. What is the reflex angle?

Solution. 360° − 60° = 300°.

Problem 7. Two angles together fit on a straight line. One is 75°. The other is ____.

Solution. 180° − 75° = 105°.

Problem 8. Two angles together fit around a point. They are equal. Each is ____.

Solution. 360° / 2 = 180°.

Problem 9. A polygon has 5 sides. The sum of its interior angles is ____.

Solution. (n − 2) × 180° = 3 × 180° = 540°.

Problem 10. Each angle of a regular hexagon is ____.

Solution. Sum = (6 − 2) × 180° = 720°. Each angle = 720° / 6 = 120°.

🎯 Chapter 2 — NCERT alignment summary

  • The basic geometric objects (point, line segment, line, ray, angle) build everything else.
  • Angles are measured in degrees: 1°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° and so on.
  • Acute (<90°), right (=90°), obtuse (>90° <180°), straight (=180°), reflex (>180° <360°), complete (=360°).
  • A protractor measures and draws angles up to 180°.
  • Angles around a point sum to 360°; angles on a straight line sum to 180°.

📋 Chapter 3 — What's Inside

  • 3.1 Numbers Can Tell Us Things
  • 3.2 Supercells
  • 3.3 Patterns of Numbers on the Number Line
  • 3.4 Playing with Digits
  • 3.5 Pretty Palindromic Patterns
  • 3.6 The Magic Number of Kaprekar
  • 3.7 Clock and Calendar Numbers
  • 3.8 Mental Math
  • 3.9 Playing with Number Patterns
  • 3.10 An Unsolved Mystery — the Collatz Conjecture!
  • 3.11 Simple Estimation
  • 3.12 Games and Winning Strategies

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 3.1–3.2 — Supercells
  • Section 3.4 — Digit Sums
  • Section 3.5–3.6 — Palindromes & Kaprekar
  • Section 3.7 — Time & Date Puzzles
  • Section 3.8–3.9 — Estimation & Patterns
  • Section 3.10–3.12 — Collatz & Mixed

Chapter 3: Number Play

🎯 Why this chapter is fun

Numbers are not just for adding and subtracting. They have personalities, hidden tricks and even mysteries that mathematicians have not yet solved. In this chapter we will play with numbers — we will turn them upside down (palindromes), juggle their digits (Kaprekar’s 6174), follow their journey on a number line, and meet a problem (the Collatz conjecture) so simple a child can understand it, yet so hard that nobody on Earth has been able to crack it. By the end you will see numbers as living things, full of surprises.

In-Depth Study — Chapter 3Each subtopic explained in depth with worked examples, diagrams and activities

§3.1 — Numbers Can Tell Us Things (In Depth)

Whenever you hear someone say a number, that number is telling you a story. The number 7 in “7 days of the week” tells one story; the same 7 in “Class 7” or “7 km away” tells a different one. Mathematics begins when we learn to read the story a number is telling.

Four ways numbers tell us things

  1. Counting — How many? Five mangoes, twelve students, twenty marbles.
  2. Ordering — Which one? First place, third bench, 7th of August.
  3. Labelling — Which item? Bus number 24, jersey number 10, room number 305.
  4. Measuring — How much? 3 kg of sugar, 15 km road, 1.5 litres of milk.

Once you know what role a number is playing, you also know what you may or may not do with it. You can add 5 apples and 3 apples to get 8 apples (counting). But it makes no sense to add “Bus 24” + “Bus 31” — those are labels.

Quick check. What kind of number is each of these?
(a) 12 in “12 students” — counting.
(b) 3 in “3rd floor” — ordering.
(c) 100 in “Note ₹100” — measuring (value).
(d) 6 in “Channel 6 on TV” — labelling.

§3.2 — Supercells (In Depth)

Put some numbers into a table. A cell is called a supercell when the number in it is greater than the numbers in all the cells touching it (its “neighbours”).

Rules for a supercell

  • In a single row, a cell has two neighbours (left and right). It is a supercell if its number is greater than both of those.
  • At the ends of the row, a cell has only one neighbour. It is a supercell if its number is greater than that neighbour.
  • In a 2-D grid, a cell has up to 4 neighbours (top, bottom, left, right). It is a supercell if it beats all of them.

Why supercells are interesting

Finding supercells is like finding the “local peaks” in a landscape of numbers. The same idea is used in real life: weather satellites look for cells (peaks) in pressure maps to spot storms; doctors look for peaks in heart-rate data to identify heartbeats. Mathematics calls these local maxima, and you have just met your first one!

Activity. Take the row 4, 13, 9, 21, 6, 18, 12, 20, 3. Find every supercell. (Answer: 13, 21, 18, 20.)

§3.3 — Patterns of Numbers on the Number Line (In Depth)

A number line is a straight line with numbers marked at equal distances. It is one of the most powerful pictures in mathematics, because every number has its own home on the line, and we can see sums, differences and patterns just by jumping along.

Things you can spot on a number line

  • Skip-counting (jumping by 2s, 3s, 5s, 10s) creates the multiples of those numbers.
  • Even numbers sit on every alternate point starting from 0; odd numbers fill the gaps.
  • Multiples of 5 appear at 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, … — spaced 5 apart.
  • Smaller numbers are to the left, larger numbers to the right.
  • The distance between two numbers = the bigger minus the smaller.

§3.4 — Playing with Digits / Digit Sum (In Depth)

Every number is made of digits (0–9). Once we know the digits, we can play many games with them — the simplest of which is the digit sum.

What is a digit sum?

The digit sum of a number is what you get when you add up all its digits. If the result is more than 9, you can add the digits of that answer again, and keep going until you reach a single digit (called the digital root).

Examples.

  • 239: 2 + 3 + 9 = 14; 1 + 4 = 5 (digital root).
  • 1998: 1 + 9 + 9 + 8 = 27; 2 + 7 = 9.
  • 72: 7 + 2 = 9.

Why is the digit sum useful?

  • If the digit sum of a number is divisible by 3, the number itself is divisible by 3.
  • If the digit sum is divisible by 9, the number itself is divisible by 9.
  • It is a fast way to check addition: digit-sum(A) + digit-sum(B) should equal digit-sum(A+B).

Other digit games

  • Reverse the digits: 123 ↔ 321. A number that reads the same forwards and backwards is a palindrome (§3.5).
  • Smallest and largest with given digits: from 4, 7, 2 you can make 247 (smallest) and 742 (largest).
  • Rearranging digits to get particular sums or products is a classic puzzle.

§3.5 — Palindromes (In Depth)

A palindrome is a number (or a word) that reads exactly the same forwards and backwards.

  • Number palindromes: 7, 11, 121, 232, 1331, 12321, 45654.
  • Word palindromes: mom, level, radar, noon.
  • Date palindromes: 02/02/2020 reads the same both ways.

How to test if a number is a palindrome

  1. Write down its digits.
  2. Reverse the order of the digits.
  3. If the reversed number is the same as the original, it is a palindrome.

The reverse-and-add trick

Take any 2-digit number that is not already a palindrome. Add it to its reverse. If the answer is not a palindrome, do it again with the answer. Surprisingly, you usually reach a palindrome in just a few steps!

Example. Start with 47. 47 + 74 = 121 (palindrome in just 1 step!) Try 59: 59 + 95 = 154; 154 + 451 = 605; 605 + 506 = 1111. Three steps!

Curiosity: for the number 196 nobody has yet been able to find a palindrome by reverse-and-add even after billions of steps! Mathematicians call such numbers “Lychrel candidates.”

§3.6 — The Kaprekar Mystery: 6174 (In Depth)

This is one of the most magical discoveries in number play. It was found by the Indian mathematician D. R. Kaprekar in 1949.

Kaprekar’s routine for 4-digit numbers

  1. Pick any 4-digit number whose digits are not all the same (e.g. not 1111 or 2222).
  2. Arrange its digits in descending order to get the largest number.
  3. Arrange its digits in ascending order to get the smallest number.
  4. Subtract: largest − smallest. Use the answer as the new number.
  5. Repeat steps 2–4.

Amazing fact: within at most 7 steps, you always reach 6174 — and after that the process just keeps giving 6174 forever! 6174 is called Kaprekar’s constant.

Worked example. Start with 3524.
Step 1: 5432 − 2345 = 3087
Step 2: 8730 − 0378 = 8352
Step 3: 8532 − 2358 = 6174.
We hit 6174 in 3 steps. From here, 7641 − 1467 = 6174 again, and again, forever.

For 3-digit numbers, the Kaprekar constant is 495. Try it with 352: 532 − 235 = 297; 972 − 279 = 693; 963 − 369 = 594; 954 − 459 = 495. Done!

§3.7 — Numbers in Our Lives: Time and Dates (In Depth)

Numbers are part of every minute of your day. Two of the most common uses are time and date.

Time

  • 1 minute = 60 seconds.
  • 1 hour = 60 minutes = 3600 seconds.
  • 1 day = 24 hours = 1440 minutes = 86 400 seconds.
  • We use 12-hour clocks (am/pm) and 24-hour clocks (00:00 to 23:59).

Conversion practice. How many minutes is 2 h 35 min? 2 × 60 + 35 = 155 minutes. How many hours and minutes is 200 minutes? 200 ÷ 60 = 3 r 20, so 3 h 20 min.

Dates and the calendar

  • 1 year = 12 months = 365 days (366 in a leap year).
  • Leap year rule: divisible by 4, but if divisible by 100 it must also be divisible by 400. So 2000 is a leap year; 1900 is not.
  • Months with 31 days: Jan, Mar, May, Jul, Aug, Oct, Dec. Months with 30 days: Apr, Jun, Sep, Nov. Feb has 28 (29 in leap).

Date palindromes

If we write dates as DD-MM-YYYY, then 02-02-2020 reads 02022020 — a palindrome! How many such dates are there in your life? It is a fun research question.

§3.8 — Mental Math & Estimation (In Depth)

You don’t always need an exact answer. Often you just need a quick estimate to make a decision — will the milk cost more than ₹100? Will all of us fit in this jeep? These are estimation questions.

Rounding to the nearest 10, 100, 1000

  • Find the place you want to round to (tens, hundreds, etc.).
  • Look at the next digit on the right.
  • If it is 5 or more, round up. If less than 5, round down.

Examples.

  • 47 rounded to the nearest 10 → 50 (because 7 ≥ 5).
  • 43 rounded to the nearest 10 → 40 (because 3 < 5).
  • 2 463 rounded to the nearest 100 → 2 500.
  • 2 463 rounded to the nearest 1000 → 2 000.

Quick mental tricks

  • Multiplying by 10, 100, 1000: just stick zeros on the right. 47 × 100 = 4700.
  • Multiplying by 9: multiply by 10 and subtract the original. 47 × 9 = 470 − 47 = 423.
  • Multiplying by 11 (2-digit): split the digits and put their sum in the middle. 36 × 11: 3, (3+6), 6 → 396.
  • Squares of numbers ending in 5: 25² = (2×3)25 = 625; 65² = (6×7)25 = 4225.

§3.9 — Number Patterns in Grids (In Depth)

When you write numbers in rows and columns (a grid), brand-new patterns suddenly appear — ones that are invisible in a single line.

The 100-grid (number chart 1–100)

Write the numbers 1 to 100 in 10 rows of 10. You will notice:

  • Going right: +1.
  • Going down: +10.
  • Going diagonally down-right: +11.
  • Going diagonally down-left: +9.
  • All multiples of 5 line up in two columns; all even numbers in five columns.

Magic squares

A magic square is a square grid in which every row, every column and both diagonals add up to the same total — the magic constant. The classic 3×3 magic square uses the numbers 1–9:

276
951
438

Every row, column and diagonal here adds to 15. Can you check?

§3.10 — The Collatz Conjecture (In Depth)

This is one of the most famous unsolved problems in mathematics. The rules are unbelievably simple:

  1. Pick any positive whole number.
  2. If it is even, divide it by 2.
  3. If it is odd, multiply it by 3 and add 1.
  4. Repeat with the new number.

The Collatz conjecture says: no matter what number you start with, you will eventually reach 1.

Example. Start with 6.

6 → 3 → 10 → 5 → 16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1. ✓

Start with 7. 7 → 22 → 11 → 34 → 17 → 52 → 26 → 13 → 40 → 20 → 10 → 5 → 16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1. (16 steps!)

Mathematicians have checked the conjecture for every number up to about 268 (that’s about 295 billion billion!) and it always works. But nobody has been able to prove it works for every number. The conjecture is so famous that the great mathematician Paul Erdős said, “Mathematics is not yet ready for such problems.”

🎯 Big picture of Chapter 3

  • Numbers can count, order, label or measure — each tells a different story.
  • In a grid, a supercell beats all its neighbours.
  • The number line visualises addition, subtraction and patterns.
  • Digit sums, palindromes, Kaprekar’s 6174 and the Collatz conjecture show that numbers hide endless surprises.
  • Estimation and mental math help us decide quickly in real life.

🧠 Extra Worked Examples (across all subtopics)

Example A (Supercells). In the row 12, 45, 8, 33, 50, 22, 60, 15, find all the supercells.

Solution. Compare each cell with its neighbours:

  • 12 has only one neighbour (45). 12 < 45 → not a supercell.
  • 45: neighbours 12 and 8. 45 > both → supercell.
  • 8: neighbours 45 and 33. 8 < both → not a supercell.
  • 33: neighbours 8 and 50. 33 < 50 → not a supercell.
  • 50: neighbours 33 and 22. 50 > both → supercell.
  • 22: neighbours 50 and 60. 22 < both → not a supercell.
  • 60: neighbours 22 and 15. 60 > both → supercell.
  • 15: only neighbour 60. 15 < 60 → not a supercell.

Supercells: 45, 50, 60.

Example B (Digit Sum). Find the digital root of 87 654.

8 + 7 + 6 + 5 + 4 = 30; 3 + 0 = 3.

Since the digital root is 3, the number 87 654 is divisible by 3. Quick check: 87 654 ÷ 3 = 29 218. ✓

Example C (Reverse-and-add palindrome). Starting from 68, how many steps to reach a palindrome?

Step 1: 68 + 86 = 154 (not a palindrome).
Step 2: 154 + 451 = 605.
Step 3: 605 + 506 = 1111. Palindrome in 3 steps.

Example D (Kaprekar 3-digit). Start with 287.
872 − 278 = 594.
954 − 459 = 495.
954 − 459 = 495.
Reaches the 3-digit Kaprekar constant 495 in 2 steps.

Example E (Time conversion). A film lasts 2 h 47 min. The interval is 20 min. What is the total time?

2 h 47 min + 20 min = 2 h 67 min = 3 h 7 min.

Example F (Estimation). A jeep can carry 1 driver + 6 passengers. Estimate how many jeeps are needed for 38 students + 4 teachers.

Total people = 42. Each jeep carries 6 passengers (driver doesn’t count for passengers).

42 ÷ 6 = 7 exactly, so 7 jeeps are enough.

Example G (Mental math). Compute 25 × 99 mentally.

25 × 99 = 25 × 100 − 25 = 2500 − 25 = 2475.

Example H (Magic square check). Verify the magic constant of the classic 3×3 magic square.

Row 1: 2 + 7 + 6 = 15. Row 2: 9 + 5 + 1 = 15. Row 3: 4 + 3 + 8 = 15.
Column 1: 2 + 9 + 4 = 15. Column 2: 7 + 5 + 3 = 15. Column 3: 6 + 1 + 8 = 15.
Diagonal: 2 + 5 + 8 = 15. Anti-diagonal: 6 + 5 + 4 = 15.

All add to 15. ✓

Example I (Collatz length). How many steps does it take 12 to reach 1?

12 → 6 → 3 → 10 → 5 → 16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1. 9 steps.

Example J (Date palindrome). Is 12-02-2021 a palindrome?

Reading 12022021 forwards: 1-2-0-2-2-0-2-1. Reading backwards: 1-2-0-2-2-0-2-1. Yes — it is a palindrome!

⚠️ Common mistakes in number play

  • Supercell at the edge: A cell at the start/end of a row has only one neighbour. Don’t pretend it has two!
  • Digit sum: Forgetting to keep adding until you get a single digit (when the question asks for the digital root).
  • Kaprekar 4-digit: Forgetting to write leading zeros. If the descending arrangement gives 7300, then the ascending is 0037, not just 37.
  • Estimation: Rounding 745 to the nearest 10 → 750 (not 740); the digit being checked is 5, so we round up.
  • Collatz: When the number is 1, we stop. Don’t apply the rules again (3 × 1 + 1 = 4 would create a loop).
Quick Reference — Chapter 3Short summary cards (the original briefer version follows)

§3.1–3.2 — Numbers Can Tell Us Things & Supercells

What is a Supercell? A number in a table is a supercell if it is greater than all its neighbours (the numbers in cells next to it).

Maximum supercells: In a row of n cells, you can have at most ⌈n/2⌉ supercells (alternating large, small, large, small...)

§3.4 — Digit Sum

The digit sum of a number is found by adding all its digits together.

Examples:

  • Digit sum of 365 = 3 + 6 + 5 = 14
  • Digit sum of 9999 = 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 = 36
  • Digit sum of 10000 = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1

Key facts:

  • Smallest number with digit sum 14 = 59 (5+9=14)
  • For 3 consecutive digits (like 345): digit sum is always a multiple of 3
  • How many 2-digit numbers with digit sum 5? → 14, 23, 32, 41, 50 = 5 numbers

§3.5 — Palindromes

A palindrome is a number that reads the same forwards and backwards.

Examples: 121, 1331, 12321, 5005

Making palindromes: Take any number, reverse it, add. Repeat until you get a palindrome!

Example: 47 → 47 + 74 = 121 ✓ (palindrome in 1 step!)

§3.6 — The Kaprekar Mystery (6174)

For any 4-digit number (with at least 2 different digits):

  1. Arrange digits in descending order (biggest first)
  2. Arrange digits in ascending order (smallest first)
  3. Subtract the smaller from the larger
  4. Repeat with the result

You will ALWAYS reach 6174 (the Kaprekar constant)!

§3.10 — The Collatz Conjecture

Start with ANY positive whole number. Apply these rules:

  • If the number is even: divide by 2
  • If the number is odd: multiply by 3 and add 1

Keep going. You will ALWAYS reach 1! (We think... nobody has proved it yet!)

Example: Start with 7 → 22 → 11 → 34 → 17 → 52 → 26 → 13 → 40 → 20 → 10 → 5 → 16 → 8 → 4 → 2 → 1 ✓

💡 This is one of the greatest unsolved problems in mathematics! A $1 million prize awaits whoever proves it.

§3.11 — Simple Estimation

Sometimes we don’t need an exact count — a quick estimate is enough. Estimation is the art of finding a number that is “close enough” without doing a long calculation.

Example (from NCERT). Paromita’s class section has 32 children. The other two sections of her class have 29 and 35 children. She estimated the number of children in Class 6 as “about 100”. Her school also has Classes 7–10, each with 3 sections. Assuming a similar number in each class, she estimated the total students as about 500.

Strategies that work:

  • Round to easy numbers — 29 + 32 + 35 ≈ 30 + 30 + 30 = 90 ≈ 100.
  • Use a sample — count one row of a packet of biscuits, then multiply by the number of rows.
  • Use known facts — one year ≈ 365 days, one hour = 60 minutes, etc.

💡 Good estimators check their guess against a quick rough calculation. Estimating well is a real-life super-power!

§3.12 — Games and Winning Strategies

Numbers can also be used to play games and discover winning strategies.

Game 1 — Reach 21. The first player says “1”, “2” or “3”. Players take turns adding 1, 2, or 3 to the previous number. The first player to reach 21 wins.

Winning strategy: the player who can say 1, 5, 9, 13, 17, 21 (i.e. numbers of the form 4k + 1) is guaranteed to win. If you can say 17, then whatever your opponent adds (1, 2 or 3), you can always reach 21.

Game 2 — Reach 99. Players take turns adding any number from 1 to 10 to the running total. First to reach 99 wins. (Try to find the pattern of “safe” numbers: 99 − 11, 99 − 22, …)

💡 Mathematics is not just about answers — finding a strategy that always works is a beautiful kind of pattern-spotting!

Figure it Out (§3.11–3.12) — Q1. Estimate the number of steps you would take to walk: (a) from your bench to the classroom door, (b) across the school ground, (c) from the school gate to your home.

Solution:

Estimates depend on each student’s school! Typical ranges:

  • Bench to door: about 10–20 steps.
  • Across the school ground: about 80–150 steps.
  • School gate to home (if 1 km away): a person takes about 1300–1500 steps per km.

Share your estimate with classmates and check by actually pacing it!

Figure it Out (§3.11–3.12) — Q2. Estimate how many times you blink your eyes (a) in a minute, (b) in an hour, (c) in a day.

Solution:

A typical person blinks 15–20 times per minute when relaxed.

  • In 1 minute: about 20 blinks.
  • In 1 hour: 20 × 60 = about 1,200 blinks.
  • In a day (16 waking hours): 1,200 × 16 = about 19,200 blinks.

Figure it Out (§3.11–3.12) — Q3. Play Game #1 (Reach 21). Which player can always win if they play correctly? What numbers should the winning player say?

Solution:

The first player can always win. The winning strategy: say 1 on your first move, then on every turn answer so that your number is 5, 9, 13, 17, 21. These are the “safe” numbers (each is 4 more than the previous). Whatever the opponent adds (1, 2 or 3), you can always bring the total back to the next safe number.

Figure it Out (§3.11–3.12) — Q4. In a Variant Game, players add 1 to 3 starting from 0; the first to reach 22 wins. What is the winning strategy now?

Solution:

22 ≡ 2 (mod 4). So the “safe” numbers are 2, 6, 10, 14, 18, 22. The first player wins by saying 2 first, then mirroring to stay on the safe-number ladder.

Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved with explanations

📝 Quick Recap Before Exercises

  • Supercell: Number greater than ALL its neighbours
  • Digit sum: Add all digits of a number (e.g., 479 → 4+7+9=20)
  • Palindrome: Same forwards and backwards (e.g., 12321)
  • Kaprekar process: Descending − Ascending → always reaches 6174 (4-digit) or 495 (3-digit)
  • Collatz: Even→÷2, Odd→×3+1, always reaches 1

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Supercells: Don't forget — corner cells only need to be bigger than ONE neighbour!
  • Kaprekar: If any step gives a 3-digit number, add a leading zero (e.g., 999 → 0999)
  • Palindromes: 10, 20, 30 are NOT palindromes (01 ≠ 10)
  • Digit sums: Don't confuse with the number itself! Digit sum of 100 = 1, not 100

Figure it Out — Section 3.1 (Numbers Tell Us Things)

Q. Think about various situations where we use numbers. List five different situations.

Solution:

  1. Time — 10:30 AM
