    Solution. Outer = 110 × 70 = 7700 m²; field = 6000 m²; track = 1700 m².
  7. Q7. The cost of fencing a rectangular field at ₹25 per metre is ₹7000. If the length is 60 m, find the breadth.
    Solution. Perimeter = 7000/25 = 280 m ⇒ l + b = 140 ⇒ b = 80 m.
  8. Q8. A rectangular swimming pool is 25 m long and 12 m wide. A tiled walkway 2 m wide surrounds it. Find the area of the walkway.
    Solution. Outer = 29 × 16 = 464 m²; pool = 300 m²; walkway = 164 m².

📝 Mini-quiz — Chapter 6

  1. The perimeter of an equilateral triangle of side 9 cm is ____.
  2. The area of a square of side 11 cm is ____.
  3. If a rectangle has perimeter 60 m and length 18 m, its breadth is ____.
  4. A regular nonagon (9 sides) of side 8 cm has perimeter ____.
  5. The area of a 5 m × 4 m rectangle is ____.
  6. The area of a square that has perimeter 48 cm is ____.
  7. One tile is 25 cm × 25 cm. A 5 m × 4 m floor needs ____ tiles.
  8. If the cost of fencing per metre is ₹30 and perimeter is 200 m, total cost is ____.
Answers:
  1. 27 cm
  2. 121 cm²
  3. 12 m
  4. 72 cm
  5. 20 m²
  6. 144 cm²
  7. 320 tiles
  8. ₹6000

🎯 Chapter 6 — Key takeaways

  • Perimeter is the total distance around a closed figure (units: cm, m, km).
  • Area is the space enclosed by a figure (units: cm², m², hectare, km²).
  • For a rectangle: P = 2(l + b), A = l × b.
  • For a square: P = 4s, A = s².
  • For a regular polygon with n sides: P = n × side.
  • For compound shapes, split into rectangles/squares and add or subtract.
Advanced Practice Lab — Chapter 6Detailed worked solutions, real-life perimeter & area problems, and a final review test

Detailed walkthrough — the football field problem

A standard football field in a school is 90 m long and 45 m wide.

Part (a): Find the perimeter of the field.

Step 1. Identify the shape — rectangle.

Step 2. Recall the formula: P = 2 (l + b).

Step 3. Substitute l = 90 m and b = 45 m.

Step 4. P = 2 (90 + 45) = 2 × 135 = 270 m.

Part (b): A boundary wire is to be put up around the field with three rows. How much wire is needed?

Total wire = 3 × perimeter = 3 × 270 = 810 m.

Part (c): Find the area of the field.

A = l × b = 90 × 45 = 4050 m².

Part (d): Find the cost of laying grass at ₹25 per m².

Cost = 4050 × 25 = ₹1,01,250.

Part (e): A coach jogs around the field 5 times. How far did he jog?

Distance = 5 × perimeter = 5 × 270 = 1350 m = 1.35 km.

Detailed walkthrough — the framed picture problem

A rectangular photograph is 24 cm long and 18 cm wide. It is mounted in a frame that adds a uniform border of 3 cm all around.

Part (a): Find the outer dimensions of the framed picture.

Outer length = 24 + 2 × 3 = 30 cm; outer width = 18 + 2 × 3 = 24 cm.

Part (b): Find the outer perimeter.

P = 2 (30 + 24) = 108 cm.

Part (c): Find the outer area.

A_outer = 30 × 24 = 720 cm².

Part (d): Find the inner photograph area.

A_inner = 24 × 18 = 432 cm².

Part (e): Find the area of the frame (the border alone).

A_frame = 720 − 432 = 288 cm².

Part (f): If the frame is painted at ₹0.50 per cm², total cost?

Cost = 288 × 0.50 = ₹144.

Detailed walkthrough — the tile-laying problem

The floor of a hall is 12 m long and 9 m wide. Square tiles of side 60 cm are to be laid.

Step 1. Convert units consistently. 12 m = 1200 cm; 9 m = 900 cm; tile side = 60 cm.

Step 2. Floor area = 1200 × 900 = 1 080 000 cm².

Step 3. Tile area = 60 × 60 = 3600 cm².

Step 4. Number of tiles = 1 080 000 / 3600 = 300 tiles.

Step 5. Verify by counting in rows and columns:

Tiles along the length = 1200 / 60 = 20.

Tiles along the width = 900 / 60 = 15.

Total tiles = 20 × 15 = 300. ✔

Step 6. If each tile costs ₹45, total cost of tiles = 300 × 45 = ₹13,500.

Detailed walkthrough — the running track problem

A rectangular sports ground measures 70 m by 40 m. A path 3 m wide runs all around the outside of the ground.

Step 1. Find the outer dimensions (including the path).

Outer length = 70 + 2 × 3 = 76 m.

Outer width = 40 + 2 × 3 = 46 m.

Step 2. Outer area = 76 × 46 = 3496 m².

Step 3. Inner ground area = 70 × 40 = 2800 m².

Step 4. Path area = 3496 − 2800 = 696 m².

Step 5. If gravel for the path costs ₹120 per m², total cost = 696 × 120 = ₹83,520.

Real-life perimeter & area in your home

Here are some everyday problems that use perimeter and area.

Problem 1 — Wallpaper: a bedroom wall is 4 m long and 3 m high. How many m² of wallpaper are needed to cover it (ignore door and window)?

Wall area = 4 × 3 = 12 m².

Problem 2 — Curtain rod: a window is 1.5 m wide. The curtain rod should be 30 cm wider on each side. What is its length?

Rod length = 1.5 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 2.1 m.

Problem 3 — Garden fence: a square garden has side 8 m. Fencing costs ₹180 per metre. Total cost?

P = 4 × 8 = 32 m; cost = 32 × 180 = ₹5760.

Problem 4 — Painting: a square wall has side 5 m. One litre of paint covers 8 m². How many litres are needed?

Area = 25 m²; litres needed = 25 / 8 = 3.125, round up to 4 litres.

Problem 5 — Stitching a tablecloth: a rectangular tabletop is 1.5 m × 1 m. A tablecloth should hang 20 cm below the edge on all sides. Find the cloth’s length and breadth.

Cloth length = 1.5 + 2 × 0.2 = 1.9 m; cloth breadth = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4 m.

Problem 6 — Border stripe: a square handkerchief of side 25 cm has a decorative ribbon stitched all around its edge. Length of ribbon?

4 × 25 = 100 cm = 1 m.

📝 Comprehensive review test — Chapter 6

Q1. A rectangular plot is 80 m long and 50 m wide. Perimeter is ____ and area is ____.

Q2. The cost of fencing a square field of side 35 m at ₹75 per m is ____.

Q3. A regular octagon of side 12 cm has perimeter ____.

Q4. Square tiles of side 30 cm are used to cover a 6 m × 3 m floor. Number of tiles needed = ____.

Q5. The area of a square equals its perimeter (numerically). The side of the square is ____.

Q6. A rectangle has perimeter 50 m and breadth 10 m. Its area is ____.

Q7. A garden 30 m × 20 m has a square pond of side 6 m cut out. The grassy area is ____ m².

Q8. The walls of a room 5 m long, 4 m wide and 3 m high are painted. Total wall area (ignore doors/windows) = ____.

Answers:
  1. Perimeter = 260 m, Area = 4000 m²
  2. P = 140 m; cost = 140 × 75 = ₹10,500
  3. P = 96 cm
  4. 200 tiles
  5. Side = 4 (units), since 4s = s² gives s = 4
  6. Length = 15 m, area = 150 m²
  7. 564 m²
  8. 2(5+4) × 3 = 54 m²

Conversion of units — cm, m, km, hectare

Lengths and areas are measured in different units. Conversions are simple but easy to mix up.

Length conversions:

1 km = 1000 m.

1 m = 100 cm.

1 cm = 10 mm.

Area conversions:

1 m² = 100 cm × 100 cm = 10 000 cm².

1 km² = 1000 m × 1000 m = 1 000 000 m².

1 hectare = 10 000 m² = 100 m × 100 m (a square of side 100 m).

1 acre ≈ 4047 m² ≈ 0.4 hectare.

Worked example. A field has area 50 000 m². Express in hectares.

50 000 / 10 000 = 5 hectares.

Worked example. A floor is 4 m × 3 m. Express its area in cm².

4 m = 400 cm; 3 m = 300 cm. Area = 400 × 300 = 1 20 000 cm².

Worked example. A rectangle has length 1.5 km and breadth 800 m. Find area in m².

1.5 km = 1500 m. Area = 1500 × 800 = 12 00 000 m² = 1.2 km².

🎯 Chapter 6 — The big picture

  • Perimeter is a 1-D quantity (length); area is a 2-D quantity (length × length).
  • Two figures can have the same perimeter but different areas (e.g. 6×1 and 3×4 rectangles both have P = 14 but areas 6 and 12).
  • Two figures can have the same area but different perimeters (e.g. 6×1 and 2×3 both have area 6 but perimeters 14 and 10).
  • Real-life uses: fencing (perimeter), tiling/painting/carpeting (area).

Estimating area — useful skill in daily life

It is often impractical to measure something exactly. We need to estimate.

Estimation by approximation. A doorway is about 2 m tall and 1 m wide — area ≈ 2 m². A football field is about 90 m by 50 m — area ≈ 4500 m².

Estimation by unit squares. Place a transparent grid sheet over an irregular leaf or country map. Count the full squares inside, then add roughly half the count of partial squares. This gives a good estimate of the area.

Estimation by enclosing rectangle. An irregular shape can be enclosed in the smallest rectangle that contains it; the shape's area is some fraction of that rectangle's area.

Worked example. A leaf placed on a 1 cm grid covers about 35 full squares and 12 partial squares.

Estimated area = 35 + 12/2 = 41 cm².

Practical use. Land surveyors and city planners estimate areas this way before precise calculations.

Perimeter and area of an irregular field — case study

A piece of land has the shape of an L. The longer side is 20 m, the longer top edge is 15 m, the indent on the right is 5 m wide and 8 m deep.

Step 1. Visualise the L by splitting it into two rectangles.

Step 2. Bigger rectangle = 15 m × 12 m (the longer side minus the indent depth).

Step 3. Smaller rectangle = 10 m × 8 m (the bottom-right piece below the cut).

Step 4. Total area = (15 × 12) + (10 × 8) = 180 + 80 = 260 m².

Step 5. Perimeter: add all outer edges going around the L.

= 15 + 12 + 5 + 8 + 10 + 20 = 70 m.

Lesson: always split a compound figure into known shapes (rectangles, squares, triangles) and add up their areas. For perimeter, simply walk around the figure!

Quick reference card — perimeter & area formulas

Keep this card handy whenever you face a perimeter or area question.

Square of side s: P = 4s; A = s².

Rectangle of length l and breadth b: P = 2(l + b); A = l × b.

Equilateral triangle of side s: P = 3s.

Isosceles triangle with equal sides a and base b: P = 2a + b.

Scalene triangle with sides a, b, c: P = a + b + c.

Regular polygon with n sides of length s: P = ns.

Compound shape: split into rectangles → sum (or subtract) areas.

Boundary of irregular shape: walk around and add edge lengths.

Estimating area from grid: full squares + half the partial squares.

💡 Final pro tips for Chapter 6

  • Always include units in your answer (cm, m, cm², m², …).
  • Double-check whether the question asks for perimeter or area — they are different concepts.
  • For compound shapes, draw the figure first and label all known lengths.
  • For unknown dimensions, write an equation and solve carefully.
  • Practise estimation — it is a powerful sanity check on your answers.
  • Memorise the basic formulas; do not derive them every time.
NCERT Deep Dive — Chapter 6Following the NCERT chapter “Perimeter and Area”

A guided tour of NCERT "Perimeter and Area"

The NCERT chapter develops the concept of perimeter and area through hands-on activities: walking around a rectangular playground, fencing a kitchen garden, tiling a wall, measuring leaves with a grid. The chapter uses the following thread of ideas:

  1. The intuitive meaning of perimeter — the total length of a boundary.
  2. Practical examples — fence, frame, lace edging.
  3. Perimeter formulas for rectangles, squares and regular polygons.
  4. The intuitive meaning of area — the number of unit squares that cover a shape.
  5. Area formulas for rectangles and squares.
  6. Estimation of irregular areas from a grid.
  7. Composite figures — break into rectangles, add or subtract.
  8. Real-life applications — carpets, tiles, painting walls, fencing fields.

By the end you should be able to (a) measure or compute the perimeter and area of any rectilinear shape, (b) estimate the area of any irregular shape, and (c) solve word problems with mixed units.

Perimeter from first principles — the walk-around method

The simplest way to find the perimeter is to walk around the boundary, adding lengths as you go.

Example 1. A rectangle 8 cm by 5 cm. Walk: 8 + 5 + 8 + 5 = 26 cm. Formula: P = 2(L + W) = 2(8+5) = 26 cm.

Example 2. An L-shaped figure made by joining a 6 by 4 rectangle on top of a 10 by 3 rectangle:

   <------6------>
   _____________
  |             |
  |   4 cm tall |
  |_____________|_________
  |                       |
  |   3 cm tall           |
  |_______________________|
   <-------10------------>

Walking around: top 6 + right side of upper rectangle 4 + step right (10−6) = 4 + right side of lower rectangle 3 + bottom 10 + left side of L 3+4 = 7.

Total: 6 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 10 + 7 = 34 cm.

Notice that the “step” and the “notch” cancel exactly with the original 6 to give the same perimeter as the outer 10×7 rectangle would have had! In fact for any rectilinear shape with horizontal and vertical edges, the perimeter equals 2 × (length of bounding box + width of bounding box).

Example 3. A regular hexagon with side 5 cm. Perimeter = 6 × 5 = 30 cm.

Example 4. An irregular pentagon with sides 7, 4, 6, 3, 5 cm. Perimeter = 7+4+6+3+5 = 25 cm.

Area through the unit-square method

NCERT teaches area as “the number of unit squares that cover the shape”.

Worked example 1. A rectangle 6 cm by 4 cm. Draw it on cm graph paper. Count squares row by row: 6 squares per row, 4 rows → 6 × 4 = 24 cm².

Worked example 2. A square 5 cm by 5 cm. 5 × 5 = 25 cm².

Worked example 3. An L-shape 6×4 + 10×3 (from previous topic). Two rectangles: 6×4 = 24 plus 10×3 = 30 → total 54 cm². (Be careful that the overlap is zero or correctly subtracted; in this L the rectangles share only an edge, not area.)

Estimation of irregular area — the leaf method.

  1. Lay a transparent grid (1 cm) over the leaf.
  2. Count full squares inside the leaf — call this F.
  3. Count more-than-half squares partially inside — call this M.
  4. Count exactly-half squares — call this H.
  5. Ignore squares less than half inside.
  6. Estimated area ≈ F + M + (H/2).

For a typical mango leaf you might count F=18, M=12, H=8 → area ≈ 18 + 12 + 4 = 34 cm².

Same perimeter, different area — and vice versa

This is a key insight emphasized in NCERT. Two shapes can have the same perimeter but completely different areas, and vice versa.

Demonstration 1 — same perimeter, different area. Take 24 cm of string. Form three rectangles:

  • 11 cm × 1 cm → area 11 cm²
  • 9 cm × 3 cm → area 27 cm²
  • 6 cm × 6 cm → area 36 cm² (square)

All have perimeter 24 cm, yet the areas range from 11 to 36 cm². The square gives the largest area among rectangles with a fixed perimeter — this is a deep mathematical principle.

Demonstration 2 — same area, different perimeter. Find three rectangles of area 36 cm²:

  • 36 cm × 1 cm → perimeter 74 cm
  • 12 cm × 3 cm → perimeter 30 cm
  • 6 cm × 6 cm → perimeter 24 cm (square again is most efficient)

Practical implication: if you have a fixed amount of fencing, you maximise the enclosed area by making the shape as “square-like” as possible. Conversely, to enclose a fixed area with the least fencing, again pick a square.

Real-life perimeter and area problems

NCERT word problems often use familiar contexts — gardens, rooms, fields, photo frames.

Problem 1 (fence). A rectangular garden is 25 m long and 18 m wide. How much fencing is needed? If fencing costs ₹40 per metre, how much will it cost?

Solution: P = 2(25+18) = 86 m. Cost = 86 × 40 = ₹3440.

Problem 2 (tiling). A bathroom floor is 5 m by 4 m. Square tiles of side 50 cm are to be laid. How many tiles are needed?

Solution: convert 5 m = 500 cm; 4 m = 400 cm. Tiles along length: 500/50 = 10. Tiles along width: 400/50 = 8. Total: 10 × 8 = 80 tiles.

Problem 3 (carpet). A room is 6.5 m by 4.5 m. A carpet covers a 5.0 m by 3.5 m portion. What area of floor is uncovered?

Solution: Floor area = 6.5 × 4.5 = 29.25 m². Carpet area = 5.0 × 3.5 = 17.5 m². Uncovered = 29.25 − 17.5 = 11.75 m².

Problem 4 (painting). A wall is 8 m by 3 m. There is a door 2 m by 1.2 m on the wall. What is the area to be painted?

Solution: Wall = 8 × 3 = 24 m². Door = 2 × 1.2 = 2.4 m². Paintable area = 24 − 2.4 = 21.6 m².

Problem 5 (frame). A photo of size 8 cm × 6 cm is placed inside a frame leaving a 2 cm border all around. Find the outer dimensions and the area of the border.

Solution: outer length = 8 + 2×2 = 12 cm. Outer width = 6 + 2×2 = 10 cm. Outer area = 12 × 10 = 120 cm². Photo area = 8 × 6 = 48 cm². Border area = 120 − 48 = 72 cm².

Problem 6 (path). A rectangular field 50 m by 30 m has a 2 m wide path running all around inside the field. Find the area of the path.

Solution: outer field = 50 × 30 = 1500 m². Inner rectangle = (50−4) × (30−4) = 46 × 26 = 1196 m². Path area = 1500 − 1196 = 304 m².

Area of triangles — a sneak preview

Although the formal triangle area formula comes in Class 7, NCERT introduces the idea via the “two triangles make a rectangle” trick.

The rectangle-cut trick. Cut any rectangle diagonally. You get two congruent right triangles. So each right triangle has half the area of the rectangle.

If the rectangle is L by W, each right triangle has area = (L × W) / 2 = base × height / 2.

Worked example. A right triangle with legs 6 cm and 4 cm. Area = 6 × 4 / 2 = 12 cm².

Non-right triangles. Drop a perpendicular from one vertex to the opposite side (the base). The perpendicular length is the “height” or “altitude”. Area = base × height / 2.

Example. A triangle with base 10 cm and height 7 cm has area 10 × 7 / 2 = 35 cm².

Activity. Cut a paper triangle. Fold it carefully to fit inside a rectangle that has the same base and the same height. The triangle covers exactly half. Hence triangle area = base × height / 2.

Mixed units — a survival guide

Many real problems mix metres, centimetres, kilometres. Always convert to a common unit before computing.

Length conversions: 1 m = 100 cm; 1 km = 1000 m = 100,000 cm.

Area conversions: 1 m² = 10,000 cm² (since 100 × 100 cm); 1 km² = 1,000,000 m².

Example. Express 350 cm + 2.5 m as cm. 2.5 m = 250 cm. Total = 600 cm.

Example. A field is 0.5 ha (hectare). What is its area in m²? 1 ha = 10,000 m², so 0.5 ha = 5000 m².

Example. A square plot has area 1 km². What is its side in metres? Side = √1,000,000 = 1000 m.

History of area measurement

The concept of area is among the oldest in mathematics.

  • Ancient Egypt (c. 3000 BCE). Annual flooding of the Nile washed away farm boundaries. Royal surveyors (the “rope-stretchers”) used ropes and the 3-4-5 right triangle to redraw plots after each flood. This is one of the earliest applications of geometry.
  • Indus Valley Civilization (c. 2500 BCE). Bricks at Mohenjo-daro were made with the standard ratio 1 : 2 : 4 (height : width : length). City planning used measured grids.
  • Sulba Sutras (c. 800 BCE). Indian texts describing geometric constructions for altars. They contain the earliest known statement of the Pythagorean theorem.
  • Euclid (c. 300 BCE). In Elements, gave systematic proofs of area properties.
  • Aryabhata (476 CE). Computed areas of triangles and discussed approximations of π.
  • Modern metric system (1795). Introduced in France; uses m, m², ha for areas. Adopted worldwide.

📝 NCERT-style review questions — Chapter 6

Q1. Find the perimeter of a square of side 7.5 cm.

Q2. A rectangle has length 12 m and area 96 m². Find its width and perimeter.

Q3. Two rectangles have the same perimeter, 20 cm. One is 8 cm long. Find the dimensions of the other if its area is 25 cm².

Q4. A rectangular field 40 m by 25 m has a 2.5 m wide path outside it all around. Find the area of the path.

Q5. A triangle has base 14 cm and height 9 cm. Find its area.

Q6. Convert 4.5 m² into cm².

Q7. A square sheet of side 1 m is cut into smaller squares of side 10 cm. How many small squares?

Q8. A picture 30 cm by 20 cm is framed by a 3 cm wide border. Find the area of the border.

Answers:
  1. 4 × 7.5 = 30 cm.
  2. Width = 96/12 = 8 m. Perimeter = 2(12+8) = 40 m.
  3. P = 20 means L + W = 10. If A = 25, dimensions are 5 cm × 5 cm.
  4. Outer rectangle = (40+5) × (25+5) = 45 × 30 = 1350 m². Field = 40 × 25 = 1000 m². Path = 1350 − 1000 = 350 m².
  5. 14 × 9 / 2 = 63 cm².
  6. 4.5 m² = 4.5 × 10,000 = 45,000 cm².
  7. 1 m = 100 cm, so 100 / 10 = 10 along each side. 10 × 10 = 100 squares.
  8. Outer 36 × 26 = 936 cm². Inner 30 × 20 = 600 cm². Border = 336 cm².

📌 Chapter 6 — Perimeter & Area skill check

  • I can find the perimeter of any polygon by adding side lengths.
  • I can use the formulas P = 2(L+W) and P = 4s.
  • I can find the area of rectangles, squares, right triangles and composite shapes.
  • I can estimate the area of irregular shapes using the grid method.
  • I understand that perimeter and area are independent: same perimeter can give different areas.
  • I can solve real-life problems (fencing, tiling, painting, framing, paths).
  • I can convert freely between cm, m and km (and cm², m², km²).
Mastery Practice Pack — Chapter 6Twenty perimeter / area drills with solutions

Perimeter drills (10 questions)

  1. Square side 12 cm → P = 48 cm.
  2. Rectangle 15 cm × 7 cm → P = 44 cm.
  3. Equilateral ▵ side 9 cm → P = 27 cm.
  4. Regular pentagon side 6 cm → P = 30 cm.
  5. Regular hexagon side 4.5 cm → P = 27 cm.
  6. Regular octagon side 3 cm → P = 24 cm.
  7. Rectangle L = 25 m, W = 18 m → P = 86 m.
  8. L-shape with sides 6, 4, 4, 3, 10, 7 cm → P = 34 cm.
  9. Triangle with sides 8, 11, 13 cm → P = 32 cm.
  10. Square garden of side 35 m fenced at ₹25/m → cost = ₹3500.

Area drills (10 questions)

  1. Square side 12 cm → A = 144 cm².
  2. Rectangle 15 cm × 7 cm → A = 105 cm².
  3. Right ▵ legs 8 and 6 cm → A = 24 cm².
  4. Triangle base 14 cm, height 9 cm → A = 63 cm².
  5. L-shape (top 6×4 + bottom 10×3) → A = 24 + 30 = 54 cm².
  6. Rectangle 5 m by 3.5 m → A = 17.5 m².
  7. Square plot 50 m side → A = 2500 m².
  8. 4.5 m² → 45000 cm².
  9. Rectangle 8 m × 5 m fenced inside by 1 m path → path area = 8×5 − 6×3 = 40 − 18 = 22 m².
  10. Frame around 20×15 cm photo with 3 cm border → border area = 26×21 − 20×15 = 546 − 300 = 246 cm².

Mixed application drills (5 questions)

P1. Cost of fencing a square field of side 28 m at ₹45 per metre.

Solution. P = 112 m; cost = 112 × 45 = ₹5040.

P2. Number of square tiles of side 25 cm needed to cover a floor 5 m by 4 m.

Solution. Floor = 500×400 cm; tiles per row = 500/25 = 20, rows = 400/25 = 16; total = 320 tiles.

P3. Rectangle area 84 cm² with length 12 cm. Find width and perimeter.

Solution. Width = 7 cm; P = 38 cm.

P4. An L-room made of a 5 m × 4 m rectangle joined to a 3 m × 2 m extension. Total area?

Solution. 20 + 6 = 26 m².

P5. A rectangular garden 30 m × 20 m has a 1 m wide pathway around it on the outside. Find the area of the pathway.

Solution. Outer = 32 × 22 = 704 m²; inner = 600 m²; path = 104 m².

Formula sheet — Chapter 6 ready reckoner

ShapePerimeterArea
Square (side s)4ss²
Rectangle (L, W)2(L+W)L×W
Equilateral ▵ (side s)3s(√3/4)s² (advanced)
Right ▵ (legs a, b)a+b+√(a²+b²)(1/2)·a·b
Triangle (base b, height h)sum of sides(1/2)·b·h
Regular n-gon (side s)n×srequires apothem (later)
Circle (radius r)2πrπr² (advanced)

Unit conversions to remember:

  • 1 m = 100 cm; 1 km = 1000 m.
  • 1 m² = 10,000 cm².
  • 1 km² = 1,000,000 m² = 100 ha.
  • 1 ha (hectare) = 10,000 m².
  • 1 acre ≈ 4047 m² (slightly less than half a hectare).

Chapter 6 glossary

  • Perimeter. Total length of the boundary of a 2D shape.
  • Area. Number of unit squares that cover a 2D shape.
  • Regular polygon. A polygon with all sides equal and all angles equal.
  • Composite shape. A shape formed by combining basic shapes (rectangle + rectangle, etc.).
  • Hectare. Metric unit of area = 10,000 m² (a square 100 m by 100 m).
  • Apothem. Perpendicular from the centre of a regular polygon to one of its sides.
  • Grid estimation. Estimating area by counting unit squares on a grid.

Exam-prep question bank — Chapter 6 (25 questions)

  1. Define perimeter and area.
  2. Perimeter of a square of side 15 cm.
  3. Perimeter of a rectangle 25 cm by 15 cm.
  4. Area of a square of side 9 cm.
  5. Area of a rectangle 18 cm by 7 cm.
  6. Area of a right triangle with legs 10 cm and 6 cm.
  7. Area of a triangle with base 14 cm and height 8 cm.
  8. Convert 5 m² to cm².
  9. Convert 0.5 km² to m².
  10. Convert 2 hectares to m².
  11. Number of 25 cm square tiles to cover 4 m × 3 m floor.
  12. Cost of fencing 45 m by 30 m field at ₹40/m.
  13. A picture 25 cm by 20 cm is framed with 2 cm border. Find border area.
  14. Perimeter of a regular pentagon side 8 cm.
  15. Perimeter of a regular hexagon side 6 cm.
  16. If a rectangle's perimeter is 30 cm and length is 10 cm, find width.
  17. If a square's area is 49 cm², find its side.
  18. L-shape: 6×4 + 8×3 (total). Find area.
  19. For a fixed perimeter, which rectangle has the largest area? (Hint: square.)
  20. For a fixed area, which rectangle has the smallest perimeter?
  21. How is the area of a triangle related to a rectangle with same base & height?
  22. If side length doubles, what happens to perimeter? And area?
  23. If radius of a circle doubles, what happens to circumference?
  24. A field of area 1 ha is square. Find its side.
  25. A garden 30 m × 20 m has 2 m wide outer path. Find path area.
Answer key (click to expand)
  1. Perimeter = total length of boundary; Area = number of unit squares covering shape.
  2. 60 cm.
  3. 80 cm.
  4. 81 cm².
  5. 126 cm².
  6. 30 cm².
  7. 56 cm².
  8. 50,000 cm².
  9. 500,000 m².
  10. 20,000 m².
  11. 192 tiles.
  12. ₹6000.
  13. Outer 29×24 = 696; inner 25×20 = 500; border = 196 cm².
  14. 40 cm.
  15. 36 cm.
  16. 5 cm.
  17. 7 cm.
  18. 24 + 24 = 48 cm².
  19. Square (most balanced).
  20. Square again.
  21. Triangle is half the rectangle.
  22. Perimeter doubles; area becomes 4x.
  23. Circumference doubles.
  24. 100 m.
  25. Outer 34×24 = 816; inner 600; path = 216 m².

Common errors and how to avoid them

  • Confusing perimeter with area — always read the question twice.
  • Forgetting units — cm vs m vs km, and cm² vs m².
  • Forgetting that area uses square units while perimeter uses linear units.
  • Not converting all measurements to the same unit before computing.
  • Using the slant side as height in triangle area — height must be perpendicular.
  • Forgetting to add or subtract corner overlap in composite shapes.
  • Misreading the scale of a diagram — always check what one unit represents.
  • Forgetting that path width “all around” adds twice the width to length and width.
  • Forgetting that 1 m² = 10,000 cm² (not 100).
  • Confusing 1 m by 1 m square with 1 cm by 1 cm square.
  • Using diameter when radius is needed (or vice versa).
  • Approximating π too aggressively in advanced problems.

Real-world applications of perimeter and area

  • Construction. Calculating how many bricks to buy for a wall.
  • Carpeting. Estimating square metres needed for a room.
  • Painting. Estimating litres of paint based on wall area.
  • Fencing. Length of wire mesh around a field.
  • Gardening. Calculating fertiliser needs per square metre.
  • Real estate. Pricing per square foot or square metre.
  • Sport. Determining court markings (basketball, football, badminton).
  • Packaging. Material needed for boxes (surface area).
  • Tailoring. Cloth required for clothing (area + seam allowance).
  • Cooking. Pizza base diameter vs serving size.
  • Photography. Aspect ratios of prints and frames.
  • Architecture. Floor plan area, building footprint, lot coverage.
  • Agriculture. Hectares of crop, yield per hectare.
  • Environmental. Area of forests, coral reefs, glacier shrinkage.

Extended chapter wrap-up — Chapter 6

Perimeter and area belong to one of the oldest branches of mathematics. Five thousand years ago, Egyptian surveyors called the harpedonaptai (literally “rope-stretchers”) used knotted ropes and the 3-4-5 right triangle to redraw farm boundaries after every annual flood of the Nile. The Indus Valley Civilization (c. 2500 BCE) built cities with perfectly rectangular bricks in a 1:2:4 ratio. The ancient Indian Sulba Sutras contain the earliest known statement of the Pythagorean theorem — a result essential to area computation.

What did we learn? First, that perimeter is the total length of a boundary — measured in linear units like cm, m, km. Second, that area is the number of unit squares covering a 2D shape — measured in square units like cm², m², hectares. Third, that perimeter and area are independent: two shapes can have the same perimeter but different areas, and vice versa. Fourth, that a square maximises area for a given perimeter and minimises perimeter for a given area — a profound optimisation principle.

We mastered formulas: P = 2(L + W) for a rectangle, P = 4s for a square, P = n × s for a regular n-gon. A = L × W for a rectangle, A = s² for a square, A = base × height / 2 for a triangle. We learned to handle composite shapes by breaking them into rectangles, and to estimate irregular areas using a grid.

We also learned unit conversions: 1 m = 100 cm; 1 m² = 10,000 cm²; 1 km² = 1,000,000 m²; 1 hectare = 10,000 m². These conversions are easy to get wrong if you confuse linear with square units. Always pause and double-check.

The real-world applications are endless: tiling a floor, fencing a garden, painting a wall, framing a photo, paving a path, packaging a box, calculating fertiliser for a field, estimating real-estate value per square metre. Mastery here pays dividends for life.

Looking ahead, in Class 7 you will meet area and perimeter of more complex shapes: parallelograms, trapeziums, circles. In Class 8 you will learn surface area and volume of 3D solids. In higher classes, calculus will give you tools to find the area of any curved region, no matter how complicated. The journey starts here, with a square of side 1 cm having area 1 cm².

Suggested further activities

Activity 1. Measure the perimeter and area of your bedroom floor in metres.

Activity 2. Estimate the area of a leaf using the grid method on graph paper.

Activity 3. Walk around your school's playground; estimate the perimeter using your stride length.

Activity 4. Calculate how many 30 cm by 30 cm tiles would be needed to cover your kitchen floor.

Activity 5. Design a small kitchen garden of total area 10 m² with a path 0.5 m wide around it.

Activity 6. Find three rectangles in your home (book, picture, mat). Measure their perimeter and area.

Activity 7. Cut a 24 cm string and form rectangles of 11×1, 9×3, 7×5, 6×6. Compare their areas.

Activity 8. Estimate the area of your handprint by tracing it on cm graph paper.

Activity 9. Find the area of an irregular pond (or any irregular shape) using the grid method.

Activity 10. Compare the area of a square plot of side 10 m with that of a rectangular plot of 20 m by 5 m. Which is bigger? Why?

Activity 11. Calculate the cost of carpeting your classroom at the rate of ₹250 per square metre.

Activity 12. Find an example of unit conversion error in a newspaper or recipe.

Activity 13. Use the “rope-stretcher” method to construct a right angle in your garden using a 12-foot rope.

Activity 14. Measure how much wall paint your home needs based on wall areas.

Activity 15. Research how surveyors today measure large fields and forests.

Quick-recap cheat sheet

Perimeter = total boundary length (linear units).

Area = number of unit squares covering shape (square units).

Square side s: P = 4s, A = s².

Rectangle L × W: P = 2(L+W), A = L × W.

Equilateral triangle side s: P = 3s.

Regular n-gon side s: P = ns.

Right triangle legs a, b: A = (1/2) × a × b.

Any triangle base b, height h: A = (1/2) × b × h (height perpendicular to base).

Composite shape: split into rectangles, add (or subtract) areas.

Irregular shape: grid estimation (full squares + half partials).

Path of width w around L × W field: path area = (L+2w)(W+2w) − LW.

Conversions: 1 m = 100 cm; 1 m² = 10,000 cm²; 1 ha = 10,000 m²; 1 km² = 100 ha.

For fixed perimeter, square has the largest area.

For fixed area, square has the smallest perimeter.

Always include units in your final answer.

Always convert all measurements to the same unit before computing.

Always label your diagram with the given dimensions.

Always read the question twice to know whether perimeter or area is asked.

Always verify reasonableness of the final answer with a rough estimate.

Always show your work step by step.

Always double-check arithmetic.

Always annotate composite figures clearly.

Always remember the formulas; do not derive them from scratch each time.

Always practise with a variety of shapes for fluency.

Always enjoy the elegance of geometric reasoning!

Always label the unit on every measurement (cm vs m vs km matters).

Always convert before adding when units differ.

Always convert before subtracting when units differ.

Always check whether a result is in cm or cm²; the unit gives the answer's meaning.

Always use scale drawing on rough paper before final neat work.

Always break composite shapes into rectangles for area computation.

Always walk around mentally to compute perimeter of an L-shape.

Always respect significant figures; do not over-round intermediate results.

📋 Chapter 7 — What's Inside

📖 Topics Covered

  • 7.1 Fractional Units and Equal Shares
  • 7.2 Fractional Units as Parts of a Whole
  • 7.3 Measuring Using Fractional Units
  • 7.4 Marking Fraction Lengths on the Number Line
  • 7.5 Mixed Fractions
  • 7.6 Equivalent Fractions
  • 7.7 Comparing Fractions
  • 7.8 Addition and Subtraction of Fractions
  • 7.9 A Pinch of History

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 7.1 — Fractional Units
  • Section 7.2 — Representing Fractions
  • Section 7.3 — Equivalent Fractions
  • Section 7.4 — Comparing & Ordering
  • Section 7.5 — Operations on Fractions

Chapter 7: Fractions

🎯 Why we need fractions

Whole numbers are great for counting things you can’t split — like people, cars or marbles. But the moment you start sharing a pizza, measuring half a cup of sugar, or telling time as “quarter past five”, you need numbers that describe parts of a whole. Those numbers are called fractions. Fractions are everywhere — in cooking, in shopping, in time, in music, in money, in maps. Once you become friends with fractions, all of arithmetic suddenly opens up.

In-Depth Study — Chapter 7Each subtopic explained step-by-step with diagrams, real-life examples and worked problems

§7.1 — Fractional Units (and Equal Shares) (In Depth)

A fractional unit is one of the equal parts when a whole is divided. If a chocolate bar is broken into 4 equal pieces, each piece is one-fourth of the bar, written 1/4. The fractional unit here is “one-fourth”.

Parts of a fraction

  • The number on top is the numerator — it tells how many parts we are talking about.
  • The number at the bottom is the denominator — it tells how many equal parts the whole was divided into.

So 3/8 means “3 parts out of 8 equal parts.”

Important: parts must be EQUAL

A pizza cut into 4 random uneven pieces does NOT give fourths. Each fractional part must be exactly the same size as the others. Otherwise the fraction is meaningless.

Real-life fractions: half an hour, quarter past 3, three-fourths of a glass, half-time in a match, two-thirds majority in voting.

§7.1 — Equal Shares (In Depth)

If three children want to share two rotis equally, how much does each get? They get 2 ÷ 3 = 2/3 of a roti each. So a fraction can also be thought of as a division!

a/b = a ÷ b

Examples.

  • 4 friends share 1 cake → each gets 1/4 of the cake.
  • 2 friends share 3 chocolates → each gets 3/2 = 1½ chocolates.
  • 5 friends share 4 oranges → each gets 4/5 of an orange.

Types of fractions

  • Proper fraction: numerator < denominator. Value is less than 1. Examples: 1/2, 3/4, 7/10.
  • Improper fraction: numerator ≥ denominator. Value is 1 or more. Examples: 5/4, 7/3, 12/12.
  • Mixed number: a whole number plus a proper fraction, like 1¾.
  • Unit fraction: numerator is 1, like 1/3 or 1/5.

§7.2 — Fractional Units as Parts of a Whole (In Depth)

When a single whole is divided into equal parts, each part is called a fractional unit. The denominator tells us how many equal parts the whole was divided into, and the numerator tells us how many of those parts we are talking about.

NCERT highlights an important warning: the parts must be equal. Cutting a roti into “three pieces” that are clearly different sizes does not give you thirds.

Reading a fraction. 3/5 means “the whole is split into 5 equal parts, and we are taking 3 of them.”

§7.3 — Measuring Using Fractional Units (In Depth)

Fractions help us measure things that don’t fit a whole number of units. Suppose a stick is shorter than 1 metre but longer than half a metre. By folding the metre rod into halves, thirds, quarters,… we can match the length of the stick using fractional units.

The smaller the fractional unit (1/2 → 1/4 → 1/8…), the more precisely we can measure. This is exactly how rulers, measuring tapes and weighing scales are made!

Try this. Take a strip of paper. Fold it in half to get 1/2. Fold again to get 1/4. Again for 1/8. You have just built a tiny fractional ruler!

§7.4 — Marking Fraction Lengths on the Number Line (In Depth)

To mark a fraction such as 3/4 on the number line:

  1. Look at the denominator (4). Divide the segment from 0 to 1 into 4 equal parts.
  2. Look at the numerator (3). Starting from 0, count 3 parts to the right.
  3. The point you reach is 3/4.

Improper fractions like 5/4 also live on the number line — they sit beyond 1. To mark 5/4, extend the line, keep the same quarter-sized steps, and count five of them from 0.

Key idea. The number line gives every fraction its own unique “address.” Equivalent fractions like 1/2 and 2/4 land on exactly the same point.

§7.6 — Equivalent Fractions (In Depth)

Two fractions are equivalent if they represent the same amount, even though they look different. For example, half a pizza is the same amount whether you cut it as 1/2 or 2/4 or 4/8.

1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8 = 5/10 = 50/100

How to make equivalent fractions

Multiply (or divide) both numerator and denominator by the same number.

  • 3/4 = (3×2)/(4×2) = 6/8.
  • 3/4 = (3×3)/(4×3) = 9/12.
  • 10/15 = (10÷5)/(15÷5) = 2/3.

Simplest form (lowest terms)

A fraction is in simplest form when its numerator and denominator have no common factor except 1 (i.e., they are co-prime). To simplify, divide top and bottom by their HCF.

Example. Simplify 24/36. HCF(24, 36) = 12. So 24/36 = 24÷12 / 36÷12 = 2/3.

§7.7 — Comparing Fractions (In Depth)

Case 1: Same denominator (“like fractions”)

The fraction with the bigger numerator is bigger. Example: 5/7 > 3/7.

Case 2: Same numerator

The fraction with the smaller denominator is bigger (fewer pieces means each piece is bigger). Example: 2/5 > 2/9.

Case 3: Different numerators AND denominators (“unlike fractions”)

Convert both to a common denominator (the LCM of the two denominators). Then compare numerators.

Example. Compare 3/4 and 5/6.
LCM(4, 6) = 12.
3/4 = 9/12;   5/6 = 10/12.
9/12 < 10/12, so 3/4 < 5/6.

Quick “cross-multiplication” trick

To compare a/b and c/d, compare a × d with b × c.

For 3/4 vs 5/6: 3 × 6 = 18, 4 × 5 = 20. 18 < 20, so 3/4 < 5/6.

§7.8 — Adding and Subtracting Fractions (In Depth)

Like fractions (same denominator)

Just add or subtract the numerators — the denominator stays the same.

  • 3/7 + 2/7 = 5/7.
  • 5/8 − 1/8 = 4/8 = 1/2.

Unlike fractions (different denominators)

  1. Find the LCM of the two denominators (the “common denominator”).
  2. Convert both fractions to equivalent fractions with that denominator.
  3. Add (or subtract) the numerators.
  4. Simplify if possible.

Example 1 (Addition). 2/3 + 1/4.
LCM(3, 4) = 12.
2/3 = 8/12 and 1/4 = 3/12.
Sum = 8/12 + 3/12 = 11/12.

Example 2 (Subtraction). 5/6 − 3/8.
LCM(6, 8) = 24.
5/6 = 20/24 and 3/8 = 9/24.
Difference = 20/24 − 9/24 = 11/24.

§7.5 — Mixed Fractions (Mixed Numbers) (In Depth)

A mixed number is a whole number plus a fraction, like 2¾ (read “two and three-quarters”). It means 2 wholes plus 3/4 of another whole.

Converting improper fractions to mixed numbers

Divide numerator by denominator. The quotient is the whole part; the remainder over the denominator is the fractional part.

Example: 17/5 → 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2. So 17/5 = 3 2/5.

Converting mixed numbers to improper fractions

(whole × denominator) + numerator, all over denominator.

Example: 2 3/4 = (2 × 4 + 3)/4 = 11/4.

Adding and subtracting mixed numbers

Convert to improper fractions first, do the operation, then convert back if you wish.

Example. 1 1/2 + 2 1/3 = 3/2 + 7/3 = 9/6 + 14/6 = 23/6 = 3 5/6.

§7.9 — A Pinch of History (In Depth)

People have been using fractions for thousands of years!

  • Ancient Egypt (about 4000 years ago). Egyptians wrote almost every fraction as a sum of unit fractions (fractions with 1 on top). For example, 3/4 was written as 1/2 + 1/4. They had a special hieroglyph that looked like an open mouth to mark a fraction.
  • Ancient India. The Shulba Sutras (around 800 BCE) used fractions in detailed measurements for building altars. Later, mathematicians like Aryabhata (5th century CE) and Brahmagupta (7th century CE) wrote down systematic rules for adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing fractions — very close to the rules we use today!
  • Brahmagupta’s notation. He wrote a fraction by stacking the numerator on top of the denominator without a bar between them. The horizontal bar (vinculum) we use today became common only much later, through Arab and European mathematicians.
  • The word “fraction” comes from the Latin fractus, meaning “broken.” A fraction is literally a “broken” piece of a whole.

Did you know? The Bakhshali Manuscript, an ancient Indian mathematical text, contains some of the earliest known examples of fractions written in a format very similar to today’s notation.

🎯 Big picture of Chapter 7

  • A fraction = part of a whole, written numerator/denominator.
  • Equivalent fractions = different forms of the same amount; simplify by dividing by HCF.
  • Compare by making the denominators equal (LCM) or by cross-multiplying.
  • Add/subtract: same denominator → just add numerators; different → use LCM first.
  • Mixed numbers convert easily to improper fractions and back.

⚠️ Common mistakes

  • Adding numerators AND denominators (1/2 + 1/3 ≠ 2/5).
  • Forgetting to simplify the final answer.
  • Treating 0 in the denominator as valid — division by 0 is never allowed!
  • Comparing fractions by “bigger denominator means bigger fraction” — it’s usually the opposite.
Quick Reference — Chapter 7The original briefer summary follows

§7.1–7.2 — What is a Fraction?

A fraction represents a part of a whole. It's written as:

Numerator⁄Denominator = How many parts we have⁄How many equal parts the whole is divided into

§7.6 — Equivalent Fractions

Equivalent fractions look different but have the same value.

Multiply or divide BOTH numerator and denominator by the same number:

1⁄2 = 2⁄4 = 3⁄6 = 4⁄8 = 5⁄10 (all equal to half!)

§7.7 — Comparing Fractions

Same denominator: Compare numerators directly. 3⁄7 > 2⁄7

Same numerator: Bigger denominator = smaller fraction. 1⁄3 > 1⁄5

Different both: Make denominators same (LCM), then compare numerators.

§7.8 — Addition & Subtraction of Fractions

Same denominator: Add/subtract numerators, keep denominator.

2⁄5 + 1⁄5 = 3⁄5

Different denominators: Find LCM, make equivalent fractions, then add.

1⁄3 + 1⁄4 = 4⁄12 + 3⁄12 = 7⁄12

Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved

📝 Quick Recap

  • Fraction = part/whole = numerator/denominator
  • Equivalent: multiply/divide both parts by same number
  • Comparing: same denominator → bigger numerator wins
  • Adding: need common denominator first!
  • Mixed number: whole + fraction (e.g., 2¾ = 2 + ¾)

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Adding fractions: DON'T add denominators! ½ + ⅓ ≠ 2/5
  • Comparing: ⅓ is NOT bigger than ¼ by looking at 3 vs 4! (⅓ > ¼ because pieces are bigger)
  • Simplifying: Only divide when BOTH parts share a factor. Can't simplify ⅗!

Figure it Out — Section 7.1

Q1. Three guavas together weigh 1 kg. Each guava roughly weighs _____ kg.

Solution:

3 guavas = 1 kg, so each = 1 ÷ 3 = ⅓ kg

Q2. 1 kg rice packed in 4 equal packets. Weight of each = _____ kg.

Solution:

Each packet = 1 ÷ 4 = ¼ kg

Q3. Four friends share 3 glasses of juice equally. Each drank _____ glass.

Solution:

Each gets = 3 ÷ 4 = ¾ glass

Q4. Big fish weighs ½ kg, small fish weighs ¼ kg. Together they weigh _____ kg.

Solution:

½ + ¼ = 2/4 + 1/4 = ¾ kg

Q5. Arrange from smallest to biggest: one and a half, three quarters, one and a quarter, half, quarter, two and a half.

Solution:

Convert to fractions: ¼, ½, ¾, 1¼, 1½, 2½

Order: ¼ < ½ < ¾ < 1¼ < 1½ < 2½

Q. Fractional units of a whole chikki — how much is each piece?

Solution:

(a) 1/12   (b) 1/4   (c) 1/8   (d) 1/6   (e) 1/8   (f) 1/6   (g) 1/24   (h) 1/24

Q. By dividing chikki into 6 equal parts in different ways, are pieces of the same size?

Solution:

Yes! Even though the shapes look different, each piece is exactly ⅙ of the whole — they have the same area (same amount of chikki).

Figure it Out — Section 7.1 (Fractional Units)

Q1. Continue the pattern:

½ of 24 = 12; ¼ of 24 = 6; ⅛ of 24 = ?

Solution:

Each time the denominator doubles, the result halves.

⅛ of 24 = 24 ÷ 8 = 3

Q2. Colour ⅓ of the given shapes in your notebooks.

Solution:

Divide the shape into 3 equal parts and colour 1 part. For example, in a rectangle divided into 3 equal strips, colour 1 strip.

Q3. Ramya says ⅓ of a chapati is more than ¼ of a chapati. Do you agree?

Solution:

Yes! When a chapati is divided into 3 parts, each part is bigger than when divided into 4 parts. More parts means each part is smaller. So ⅓ > ¼.

Figure it Out — Section 7.2 (Measuring Using Fractions)

Q1. Write the fraction of the shaded portion: (a) 3 out of 4 parts shaded (b) 2 out of 7 parts shaded (c) 4 out of 6 parts shaded

Solution:

(a) ¾

(b) 2/7

(c) 4/6 = 2/3

Figure it Out — Section 7.3 (Equivalent Fractions)

Q1. Are these fractions equivalent? (a) ⅓ and 2/6 (b) ⅖ and 4/10 (c) 3/4 and 12/16

Solution:

(a) ⅓ = 2/6 (multiply top and bottom by 2). Yes, equivalent ✔

(b) 2/5 = 4/10 (multiply by 2). Yes, equivalent ✔

(c) 3/4 = 12/16 (multiply by 4). Yes, equivalent ✔

Q2. Write three equivalent fractions of: (a) 2/3 (b) 4/5 (c) 1/6

Solution:

(a) 2/3 = 4/6 = 6/9 = 8/12

(b) 4/5 = 8/10 = 12/15 = 16/20

(c) 1/6 = 2/12 = 3/18 = 4/24

Q3. Write the simplest form of: (a) 6/18 (b) 12/20 (c) 15/45

Solution:

(a) 6/18 ÷ 6/6 = 1/3

(b) 12/20 ÷ 4/4 = 3/5

(c) 15/45 ÷ 15/15 = 1/3

Q4. Arrange in ascending order: ½, ⅓, ¼, ⅕, ⅙

Solution:

For unit fractions (numerator = 1), larger denominator means SMALLER fraction:

⅙ < ⅕ < ¼ < ⅓ < ½

Figure it Out — Section 7.4 (Comparing Fractions)

Q1 (NCERT). On a number line, draw lines of lengths 1/10, 3/10 and 4/5.

Solution:

Divide the segment from 0 to 1 into 10 equal parts. Each tick is 1/10.

  • 1/10 — line from 0 to the 1st tick.
  • 3/10 — line from 0 to the 3rd tick.
  • 4/5 — first rewrite 4/5 = 8/10. Line from 0 to the 8th tick.

Q2 (NCERT). Write five more fractions of your choice and mark them on the number line.

Sample solution:

Choose 1/4, 1/2, 2/3, 5/6 and 9/10.

To mark them on the same number line, convert to a common denominator (LCM of 4, 2, 3, 6, 10 = 60):

1/4 = 15/60,   1/2 = 30/60,   2/3 = 40/60,   5/6 = 50/60,   9/10 = 54/60.

Divide 0 to 1 into 60 equal parts and mark each fraction at the matching tick.

Q3 (NCERT). How many fractions lie between 0 and 1? Think, discuss with your classmates, and write your answer.

Solution:

Infinitely many!

Between any two fractions you can always find another fraction (for example, their average). So between 1/2 and 3/4 there is 5/8; between 1/2 and 5/8 there is 9/16; and so on without end.

So there are infinitely many fractions in any small piece of the number line, including the segment from 0 to 1.

Q4 (NCERT). The segment from 0 to 1 is divided into two equal parts. The blue line goes from 0 to the middle. What is the length of the blue line? Write the length of the black line in the box.

Solution:

Length of blue line = 1/2 unit (it spans one of the two equal parts).

The black line goes from 0 to the other tick, so its length is also 1/2 unit. (If it extends from 1/2 to 1, the length is still 1/2.)

Q5 (NCERT). The number line from 0 to 1 is divided into five equal parts (ticks at 1/5, 2/5, 3/5, 4/5). Write the fraction that gives the length of each black line drawn between 0 and the marked points.

Solution:

  • Line from 0 to first tick: 1/5
  • Line from 0 to second tick: 2/5
  • Line from 0 to third tick: 3/5
  • Line from 0 to fourth tick: 4/5
  • Line from 0 to 1: 5/5 = 1 (whole unit)

Q6. Compare using <, > or =: (a) 3/5 and 4/5 (b) 2/7 and 2/3 (c) 5/8 and 3/8

Solution:

(a) Same denominator: compare numerators. 3 < 4, so 3/5 < 4/5

(b) Same numerator: larger denominator = smaller fraction. 7 > 3, so 2/7 < 2/3

(c) Same denominator: 5 > 3, so 5/8 > 3/8

Q7. Compare: (a) 2/3 and 3/4 (b) 5/6 and 7/8

Solution:

(a) Make denominators same: 2/3 = 8/12, 3/4 = 9/12. Since 8 < 9: 2/3 < 3/4

(b) 5/6 = 20/24, 7/8 = 21/24. Since 20 < 21: 5/6 < 7/8

Figure it Out — Section 7.5 (Mixed Numbers)

Q1. Figure out the number of whole units in each fraction: (a) 7/2 (b) 13/4 (c) 18/5 (d) 23/6

Solution:

(a) 7 ÷ 2 = 3 remainder 1. So 7/2 = 3½

(b) 13 ÷ 4 = 3 remainder 1. So 13/4 = 3¼

(c) 18 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 3. So 18/5 = 3⅛ (3 and 3/5)

(d) 23 ÷ 6 = 3 remainder 5. So 23/6 = 3 and 5/6

Q2. Convert to improper fractions: (a) 2⅓ (b) 4½ (c) 3⅞

Solution:

(a) 2⅓ = (2×3 + 1)/3 = 7/3

(b) 4½ = (4×2 + 1)/2 = 9/2

(c) 3¾ = (3×4 + 3)/4 = 15/4

Figure it Out — Section 7.6 (Adding & Subtracting Fractions)

Q1. Add: (a) ⅓ + ⅓ (b) 2/7 + 3/7 (c) ¼ + ½ (d) 2/3 + ⅙

Solution:

(a) ⅓ + ⅓ = 2/3

(b) 2/7 + 3/7 = 5/7

(c) ¼ + ½ = ¼ + 2/4 = ¾

(d) 2/3 + ⅙ = 4/6 + 1/6 = 5/6

Q2. Subtract: (a) 5/6 − ⅓ (b) ¾ − ¼ (c) 5/8 − ½ (d) 2/3 − ½

Solution:

(a) 5/6 − 1/3 = 5/6 − 2/6 = 3/6 = ½

(b) 3/4 − 1/4 = 2/4 = ½

(c) 5/8 − 1/2 = 5/8 − 4/8 = 1/8

(d) 2/3 − 1/2 = 4/6 − 3/6 = 1/6

Q3. Rahim had ¾ of a roti and ate ½ of it. How much is left?

Solution:

Left = ¾ − ½ = ¾ − 2/4 = ¼ of the roti

Q4. Add: (a) 2⅓ + 1½ (b) 3¼ + 2⅓

Solution:

(a) Whole parts: 2+1 = 3. Fraction parts: ⅓ + ½ = 2/6 + 3/6 = 5/6. Answer: 3 and 5/6

(b) Whole parts: 3+2 = 5. Fraction parts: ¼ + ⅓ = 3/12 + 4/12 = 7/12. Answer: 5 and 7/12

Q5. A ribbon is 3½ m long. A piece of 1¾ m is cut off. What length remains?

Solution:

3½ − 1¾ = 7/2 − 7/4 = 14/4 − 7/4 = 7/4 = 1¾ m

Figure it Out — Section 7.7 (Comparing Fractions using Common Denominators)

Q1. Compare the following fractions and justify your answers:

(a) 8/3 and 5/2   (b) 4/9 and 3/7   (c) 7/10 and 9/14   (d) 12/5 and 8/5   (e) 9/4 and 5/2

Solution:

(a) 8/3 = 16/6, 5/2 = 15/6. Since 16 > 15: 8/3 > 5/2

(b) 4/9 = 28/63, 3/7 = 27/63. Since 28 > 27: 4/9 > 3/7

(c) 7/10 = 49/70, 9/14 = 45/70. Since 49 > 45: 7/10 > 9/14

(d) Same denominator (5). Since 12 > 8: 12/5 > 8/5

(e) 9/4 = 9/4, 5/2 = 10/4. Since 9 < 10: 9/4 < 5/2

Q2. Write the following fractions in ascending order:

(a) 7/10, 11/15, 2/5   (b) 19/24, 5/6, 7/12

Solution:

(a) LCM of 10, 15, 5 = 30. Convert: 7/10 = 21/30, 11/15 = 22/30, 2/5 = 12/30

Order: 12/30 < 21/30 < 22/30 ⇒ 2/5 < 7/10 < 11/15

(b) LCM of 24, 6, 12 = 24. Convert: 19/24 = 19/24, 5/6 = 20/24, 7/12 = 14/24

Order: 14/24 < 19/24 < 20/24 ⇒ 7/12 < 19/24 < 5/6

Q3. Write the following fractions in descending order:

(a) 25/16, 7/8, 13/4, 17/32   (b) 3/4, 12/5, 7/12, 5/4

Solution:

(a) LCM of 16, 8, 4, 32 = 32. Convert: 25/16 = 50/32, 7/8 = 28/32, 13/4 = 104/32, 17/32 = 17/32

Order: 104 > 50 > 28 > 17 ⇒ 13/4 > 25/16 > 7/8 > 17/32

(b) LCM of 4, 5, 12, 4 = 60. Convert: 3/4 = 45/60, 12/5 = 144/60, 7/12 = 35/60, 5/4 = 75/60

Order: 144 > 75 > 45 > 35 ⇒ 12/5 > 5/4 > 3/4 > 7/12

Figure it Out — Section 7.8 (Addition of Fractions — Brahmagupta’s Method)

Q1. Add the following fractions using Brahmagupta’s method:

(a) 2/7 + 5/7 + 6/7   (b) 3/4 + 1/3   (c) 2/3 + 5/6   (d) 2/3 + 2/7

(e) 3/4 + 1/3 + 1/5   (f) 2/3 + 4/5   (g) 4/5 + 2/3   (h) 3/5 + 5/8

(i) 9/2 + 5/4   (j) 8/3 + 2/7   (k) 3/4 + 1/3 + 1/5   (l) 2/3 + 4/5 + 3/7   (m) 9/2 + 5/4 + 7/6

Solution (Brahmagupta’s method: find common denominator, add numerators):

(a) Same denominator: (2+5+6)/7 = 13/7

(b) LCD=12: 9/12 + 4/12 = 13/12

(c) LCD=6: 4/6 + 5/6 = 9/6 = 3/2 (or 1½)

(d) LCD=21: 14/21 + 6/21 = 20/21

(e) LCD=60: 45/60 + 20/60 + 12/60 = 77/60

(f) LCD=15: 10/15 + 12/15 = 22/15

(g) LCD=15: 12/15 + 10/15 = 22/15 (same as (f) — addition is commutative!)

(h) LCD=40: 24/40 + 25/40 = 49/40

(i) LCD=4: 18/4 + 5/4 = 23/4

(j) LCD=21: 56/21 + 6/21 = 62/21

(k) LCD=60: 45/60 + 20/60 + 12/60 = 77/60

(l) LCD=105: 70/105 + 84/105 + 45/105 = 199/105

(m) LCD=12: 54/12 + 15/12 + 14/12 = 83/12

Q2. Rahim mixes 2/3 litres of yellow paint with 3/4 litres of blue paint to make green paint. What is the volume of green paint he has made?

Solution:

Total = 2/3 + 3/4 = 8/12 + 9/12 = 17/12 litres = 1 and 5/12 litres

Q3. Geeta bought 2/5 m of lace and Shamim bought 3/4 m of lace. The table cloth perimeter is 1 m. Find total lace bought. Will it be sufficient?

Solution:

Total = 2/5 + 3/4 = 8/20 + 15/20 = 23/20 m = 1 and 3/20 m

Yes, the lace is sufficient! They have 23/20 m which is more than 1 m (the perimeter).

Figure it Out — Section 7.8 (Subtraction of Fractions — Brahmagupta’s Method)

Q1. Carry out the following subtractions using Brahmagupta’s method:

(a) 8/15 − 3/15   (b) 2/5 − 4/15   (c) 5/6 − 4/9   (d) 2/3 − 1/2

Solution:

(a) Same denominator: (8−3)/15 = 5/15 = 1/3

(b) LCD=15: 6/15 − 4/15 = 2/15

(c) LCD=18: 15/18 − 8/18 = 7/18

(d) LCD=6: 4/6 − 3/6 = 1/6

Q2. Subtract as indicated:

(a) 13/4 from 10/3   (b) 18/5 from 23/3   (c) 29/7 from 45/7

Solution:

(a) 10/3 − 13/4 = 40/12 − 39/12 = 1/12

(b) 23/3 − 18/5 = 115/15 − 54/15 = 61/15

(c) Same denominator: 45/7 − 29/7 = 16/7

Q3. Solve:

(a) Jaya’s school is 7/10 km away. She takes an auto for 1/2 km and walks the rest. How much does she walk?

(b) Jeevika takes 10/3 min for a round of the park, Namit takes 13/4 min. Who takes less time and by how much?

Solution:

(a) Distance walked = 7/10 − 1/2 = 7/10 − 5/10 = 2/10 = 1/5 km

(b) Compare: 10/3 = 40/12, 13/4 = 39/12. Since 39 < 40, Namit takes less time.

Difference = 40/12 − 39/12 = 1/12 minutes

💭 Strategy Tip

To compare fractions quickly: Cross-multiply! For a/b vs c/d: if a×d > c×b, then a/b is bigger. Example: ⅔ vs ¾ → 2×4=8 vs 3×3=9. Since 8 < 9, ⅔ < ¾.

🧠 Self-Check Quiz

  1. Which is bigger: ⅗ or ⅔?
  2. Simplify 12/18.
  3. Add: ⅓ + ⅙ = ?
  4. Convert 2¾ to improper fraction.
  1. ⅔ → 9/15 vs 10/15, so ⅔ wins
  2. ⅔ → divide both by 6
  3. ½ → 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = ½
  4. 11/4 → (2×4+3)/4 = 11/4
Mastery Practice — Chapter 7Extensive fraction practice with worked solutions and a self-test

Worked Example Set A — Equivalent fractions and lowest terms

  1. Q1. Write four equivalent fractions of 2/5.
    Solution. 4/10, 6/15, 8/20, 10/25.
  2. Q2. Express 18/24 in lowest terms.
    Solution. HCF(18,24)=6. 18÷6 / 24÷6 = 3/4.
  3. Q3. Express 35/45 in lowest terms.
    Solution. HCF(35,45)=5. 35/5 over 45/5 = 7/9.
  4. Q4. Are 8/12 and 14/21 equivalent?
    Solution. 8×21 = 168 and 12×14 = 168. Equal ⇒ yes, equivalent.
  5. Q5. Find x if x/9 = 4/6.
    Solution. Cross-multiply: 6x = 36 ⇒ x = 6.

Worked Example Set B — Comparing fractions

  1. Q1. Which is bigger, 2/3 or 3/5?
    Solution. LCM(3,5)=15. 2/3 = 10/15; 3/5 = 9/15. So 2/3 > 3/5.
  2. Q2. Which is bigger, 5/8 or 7/12?
    Solution. LCM(8,12)=24. 5/8 = 15/24; 7/12 = 14/24. So 5/8 > 7/12.
  3. Q3. Order from smallest to largest: 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6.
    Solution. LCM(2,3,4,6)=12. They become 6/12, 8/12, 9/12, 10/12. Order: 1/2 < 2/3 < 3/4 < 5/6.
  4. Q4. Compare 11/15 and 7/10.
    Solution. LCM(15,10)=30. 11/15 = 22/30, 7/10 = 21/30. So 11/15 > 7/10.

Worked Example Set C — Adding and subtracting fractions

  1. Q1. 3/8 + 1/8 = ?
    Solution. Same denominator ⇒ 4/8 = 1/2.
  2. Q2. 5/6 + 1/4 = ?
    Solution. LCM(6,4)=12. 5/6 = 10/12, 1/4 = 3/12. Sum = 13/12 = 1 1/12.
  3. Q3. 2/3 + 3/4 + 5/6 = ?
    Solution. LCM(3,4,6)=12. = 8/12 + 9/12 + 10/12 = 27/12 = 9/4 = 2 1/4.
  4. Q4. 5/6 − 1/3 = ?
    Solution. LCM=6. = 5/6 − 2/6 = 3/6 = 1/2.
  5. Q5. 7/10 − 2/5 = ?
    Solution. = 7/10 − 4/10 = 3/10.
  6. Q6. A jug had 3/4 litre milk. 1/3 litre was used. Milk left?
    Solution. 3/4 − 1/3 = 9/12 − 4/12 = 5/12 litre.

Worked Example Set D — Mixed numbers

  1. Q1. Convert 17/4 to a mixed number.
    Solution. 17 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 1. So 17/4 = 4 1/4.
  2. Q2. Convert 3 2/5 to an improper fraction.
    Solution. (3×5 + 2)/5 = 17/5.
  3. Q3. 2 1/2 + 1 1/4 = ?
    Solution. = 5/2 + 5/4 = 10/4 + 5/4 = 15/4 = 3 3/4.
  4. Q4. 4 2/3 − 1 1/6 = ?
    Solution. = 14/3 − 7/6 = 28/6 − 7/6 = 21/6 = 7/2 = 3 1/2.
  5. Q5. A recipe needs 2 1/4 cups flour. We doubled it. Total flour?
    Solution. 2 × 2 1/4 = 2 × 9/4 = 18/4 = 4 1/2 cups.

📝 Mini-quiz — Chapter 7

  1. 2/4 in lowest terms is ____.
  2. Which is bigger: 3/8 or 1/2? ____.
  3. 1/3 + 1/6 = ____.
  4. 11/3 as mixed number = ____.
  5. 2 3/5 as improper fraction = ____.
  6. 3/4 − 1/2 = ____.
Answers:
  1. 1/2
  2. 1/2
  3. 1/2
  4. 3 2/3
  5. 13/5
  6. 1/4

🎯 Chapter 7 — Key takeaways

  • A fraction has a numerator (part taken) and denominator (total parts).
  • Equivalent fractions = same value, different numbers (multiply or divide top and bottom by same non-zero number).
  • Lowest terms = numerator and denominator share no common factor > 1.
  • To add or subtract: convert to common denominator first.
  • Mixed number ↔ improper fraction interconversion is essential.
Advanced Practice Lab — Chapter 7Story problems on fractions and a final mastery quiz

Story problems on fractions

Problem 1 — The pizza party. Anita and her three friends order a large pizza cut into 8 equal slices.

Anita eats 2 slices, Suresh eats 1, Rohan eats 3 and Maya eats 1.

(a) What fraction of the pizza did each person eat?

Anita 2/8 = 1/4; Suresh 1/8; Rohan 3/8; Maya 1/8.

(b) Together they ate what fraction of the pizza?

2/8 + 1/8 + 3/8 + 1/8 = 7/8.

(c) What fraction is left?

1 − 7/8 = 1/8.

Problem 2 — The chocolate bar. A bar of chocolate has 12 equal pieces.

Rahul ate 1/3 of the bar, his sister ate 1/4 of it.

(a) Who ate more, and by how much (as fraction)?

1/3 = 4/12; 1/4 = 3/12. Rahul ate more by 1/12 of the bar.

(b) Together they ate what fraction?

4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12.

(c) How many pieces are left?

12 − 7 = 5 pieces (= 5/12 of the bar).

Problem 3 — The water tank. A water tank is 3/4 full.

It can hold 200 litres when completely full.

(a) How many litres of water are in the tank now?

3/4 of 200 = 150 litres.

(b) If 50 litres are used, what fraction of the tank is filled now?

(150 − 50) / 200 = 100/200 = 1/2.

(c) How much more water is needed to fill the tank?

200 − 100 = 100 litres.

Problem 4 — Sharing fruits. Mrs Verma has 24 apples.

She gives 1/4 of them to her children, 1/3 of the remaining to her neighbour.

(a) Apples given to children?

1/4 of 24 = 6 apples.

(b) Remaining apples?

24 − 6 = 18 apples.

(c) Apples given to neighbour?

1/3 of 18 = 6 apples.

(d) Apples left with Mrs Verma?

18 − 6 = 12 apples.

Problem 5 — The journey. Vinod travels from home to school which is 6 km away.

He covers 1/2 of the distance by bus, 1/4 of the remaining by auto-rickshaw, and walks the rest.

(a) Distance by bus?

1/2 of 6 = 3 km.

(b) Remaining distance after bus?

3 km.

(c) Distance by auto?

1/4 of 3 = 0.75 km = 3/4 km.

(d) Distance walked?

3 − 0.75 = 2.25 km = 2¼ km.

Fraction visualisations

Understanding fractions becomes easy with pictures. Try these mental images:

Pizza slices: a pizza divided into n equal slices — each slice is 1/n of the pizza.

Number line: 0 to 1 divided into n equal parts — the kth mark from 0 is k/n.

Rectangle strips: a strip divided into equal cells; shading m of n gives m/n.

Coloured bars: useful for comparing fractions side by side.

Real objects: chocolate bars, ribbons, glasses of water, slices of cake.

Money: a 50 paise coin is 1/2 of a rupee; a 25 paise coin is 1/4 of a rupee.

Time: half an hour = 1/2 hour; quarter past = 1/4 hour past; quarter to = 3/4 hour past.

📝 Final mastery quiz — Chapter 7

Q1. Which is bigger: 5/6 or 4/5?

Q2. Find the LCM of 4 and 6, then add 1/4 + 1/6.

Q3. Convert 23/4 to a mixed number.

Q4. Find the missing numerator: 7/12 = ?/36.

Q5. A jug has 1 1/2 litres of milk. 3/4 litre is poured out. How much milk remains?

Q6. 2 1/3 + 1 1/6 = ?

Q7. Express 24/36 in lowest terms.

Q8. 5 − 2 1/4 = ?

Answers:
  1. 5/6 = 25/30, 4/5 = 24/30 ⇒ 5/6 is bigger.
  2. LCM(4,6) = 12; 1/4 + 1/6 = 3/12 + 2/12 = 5/12.
  3. 23/4 = 5 3/4.
  4. 7/12 = 21/36.
  5. 1 1/2 − 3/4 = 3/2 − 3/4 = 6/4 − 3/4 = 3/4 litre.
  6. 2 1/3 + 1 1/6 = 7/3 + 7/6 = 14/6 + 7/6 = 21/6 = 7/2 = 3 1/2.
  7. 24/36 = 2/3.
  8. 5 − 9/4 = 20/4 − 9/4 = 11/4 = 2 3/4.
NCERT Deep Dive — Chapter 7Following the NCERT chapter “Fractions”

A guided tour of NCERT "Fractions"

The NCERT chapter develops fractions through three interlocking ideas: a fraction as part of a whole, a fraction as division, and a fraction as a point on the number line. The chapter is structured as:

  1. Fractions in everyday life — pieces of pizza, parts of a chocolate bar, hours of a day.
  2. Fractions as parts of a whole and parts of a collection.
  3. Fractions on the number line.
  4. Equivalent fractions and the simplest form.
  5. Like fractions and unlike fractions; how to compare them.
  6. Addition and subtraction (same and different denominators).
  7. Mixed numbers ↔ improper fractions.
  8. Brahmi numerals and the history of fractions in India.

The fraction strip activity

NCERT's favourite hands-on activity is building fraction strips. Take 8 equal strips of paper. Label them and fold:

  • Leave strip 1 whole — label “1”.
  • Fold strip 2 in half — each part is “1/2”.
  • Fold strip 3 in thirds — each part “1/3”.
  • Fold strip 4 in fourths — each part “1/4”.
  • Continue: 1/5, 1/6, 1/8, 1/12.

What the strips show:

  • 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8 (equivalent fractions).
  • 1/3 > 1/4 > 1/6 (when numerator is fixed, larger denominator means smaller fraction).
  • 1/2 + 1/4 = 3/4 (visually two pieces of 1/2 strip equal one whole; adding a 1/4 piece leaves 3/4).
  • 1/3 + 1/6 = 1/2 (the 1/6 piece plus the 1/3 piece visibly match the 1/2 piece).

Once you have the strips, addition and subtraction become physical operations.

Number-line model of fractions

The number line is the unifying picture for all numbers — whole, fraction, decimal, integer, real.

Halves on the line: 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, 2, 5/2, 3, …

Thirds on the line: 0, 1/3, 2/3, 1, 4/3, 5/3, 2, …

Quarters on the line: 0, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1, 5/4, 6/4, 7/4, 2, …

Notice 2/4 sits exactly where 1/2 does — visual proof that 2/4 = 1/2.

Activity: on a single number line from 0 to 2, mark every third (0, 1/3, 2/3, 1, 4/3, 5/3, 2) and every quarter (0, 1/4, 2/4, …, 8/4). Where do they coincide? Only at integers (0, 1, 2). Where is 7/12? It is at 7 small steps of length 1/12, which doesn't coincide with any third or quarter mark on the line.

Equivalent fractions — the multiplication identity

Two fractions are equivalent if they represent the same point on the number line.

Key principle: if you multiply (or divide) both numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number, the value is unchanged.

So: 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8 = 5/10 = 100/200 = … (infinitely many equivalents).

Simplest form (lowest terms). Divide both top and bottom by their HCF until no common factor > 1 remains.

Example. Reduce 36/48. HCF(36, 48) = 12. 36/12 = 3, 48/12 = 4. So 36/48 = 3/4.

Example. Reduce 84/126. HCF(84, 126) = 42. 84/42 = 2, 126/42 = 3. So 84/126 = 2/3.

Example. Reduce 100/250. HCF = 50. 100/50 = 2, 250/50 = 5. So 100/250 = 2/5.

Cross-product check. Two fractions a/b and c/d are equivalent if and only if a×d = b×c.

Example. Is 12/15 = 16/20? Cross: 12×20 = 240; 15×16 = 240. Yes!

Example. Is 7/9 = 11/15? Cross: 7×15 = 105; 9×11 = 99. No (105 ≠ 99).

Comparing fractions — three methods

NCERT teaches multiple comparison methods.

Method 1 — Same denominator. Bigger numerator means bigger fraction.

3/7 vs 5/7 ⇒ 5/7 is bigger.

Method 2 — Same numerator. Smaller denominator means bigger fraction.

4/9 vs 4/11 ⇒ 4/9 is bigger.

Method 3 — Different numerator and denominator: convert to like fractions using LCM of denominators.

Compare 5/6 and 7/9. LCM(6, 9) = 18. 5/6 = 15/18; 7/9 = 14/18. Since 15 > 14, 5/6 > 7/9.

Method 4 — Cross multiplication. To compare a/b and c/d, compare a×d with b×c.

Compare 5/6 and 7/9. 5×9 = 45; 6×7 = 42. Since 45 > 42, 5/6 > 7/9. (Same answer, faster!)

Worked example. Order from least to greatest: 1/2, 2/3, 3/5, 5/8.

LCM(2,3,5,8) = 120. 1/2 = 60/120; 2/3 = 80/120; 3/5 = 72/120; 5/8 = 75/120. Order: 60 < 72 < 75 < 80, so 1/2 < 3/5 < 5/8 < 2/3.

Addition and subtraction — the universal recipe

Same denominator: add (or subtract) numerators, keep the denominator.

2/9 + 5/9 = 7/9.

Different denominators:

  1. Find the LCM of the denominators.
  2. Convert each fraction to an equivalent with the LCM as the new denominator.
  3. Add (or subtract) the new numerators.
  4. Simplify the result.

Example. 5/6 + 7/8. LCM(6,8) = 24. 5/6 = 20/24; 7/8 = 21/24. Sum = 41/24 = 1 17/24.

Example. 11/12 − 7/18. LCM(12,18) = 36. 11/12 = 33/36; 7/18 = 14/36. Difference = 33/36 − 14/36 = 19/36.

Mixed numbers. Convert to improper fractions first.

Example. 2 1/3 + 1 1/4. = 7/3 + 5/4. LCM(3,4) = 12. 7/3 = 28/12; 5/4 = 15/12. Sum = 43/12 = 3 7/12.

Example. 5 − 2 3/8. = 5 − 19/8 = 40/8 − 19/8 = 21/8 = 2 5/8.

Real-life fraction word problems

Problem 1. Riya drinks 2/5 litre of water in the morning and 3/8 litre in the afternoon. How much in total?

Solution: LCM(5,8)=40. 2/5 = 16/40; 3/8 = 15/40. Total = 31/40 litre.

Problem 2. A piece of ribbon is 5 1/2 metres long. 2 3/4 metres are cut off. How much is left?

Solution: 5 1/2 − 2 3/4 = 11/2 − 11/4 = 22/4 − 11/4 = 11/4 = 2 3/4 metres.

Problem 3. A farmer used 3/4 of his land for wheat, 1/8 for rice and the rest for vegetables. What fraction is used for vegetables?

Solution: vegetables = 1 − 3/4 − 1/8 = 8/8 − 6/8 − 1/8 = 1/8.

Problem 4. Out of 60 students in a class, 2/5 are girls. How many boys?

Solution: girls = 2/5 × 60 = 24. Boys = 60 − 24 = 36.

Problem 5. A jug holds 3 1/2 litres of juice. Glasses of capacity 1/4 litre are filled. How many glasses?

Solution: 3 1/2 ÷ 1/4 = 7/2 ÷ 1/4 = 7/2 × 4 = 14 glasses.

History of fractions — the Indian story

Fractions have a rich history, with major Indian contributions.

  • Egyptian unit fractions (c. 2000 BCE). Egyptians wrote all fractions as sums of distinct unit fractions: 3/4 = 1/2 + 1/4; 2/5 = 1/3 + 1/15.
  • Greek fractions. Greek mathematicians wrote fractions awkwardly, often in words.
  • India — Bakhshali manuscript (c. 200 CE). Earliest known use of a notation similar to today's: writing one number above another (without a horizontal bar yet).
  • Brahmagupta (628 CE). Gave rules for addition, subtraction, multiplication and division of fractions, including negative fractions.
  • Bhaskara II (1150 CE) — Lilavati. Treats fractions extensively with beautiful word problems and verses. Introduced the horizontal bar between numerator and denominator.
  • Al-Hassar (Morocco, 12th century). Adopted the horizontal bar notation from India and spread it westwards.
  • Europe (15th century). Adopted the modern notation only after Renaissance, via Arab and Indian texts.

So the next time you write 3/4, remember: the bar between 3 and 4 was an Indian invention!

📝 NCERT-style review questions — Chapter 7

Q1. Express 7/3 as a mixed number.

Q2. Order from smallest to largest: 3/4, 5/8, 2/3.

Q3. 5/6 + 1/4 = ?

Q4. 3 2/5 − 1 3/10 = ?

Q5. Reduce 56/84 to lowest terms.

Q6. Out of 35 students, 3/7 ride a bike to school. How many bike riders?

Q7. A rope 4 1/2 m long is cut into 3/4 m pieces. How many pieces?

Q8. Are 18/24 and 27/36 equivalent? Justify by cross multiplication.

Answers:
  1. 2 1/3.
  2. LCM(4,8,3)=24: 3/4=18/24, 5/8=15/24, 2/3=16/24 ⇒ 5/8 < 2/3 < 3/4.
  3. LCM(6,4)=12 ⇒ 10/12 + 3/12 = 13/12 = 1 1/12.
  4. 17/5 − 13/10 = 34/10 − 13/10 = 21/10 = 2 1/10.
  5. HCF(56,84)=28 ⇒ 2/3.
  6. 3/7 × 35 = 15.
  7. 9/2 ÷ 3/4 = 9/2 × 4/3 = 36/6 = 6 pieces.
  8. Cross: 18×36 = 648; 24×27 = 648. Equal ⇒ yes, equivalent.

📌 Chapter 7 — Fractions skill check

  • I can interpret a fraction as part of a whole, part of a collection and a point on the number line.
  • I can build fraction strips and use them to add and subtract.
  • I can find equivalent fractions and reduce to lowest terms.
  • I can compare fractions by same-denominator, same-numerator, LCM and cross-multiplication methods.
  • I can add and subtract any pair of fractions, including mixed numbers.
  • I can solve real-life problems involving fractions.
  • I know the Indian origins of the fraction bar notation.
Mastery Practice Pack — Chapter 7Equivalents, simplification and operations

Equivalent-fraction drills (10 questions)

Fraction3 equivalents
1/22/4, 5/10, 50/100
2/34/6, 10/15, 24/36
3/46/8, 9/12, 75/100
4/58/10, 12/15, 40/50
5/610/12, 15/18, 50/60
3/76/14, 9/21, 30/70
2/94/18, 6/27, 20/90
5/1210/24, 15/36, 50/120
7/814/16, 21/24, 70/80
11/1322/26, 33/39, 110/130

Reduce-to-lowest-terms drill (10 questions)

FractionHCFLowest terms
14/2172/3
18/2463/4
25/3555/7
36/48123/4
45/60153/4
54/72183/4
72/96243/4
84/126422/3
100/250502/5
225/300753/4

Add & subtract drill (10 questions)

  1. 1/2 + 1/3 = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6.
  2. 2/5 + 3/10 = 4/10 + 3/10 = 7/10.
  3. 1/4 + 5/6 = 3/12 + 10/12 = 13/12 = 1 1/12.
  4. 7/8 − 1/4 = 7/8 − 2/8 = 5/8.
  5. 5/6 − 2/9 = 15/18 − 4/18 = 11/18.
  6. 2 1/3 + 1 1/2 = 7/3 + 3/2 = 14/6 + 9/6 = 23/6 = 3 5/6.
  7. 3 − 1 5/8 = 24/8 − 13/8 = 11/8 = 1 3/8.
  8. 4 1/4 + 2 2/3 = 17/4 + 8/3 = 51/12 + 32/12 = 83/12 = 6 11/12.
  9. 5/6 + 1/4 + 2/3 = 10/12 + 3/12 + 8/12 = 21/12 = 7/4 = 1 3/4.
  10. 9/10 − 3/5 = 9/10 − 6/10 = 3/10.

Word-problem set (5 questions)

P1. Tina ate 2/5 of a chocolate bar; her brother ate 3/10. Who ate more and by how much?

Solution. 2/5 = 4/10. Tina ate more by 4/10 − 3/10 = 1/10.

P2. A water tank is 3/4 full. After 1/4 of it is used, what fraction remains?

Solution. 3/4 − 3/16 = 12/16 − 3/16 = 9/16.

P3. A piece of cloth 7 1/2 m long is cut into pieces of 5/6 m each. How many pieces?

Solution. 15/2 ÷ 5/6 = 15/2 × 6/5 = 90/10 = 9 pieces.

P4. Add 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + 1/5. (Use LCM = 60.)

Solution. 30/60 + 20/60 + 15/60 + 12/60 = 77/60 = 1 17/60.

P5. A jar contains 5 2/3 kg of sugar. 2 5/6 kg are removed. How much remains?

Solution. 17/3 − 17/6 = 34/6 − 17/6 = 17/6 = 2 5/6 kg.

Fraction operations — quick reference table

OperationRuleExample
Like-denominator adda/c + b/c = (a+b)/c2/7 + 3/7 = 5/7
Like-denominator subtracta/c − b/c = (a−b)/c5/9 − 2/9 = 3/9 = 1/3
Unlike-denominator addconvert to LCM denom1/3 + 1/4 = 4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12
Multiply fractionsa/b × c/d = ac/bd2/3 × 4/5 = 8/15
Divide fractionsa/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c3/4 ÷ 2/5 = 15/8
Mixed → impropera b/c = (ac+b)/c3 2/5 = 17/5
Improper → mixeddivide, get quotient + remainder17/5 = 3 r 2 = 3 2/5
Reducedivide top and bottom by HCF18/24 = 3/4 (HCF 6)
Comparecross-multiply: a/b vs c/d → ad vs bc2/3 vs 3/5 → 10 vs 9 → 2/3 > 3/5

Chapter 7 glossary

  • Fraction. A number of the form a/b, with b ≠ 0; represents a parts out of b equal parts.
  • Numerator. The top number (above the bar).
  • Denominator. The bottom number (below the bar).
  • Proper fraction. Numerator < denominator (e.g. 3/4).
  • Improper fraction. Numerator ≥ denominator (e.g. 7/4).
  • Mixed number. Sum of a whole number and a proper fraction (e.g. 1 3/4).
  • Equivalent fractions. Fractions representing the same value (1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6).
  • Lowest terms (simplest form). Fraction with HCF of numerator and denominator equal to 1.
  • Like fractions. Same denominator.
  • Unlike fractions. Different denominators.
  • Reciprocal. The fraction obtained by swapping numerator and denominator.

Exam-prep question bank — Chapter 7 (25 questions)

  1. What is a fraction? Define numerator and denominator.
  2. Express 9/4 as a mixed number.
  3. Express 3 5/8 as an improper fraction.
  4. List 3 equivalents of 2/5.
  5. Reduce 60/72 to lowest terms.
  6. Compare 3/4 and 5/8.
  7. Compare 2/3 and 7/10.
  8. Order 1/2, 3/4, 5/8, 7/12 from smallest to largest.
  9. 1/3 + 1/6 = ?
  10. 5/8 + 1/4 = ?
  11. 7/9 − 4/9 = ?
  12. 3/4 − 5/12 = ?
  13. 2 1/4 + 1 3/8 = ?
  14. 5 − 1 5/6 = ?
  15. 2/3 × 3/4 = ?
  16. 5/6 ÷ 2/3 = ?
  17. Is 18/24 equal to 27/36? Justify.
  18. A bucket is 3/4 full. After using 1/4 of total capacity, what remains?
  19. A rope 8 m long is cut into pieces of 2/3 m each. How many pieces?
  20. Of 36 students, 7/12 wear glasses. How many?
  21. Add 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4.
  22. Subtract 9/10 from 1.
  23. Convert 0.75 to a fraction in lowest terms.
  24. Convert 3/8 to a decimal.
  25. Find half of 5/6.
Answer key (click to expand)
  1. A fraction a/b means a parts out of b. Top is numerator, bottom is denominator.
  2. 2 1/4.
  3. 29/8.
  4. 4/10, 6/15, 20/50.
  5. 5/6 (HCF = 12).
  6. 3/4 = 6/8 > 5/8.
  7. 2/3 = 20/30; 7/10 = 21/30 ⇒ 7/10 > 2/3.
  8. LCM 24: 12/24, 18/24, 15/24, 14/24 ⇒ 7/12 < 5/8 < 1/2 < 3/4. Wait re-check: 1/2 = 12/24, 3/4 = 18/24, 5/8 = 15/24, 7/12 = 14/24. Order: 12, 14, 15, 18 ⇒ 1/2 < 7/12 < 5/8 < 3/4.
  9. 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2.
  10. 5/8 + 2/8 = 7/8.
  11. 3/9 = 1/3.
  12. 9/12 − 5/12 = 4/12 = 1/3.
  13. 9/4 + 11/8 = 18/8 + 11/8 = 29/8 = 3 5/8.
  14. 30/6 − 11/6 = 19/6 = 3 1/6.
  15. 6/12 = 1/2.
  16. 5/6 × 3/2 = 15/12 = 5/4 = 1 1/4.
  17. Cross: 18×36 = 648; 24×27 = 648. Yes, equal.
  18. 3/4 − 1/4 = 2/4 = 1/2.
  19. 8 ÷ 2/3 = 8 × 3/2 = 12 pieces.
  20. 7/12 × 36 = 21.
  21. LCM 12: 6/12 + 4/12 + 3/12 = 13/12 = 1 1/12.
  22. 10/10 − 9/10 = 1/10.
  23. 75/100 = 3/4.
  24. 0.375.
  25. 5/12.

Common errors and how to avoid them

  • Adding numerators AND denominators — wrong! 1/2 + 1/3 is NOT 2/5.
  • Forgetting to convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before computing.
  • Forgetting to convert back to mixed number at the end.
  • Forgetting to reduce to lowest terms.
  • Not finding a common denominator before adding or subtracting.
  • Using the smaller common multiple instead of the LCM (still works, but extra work).
  • Confusing “greater fraction” with “greater denominator”.
  • Forgetting the negative sign when subtracting larger from smaller.
  • Mistakes in multiplication when reducing across fractions (only reduce vertically, or recognise common factors before multiplying).
  • Dividing by a fraction by adding instead of multiplying by the reciprocal.
  • Forgetting that a/b = a ÷ b (a fraction is also a division).
  • Confusing the reciprocal with the negative.

Real-world applications of fractions

  • Cooking and baking. Recipes use 1/2 cup, 3/4 teaspoon, 1/3 of a stick.
  • Music. Half notes, quarter notes, eighth notes, sixteenth notes.
  • Time. Quarter past, half past, three-quarters of an hour.
  • Shopping discounts. 1/4 off, half-price sales.
  • Stock market. Share prices and percentage gains/losses.
  • Construction. Pipe diameters in inches (e.g. 3/4″ pipe).
  • Sports statistics. Batting averages, free-throw percentages.
  • Probability. The chance of an event is a fraction between 0 and 1.
  • Maps and scales. 1:50,000 is a fraction scale.
  • Carpentry. Length measurements like 3 5/8 inches.
  • Geometry. Areas of irregular plots often involve fractional dimensions.
  • Daily activity. Fractions of a day for tasks, fractions of a class for groups.

Extended chapter wrap-up — Chapter 7

Fractions extend the system of whole numbers to a much richer landscape. Without fractions, we could not divide a pizza, measure a recipe, share rent, calculate a discount, or describe an athletic record. The concept of a fraction is among the most useful ideas in mathematics, with applications in every walk of life.

What did we learn? First, that a fraction has three interpretations: a part of a whole (1/2 of a chocolate bar), a part of a collection (1/3 of a class of 30), and a point on the number line (1/4 sits between 0 and 1). Second, that equivalent fractions represent the same value — we can multiply (or divide) both numerator and denominator by the same number without changing the value. Third, that the simplest form is obtained by dividing top and bottom by their HCF. Fourth, that comparing fractions requires either a common denominator or cross-multiplication.

We learned operations: addition and subtraction require a common denominator (use LCM); multiplication simply multiplies tops and bottoms; division means multiplying by the reciprocal. Mixed numbers are converted to improper fractions before computing, then converted back at the end.

Historically, fractions have a deep Indian origin. The Bakhshali manuscript (c. 200 CE) and Brahmagupta's rules (628 CE) systematised fraction arithmetic. Bhaskara II's Lilavati (1150 CE) introduced the horizontal bar between numerator and denominator that we still use today. Arab mathematicians adopted this notation from India and spread it westwards, so the modern world owes the appearance of fractions to Indian mathematics.

In real life, fractions appear in cooking (1/2 cup, 3/4 teaspoon), music (half notes, quarter notes), time (quarter past, half past), shopping (1/4 off, half-price sale), sports (batting averages), maps (1:50,000 scale), and countless other places.

Looking ahead, in Class 7 you will multiply and divide fractions with confidence, and meet decimal fractions (0.5 = 1/2, 0.25 = 1/4). In Class 8 you will study rational numbers (fractions with possibly negative numerators or denominators). In higher classes you will meet irrational numbers, real numbers, and eventually complex numbers. The journey starts here, with the simple question: how do we share one cake among three friends?

Suggested further activities

Activity 1. Cut 8 paper strips and build fraction strips for 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5, 1/6, 1/8, 1/12.

Activity 2. Use your fraction strips to verify 1/2 + 1/4 = 3/4 and 1/3 + 1/6 = 1/2.

Activity 3. Find a recipe in your home and rewrite all measurements as fractions in lowest terms.

Activity 4. Mark all fractions with denominator 6 from 0 to 2 on a number line.

Activity 5. List 5 fractions equivalent to 7/9. Verify by cross-multiplication.

Activity 6. Take a chocolate bar and divide it into 12 equal pieces. Express different shares as fractions.

Activity 7. Find 10 fractions on a clock face (1/12 of an hour, 1/4 of an hour, etc.).

Activity 8. Compare the fractions 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5, 1/6 on a number line. Observe the spacing.

Activity 9. Research the history of the fraction bar; write a short report on Bhaskara II.

Activity 10. Solve 10 mixed-number addition/subtraction problems from a textbook or worksheet.

Activity 11. Convert 0.125, 0.375, 0.625, 0.875 to fractions in lowest terms.

Activity 12. Convert 1/8, 3/8, 5/8, 7/8 to decimals.

Quick-recap cheat sheet

Fraction: a/b means a parts out of b equal parts.

Numerator (top): how many parts you have.

Denominator (bottom): total equal parts.

Proper fraction: numerator < denominator (e.g. 3/5).

Improper fraction: numerator ≥ denominator (e.g. 7/4).

Mixed number: whole + proper fraction (e.g. 1 3/4 = 7/4).

Equivalent fractions: multiply (or divide) top and bottom by the same nonzero number.

Lowest terms: divide top and bottom by their HCF.

Comparing: same denominator ⇒ bigger numerator wins. Different ⇒ convert to common denominator or cross-multiply.

Addition: same denominator ⇒ add numerators. Different ⇒ convert to LCM denominator first.

Subtraction: same denominator ⇒ subtract numerators. Different ⇒ convert to LCM denominator first.

Multiplication: (a/b) × (c/d) = (ac)/(bd). Reduce before multiplying when possible.

Division: (a/b) ÷ (c/d) = (a/b) × (d/c). Multiply by the reciprocal.
