Mixed-number arithmetic: convert to improper fractions first, then operate, then convert back.

Cross-product equality: a/b = c/d iff ad = bc.

Indian heritage: the horizontal bar in fraction notation comes from Bhaskara II.

Reciprocal: swap numerator and denominator.

Decimal equivalents: 1/2 = 0.5; 1/4 = 0.25; 3/4 = 0.75; 1/5 = 0.2; 1/8 = 0.125.

Common pitfall: never add denominators (1/2 + 1/3 is NOT 2/5).

📋 Chapter 8 — What's Inside

📖 Topics Covered

  • 8.1 Artwork & Constructions
  • 8.2 Circles
  • 8.3 Drawing Circles with Compass
  • 8.4 Constructing Line Segments
  • 8.5 Constructing Squares & Rectangles
  • 8.6 Points Equidistant from Two Given Points

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 8.1 — Artwork Patterns
  • Section 8.2 — Circle Parts
  • Section 8.3 — Compass Constructions
  • Section 8.4 — Squares & Rectangles
  • Section 8.5 — Equidistant Points

Chapter 8: Playing with Constructions

🎯 Why we learn constructions

Geometry is not just for looking at — it is for making! Architects, designers, engineers, carpenters and even artists need to draw shapes precisely. With just two simple tools — a ruler (for straight edges) and a compass (for circles and arcs) — you can construct an amazing variety of perfect figures: equal segments, perpendicular lines, perfect angles, regular polygons, beautiful rose-petal patterns, and more. In this chapter you will master both the tools and the skill of construction.

In-Depth Study — Chapter 8Each construction explained step-by-step with diagrams

§8.1 — Artwork & the Tools of Construction (In Depth)

The geometry box contains a small set of tools, each designed for a specific job:

  • Ruler (scale): draws straight lines and measures lengths.
  • Compass: draws circles and arcs of any radius (and copies lengths).
  • Divider: picks up a length and transfers it to another place.
  • Protractor: measures and marks angles in degrees.
  • Set-squares (30°-60°-90° and 45°-45°-90°): draw perpendiculars and parallels quickly.

Why ruler-and-compass constructions are special

Using only a ruler (without measurements) and a compass, the Greeks discovered they could build many perfect shapes. This is “classical geometry,” and it has rules:

  1. You may draw a straight line through any two points.
  2. You may draw a circle (or arc) with any centre and any radius.
  3. You may mark a point where two lines, two arcs, or a line and an arc meet.

That’s it! From these three actions, thousands of beautiful geometric truths follow.

§8.2 — Parts of a Circle (In Depth)

A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at the same fixed distance from a single point. That single point is the centre, and the fixed distance is the radius.

PartMeaning
Centre (O)The middle point of the circle.
Radius (r)Distance from the centre to any point on the circle.
Diameter (d)A chord passing through the centre. d = 2r.
ChordA line segment joining any two points on the circle.
ArcA part of the circle’s boundary (a curve).
SectorThe region between two radii and the arc (like a pizza slice).
SegmentThe region between a chord and the arc above it.
CircumferenceThe total boundary length of the circle.

Did you know? The diameter is the longest chord of a circle. No other chord can be longer than a diameter.

§8.3 — Drawing Line Segments and Circles (In Depth)

How to draw a line segment of a given length

  1. Place the ruler firmly on the paper.
  2. Mark a starting point A.
  3. Read along the ruler until you reach the required length (e.g., 5 cm) and mark point B.
  4. Join A and B with a sharp pencil. Segment AB = 5 cm.

How to draw a circle of a given radius

  1. Mark the centre O on the paper.
  2. Open the compass to the required radius (use a ruler to set the compass distance).
  3. Place the pointed leg on O.
  4. Without changing the opening, rotate the pencil leg all the way around.
  5. The pencil traces out a perfect circle.

Copying a line segment with a compass

  1. Place the compass tip on A, pencil tip on B (so it measures AB).
  2. Lift the compass — do NOT change the opening.
  3. Place the tip at the new starting point P.
  4. Strike an arc and mark Q where it crosses your new line. PQ = AB.

§8.4 — Important Compass Constructions (In Depth)

Construction 1: A perpendicular bisector of a line segment

  1. Open the compass to more than half of AB.
  2. From A, draw an arc above and below AB.
  3. Without changing the opening, repeat from B. The two pairs of arcs cross at two points P and Q.
  4. Join P and Q. PQ is perpendicular to AB and cuts it in half at its midpoint M.

Construction 2: A 60° angle

  1. Draw a ray AB.
  2. With centre A and any radius, draw an arc cutting AB at X.
  3. With centre X and the same radius, draw another arc cutting the first arc at Y.
  4. Join AY. ∠BAY = 60°. (This works because triangle AXY is equilateral.)

Construction 3: An equilateral triangle of side a

  1. Draw segment BC of length a.
  2. With centre B and radius a, draw an arc above BC.
  3. With centre C and radius a, draw another arc that crosses the first at point A.
  4. Join AB and AC. Triangle ABC is equilateral with each side a.

Construction 4: The rose / flower pattern

This is the famous “6-petal flower” you can draw with just a compass:

  1. Draw a circle of any radius r.
  2. Keeping the same radius, place the compass tip on the circle and draw an arc that passes through the centre.
  3. Where each new arc meets the original circle, move the compass tip there and repeat.
  4. You will get 6 petals arranged around the centre — the “flower of life.”

Why does the 6-petal flower work? Each petal arc has the same radius as the main circle. Six of them fit perfectly because the chord of a 60° arc equals the radius — and 6 × 60° = 360°.

§8.5 — Constructing Squares and Rectangles (In Depth)

Constructing a square of side a

  1. Draw segment AB = a.
  2. At A, construct a perpendicular and mark D at distance a above A.
  3. At B, construct another perpendicular and mark C at distance a above B.
  4. Join CD. ABCD is a square.

Constructing a rectangle of sides l and b

Exactly the same steps, but use length l for AB and length b for the perpendiculars.

Tips for clean constructions

  • Use a sharp pencil — thin lines give precise intersections.
  • Don’t press too hard on the compass — it can slip.
  • Make light construction arcs first; darken final lines only at the end.
  • Never change the compass width in the middle of a step unless told to.

§8.6 — Points Equidistant from Two Given Points (In Depth)

Suppose two friends, A and B, are standing in a field. Where should you stand so that you are the same distance from both of them?

If you try a few positions, you’ll discover something beautiful: every “fair” spot lies on a single straight line. That line is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

How to construct the set of equidistant points

  1. Take the two given points A and B and join them.
  2. Open the compass to more than half of AB.
  3. With centre A, draw a long arc above and below AB.
  4. With the same radius and centre B, draw another arc that crosses the first one at two points, P and Q.
  5. Join P and Q. Every point on this line is at the same distance from A as from B.

Why does this work? The arcs from A and B have the same radius. So any intersection point is the same distance from A as from B. NCERT calls this the equidistant property, and it’s the secret behind the construction of the perpendicular bisector.

A real-life use

If two villages A and B want to build a water tank at a place that is equally far from both, the tank should be placed somewhere on the perpendicular bisector of AB. The same idea is used to design fair bus stops, mobile towers, and shared playgrounds.

Try this. Mark any three non-collinear points A, B, C on paper. Draw the perpendicular bisectors of AB, BC and CA. All three meet at a single point — the circumcentre, which is equally far from A, B and C!

🎯 Big picture of Chapter 8

  • Geometry box tools: ruler, compass, divider, protractor, set-squares.
  • A circle is defined by its centre and radius; chord, diameter, arc, sector follow.
  • Compass = constant-distance tool; rules out by which we transfer lengths and build perfect shapes.
  • Key constructions: perpendicular bisector, 60° angle, equilateral triangle, 6-petal flower, square.

⚠️ Common mistakes

  • Changing the compass radius accidentally mid-construction.
  • Drawing arcs too short, so they don’t intersect.
  • Using a blunt pencil — intersection points become uncertain.
  • Forgetting to label points or marks; later steps then become confusing.
Quick Reference — Chapter 8The original briefer summary follows

§8.2 — Parts of a Circle

§8.3–8.5 — Constructions with Compass

Tools needed: Ruler (for straight lines) + Compass (for circles/arcs)

Key constructions:

  • Circle: Set compass to radius, place tip on centre, rotate full turn
  • Line segment of given length: Mark one end, use ruler to measure, mark other end
  • Square: Draw base, construct right angles at each corner, mark equal sides
  • Regular hexagon: Draw circle, walk the compass around (radius = side)!

Flower patterns: Draw a circle. Without changing compass, place tip on any point on the circle and draw an arc. Move to where arc meets circle, repeat. Creates beautiful 6-petal flowers!

Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved

📝 Quick Recap

  • Circle: all points at same distance from centre
  • Radius: centre to circumference | Diameter: 2× radius
  • Chord: line joining any two points on circle (diameter is longest chord)
  • Compass constructions use the fact that compass keeps a fixed distance

Figure it Out — Section 8.1 (Artwork)

Q1. Draw the following patterns using compass and ruler.

Solution (Method):

6-petal flower:

  1. Draw a circle with any radius r
  2. Without changing compass width, put tip on any point on the circle
  3. Draw an arc that crosses the circle
  4. Move compass tip to where arc crosses circle
  5. Repeat — after 6 arcs you get a beautiful flower!

💡 This works because a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle has side = radius!

Q. Draw a circle of radius 3 cm. Draw 5 radii. Are all equal?

Solution:

Yes! All radii of the same circle are equal in length. That's the definition of a circle — all points on it are at the SAME distance from the centre.

All 5 radii = 3 cm each.

Q. Construct a square with side 4 cm.

Solution (Steps):

  1. Draw base AB = 4 cm using ruler
  2. At A, construct a 90° angle (use compass: draw arc, then perpendicular)
  3. Mark point D at 4 cm along the perpendicular
  4. At B, construct another 90° angle, mark point C at 4 cm
  5. Join D to C

Verify: All sides = 4 cm, all angles = 90° ✓

Q. Construct a rectangle with length 5 cm and breadth 3 cm.

Solution (Steps):

  1. Draw base AB = 5 cm
  2. At A, construct 90° and mark D at 3 cm
  3. At B, construct 90° and mark C at 3 cm
  4. Join D to C (should be 5 cm — verify!)

Figure it Out — Section 8.2 (Squares & Rectangles on Dot Grid)

Q1. Which of the following is NOT a valid name for a square with corners P, Q, R, S (in order)?

(1) PQSR   (2) SPQR   (3) RSPQ   (4) QRSP

Solution:

(1) PQSR is NOT a valid name.

In a valid name, consecutive letters must be adjacent corners. In PQSR, going P→Q→S→R, Q and S are diagonal (not adjacent), so this doesn’t trace the boundary.

Q2. Identify if there are any squares in this collection of quadrilaterals drawn on a dot grid. Use measurements if needed.

Solution:

Check two properties: (i) all four sides equal, (ii) all angles = 90°.

On a dot grid, you can verify by counting dots along sides and checking that adjacent sides are perpendicular (one goes horizontal/vertical, other goes at right angles).

A figure is a square only if it satisfies both properties. Figures that have equal sides but angles ≠ 90° are rhombuses, not squares.

Q3. Draw at least 3 rotated squares and rectangles on a dot grid such that their corners are on the dots. Verify their properties.

Solution:

Method: A “tilted” square on a dot grid can be drawn by going (say) 2 dots right and 1 dot up for one side, then turning 90°: 1 dot left and 2 dots up for the next side, and so on.

Verification:

  • Side length = √(2² + 1²) = √5 for all sides → Equal ✓
  • Adjacent sides are perpendicular (dot product = 0) → 90° angles ✓

For a tilted rectangle: use different “step patterns” for length and breadth but keep them perpendicular.

Figure it Out — Section 8.3 (Constructing Rectangles)

Q1. Draw a rectangle with sides of length 4 cm and 6 cm. After drawing, check if it satisfies both rectangle properties.

Solution:

Steps:

  1. Draw AB = 6 cm (base)
  2. At A, use compass to construct 90°; mark D at 4 cm
  3. At B, construct 90°; mark C at 4 cm
  4. Join DC

Verification:

  • Property 1: Opposite sides equal → AB = DC = 6 cm, AD = BC = 4 cm ✓
  • Property 2: All angles = 90° ✓

Q2. Draw a rectangle of sides 2 cm and 10 cm. After drawing, check if it satisfies both rectangle properties.

Solution:

Same method as Q1. Draw PQ = 10 cm as base, then perpendiculars of 2 cm at each end.

Verification: PQ = SR = 10 cm, PS = QR = 2 cm ✓; All angles = 90° ✓

💡 This rectangle is very long and thin — almost like a ribbon!

Q3. Is it possible to construct a 4-sided figure in which all angles are 90° but opposite sides are not equal?

Solution:

No! It is not possible.

Reason: If all four angles are 90°, then the figure must be a rectangle (this can be proved). And in every rectangle, opposite sides are always equal. So you cannot have all angles 90° without opposite sides being equal.

Figure it Out — Section 8.4 (Exploring Diagonals)

Q. Construct a rectangle that can be divided into 3 identical squares. What should the relationship between length and breadth be?

Solution:

If breadth = b, then each square has side b. Three squares in a row means:

Length = 3 × Breadth

Example: breadth = 2 cm ⇒ length = 6 cm. The rectangle 6 cm × 2 cm can be divided into three 2 cm × 2 cm squares.

Q. Give the lengths of sides of a rectangle that CANNOT be divided into (a) two identical squares (b) three identical squares.

Solution:

(a) For division into 2 identical squares, we need length = 2 × breadth. So any rectangle where length ≠ 2 × breadth works.

Example: 4 cm × 2.5 cm (since 4 ≠ 2 × 2.5 = 5)

(b) For division into 3 identical squares, we need length = 3 × breadth.

Example: 7 cm × 2 cm (since 7 ≠ 3 × 2 = 6)

Q4. How do you construct a “Square with a Hole” (a square with a circular hole at its centre)?

Solution:

  1. Construct a square ABCD of desired side length
  2. Draw both diagonals AC and BD
  3. The point where diagonals meet is the centre of the square
  4. Place compass tip at this centre point
  5. Choose a radius smaller than half the side length
  6. Draw the circle — this creates the hole!

💡 The centre of a square is always at the intersection of its diagonals.

Figure it Out — Section 8.5 (Rectangles & Diagonals)

Q. How should a rectangle be constructed so that its diagonal divides the opposite angles into equal parts?

Solution:

The rectangle must be a square!

Reason: A diagonal divides opposite angles into equal parts only when adjacent sides are equal. In a rectangle, this happens only when length = breadth, i.e., when it becomes a square.

In a square, each diagonal divides the 90° corners into two 45° angles.

Q1. Construct a rectangle in which one of the diagonals divides the opposite angles into 50° and 40°.

Solution:

  1. Draw base AB of suitable length
  2. At A, construct angle of 50° above AB (this will be part of the corner angle)
  3. At B, construct angle of 40° above AB
  4. Where these lines meet gives a vertex of the triangle formed by the diagonal
  5. Complete the rectangle using perpendiculars

The diagonal makes 50° with one side and 40° with the adjacent side (50° + 40° = 90°).

Q2. Construct a rectangle in which one of the diagonals divides the opposite angles into 45° and 45°. What do you observe about the sides?

Solution:

When the diagonal divides the angle into two equal parts of 45° each, the rectangle turns out to be a square!

Observation: All four sides are equal in length.

This confirms that a diagonal bisects the corner angles only in a square.

Q3. Construct a rectangle one of whose sides is 4 cm and the diagonal is of length 8 cm.

Solution:

  1. Draw AB = 4 cm
  2. At A, construct 90°
  3. From B, with compass opened to 8 cm, draw an arc that cuts the perpendicular at D
  4. AD is the other side of the rectangle
  5. Complete the rectangle ABCD

Using Pythagoras: AD = √(8² − 4²) = √(64 − 16) = √48 = 4√3 ≈ 6.93 cm

Q4. Construct a rectangle one of whose sides is 3 cm and the diagonal is of length 7 cm.

Solution:

Same method as Q3.

Other side = √(7² − 3²) = √(49 − 9) = √40 = 2√10 ≈ 6.32 cm

So the rectangle has dimensions 3 cm × 6.32 cm with diagonal = 7 cm.

Figure it Out — Section 8.6 (House Construction)

Q1. Construct a bigger house in which all the sides are of length 7 cm.

Solution:

  1. Construct a square ABCD with side 7 cm (the walls)
  2. Find midpoints of top side — mark midpoint M of DC
  3. From D and C, draw arcs of radius 7 cm; they meet at point E above M
  4. Join DE and CE — this forms the triangular roof

The house shape is a pentagon with all sides = 7 cm. The roof is an equilateral triangle sitting on top of the square.

Q3. Is there a 4-sided figure in which all sides are equal in length but which is NOT a square? If yes, can you construct it?

Solution:

Yes! A rhombus (that is not a square) has all four sides equal but angles are NOT 90°.

Construction:

  1. Draw AB = 5 cm
  2. At A, construct an angle of 60° (not 90°!)
  3. Mark D at 5 cm along this line
  4. From D, draw arc of 5 cm; from B, draw arc of 5 cm
  5. Where arcs intersect is C. Join DC and BC.

Result: ABCD has all sides = 5 cm but angles are 60°, 120°, 60°, 120° — it’s a rhombus!

💭 Strategy Tip

For compass constructions: (1) Never change the compass width in the middle of a construction unless instructed, (2) Make light arcs — you can darken the final lines later, (3) The "flower" pattern is the basis for many complex designs — master it!

🧠 Self-Check Quiz

  1. If radius = 5 cm, what is the diameter?
  2. Is a diameter also a chord?
  3. How many petals does the basic compass flower have?
  1. 10 cm → diameter = 2 × radius
  2. Yes! → It's the longest chord (passes through centre)
  3. 6 petals → because hexagon has 6 sides
Mastery Practice — Chapter 8Construction drill — extra examples with step-by-step instructions and a self-test

Worked Example Set A — Line segments and copying

  1. Q1. Construct a line segment AB of length 6.4 cm using ruler.
    Steps. (i) Place ruler on paper. (ii) Mark point A at 0 cm. (iii) Mark point B at 6.4 cm. (iv) Join A to B with sharp pencil.
  2. Q2. Copy a given segment PQ onto a new line using compass.
    Steps. (i) Open compass to length PQ. (ii) On a new line, mark a point P′. (iii) With compass on P′, draw an arc cutting the line at Q′. (iv) P′Q′ = PQ.
  3. Q3. Construct a segment of length equal to AB + CD, where AB = 4 cm and CD = 3 cm.
    Steps. Draw a long line. Mark point P. Using compass with radius AB = 4 cm, draw arc from P cutting the line at Q. From Q, using compass with radius CD = 3 cm, draw arc cutting the line at R. PR = 7 cm.
  4. Q4. Construct a segment of length AB − CD where AB = 7 cm, CD = 2.5 cm.
    Steps. Mark P on a line. Mark Q at AB = 7 cm. From Q, draw an arc of radius CD = 2.5 cm towards P, cutting at R. PR = 4.5 cm.

Worked Example Set B — Circles and arcs

  1. Q1. Draw a circle of radius 4.5 cm.
    Steps. (i) Mark centre O. (ii) Open compass to 4.5 cm using ruler. (iii) Place needle at O and rotate pencil to draw the circle.
  2. Q2. Draw two circles of radii 3 cm and 5 cm with the same centre (concentric circles).
    Steps. Mark O. Draw inner circle of radius 3 cm. Without moving O, draw outer circle of radius 5 cm.
  3. Q3. Draw a circle of radius 4 cm and mark a chord AB of length 6 cm.
    Steps. Draw circle of radius 4 cm. Mark any point A on the circle. With compass open to 6 cm, place at A and draw arc cutting the circle at B. AB is the chord.
  4. Q4. Draw a circle of radius 5 cm and inscribe a regular hexagon inside it.
    Steps. Draw the circle. Mark any point A on it. With compass still open to 5 cm (= radius), step around the circle marking points B, C, D, E, F. Join all six points. Hexagon ABCDEF is regular.
  5. Q5. Draw a 6-petal flower inside a circle of radius 4 cm.
    Steps. Draw the circle. Step around it with the same radius to mark 6 points. From each marked point, draw an arc of the same radius inside the circle. The 6 arcs form 6 overlapping petals.

Worked Example Set C — Squares and rectangles

  1. Q1. Construct a square ABCD of side 5 cm.
    Steps. (i) Draw AB = 5 cm. (ii) At A, draw a perpendicular using set-square or compass. (iii) On the perpendicular, cut AD = 5 cm. (iv) Repeat at B to mark C. (v) Join CD.
  2. Q2. Construct a rectangle of length 7 cm and breadth 4 cm.
    Steps. (i) Draw AB = 7 cm. (ii) At A and B, draw perpendiculars. (iii) Cut AD = 4 cm and BC = 4 cm. (iv) Join CD.
  3. Q3. Construct an equilateral triangle of side 6 cm.
    Steps. Draw AB = 6 cm. From A, draw arc of radius 6 cm. From B, draw arc of radius 6 cm. The two arcs meet at C. Join AC and BC.
  4. Q4. Construct a perpendicular bisector of segment AB = 8 cm.
    Steps. Draw AB. With centre A and radius more than half AB, draw arcs above and below. Repeat from B. The two pairs of arcs intersect at P (above) and Q (below). Line PQ is the perpendicular bisector.
  5. Q5. Construct an angle of 60° at a given point A.
    Steps. Draw a ray from A. With centre A and any convenient radius, draw an arc cutting the ray at P. With centre P and same radius, draw an arc cutting the first arc at Q. Join AQ. Angle PAQ = 60°.
  6. Q6. Construct an angle of 30°.
    Steps. First construct a 60° angle. Then bisect it by drawing two arcs from the two arms’ intersections and joining their crossing point to the vertex.
  7. Q7. Construct an angle of 90° using only compass and ruler.
    Steps. Draw a line. Mark point A. Draw a semicircle of any radius from A cutting the line at P. From P with same radius, draw an arc cutting the semicircle at Q. From Q with same radius, draw another arc cutting the semicircle at R. Bisect arc QR; the bisector with vertex A gives 90°.

Worked Example Set D — Real-life constructions

  1. Q1. Suresh wants to draw the floor plan of a square room of side 4 m using a scale of 1 cm = 1 m. What is the side of the drawing?
    Solution. Side in drawing = 4 cm.
  2. Q2. A circular flower bed has diameter 3 m. Draw it to a scale of 1 cm = 50 cm.
    Solution. Diameter on paper = 3 m / 50 cm = 6 cm; so radius on paper = 3 cm. Draw circle of radius 3 cm.
  3. Q3. Construct a rectangular kitchen garden plan, 6 m × 4 m, scale 1 cm = 1 m.
    Solution. Draw rectangle 6 cm × 4 cm.
  4. Q4. A pizza of diameter 12 cm is to be cut into 6 equal slices. Show how to mark the cuts.
    Solution. Draw circle of radius 6 cm. Mark a point A on it. Step around with radius 6 cm to mark 6 points. Join opposite points through centre ⇒ 6 equal sectors.

📝 Mini-quiz — Chapter 8

  1. Two main tools used for geometric constructions are ____ and ____.
  2. The radius of a circle is half the ____.
  3. A regular hexagon inscribed in a circle has side equal to ____.
  4. The perpendicular bisector of a segment is found by drawing two arcs from each ____ of the segment.
  5. To construct a 60° angle, place the compass needle at the vertex and the pencil at the ____.
  6. The diagonals of a rectangle drawn carefully always meet at the ____ of the rectangle.
Answers:
  1. ruler, compass
  2. diameter
  3. radius of the circle
  4. endpoint
  5. same radius arc point
  6. centre

🎯 Chapter 8 — Key takeaways

  • A compass draws arcs and circles of fixed radius.
  • A ruler draws straight segments and measures lengths.
  • Special compass constructions: copying a segment, perpendicular bisector, 60° angle, equilateral triangle, regular hexagon, 6-petal flower.
  • Set-squares help draw 90° and 60°/45° angles quickly.
  • Constructions are accurate when you keep the compass radius unchanged where needed.
Advanced Practice Lab — Chapter 8Detailed step-by-step construction projects and a guided puzzle workshop

Project 1 — Constructing a square using only compass and ruler

This is a beautiful classical construction.

Step 1. Draw a horizontal line and mark a point A on it.

Step 2. Using a compass, draw a circle of suitable radius (say 4 cm) centred at A. Let it cut the line at point P on the right of A.

Step 3. Now we need to erect a perpendicular at A. Place the compass at P (still open to the same radius 4 cm) and draw an arc cutting the circle again at Q.

Step 4. Without changing the radius, place compass at Q and draw another arc cutting the circle at R.

Step 5. Bisect arc QR by drawing two arcs from Q and R of any equal radius, intersecting at S above the line.

Step 6. Draw line AS. This line is perpendicular to the original line at A.

Step 7. On the perpendicular AS, mark point B such that AB equals the desired side length (say 5 cm) using the compass.

Step 8. Now mark C on the horizontal line so that AC = 5 cm.

Step 9. From B, draw an arc of radius 5 cm. From C, draw another arc of radius 5 cm. They intersect at D.

Step 10. Join BD and CD. Square ABDC is complete with all four sides = 5 cm and all four angles = 90°.

Why it works: the perpendicular construction in Steps 3 to 6 uses the fact that arcs of equal radius from symmetric points give us right angles.

Project 2 — Bisecting any given angle

Given: any angle ∠ABC.

Step 1. Place compass needle at vertex B.

Step 2. Draw an arc of convenient radius cutting BA at P and BC at Q.

Step 3. Place compass at P, draw an arc (radius greater than half PQ) towards the interior of the angle.

Step 4. Without changing radius, place compass at Q and draw another arc. Let the two arcs intersect at point R inside the angle.

Step 5. Join BR. The ray BR bisects the angle ∠ABC.

Verification: with a protractor you can check that ∠ABR = ∠RBC.

Why it works: triangles BPR and BQR are congruent (three pairs of equal sides), so the angles at B are equal.

Project 3 — Constructing a regular hexagon and a six-petal flower

This is one of the most pleasing classical constructions.

Step 1. Draw a circle of radius r (any convenient size, say 4 cm) centred at O.

Step 2. Mark any point A on the circle.

Step 3. Without changing the compass radius (= r), place the compass needle at A and draw a small arc cutting the circle at B.

Step 4. Now move compass needle to B; draw a small arc cutting the circle at C.

Step 5. Continue stepping around: from C mark D, from D mark E, from E mark F.

Step 6. After 6 steps you return to A. Six equally spaced points are now marked.

Step 7. Join consecutive points: AB, BC, CD, DE, EF, FA. You have a perfect regular hexagon inscribed in the circle.

Step 8. For the flower: instead of joining the points, with the compass still open to r, draw a full arc from each of the 6 points (with centre at the point, sweeping through the inside of the circle).

Step 9. The six arcs overlap inside the circle in a beautiful six-petal pattern. This is the classical “Rosette” or “Flower of Life” pattern.

Why it works: the side of a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle is exactly equal to the radius of the circle (this can be proved by drawing the six equilateral triangles formed by O and the six vertices).

Project 4 — Constructing a 45° angle, 30° angle, and 15° angle

45° angle:

  • First construct a 90° angle (see Project 1, Steps 3 to 6).
  • Then bisect the 90° angle using the angle bisector method (Project 2).
  • The bisector creates two 45° angles.

30° angle:

  • First construct a 60° angle (use the compass-radius technique).
  • Bisect the 60° angle to get a 30° angle.

15° angle:

  • Bisect a 30° angle to get 15°.
  • This works because every bisection halves the angle.

Other angles obtainable by compass-and-ruler alone: 90°, 60°, 45°, 30°, 22.5°, 15°, 7.5° … (any angle obtainable by repeated bisection of 90° or 60°).

Famous fact: not every angle can be trisected (divided into 3 equal parts) using only a compass and ruler. For example, a 60° angle cannot be trisected into three 20° angles by these tools alone — this was proved by mathematicians over 2000 years after the question was first asked!

Project 5 — Constructing a triangle when three sides are given (SSS)

Given: three side lengths a, b, c (with the triangle inequality a + b > c, etc., satisfied).

Example: construct a triangle with sides 5 cm, 6 cm, 7 cm.

Step 1. Draw a line and mark a segment BC = 7 cm on it.

Step 2. Open compass to 5 cm. Place needle at B and draw an arc above BC.

Step 3. Open compass to 6 cm. Place needle at C and draw another arc above BC.

Step 4. The two arcs intersect at A.

Step 5. Join AB and AC. Triangle ABC is the required triangle with sides 5, 6, 7.

Important: the triangle inequality must hold — any one side must be less than the sum of the other two. If you try sides 2 cm, 3 cm, 7 cm, the two arcs from B and C will never meet (because 2 + 3 < 7).

Project 6 — Drawing concentric circles with prescribed widths (target board)

Aim: construct a target board (like a dart board) with four concentric rings of widths 1 cm each, surrounding a central red bullseye of radius 1 cm.

Step 1. Mark a centre O on paper.

Step 2. Draw a circle of radius 1 cm centred at O (the bullseye).

Step 3. Without moving the needle, open the compass to 2 cm and draw the next circle.

Step 4. Open to 3 cm and draw the next.

Step 5. Open to 4 cm and draw the next.

Step 6. Open to 5 cm for the outermost ring.

Step 7. Colour the rings alternately (bullseye red, then yellow, blue, black, white).

The final figure is a target board with 5 zones, each 1 cm wide.

Construction puzzles for the curious learner

Puzzle 1. Use compass and ruler to divide a given segment into 3 equal parts. (Hint: draw an auxiliary line, step off 3 equal small segments, then use parallel projection.)

Puzzle 2. Given a circle, find its centre using only compass and ruler. (Hint: draw two chords; the perpendicular bisectors of the chords meet at the centre.)

Puzzle 3. Construct a square with the same area as a given rectangle. (Hint: this requires geometric mean — a beautiful classical problem.)

Puzzle 4. Draw a triangle with two of its sides 5 cm and 4 cm and the angle between them 60°. (Use the 60° compass construction.)

Puzzle 5. Construct a regular octagon by inscribing it in a square. (Hint: draw a square, draw its diagonals, then draw arcs from each vertex of radius equal to half the diagonal.)

Puzzle 6. Bisect a given line segment without folding the paper. (Use the perpendicular bisector construction.)

Geometry vocabulary — a quick reference

These words appear throughout this chapter. Knowing them well helps a lot.

Point: an exact location with no size.

Line: straight, extends in both directions, infinite length.

Line segment: straight, has two endpoints, finite length.

Ray: straight, has one endpoint, extends in one direction.

Angle: formed by two rays meeting at a common endpoint (the vertex).

Perpendicular: two lines meeting at 90°.

Parallel: two lines that never meet, always the same distance apart.

Bisector: a line that divides something (segment or angle) into two equal halves.

Vertex: the common endpoint of the two arms of an angle (or the corner of a shape).

Arc: a part of a circle's edge.

Chord: a straight segment joining two points on a circle.

Diameter: the longest chord of a circle, passing through the centre.

Radius: a segment from the centre of a circle to any point on it; half the diameter.

Circumference: the boundary (perimeter) of a circle.

Concentric circles: circles with the same centre but different radii.

Compass: the geometry tool used to draw arcs and circles.

Protractor: the semicircular tool used to measure or draw angles.

Set-square: a triangular plastic tool used to draw perpendicular and standard-angle lines quickly.

Why construction matters — connections to the real world

Geometric constructions are not just classroom exercises. They are the basis of engineering drawings, architectural blueprints, machine design, and even animation.

Architecture. Before computers, every floor plan, building elevation, and structural drawing was made with compass, ruler, and set-square. Even today, learning hand-drafting trains the architect's eye.

Engineering. A mechanical part's drawing must be precise to the millimetre. Compass and ruler constructions form the foundation of technical drawing courses.

Carpentry and tailoring. A carpenter who cuts wooden squares for furniture or a tailor who marks fabric for shirts both use construction principles.

Art and design. Symmetrical mandalas, mosaics, stained glass windows, Islamic geometric patterns — all are constructed from circles, lines and arcs.

Logo design. Many famous logos (Apple, Pepsi, Audi) use geometric constructions based on circles intersecting in precise ways.

Mathematics history. Greek mathematicians like Euclid, Archimedes and Apollonius developed systematic geometric constructions over 2000 years ago. Their methods are still taught today!

Common errors in geometric constructions

Even a good student can lose marks because of small construction errors. Here are the common pitfalls:

Error 1. Loose compass. If the compass joint is loose, the radius can change mid-construction. Fix: tighten the joint and avoid pressing too hard.

Error 2. Blunt pencil. A thick line spoils precision. Fix: use a freshly sharpened pencil for all constructions.

Error 3. Not marking arcs clearly. Arcs should be clean and crisp. Fix: draw arcs slightly longer than needed so the intersection is unmistakable.

Error 4. Ruler slipping. Fix: hold the ruler firmly with one hand while drawing with the other.

Error 5. Wrong protractor reading. Many protractors have two scales (inner and outer). Fix: always use the scale that starts from 0° at the arm.

Error 6. Inaccurate intersection of arcs. Fix: draw the second arc with the same radius as the first, and aim for a precise crossing.

Error 7. Not labelling clearly. Fix: always label points (A, B, C, …), angles (∠ABC) and lengths in your construction.

Error 8. Erasing too much. Construction arcs are evidence of correct method — do not erase them.

🎯 Chapter 8 — The big picture

  • Compass + ruler = the classical “Euclidean” tools of construction.
  • Special angles obtainable: 90°, 60°, 45°, 30°, 15°.
  • Special shapes obtainable: equilateral triangle, square, regular hexagon, perpendicular bisector, angle bisector.
  • The side of a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle equals the radius of the circle.
  • Geometric constructions train precision — a life skill far beyond mathematics.

Step-by-step recap: 10 essential constructions

Here is a checklist of the constructions every Class 6 student should master.

1. Draw a segment of given length (ruler).

2. Copy a given segment to a new line (compass).

3. Draw a circle of given radius (compass).

4. Draw concentric circles with the same centre (compass without moving).

5. Construct the perpendicular bisector of a segment (compass + ruler).

6. Construct an angle bisector (compass + ruler).

7. Construct a 60° angle (single radius arc twice).

8. Construct a 90° angle (semicircle + two-arc method).

9. Construct an equilateral triangle (intersection of two arcs).

10. Construct a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle (radius-step method).

If you can do all ten of these confidently, you have a solid foundation for the constructions you will meet in Classes 7, 8 and 9 (constructing triangles by SSS, SAS, ASA; constructing parallels; dividing a segment into n equal parts; and many more).

Famous theorems behind the constructions

Behind every construction lies a beautiful theorem. Here are some that you have used already (often without realising!).

Theorem 1. The perpendicular bisector of a segment is the set of all points equidistant from its two endpoints.

This is why two arcs of equal radius from the two endpoints, intersecting above and below, mark exactly the perpendicular bisector line.

Theorem 2. If two arcs of equal radius from two endpoints of a segment intersect, the segment from the vertex to the intersection bisects the angle.

This justifies the angle-bisector construction.

Theorem 3. In a circle, a regular hexagon's side equals the radius.

This is why we can step around the circle with the compass at its current radius and get 6 equally spaced points.

Theorem 4. A triangle is uniquely determined (up to congruence) by its three sides (SSS), provided the triangle inequality is satisfied.

This justifies the SSS construction.

Theorem 5. The diagonals of a rectangle bisect each other and are equal in length.

This is why a rectangle's diagonals always meet at its centre.

💡 Final pro tips for Chapter 8

  • Always use a sharp pencil and a tight compass.
  • Mark each constructed point with a clear letter (A, B, C, …).
  • Do not erase construction arcs — they show your method.
  • Practise the perpendicular bisector and angle bisector constructions until they are automatic.
  • Verify each construction by measuring with ruler or protractor.
  • Geometry is best learned by doing — construct daily for fluency.
  • Draw the figure to scale on rough paper before the final neat construction.
  • Keep a separate page for tools and basic constructions for quick revision.
  • Geometric construction is a beautiful blend of art and logic — enjoy it!
  • Always justify each step of a construction with the reason (e.g. “both arcs of the same radius”).
  • Construction questions in exams usually carry good marks — never skip them.
  • Remember: precise construction is a skill that improves with practice.
NCERT Deep Dive — Chapter 8Following the NCERT chapter “Playing with Constructions”

A guided tour of NCERT "Playing with Constructions"

The NCERT chapter celebrates the joy of geometric construction. It uses only two tools — the ruler (unmarked, used only for straight edges) and the compass (a fixed-arm tool to draw circles and arcs). The narrative flows through:

  1. The fascinating world of straight lines, curves and circles.
  2. Constructing line segments of given length.
  3. Constructing circles of any radius.
  4. Constructing perpendiculars and perpendicular bisectors.
  5. Constructing angles — copying, bisecting, drawing standard angles.
  6. Constructing triangles when three pieces of information are given.
  7. Constructing rectangles, squares and regular polygons.
  8. Famous classical construction puzzles — squaring the circle, trisecting an angle.

By the end you should be a confident user of ruler and compass, able to produce neat, justified constructions.

The Euclidean toolbox — only two tools allowed

Classical construction (since 300 BCE) uses just two tools:

  • Straight edge. A ruler used only to draw straight lines; markings are ignored.
  • Compass. Used to draw circles or arcs of any radius. Modern compasses can transport a radius from one place to another (“collapsible vs rigid” debate — both give the same constructible set).

Why such restrictive tools? Because these are the simplest tools, and they correspond to the simplest mathematical operations: drawing the line through two points and drawing the circle through a point with given centre.

Surprisingly, with just these two tools you can perform a vast range of constructions — bisect any segment or angle, copy any angle, build any regular polygon with 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 16, 17, 20 sides (and many more).

The perpendicular bisector — constructed step-by-step

Given a line segment AB, construct its perpendicular bisector.

  1. With the compass at A and radius greater than half of AB, draw an arc above and below AB.
  2. Without changing the compass radius, place the compass at B and draw two more arcs above and below AB, intersecting the first two arcs at two points, P and Q.
  3. Use the ruler to draw the straight line through P and Q.
  4. This line is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

Why does this work? The points P and Q are each equidistant from A and B (both arcs had the same radius). So P and Q both lie on the locus of points equidistant from A and B, which is exactly the perpendicular bisector.

The midpoint M of AB is where the bisector PQ meets AB.

Application. To find the centre of a circle: choose any chord, construct its perpendicular bisector. Repeat with a second chord. The two bisectors meet at the centre. (This is one of the most important constructions in geometry!)

The angle bisector — constructed step-by-step

Given an angle ∠BAC, construct its bisector.

  1. With the compass at A, draw an arc cutting both arms of the angle at points D (on AB) and E (on AC).
  2. With the compass at D, draw an arc inside the angle.
  3. With the same compass radius at E, draw another arc inside the angle, intersecting the previous arc at F.
  4. Draw the ray from A through F.
  5. Ray AF bisects ∠BAC.

Why does this work? AD = AE (same radius from A). DF = EF (same radius from D and E). AF is common. So triangles ADF and AEF are congruent (SSS), hence ∠DAF = ∠EAF.

Special angles by repeated bisection.

  • Construct 90° via perpendicular.
  • Bisect 90° → 45°.
  • Bisect 45° → 22.5°.
  • Construct 60° via equilateral triangle.
  • Bisect 60° → 30°.
  • Bisect 30° → 15°.
  • 60° + 30° = 90° (composition).
  • 30° + 45° = 75°.
  • 30° + 60° = 90°.
  • 45° + 45° + 15° = 105°.

Constructing a 60-degree angle and an equilateral triangle

Construction of a 60° angle is one of the most beautiful and simplest constructions.

Steps for 60° at point A on line AB:

  1. Draw line AB.
  2. With compass at A and any radius r, draw an arc cutting AB at P.
  3. Without changing the radius, place compass at P and draw an arc cutting the first arc at Q.
  4. Draw ray AQ. The angle ∠BAQ is exactly 60°.

Why does this work? Triangle APQ has all three sides equal to r (AP, AQ, PQ are all radii of arcs of radius r). It is equilateral, so all angles are 60°.

Construction of an equilateral triangle of side 5 cm:

  1. Draw AB = 5 cm.
  2. With compass at A, radius 5 cm, draw an arc above AB.
  3. With compass at B, radius 5 cm, draw an arc cutting the first at C.
  4. Join AC and BC. ABC is equilateral.

Triangle constructions — the three classical cases

A triangle is determined by three independent pieces of information. NCERT covers three cases.

Case 1 (SSS). Three sides given. Construct ▵ABC where AB = 5 cm, BC = 6 cm, CA = 4 cm.

  1. Draw AB = 5 cm.
  2. With compass at A, radius 4 cm, draw an arc.
  3. With compass at B, radius 6 cm, draw an arc cutting the first at C.
  4. Join AC and BC.

Case 2 (SAS). Two sides and the included angle. Construct ▵ABC where AB = 5 cm, ∠A = 60°, AC = 4 cm.

  1. Draw AB = 5 cm.
  2. At A, construct a 60° angle (as shown earlier).
  3. Along the 60° ray, mark AC = 4 cm.
  4. Join CB.

Case 3 (ASA). One side and two angles. Construct ▵ABC where AB = 6 cm, ∠A = 60°, ∠B = 45°.

  1. Draw AB = 6 cm.
  2. At A, draw a 60° angle.
  3. At B, draw a 45° angle.
  4. The two new rays meet at C.

(The fourth case AAS is essentially the same as ASA after finding the third angle from angle sum = 180°.)

Rectangle and square constructions

Square of side 5 cm:

  1. Draw AB = 5 cm.
  2. At A, construct a perpendicular to AB.
  3. Along the perpendicular, mark AD = 5 cm.
  4. At B, construct a perpendicular to AB.
  5. Along this perpendicular, mark BC = 5 cm.
  6. Join CD. ABCD is a square.

Rectangle 6 cm by 4 cm:

  1. Draw AB = 6 cm.
  2. At A and B, construct perpendiculars.
  3. Mark AD = BC = 4 cm.
  4. Join CD.

Verify each construction: the diagonals of a rectangle should be equal (measure AC and BD).

Three famous impossible constructions

For 2300 years, mathematicians struggled with three classical construction problems. All three were proven impossible in the 19th century!

1. Squaring the circle. Construct a square whose area equals that of a given circle. Impossible because π is transcendental (Lindemann, 1882).

2. Trisecting a general angle. Cut an arbitrary angle into three equal parts using only ruler and compass. Impossible in general (Wantzel, 1837). Specific angles like 90° can be trisected (90/3 = 30 is constructible), but 60° cannot (20° is not constructible).

3. Doubling the cube. Construct a cube with exactly twice the volume of a given cube. Impossible because &cbrt;2 is not constructible (Wantzel, 1837).

These results required the deep mathematics of field theory, but the questions are simple enough for a Class 6 student to appreciate. They show that even simple geometric questions can hide profound truths.

The Indian construction tradition

India has a long tradition of geometric construction, especially in religious and architectural contexts.

  • Sulba Sutras (800–500 BCE). Detailed instructions for building Vedic fire altars (yajna-shalas) of specific shapes — falcon-shaped, wheel-shaped, tortoise-shaped — using only ropes (cords) for measurement.
  • Baudhayana's theorem (800 BCE). States the Pythagorean theorem in geometric form, used to construct right angles for altars.
  • Indus Valley (2500 BCE). Precision-cut bricks and town planning indicate sophisticated geometric construction.
  • Temple architecture. Indian temples (Khajuraho, Konark, Madurai) employ complex geometric patterns based on grid constructions.
  • Rangoli/Kolam art. Daily geometric drawings made by women using rice flour, often based on grids and reflective patterns.

📝 NCERT-style review questions — Chapter 8

Q1. What two tools are allowed in classical construction?

Q2. Describe in your own words how to construct a perpendicular bisector of a segment.

Q3. Construct (on paper) an angle of 60°. List the steps.

Q4. How can you produce a 30° angle using only ruler and compass?

Q5. Construct ▵ABC with AB = 4 cm, BC = 5 cm, AC = 6 cm (SSS).

Q6. Why is the diagonal of a constructed square equal in length to the other diagonal?

Q7. Name one classical construction that is provably impossible.

Q8. Which ancient Indian text gives step-by-step instructions for building altars?

Answers:
  1. Straight edge (unmarked ruler) and compass.
  2. Place compass at A and B successively with the same radius (> AB/2) and draw two pairs of arcs that meet above and below AB. The line through the intersections is the perpendicular bisector.
  3. Steps: draw a line AB; place compass at A, draw arc cutting AB at P; same radius at P, draw arc cutting first arc at Q; join AQ. ∠BAQ = 60°.
  4. Construct 60° first, then bisect it.
  5. Standard SSS construction (see Chapter 8 text).
  6. Because triangles ABD and BAC are congruent (SAS).
  7. Squaring the circle / Trisecting a general angle / Doubling the cube.
  8. Sulba Sutras.

📌 Chapter 8 — Constructions skill check

  • I can use only ruler and compass for classical constructions.
  • I can construct the perpendicular bisector of any segment.
  • I can bisect any angle using ruler and compass.
  • I can construct 60°, 30°, 45°, 90°, 75°, 105° and all multiples of 15° up to 180°.
  • I can construct any triangle from SSS, SAS or ASA data.
  • I can construct a rectangle and a square of given dimensions.
  • I know three classical impossible constructions.
  • I appreciate the rich Indian tradition of geometric construction (Sulba Sutras).
Mastery Practice Pack — Chapter 8Construction drills with step-by-step protocols

Drill 1 — Line segments & perpendicular bisectors

D1. Construct a line segment of length 6.4 cm. Verify with the ruler.

D2. Construct the perpendicular bisector of a 7 cm segment. Mark the midpoint and verify both halves are 3.5 cm.

D3. Construct a perpendicular to a given line at a marked point on it.

Steps for D3. Place compass at the marked point P on line l; draw arcs cutting l at A and B (equal distances either side). With compass at A and B and equal radius (> AP), draw two arcs above l meeting at Q. Join PQ — this is perpendicular to l.

D4. Construct a perpendicular to a given line from a point NOT on it.

Steps for D4. From external point P, draw arcs cutting line l at A and B. Bisect AB to get midpoint M. Join PM — this is perpendicular to l.

D5. Construct the perpendicular bisector of a chord in a circle. Where does it pass?

Answer. Through the centre of the circle (key fact!).

Drill 2 — Angles & bisection

D6. Construct an angle of 60°.

D7. Bisect a 60° angle to get 30°.

D8. Bisect a 90° angle to get 45°.

D9. Construct 75° (as 60° + 15°).

Steps for D9. Construct 60°. Bisect 60° to get 30°. Bisect 30° to get 15°. Add 60° + 15° = 75°.

D10. Construct 105° (as 90° + 15°).

D11. Construct an angle of 120° (as 60° + 60° or equivalently 180° − 60°).

Drill 3 — Triangles by SSS, SAS, ASA

D12 (SSS). Construct ▵ABC with sides AB = 5 cm, BC = 6 cm, CA = 7 cm.

D13 (SAS). Construct ▵PQR with PQ = 4 cm, ∠Q = 60°, QR = 5 cm.

D14 (ASA). Construct ▵XYZ with XY = 6 cm, ∠X = 45°, ∠Y = 60°.

D15 (Right triangle). Construct a right triangle with legs 3 cm and 4 cm. Measure the hypotenuse.

Expected. 5 cm (Pythagorean triple).

D16 (Equilateral). Construct an equilateral triangle of side 5 cm.

D17 (Isosceles). Construct an isosceles triangle with base 6 cm and equal sides 5 cm.

Drill 4 — Quadrilaterals & regular polygons

D18. Construct a square of side 5.5 cm.

D19. Construct a rectangle 7 cm by 4 cm. Measure the diagonals (should be equal).

D20. Construct a regular hexagon of side 4 cm inside a circle of radius 4 cm.

Steps for D20. Draw the circle. With the same radius, mark off 6 successive arcs around the circumference. Join the 6 marked points to form a regular hexagon.

D21. Construct a regular octagon by first inscribing a square in a circle, then bisecting each side's arc.

Construction protocols — quick reference

ConstructionTools neededKey idea
Segment of length LRuler, compassMark off length with compass on a line.
Copy a segmentCompassSet radius to the segment, transfer.
Copy an angleCompassUse two arcs of equal radius from the vertex.
Perpendicular bisectorCompassTwo arcs from each endpoint meeting above and below.
Angle bisectorCompassArc from vertex, then equal arcs from the two intersections.
Perpendicular at point on lineCompassTwo arcs from points equidistant from given point.
Perpendicular from external pointCompassBisect a chord through the external point.
60° angleCompassEquilateral triangle of any side.
30° angleCompassBisect a 60° angle.
45° angleCompassBisect a 90° angle.
SSS triangleCompassMark base; arc from each end with respective side; intersect.
SAS triangleCompassMark base; draw angle; mark second side; join.
ASA triangleCompassMark base; draw angles at both ends; rays meet at third vertex.
Square / rectangleCompassCombine perpendiculars + mark dimensions.
Regular hexagonCompassMark off side length 6 times around a circle.

Chapter 8 glossary

  • Compass. Two-armed tool to draw circles and arcs.
  • Straight edge. Unmarked ruler used only for drawing lines.
  • Perpendicular bisector. Line cutting a segment into two equal parts at right angles.
  • Angle bisector. Line that divides an angle into two equal halves.
  • Arc. Part of a circle's circumference.
  • Chord. A segment with both endpoints on a circle.
  • Diameter. A chord that passes through the centre.
  • Radius. A segment from the centre to any point on the circle.
  • Inscribed polygon. A polygon with all vertices on a circle.
  • Construction. Drawing a geometric figure with prescribed tools.
  • SSS / SAS / ASA. Triangle congruence conditions used in construction.

Exam-prep question bank — Chapter 8 (25 questions)

  1. Name the two tools used in classical geometric construction.
  2. Why must the ruler be unmarked in classical construction?
  3. State the three classical impossible constructions.
  4. Describe the steps to construct a perpendicular bisector of segment AB.
  5. Describe the steps to bisect an angle.
  6. How is a 60° angle constructed?
  7. How is a 30° angle constructed?
  8. How is a 45° angle constructed?
  9. What is the angle between two perpendicular lines?
  10. If two arcs of equal radius are drawn from the ends of a segment, what does the line through their intersections give?
  11. State the SSS construction condition for a triangle.
  12. State the SAS construction condition.
  13. State the ASA construction condition.
  14. Construct (describe) an equilateral triangle of side 6 cm.
  15. Construct (describe) a square of side 4 cm.
  16. Construct (describe) a rectangle of 6 cm by 4 cm.
  17. How many sides does a regular hexagon have?
  18. How is a regular hexagon constructed inside a circle of radius r?
  19. Why does the “side equals radius” trick give a regular hexagon?
  20. What is the perpendicular from a point on a line called?
  21. What ancient Indian text gives instructions for altar construction?
  22. Name an Indian mathematician who used geometric construction.
  23. What does it mean to inscribe a polygon in a circle?
  24. What does it mean to circumscribe a circle around a polygon?
  25. Which is more accurate — a freehand drawing or a compass construction? Why?
Answer key (click to expand)
  1. Straight edge (unmarked ruler) and compass.
  2. To restrict construction to pure geometric operations — lines and circles only.
  3. Squaring the circle, trisecting a general angle, doubling the cube.
  4. From each endpoint draw arcs of equal radius (> half AB) above and below; join the two intersections.
  5. From the vertex draw an arc cutting both rays; from those two cuts draw equal-radius arcs meeting inside; join vertex to meeting point.
  6. Draw a line, mark a point, draw an arc, transfer the radius along the arc to get the second arc's intersection, join.
  7. Construct 60°, then bisect it.
  8. Construct 90°, then bisect it.
  9. 90°.
  10. The perpendicular bisector of the segment.
  11. Three sides given.
  12. Two sides and the included angle.
  13. One side and two angles (the angles at the ends of that side).
  14. Draw AB = 6 cm; from each endpoint draw an arc of radius 6 cm meeting at C; join AC and BC.
  15. Draw AB = 4 cm; construct perpendiculars at A and B; mark AD = BC = 4 cm; join CD.
  16. Draw AB = 6 cm; construct perpendiculars at A and B; mark AD = BC = 4 cm; join CD.
  17. Six.
  18. Mark off side r on the circle; six chords of length r fit exactly.
  19. Because adjacent radii and a chord of length r form an equilateral triangle — six such triangles tile the circle.
  20. Perpendicular through a point on the line.
  21. Sulba Sutras.
  22. Brahmagupta, Bhaskara II.
  23. To draw it with all vertices on the circle.
  24. To draw a circle touching all sides of the polygon (or passing through all vertices).
  25. Compass construction — uses exact geometric properties, no eye-balling.

Common construction errors

  • Compass slipping during arc — redraw with firmer grip.
  • Pencil tip too blunt — sharpen for thin precise lines.
  • Marking points without labels (A, B, C, …) — always label.
  • Erasing the construction arcs — they show your work and should remain.
  • Using the ruler's scale instead of compass for measurement — classical rules disallow this.
  • Drawing arcs that are too small to clearly intersect.
  • Choosing radius equal to or less than half the segment in perpendicular bisector — must be greater.
  • Forgetting that “equal arcs” means the same compass setting.
  • Confusing perpendicular bisector with angle bisector.
  • Forgetting to state what you have constructed at the end.
  • Ignoring uniqueness — a construction must produce a single, specific figure.
  • Skipping intermediate steps — show every arc and every line.

Construction in modern technology

  • Architecture. Modern CAD software performs digital geometric construction.
  • Engineering drawings. Precise circles, perpendiculars and bisectors form the basis of all blueprints.
  • Computer graphics. Rendering uses geometric construction algorithms.
  • Robotics. Path planning uses bisectors and perpendiculars.
  • GPS / navigation. Triangulation uses geometric constructions.
  • Surveying. Field surveyors use compass and chain — modern versions of classical tools.
  • Printing and design. Logos and typography use geometric constructions.
  • Origami. Paper folding is governed by the same construction principles.
  • Cake decoration. Geometric patterns on cakes use ruler-and-compass-style designs.
  • Tile design. Patterns on bathroom and kitchen tiles use symmetric constructions.
  • Textile design. Block prints, embroidery and weaving use geometric patterns.
  • Jewellery design. Precision in cutting gemstones requires geometric construction.

Extended chapter wrap-up — Chapter 8

Geometric construction with ruler and compass is one of the oldest mathematical disciplines. From the Vedic altar-builders of India (c. 800 BCE) who used cords to construct precise right angles, to Euclid of Alexandria (c. 300 BCE) who codified the rules of classical construction, to the medieval Arab and Indian mathematicians who extended the field, geometric construction has been refined for over three thousand years.

The beauty of classical construction lies in its restraint: only two tools, the unmarked straight edge and the compass. With these you can construct an astonishing variety of figures — perpendiculars, bisectors, angles, triangles, regular polygons of 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 16, 17, 20 sides, and many more. The German mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauss famously showed at age 19 that a regular 17-gon is constructible — a discovery so important to him that he asked it be carved on his tombstone.

What did we learn? First, the basic techniques: drawing a segment of given length, constructing a perpendicular bisector, bisecting an angle, constructing standard angles (60°, 30°, 45°, 90° and all multiples of 15°). Second, the three triangle constructions (SSS, SAS, ASA). Third, constructions of quadrilaterals (square, rectangle) and regular polygons.

We also learned three classical impossible constructions: squaring the circle, trisecting a general angle, and doubling the cube. For 2300 years mathematicians tried to solve these. In the 19th century, French mathematician Pierre Wantzel and German mathematician Ferdinand von Lindemann proved them impossible using deep tools from algebra. These impossibility results are themselves among the great achievements of mathematics.

India's contribution is deep: the Sulba Sutras (800–500 BCE) contain detailed instructions for building altars of specific shapes using only ropes, and include the earliest known statement of the Pythagorean theorem. Indian temples, rangolis, kolams and mandalas all reflect a deep mastery of geometric construction.

Looking ahead, in Class 7 you will construct more complex figures including quadrilaterals from given conditions, and learn to construct angles like 105°, 75°, and beyond. In Class 9 and 10 you will meet the formal theory of geometric construction and the connection to algebra. Modern computer-aided design (CAD) software is the digital descendant of classical construction. The journey starts here, with the simple act of bisecting a line segment.

Suggested further activities

Activity 1. Construct equilateral triangles of sides 3 cm, 5 cm and 7 cm. Compare their perimeters.

Activity 2. Construct angles of 60°, 30°, 15°, 75°, 90°, 45° on a single sheet.

Activity 3. Bisect three different angles with ruler and compass.

Activity 4. Construct a regular hexagon of side 4 cm.

Activity 5. Construct an inscribed regular hexagon in a circle of radius 5 cm.

Activity 6. Construct a right triangle with legs 3 cm and 4 cm. Measure the hypotenuse.

Activity 7. Construct ▵ABC with AB = 6 cm, BC = 8 cm, CA = 10 cm. (Hint: this is a right triangle!)

Activity 8. Make a rangoli of your own with a 60° rotational symmetry.

Activity 9. Construct a square of side 5 cm and measure its diagonals.

Activity 10. Research Indian construction in Sulba Sutras; draw one altar design.

Quick-recap cheat sheet

Classical tools: unmarked ruler (straight edge) + compass.

Perpendicular bisector: equal arcs from each endpoint meeting above and below.

Angle bisector: arc from vertex, then equal arcs from the two cuts.

60° angle: equilateral-triangle technique with two arcs of the same radius.

30°: bisect 60°.

45°: bisect 90°.

SSS: three sides given.

SAS: two sides + included angle.

ASA: one side + two end angles.

Square: draw base, construct perpendiculars, mark equal heights.

Hexagon: mark off the radius six times along the circumference.

Impossible constructions: squaring the circle, trisecting a general angle, doubling the cube.

📋 Chapter 9 — What's Inside

📖 Topics Covered

  • 9.1 Line of Symmetry
  • 9.2 Rotational Symmetry

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 9.1 — Finding Lines of Symmetry
  • Section 9.2 — Completing Symmetric Figures
  • Section 9.3 — Rotational Symmetry

Chapter 9: Symmetry

🎯 Why symmetry matters

Symmetry is the mathematical idea behind beauty and balance. A butterfly’s wings, your own face, the Taj Mahal, a snowflake, the Indian flag, a flower — all are balanced because they have symmetry. Symmetry helps artists make pleasing designs, architects build stable buildings, scientists understand crystals and molecules, and engineers create machines that work smoothly. In this chapter we will learn two main kinds of symmetry: line (reflection) symmetry and rotational symmetry, and how to spot them in shapes, letters and the world around us.

In-Depth Study — Chapter 9Each subtopic explained with diagrams, examples and real-life applications

§9.1 — Line of Symmetry (Reflection Symmetry) (In Depth)

A figure has line symmetry (also called reflection or mirror symmetry) if there is at least one line you can draw on it that splits the figure into two halves which are exact mirror images of each other. That line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry.

The easiest way to test for line symmetry is to fold the paper along the chosen line. If both halves coincide exactly, the figure has line symmetry along that fold.

Lines of symmetry in common shapes

ShapeNumber of lines of symmetry
Equilateral triangle3
Isosceles triangle1
Scalene triangle0
Square4
Rectangle2
Rhombus2
Regular pentagon5
Regular hexagon6
Regular n-gonn
Circleinfinite (any diameter)

Lines of symmetry in alphabets

  • Vertical line of symmetry: A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y
  • Horizontal line of symmetry: B, C, D, E, H, I, K, O, X
  • Both: H, I, O, X
  • No symmetry: F, G, J, L, N, P, Q, R, S, Z

Symmetry in nature and design

  • The two halves of a butterfly’s wings.
  • The two sides of a human face (almost).
  • The leaves of many plants (banyan, neem).
  • The petals of a flower.
  • The Taj Mahal’s front view.
  • The Indian flag (vertical line of symmetry).

§9.2 — Rotational Symmetry (In Depth)

A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated about a central point by some angle less than a full turn. The number of times this happens in one full turn is called the order of rotational symmetry.

ShapeOrderAngle of rotation
Equilateral triangle3120°
Square490°
Regular pentagon572°
Regular hexagon660°
Regular octagon845°
Circleinfiniteany angle
Rectangle (not square)2180°
Parallelogram (non-rectangular)2180°

Formula

Angle of rotation = 360° / order

Real-life rotational symmetry:

  • A car’s steering wheel.
  • The blades of a ceiling fan.
  • A bicycle wheel.
  • The Ashoka Chakra (24-fold rotational symmetry).
  • Flowers with many regular petals.

Beyond NCERT — Symmetry Around Us (Extension Notes)

Once you start looking, symmetry is everywhere — in art, architecture, nature, machines and even in your own body!

Symmetry in art and architecture

  • Mandala designs in Indian and Tibetan traditions are full of rotational symmetry.
  • The Taj Mahal’s front facade has a perfect vertical line of symmetry.
  • Rangoli patterns drawn during festivals often have 4-fold or 8-fold symmetry.
  • Mosques and temples often have domes with both line and rotational symmetry.

Symmetry in nature

  • Snowflakes: 6-fold rotational symmetry.
  • Honeycombs: hexagonal symmetry.
  • Starfish: 5-fold rotational symmetry.
  • Most flowers: 5-, 6- or 8-fold rotational symmetry.
  • Animals: bilateral (line) symmetry — left and right halves match.

Symmetry in machines

Rotating parts (wheels, gears, propellers, fans) must have rotational symmetry, otherwise they would wobble dangerously. The strong symmetry helps them spin smoothly.

Activity: paper-fold symmetry art

  1. Take a square sheet of paper.
  2. Fold it in half — you get two halves with a vertical line of symmetry.
  3. Fold it again at right angles — you get 4-fold symmetry.
  4. Cut small shapes from the edges of the folded packet.
  5. Unfold — you have a symmetric snowflake-like design!

🎯 Big picture of Chapter 9

  • Line symmetry: a figure folds onto itself along the symmetry line.
  • Rotational symmetry: a figure matches itself after a rotation less than 360°.
  • The order of rotational symmetry tells how many times the shape coincides with itself in one full turn.
  • Both kinds appear together in highly symmetric shapes (square, regular hexagon, circle).
  • Symmetry surrounds us in nature, art, architecture and machines.

⚠️ Common mistakes

  • Confusing a rectangle’s diagonals as lines of symmetry — they are not, unless the rectangle is a square.
  • Saying a parallelogram has line symmetry — in general it does not (only rotational symmetry of order 2).
  • Counting 360° rotation as part of rotational symmetry order — every shape has 360° rotation, so we don’t count it.
Quick Reference — Chapter 9The original briefer summary follows

§9.1 — Line of Symmetry (Reflection/Mirror Symmetry)

A line of symmetry divides a figure into two halves that are mirror images of each other.

If you fold along the line, both halves match exactly!

§9.2 — Rotational Symmetry

A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being rotated (less than a full turn) about its centre.

Order of rotational symmetry = number of times it matches itself in one full turn.

  • Square: order 4 (matches at 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°)
  • Equilateral triangle: order 3 (at 120°, 240°, 360°)
  • Regular hexagon: order 6
  • Circle: order infinite
Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved

📝 Quick Recap

  • Line of symmetry: fold line that makes both halves match
  • Regular polygon with n sides → n lines of symmetry, rotational order n
  • Circle: infinite lines of symmetry, infinite rotational order
  • Alphabet symmetry: A, H, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y have vertical line; B, C, D, E, K have horizontal line; H, I, O, X have both

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Rectangle ≠ Square: Rectangle has 2 lines (not 4!) — diagonals are NOT lines of symmetry for rectangles
  • Parallelogram: has NO line of symmetry (but has rotational symmetry of order 2)
  • Isosceles triangle: has exactly 1 line of symmetry (not 3!)

Figure it Out — Exercises

Q1. Find the number of lines of symmetry for each shape: square, rectangle, equilateral triangle, isosceles triangle, circle, regular pentagon.

Solution:

  • Square: 4 lines (2 through midpoints of sides + 2 diagonals)
  • Rectangle: 2 lines (through midpoints of opposite sides only)
  • Equilateral triangle: 3 lines (each from vertex to midpoint of opposite side)
  • Isosceles triangle: 1 line (through the unequal vertex to base midpoint)
  • Circle: Infinite lines (any diameter is a line of symmetry)
  • Regular pentagon: 5 lines

Q2. Which English letters have a line of symmetry?

Solution:

Vertical line of symmetry: A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y

Horizontal line of symmetry: B, C, D, E, H, I, K, O, X

Both: H, I, O, X

No line of symmetry: F, G, J, L, N, P, Q, R, S, Z

Q3. Complete the figure so it has the given line as a line of symmetry.

Solution (Method):

For each point on one side of the line:

  1. Measure the perpendicular distance from the point to the mirror line
  2. Mark a point at the SAME distance on the OTHER side
  3. Connect the new points in the same order

💡 Think: if you fold along the line, each point must land exactly on its mirror point!

Q. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a regular hexagon?

Solution:

Order 6. It looks the same every 60° of rotation (360° ÷ 6 = 60°).

Matches at: 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°, 360° — that's 6 times in one full turn.

Figure it Out — Section 9.1 (Page 219 — Lines of Symmetry)

Q1. Do you see any line of symmetry in the figures at the start of the chapter (flower, rangoli, butterfly, pinwheel, cloud)?

Solution:

  • Flower: 6 lines of symmetry (each petal axis)
  • Rangoli: 4 lines of symmetry (2 through midpoints + 2 diagonals)
  • Butterfly: 1 line of symmetry (vertical, through the body)
  • Pinwheel: No line of symmetry (but has rotational symmetry!)
  • Cloud: No line of symmetry (irregular shape)

Q2. For each of the following figures, identify the line(s) of symmetry if it exists.

Solution:

Method: Try to draw a line such that folding along it makes both halves overlap perfectly.

  • Regular shapes: count = number of sides (triangle=3, square=4, pentagon=5, hexagon=6)
  • Arrow shape: 1 line (along the direction of the arrow)
  • Letter shapes: varies (A=1, H=2, S=0)
  • Irregular shapes: may have 0 lines

Figure it Out — Section 9.1 (Page 221 — Multiple Lines of Symmetry)

Q. Is there any other way to fold the square so that the two halves overlap? How many lines of symmetry does the square shape have?

Solution:

A square has 4 lines of symmetry:

  1. Vertical line (through midpoints of top and bottom sides)
  2. Horizontal line (through midpoints of left and right sides)
  3. Diagonal from top-left to bottom-right
  4. Diagonal from top-right to bottom-left

There is no other way to fold it into matching halves. These 4 are all the lines of symmetry.

Q. We saw that the diagonal of a square is a line of symmetry. Is the diagonal of a rectangle (that is not a square) also a line of symmetry?

Solution:

No! The diagonal of a rectangle is NOT a line of symmetry.

Reason: When you fold a rectangle along its diagonal, the two triangular halves do NOT overlap — one is wider and shorter, the other is taller and narrower.

A rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry: the vertical and horizontal lines through the centre (not the diagonals).

Q. What if we reflect a square along the diagonal from A to C? Where do points A, B, C and D go?

Solution:

When reflecting along diagonal AC:

  • A stays at A (it’s on the line of symmetry)
  • C stays at C (it’s on the line of symmetry)
  • B goes to the position of D
  • D goes to the position of B

When reflecting along the horizontal line: A→D’s position, B→C’s position, and vice versa.

Figure it Out — Section 9.1 (Page 223 — Punching Game)

Q1. In each figure, a hole was punched in a folded square sheet of paper and then unfolded. Identify the line along which the paper was folded. Figure (d) was created by punching a single hole — how was the paper folded?

Solution:

The fold line is the line of symmetry between each pair of holes:

  • (a) Vertical fold (holes are left-right symmetric)
  • (b) Horizontal fold (holes are top-bottom symmetric)
  • (c) Diagonal fold (holes are diagonally placed)
  • (d) Paper was folded twice — first vertically, then horizontally (or vice versa). One punch created 4 symmetric holes!

Q2. Given the line(s) of symmetry, find the other hole(s).

Solution:

Method: For each existing hole, find its mirror image across the given line of symmetry:

  1. Draw a perpendicular from the hole to the line of symmetry
  2. Extend it the same distance on the other side
  3. That’s where the matching hole should be

If there are two lines of symmetry, reflect across both lines — you may get 3 additional holes from one original hole!

Q4. After each of the following cuts on a folded paper, predict the shape of the hole when the paper is opened.

Solution:

Key insight: The cut shape gets reflected across the fold line.

  • A triangular cut on a vertical fold → opens to a diamond/rhombus shape
  • A semicircular cut on the fold → opens to a full circle
  • A rectangular cut at the fold edge → opens to a wider rectangle
  • A cut on a paper folded twice → creates a shape with two lines of symmetry

💡 Make the actual cutouts to verify your predictions!

Q5. How would you get these shapes with some folds and a single straight cut? (a) Square hole in centre (b) Square hole in centre (tilted)

Solution:

(a) Square hole (aligned with sides):

  1. Fold the paper horizontally (in half)
  2. Fold again vertically (now it’s in quarters)
  3. Cut a small triangle at the corner where all folds meet
  4. Open — you get a square hole in the centre!

(b) Square hole (tilted 45°):

  1. Fold horizontally, then vertically
  2. At the closed corner, cut along a slanting line
  3. Open — the hole is a tilted square (diamond shape)

💡 Check that the 4-sided figures satisfy both properties of a square: equal sides AND right angles.

Q6. How many lines of symmetry do these shapes have? (a) A regular shape (b) A triangle with equal sides and equal angles (c) A hexagon with equal sides and equal angles

Solution:

(a) Depends on the shape — count the number of fold lines that give matching halves.

(b) An equilateral triangle (equal sides and equal angles): 3 lines of symmetry (each from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side)

(c) A regular hexagon (equal sides and equal angles): 6 lines of symmetry (3 through opposite vertices + 3 through midpoints of opposite sides)

💡 Pattern: A regular polygon with n sides has exactly n lines of symmetry.

Q7. Trace each figure and draw the lines of symmetry, if any.

Solution:

Method: For each figure, check every possible fold direction:

  • Vertical, horizontal, and diagonal lines
  • For each potential line: would both halves match exactly?

Common results:

  • Scalene triangle: 0 lines
  • Isosceles triangle: 1 line (from the unequal vertex to base midpoint)
  • Parallelogram (not rectangle): 0 lines
  • Kite: 1 line (along the longer diagonal)
  • Arrow: 1 line (along direction of arrow)

Q8. Find the lines of symmetry for the kolam design below.

Solution:

A typical kolam design has 4 lines of symmetry:

  1. Vertical line through the centre
  2. Horizontal line through the centre
  3. Diagonal from top-left to bottom-right
  4. Diagonal from top-right to bottom-left

The kolam is built on a dot grid with the pattern repeating in all 4 quadrants, giving it this beautiful four-fold symmetry.

Q9. Draw the following: (a) A triangle with exactly one line of symmetry (b) A triangle with exactly three lines of symmetry (c) A triangle with no line of symmetry. Is it possible to draw a triangle with exactly two lines of symmetry?

Solution:

(a) Isosceles triangle (two sides equal) → exactly 1 line of symmetry (from vertex angle to base midpoint)

(b) Equilateral triangle (all sides equal) → exactly 3 lines of symmetry

(c) Scalene triangle (all sides different) → no line of symmetry

Two lines of symmetry? NO, it is not possible!

Reason: If a triangle has 2 lines of symmetry, the reflections force the third line to also be a line of symmetry, making it equilateral (with 3 lines). So a triangle can have 0, 1, or 3 lines — never exactly 2.

Q10. Draw figures containing at least one curved boundary with: (a) exactly one line of symmetry (b) exactly two lines of symmetry (c) exactly four lines of symmetry.

Solution:

(a) One line: A semicircle (line of symmetry is the perpendicular bisector of the diameter)

(b) Two lines: An ellipse (horizontal and vertical axes are both lines of symmetry)

(c) Four lines: A circle with a plus sign (+) drawn inside it (or a flower shape with 4 equal petals)

💡 A plain circle has infinite lines of symmetry. Adding features reduces the number!

Q11. Copy figures on squared paper and complete them so that the given line is a line of symmetry.

Solution:

Method for completing symmetric figures on squared paper:

  1. For each coloured/filled square on one side of the line, count its distance from the line of symmetry
  2. On the other side, colour the square at the same distance from the line
  3. Work systematically row by row (or column by column)

💡 Hint for parts (c) and (f): If the line of symmetry is diagonal, try rotating the book 45° so the line appears vertical — then complete as usual!

Q12. Copy figures on squared paper. Complete each so that the resulting figure has TWO lines of symmetry (both given blue lines).

Solution:

Method: When a figure must have two lines of symmetry:

  1. First reflect the given part across Line 1
  2. Then reflect the combined figure across Line 2
  3. Any new squares added in step 2 must also be reflected back across Line 1

The result will have 4 “copies” of the original pattern — one in each quadrant formed by the two lines!

Q13. Copy figures on a dot grid. For each figure, draw two more lines to make a shape that has a line of symmetry.

Solution:

Strategy: Look at the existing lines and find a potential line of symmetry. Then add two lines that make the figure symmetric about that line.

The added lines should be mirror images of existing lines with respect to the chosen line of symmetry.

Figure it Out — Section 9.2 (Page 235 — Rotational Symmetry)

Q1. Find the angles of symmetry for the given figures about the point marked.

Solution:

(a) Figure with 4-fold symmetry: Angles = 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°

(b) Figure with only 360° symmetry: Angle = 360° only (no rotational symmetry besides full turn)

(c) Figure with 2-fold symmetry: Angles = 180°, 360°

💡 To find angles: rotate the figure about the marked point and note every angle where it looks exactly the same as the original.

Q2. Which of the following figures have more than one angle of symmetry?

Solution:

A figure has more than one angle of symmetry if it looks the same at some rotation less than 360°.

Figures with more than one angle of symmetry include those with:

  • Multiple identical parts arranged evenly around a centre
  • Regular polygons (square has 4 angles, hexagon has 6)
  • Propeller/windmill shapes with equal blades

Figures with only 360° as angle of symmetry (i.e., no rotational symmetry) are those with no repeated pattern around the centre.

Q3. Give the order of rotational symmetry for each figure.

Solution:

The order of rotational symmetry = number of angles of symmetry = 360° ÷ smallest angle of symmetry.

  • Equilateral triangle: Order = 3 (angles: 120°, 240°, 360°)
  • Square: Order = 4 (angles: 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°)
  • Regular pentagon: Order = 5
  • Regular hexagon: Order = 6
  • Circle: Order = infinite
  • Parallelogram: Order = 2 (angles: 180°, 360°)

Figure it Out — Section 9.2 (Page 236 — True or False)

Q. True or False: (a) Every figure will have 360° as an angle of symmetry. (b) If the smallest angle of symmetry is a natural number in degrees, then it is a factor of 360.

Solution:

(a) TRUE. Every figure returns to its original position after a full 360° rotation, so 360° is always an angle of symmetry.
