(b) TRUE. All other angles of symmetry are multiples of the smallest one. Since 360° must be one of these multiples, the smallest angle must divide 360 evenly — meaning it is a factor of 360.

Figure it Out — Section 9.2 (Page 238 — Combining Symmetries)

Q1. Colour sectors of a circle (divided into 12 equal parts) so that the figure has: (i) 3 angles of symmetry (ii) 4 angles of symmetry (iii) What are the possible numbers of angles of symmetry you can obtain?

Solution:

(i) 3 angles of symmetry: Colour every 4th sector the same colour (pattern repeats every 120°). Angles: 120°, 240°, 360°.

(ii) 4 angles of symmetry: Colour every 3rd sector the same colour (pattern repeats every 90°). Angles: 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°.

(iii) Possible numbers: The factors of 12 give the possible orders: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12 angles of symmetry. So you can achieve 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 12 angles of symmetry by different colourings.

Q2. Draw two figures other than a circle and a square that have BOTH reflection symmetry and rotational symmetry.

Solution:

Figure 1: Regular hexagon

  • Lines of symmetry = 6
  • Order of rotational symmetry = 6

Figure 2: Equilateral triangle

  • Lines of symmetry = 3
  • Order of rotational symmetry = 3

💡 In general, all regular polygons have both reflection and rotational symmetry!

Q3. Draw, wherever possible: (a) A triangle with at least 2 lines of symmetry and at least 2 angles of symmetry (b) A triangle with only 1 line of symmetry but no rotational symmetry (c) A quadrilateral with rotational symmetry but no reflection symmetry (d) A quadrilateral with reflection symmetry but no rotational symmetry

Solution:

(a) Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry, 3 angles of symmetry (120°, 240°, 360°) ✓

(b) Isosceles triangle: 1 line of symmetry (the altitude from the apex), and only 360° as angle of symmetry (no rotational symmetry) ✓

(c) Parallelogram (not rectangle): Has rotational symmetry of order 2 (180°, 360°) but NO line of symmetry ✓

(d) Kite (or isosceles trapezium): Has 1 line of reflection symmetry but no rotational symmetry (only 360°) ✓

Q4. In a figure, 60° is the smallest angle of symmetry. What are the other angles of symmetry?

Solution:

All angles of symmetry are multiples of the smallest angle:

60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°, 360°

The figure has 6 angles of symmetry (order 6). It behaves like a regular hexagon under rotation.

Q5. In a figure, 60° is an angle of symmetry. The figure has two angles of symmetry less than 60°. What is its smallest angle of symmetry?

Solution:

If 60° is an angle of symmetry, the smallest angle must be a factor of 60°.

There are two angles less than 60°, so the smallest angle times 1, 2, and 3 should give the first three angles, with the third being 60°.

Smallest angle = 60° ÷ 3 = 20°

The angles less than 60° are: 20° and 40°.

All angles: 20°, 40°, 60°, 80°, ..., 340°, 360° (order = 18)

Q6. Can we have a figure with rotational symmetry whose smallest angle of symmetry is: (a) 45°? (b) 17°?

Solution:

(a) 45° — Yes! Because 360° ÷ 45° = 8 (a whole number). So a figure with 8-fold rotational symmetry (like a regular octagon) has 45° as smallest angle.

(b) 17° — No! Because 360° ÷ 17° = 21.18... (not a whole number). Since 17 is not a factor of 360, we cannot have a figure with 17° as smallest angle of symmetry.

💡 Rule: The smallest angle of symmetry must be a factor of 360°.

Figure it Out — Section 9.1 (Lines of Symmetry — Additional)

Q1. For each of the following figures, identify the lines of symmetry:

(a) Equilateral triangle (b) Square (c) Regular pentagon (d) Regular hexagon (e) Circle

Solution:

(a) Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry (each from vertex to midpoint of opposite side)

(b) Square: 4 lines of symmetry (2 through midpoints of opposite sides + 2 diagonals)

(c) Regular pentagon: 5 lines of symmetry

(d) Regular hexagon: 6 lines of symmetry

(e) Circle: Infinite lines of symmetry (any diameter is a line of symmetry!)

💡 Pattern: A regular polygon with n sides has n lines of symmetry.

Q2. Which English capital letters have: (a) one line of symmetry (b) two lines of symmetry (c) no line of symmetry?

Solution:

(a) One line: A, B, C, D, E, K, M, T, U, V, W, Y (vertical or horizontal)

(b) Two lines: H, I, O, X (both vertical and horizontal)

(c) No line: F, G, J, L, N, P, Q, R, S, Z

Q3. Complete the figure so that the dotted line is the line of symmetry. (Mirror image drawing exercises)

Solution:

Method: For each point on the given half, find the point at the same perpendicular distance on the other side of the line. Connect these mirror points in the same way as the original.

💡 Use a mirror placed along the line of symmetry to check your answer!

Figure it Out — Section 9.2 (Rotational Symmetry)

Q4. Find the order of rotational symmetry for: (a) Square (b) Equilateral triangle (c) Regular hexagon (d) Circle

Solution:

(a) Square: rotates onto itself every 90°. Order = 360° ÷ 90° = 4

(b) Equilateral triangle: every 120°. Order = 360° ÷ 120° = 3

(c) Regular hexagon: every 60°. Order = 360° ÷ 60° = 6

(d) Circle: any angle works. Order = infinite

Q5. Which shapes have rotational symmetry but NO line of symmetry? Give an example.

Solution:

A parallelogram (that is not a rectangle) has rotational symmetry of order 2 (180°) but has NO line of symmetry.

Similarly, the letter S or Z has rotational symmetry of order 2 but no line symmetry.

💭 Strategy Tip

To find lines of symmetry: Try folding! If both halves match perfectly, you've found a line. For regular polygons, the answer is always = number of sides.

🧠 Self-Check Quiz

  1. How many lines of symmetry does a regular octagon have?
  2. Does the letter 'S' have a line of symmetry?
  3. What is the rotational symmetry order of a rectangle?
  1. 8 → regular n-gon has n lines
  2. No → S has rotational symmetry but no mirror line
  3. 2 → looks same at 180° and 360°
Mastery Practice — Chapter 9Extra problems on line symmetry, rotational symmetry, alphabets and real-world examples

Worked Example Set A — Line symmetry

  1. Q1. How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
    Solution. 5 lines (one from each vertex through the midpoint of the opposite side).
  2. Q2. How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have?
    Solution. 6 lines (3 through pairs of opposite vertices, 3 through midpoints of opposite sides).
  3. Q3. How many lines of symmetry does a scalene triangle have?
    Solution. 0 (all sides different).
  4. Q4. Does a parallelogram (not a rectangle, not a rhombus) have any line of symmetry?
    Solution. No.
  5. Q5. A rhombus that is not a square has ____ lines of symmetry.
    Solution. 2 (the two diagonals).
  6. Q6. A semicircle has how many lines of symmetry?
    Solution. 1 (the perpendicular from the centre of the diameter).
  7. Q7. A kite has how many lines of symmetry?
    Solution. 1 (the long diagonal).
  8. Q8. How many lines of symmetry does the letter H have?
    Solution. 2 (vertical and horizontal).

Worked Example Set B — Alphabet symmetry analysis

LetterLines of symmetryRotational symmetry (order)
A1 (vertical)1 (none)
B1 (horizontal)1
C1 (horizontal)1
D1 (horizontal)1
E1 (horizontal)1
H2 (vertical + horizontal)2 (180°)
I22
M1 (vertical)1
N02 (180°)
Oinfinite (treated as circle)infinite
S02 (180°)
T1 (vertical)1
U1 (vertical)1
V1 (vertical)1
W1 (vertical)1
X2 (or 4)2 (or 4)
Y1 (vertical)1
Z02 (180°)

Worked Example Set C — Rotational symmetry

  1. Q1. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
    Solution. 4 (looks same after 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°).
  2. Q2. What is the order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
    Solution. 3 (rotations of 120°, 240°, 360°).
  3. Q3. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a regular octagon?
    Solution. 8 (rotations of 45°, 90°, 135°, …).
  4. Q4. What is the smallest angle of rotation for a regular pentagon?
    Solution. 360°/5 = 72°.
  5. Q5. A wheel with 12 spokes has rotational symmetry of order ____ and smallest angle ____.
    Solution. Order 12; angle 30°.
  6. Q6. The Ashoka Chakra has 24 spokes. Its order of rotational symmetry is ____ and angle ____.
    Solution. Order 24; angle 15°.

Worked Example Set D — Real-life symmetry

  1. Q1. A starfish typically has 5-fold rotational symmetry. The smallest angle of rotation is ____.
    Solution. 72°.
  2. Q2. A snowflake usually has 6-fold rotational symmetry. The smallest angle is ____.
    Solution. 60°.
  3. Q3. The Taj Mahal’s front view has how many lines of symmetry?
    Solution. 1 (vertical).
  4. Q4. Which has more lines of symmetry — the Indian flag or a circle?
    Solution. A circle (infinite lines), much more than the flag (which has just 1 vertical line of symmetry).
  5. Q5. A rangoli with 8-fold rotational symmetry will look the same after a rotation of ____.
    Solution. 45°.
  6. Q6. Why are rotational parts in machines designed to be highly symmetric?
    Solution. So that the centre of mass is at the axis of rotation and the parts spin smoothly without wobbling.

📝 Mini-quiz — Chapter 9

  1. An equilateral triangle has ____ lines of symmetry and order of rotational symmetry ____.
  2. The letter X has ____ lines of symmetry.
  3. The order of rotational symmetry of a circle is ____.
  4. The smallest rotation angle for a regular hexagon is ____.
  5. A rectangle has rotational symmetry of order ____.
  6. A scalene triangle has rotational symmetry of order ____.
Answers:
  1. 3; 3
  2. 2 (or 4)
  3. infinite
  4. 60°
  5. 2
  6. 1

🎯 Chapter 9 — Key takeaways

  • Line of symmetry: a fold line where two halves coincide.
  • Rotational symmetry: figure matches itself after rotation < 360°.
  • Order: number of matches in one full rotation.
  • Angle of rotation = 360° / order.
  • Highly symmetric shapes: regular polygons, circles, snowflakes, flowers.
  • Symmetry appears widely in art, nature, architecture and machines.
Advanced Practice Lab — Chapter 9Hands-on symmetry projects, exploration of national symbols, and a mastery quiz

Hands-on Project 1 — Paper snowflake

Make a beautiful 6-fold symmetric snowflake.

Step 1. Take a square sheet of plain paper.

Step 2. Fold the square in half along the diagonal to form a triangle.

Step 3. Fold the resulting triangle into three equal parts (each 60°).

Step 4. You now have a thick triangular packet.

Step 5. With scissors, cut small shapes (circles, V's, semi-circles) along the edges of the packet.

Step 6. Carefully unfold — you have a beautiful snowflake with 6-fold rotational symmetry and 6 lines of reflection symmetry!

Why it works: each fold halves or thirds the paper, and so each cut is replicated symmetrically in every section.

Hands-on Project 2 — Mirror images of letters

Take a small mirror and stand it vertically along a letter.

Try this for the letter A: place the mirror along the vertical line of A. The right half of A reflects into the left half — result looks like the original A. So A has a vertical line of symmetry.

Try this for the letter F: there is no line where the mirror image equals the original. So F has no line of symmetry.

Try this for B: place the mirror horizontally through the middle. The top half reflects into the bottom — gives back B. So B has a horizontal line of symmetry.

Try this for H: works for both vertical and horizontal mirror lines. H has 2 lines of symmetry.

Try this for O: works for any line through the centre. O has many lines of symmetry (almost infinitely many).

Try this for S: no line of symmetry, but if you rotate S by 180°, it still looks like S. So S has rotational symmetry of order 2.

Hands-on Project 3 — The kaleidoscope effect

A kaleidoscope uses three mirrors at 60° angles to create a beautiful pattern with 6-fold rotational and reflection symmetry.

Simple model:

Step 1. Take three rectangular mirrors of equal size.

Step 2. Tape them together to form a triangular prism with the reflective sides facing inward.

Step 3. Drop a few colourful beads or sequins inside.

Step 4. Look through one end — the beads multiply into a beautiful symmetric pattern!

The pattern changes every time you shake or rotate the kaleidoscope, but it always has 6-fold symmetry.

Symmetry of national symbols and famous structures

Indian flag. The horizontal tricolour with the Ashoka Chakra in the centre has one line of symmetry (vertical). The Chakra itself has 24-fold rotational symmetry.

Taj Mahal. The front facade is famous for its perfect bilateral symmetry — a vertical line of symmetry through the central dome.

India Gate, New Delhi. A massive arch with vertical line of symmetry.

Lotus Temple, New Delhi. The flower-shaped temple has 9-fold rotational symmetry — 27 petals arranged in three layers of 9.

Sun Temple, Konark. The temple chariot has 12 pairs of wheels, each with 8-spoke symmetry.

Mandala patterns. Traditional Indian and Tibetan mandalas use 4-, 8-, 12- or 16-fold rotational symmetry.

Eiffel Tower, Paris. Has 4-fold rotational symmetry and 4 lines of reflection symmetry.

Pyramids of Giza. Square base — 4-fold rotational symmetry.

📝 Mastery quiz — Chapter 9

Q1. A regular dodecagon (12 sides) has ____ lines of symmetry and order of rotational symmetry ____.

Q2. The smallest angle of rotation for an order-9 figure is ____.

Q3. Which letter has BOTH a vertical and horizontal line of symmetry: A, B, H, O, or X?

Q4. The number of lines of symmetry of an isosceles trapezium is ____.

Q5. The Ashoka Chakra has ____ -fold rotational symmetry.

Q6. A figure with rotational symmetry of order 1 means ____.

Answers:
  1. 12; 12
  2. 40°
  3. H, O, X all qualify (more than one correct)
  4. 1 (vertical line through midpoints of the parallel sides)
  5. 24
  6. It has no rotational symmetry (only the trivial 360° rotation maps to itself)
NCERT Deep Dive — Chapter 9Following the NCERT chapter “Symmetry”

A guided tour of NCERT "Symmetry"

The NCERT chapter on symmetry is one of the most beautiful and accessible in the book. It connects mathematics to art, architecture and nature. The chapter develops:

  1. Visual idea of symmetry — matching parts.
  2. Lines of symmetry (also called axes of symmetry).
  3. Multiple lines of symmetry in regular shapes.
  4. Rotational symmetry — order and angle of rotation.
  5. Symmetry of letters of the alphabet.
  6. Symmetry in nature — leaves, flowers, butterflies, snowflakes.
  7. Symmetry in Indian art — rangoli, kolam, mandala, temple architecture.
  8. Symmetry in everyday objects — cycle wheels, fans, logos.

By the end you should be able to identify lines and orders of symmetry for any common figure, and create new symmetric designs of your own.

Lines of symmetry — the fold-and-match test

A line of symmetry is a line about which a figure can be folded so that the two halves match exactly.

This is also called a mirror line or axis of symmetry.

The fold test. Cut a paper shape. Try folding it along different lines. Wherever the fold produces a perfect match between the two halves, you have found a line of symmetry.

Standard examples:

  • Square: 4 lines of symmetry (2 diagonals + 2 lines through midpoints of opposite sides).
  • Rectangle (non-square): 2 lines of symmetry (through midpoints of opposite sides). Diagonals are NOT lines of symmetry!
  • Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry (from each vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side).
  • Isosceles triangle: 1 line of symmetry (perpendicular bisector of the base).
  • Scalene triangle: 0 lines of symmetry.
  • Regular pentagon: 5 lines of symmetry.
  • Regular hexagon: 6 lines of symmetry.
  • Regular n-gon: n lines of symmetry.
  • Circle: infinitely many lines of symmetry (every diameter).
  • Rhombus: 2 lines of symmetry (both diagonals).
  • Parallelogram (non-rhombus, non-rectangle): 0 lines of symmetry!
  • Kite: 1 line of symmetry (the axis through the equal-side vertices).

Symmetry in the English alphabet

NCERT's favourite exercise: classify the 26 uppercase letters by their symmetry.

Vertical line of symmetry only: A, M, T, U, V, W, Y.

Horizontal line of symmetry only: B, C, D, E, K.

Both vertical and horizontal: H, I, O, X.

Rotational symmetry only (order 2): N, S, Z.

Rotational symmetry of order 4: none in the standard uppercase alphabet (only some font designs of O qualify).

No symmetry: F, G, J, L, P, Q, R.

(Some letters depend on the typeface; e.g. lowercase “o” has more symmetries than uppercase O in some fonts.)

Rotational symmetry — order and angle

A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a rotation of less than 360° about a fixed point (the centre of rotation).

The order of rotational symmetry is the number of distinct positions in which the figure looks identical (counting the original).

The angle of rotational symmetry is 360° / order.

Examples:

  • Equilateral triangle: order 3, angle 120°.
  • Square: order 4, angle 90°.
  • Regular pentagon: order 5, angle 72°.
  • Regular hexagon: order 6, angle 60°.
  • Regular octagon: order 8, angle 45°.
  • Regular n-gon: order n, angle 360°/n.
  • Circle: infinite order (every angle is a symmetry).
  • Letter S: order 2, angle 180°.
  • Letter F: order 1 (only the trivial 360° rotation), often described as “no rotational symmetry”.
  • Recycling logo / triskelion: order 3, angle 120°.
  • Ashoka Chakra (24 spokes): order 24, angle 15°.

Combining line and rotational symmetry

Many shapes have both line and rotational symmetry. For regular polygons:

ShapeLines of symmetryOrder of rotation
Equilateral ▵33
Square44
Regular pentagon55
Regular hexagon66
Regular octagon88
Circle∞∞

For a regular n-gon, both line-symmetry count and order of rotation equal n.

Some shapes have rotational but no line symmetry (e.g. S, swastika, recycling logo). Some have line but no rotational (other than trivial) symmetry (e.g. letter A, isosceles trapezium).

Indian symmetry traditions — rangoli, kolam, mandala

India has one of the world's richest symmetry traditions, deeply integrated into daily life.

Rangoli is the practice of drawing geometric patterns on the ground using coloured powders, flower petals or rice flour. Common during Diwali and weddings. Most rangolis exhibit rotational symmetry of order 4, 6 or 8, with multiple lines of symmetry.

Kolam is the South Indian tradition of drawing geometric designs around a dot grid using rice flour. It is performed daily at the entrance to homes. Kolam patterns often feature complex symmetries and continuous loops.

Mandalas are circular geometric designs symbolising the universe. They feature high-order rotational symmetry (often 4, 8, 12, 16) and many lines of symmetry. Buddhist, Hindu and Jain traditions all use mandalas.

Temple architecture. Indian temples such as Khajuraho, Konark and Madurai use complex symmetric patterns based on mathematical ratios and grid systems.

Yantra. A geometric symbol used for meditation. The Sri Yantra is a famous example with 9 interlocking triangles displaying intricate symmetry.

Activity: design your own rangoli on a 8×8 dot grid. Choose a symmetry: 4-fold rotation or 2 perpendicular mirror lines. Watch how the constraint guides your creativity.

Symmetry in nature

Nature is full of symmetric forms. Here are some categories.

Bilateral symmetry (1 line).

  • Human face and body (approximately).
  • Butterflies, moths, beetles.
  • Most leaves of trees.
  • Birds with outspread wings.
  • Fish.

Radial symmetry (rotational).

  • Flowers — lily (3-fold), tulip (3-fold), orchid (varies), sunflower (very high-fold).
  • Starfish (5-fold).
  • Sea urchins (5-fold).
  • Jellyfish (4-fold).
  • Snowflakes (6-fold).

Spiral symmetry.

  • Nautilus shell.
  • Sunflower seed head.
  • Galaxies (Milky Way is a spiral galaxy).
  • Hurricane patterns.

Fractal symmetry (self-similar at multiple scales).

  • Romanesco broccoli.
  • Fern fronds.
  • River branching patterns.
  • Tree branches.

Symmetry in design and engineering

Symmetry isn't just beautiful — it's also practical.

  • Bicycle wheels. Many spokes radiating from the hub provide rotational symmetry, distributing stress evenly.
  • Aeroplanes and cars. Bilateral symmetry ensures even weight distribution and balanced handling.
  • Buildings. Pillars, windows and doorways are often symmetric for stability and aesthetic appeal.
  • Logos and brands. Many logos use symmetry for memorability: Mercedes (3-fold), BMW (2 axes), Toyota (concentric ovals).
  • Sports equipment. Tennis rackets, cricket bats, frisbees all use bilateral or rotational symmetry.
  • Crystals. Salt (cubic), quartz (hexagonal) and diamond (cubic) all have specific symmetries that determine their physical properties.
  • DNA. The double helix has a 2-fold rotational symmetry.

Symmetry mini-project

Try this hands-on project to deepen your understanding.

Project: A symmetry portfolio.

  1. Collect 5 leaves from different plants. Sketch each, then mark the line(s) of symmetry.
  2. Find 3 flowers. Identify the rotational symmetry order of each.
  3. Photograph 3 buildings near you. Identify lines of symmetry.
  4. Find 5 logos in newspapers or magazines. Classify them by symmetry type.
  5. Design an original rangoli with 4-fold rotational symmetry on an 8×8 grid.
  6. Write a short paragraph explaining what surprised you most about symmetry in your world.

Compile everything into a portfolio. You will be amazed at how symmetric the world really is!

📝 NCERT-style review questions — Chapter 9 (additional)

Q1. How many lines of symmetry does a regular nonagon (9 sides) have?

Q2. Write any two letters of the alphabet that have horizontal but not vertical line of symmetry.

Q3. A figure has rotational symmetry of order 5. What is the angle of rotational symmetry?

Q4. Name a flower with 5-fold rotational symmetry.

Q5. Does a parallelogram (non-rectangle) have any line of symmetry? Explain.

Q6. Sketch any letter that has both horizontal and vertical lines of symmetry.

Q7. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a swastika?

Q8. What is the rotational symmetry order of the recycling logo (three arrows)?

Answers:
  1. 9 (regular n-gon has n lines).
  2. B, C, D, E, K (any two).
  3. 360 / 5 = 72°.
  4. Hibiscus, Lily (with 5 petals).
  5. No. Folding doesn't map it to itself except for special cases (rhombus, rectangle).
  6. H, I, O or X.
  7. 4 (each rotation by 90° maps it to itself).
  8. 3 (each rotation by 120° maps it to itself).

📌 Chapter 9 — Symmetry skill check

  • I can identify lines of symmetry for triangles, quadrilaterals and regular polygons.
  • I know that a regular n-gon has n lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order n.
  • I can classify the 26 alphabet letters by their symmetry type.
  • I can determine the order and angle of rotational symmetry of a figure.
  • I appreciate symmetry in nature (leaves, flowers, snowflakes, shells).
  • I appreciate symmetry in Indian art (rangoli, kolam, mandala, yantra, temple architecture).
  • I can design my own symmetric pattern with chosen symmetry constraints.
Mastery Practice Pack — Chapter 9Lines & rotational symmetry drills

Drill 1 — Lines of symmetry of common shapes

ShapeLines of symmetry
Square4
Rectangle (non-square)2
Rhombus (non-square)2
Parallelogram (general)0
Kite1
Isosceles trapezium1
Equilateral triangle3
Isosceles triangle1
Scalene triangle0
Regular pentagon5
Regular hexagon6
Regular octagon8
Regular decagon10
Regular n-gonn
Circleinfinite

Drill 2 — Rotational symmetry order

Shape / objectOrderAngle
Equilateral triangle3120°
Square490°
Regular pentagon572°
Regular hexagon660°
Regular octagon845°
Letter S2180°
Letter Z2180°
Letter N2180°
Letter X2180°
Letter Oinfinite (circle)any
Recycling logo3120°
Mercedes-Benz logo3120°
BMW logo2180°
Swastika490°
Ashoka Chakra (24 spokes)2415°

Drill 3 — Letter classification (full alphabet)

Vertical line only: A, M, T, U, V, W, Y.

Horizontal line only: B, C, D, E, K.

Both H/V: H, I, O, X.

Rotational only (order 2): N, S, Z.

No symmetry: F, G, J, L, P, Q, R.

Verify by drawing each letter on graph paper and folding (for lines) or rotating (for order).

Drill 4 — Design challenges

Challenge 1. Design a rangoli on an 8×8 grid with 4 lines of symmetry (vertical, horizontal, and two diagonals).

Challenge 2. Design a logo with 3-fold rotational symmetry.

Challenge 3. Design a figure that has rotational symmetry of order 2 but NO line of symmetry. (Hint: think S-shape or Z-shape.)

Challenge 4. Find a real-life object in your home with rotational symmetry of order >= 5 (e.g. cycle wheel, fan, biscuit).

Challenge 5. Identify the symmetry of an Indian flag (the flag itself, not the wheel inside). Answer: 1 horizontal line of symmetry (since the colours are different top and bottom). The Ashoka Chakra inside has order 24.

Bonus — symmetry in mathematics beyond shapes

Symmetry is one of the deepest ideas in mathematics — it extends far beyond shapes.

  • Symmetry in numbers. Palindromes (12321 reads the same both ways).
  • Symmetry in equations. The equation x + y = y + x shows the symmetric property of addition.
  • Symmetry in functions. f(x) = f(-x) means the graph is symmetric about the y-axis (e.g. y = x²).
  • Symmetry in physics. Many laws of physics remain unchanged under rotation, reflection or translation — deep "conservation laws" follow.
  • Symmetry in music. Themes can be played forwards (original), backwards (retrograde), upside-down (inverted), or backwards-upside-down (retrograde-inverted).
  • Symmetry in molecules. Water (H₂O) has a vertical line of symmetry; benzene has 6-fold rotational symmetry.
  • Galois symmetry. The deepest application: the symmetries of equations underlie modern algebra and number theory.

Chapter 9 glossary

  • Symmetry. A shape's property of looking the same after some transformation.
  • Line of symmetry / axis of symmetry / mirror line. A line that divides a figure into two matching halves.
  • Rotational symmetry. A figure that looks the same after a rotation of less than 360°.
  • Order of rotational symmetry. Number of distinct positions in 360° that look the same.
  • Angle of rotational symmetry. 360° / order.
  • Bilateral symmetry. One line of symmetry (left-right mirror).
  • Radial symmetry. Rotational symmetry about a centre point.
  • Mandala. A circular symmetric design used in Indian and Asian traditions.
  • Rangoli. Indian floor decoration drawn with coloured powders / flowers / rice flour.
  • Kolam. South-Indian dot-grid floor design.
  • Yantra. Geometric symbol used for meditation, often with rotational symmetry.

Exam-prep question bank — Chapter 9 (25 questions)

  1. Define a line of symmetry.
  2. How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
  3. How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle (non-square) have?
  4. How many lines of symmetry does an equilateral triangle have?
  5. How many lines of symmetry does an isosceles triangle have?
  6. How many lines of symmetry does a scalene triangle have?
  7. How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
  8. How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have?
  9. How many lines of symmetry does a circle have?
  10. How many lines of symmetry does a parallelogram (non-special) have?
  11. Define rotational symmetry.
  12. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
  13. What is the angle of rotational symmetry of a regular pentagon?
  14. What is the order of rotational symmetry of a circle?
  15. What is the order of rotational symmetry of the letter S?
  16. Which letters of the alphabet have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry?
  17. Which letters have rotational but no line symmetry?
  18. Name a flower with 5-fold rotational symmetry.
  19. Name a starfish's symmetry order.
  20. What is a mandala?
  21. What is a rangoli?
  22. What is a kolam?
  23. What is the symmetry order of the Ashoka Chakra?
  24. What is the angle of one of the Ashoka Chakra rotations?
  25. What is the symmetry of the swastika?
Answer key (click to expand)
  1. A line such that folding the figure along it makes the two halves coincide exactly.
  2. 4.
  3. 2.
  4. 3.
  5. 1.
  6. 0.
  7. 5.
  8. 6.
  9. Infinite.
  10. 0.
  11. Looking the same after a rotation of less than 360°.
  12. 4.
  13. 72°.
  14. Infinite.
  15. 2.
  16. H, I, O, X.
  17. N, S, Z.
  18. Hibiscus, lily, or any 5-petalled flower.
  19. 5.
  20. A circular symmetric design used in spiritual traditions.
  21. Indian floor decoration with coloured powders or flowers.
  22. South-Indian dot-grid floor pattern.
  23. 24.
  24. 15°.
  25. Order 4 rotational; no line symmetry.

Bonus — symmetry around the world

  • Taj Mahal. Perfect bilateral symmetry across vertical axis.
  • Eiffel Tower. Bilateral symmetry across vertical axis.
  • Pyramids of Giza. 4-fold rotational symmetry from above.
  • Stonehenge. Circular layout with high rotational symmetry.
  • Sydney Opera House. Multiple curved sail-shapes with reflective symmetry.
  • Chinese pagodas. Vertical axis symmetry through each floor.
  • Spanish Mosque-Cathedral of Córdoba. Repeated arches showing translational symmetry.
  • Greek Parthenon. Bilateral symmetry, golden ratio proportions.
  • Buddhist stupas. Circular plan with rotational symmetry.
  • Islamic tilings. Sophisticated wallpaper symmetries; Alhambra Palace.
  • Mughal gardens. 4-fold or 8-fold rotational layout.
  • Sri Yantra. Intricate symmetric design with 9 interlocking triangles.
  • Mandalas. Used in Hindu, Buddhist, Jain art for meditation.
  • Crystals. Salt has cubic symmetry, quartz has 6-fold symmetry, diamond has cubic.
  • Snowflakes. Six-fold rotational symmetry due to water molecule geometry.
  • Spider webs. Radial symmetry in orb webs.

Common misconceptions about symmetry

  • Thinking a rectangle's diagonals are lines of symmetry — they are NOT (except for the square).
  • Thinking a parallelogram has line symmetry — it does NOT (only rotational of order 2).
  • Confusing “axis of symmetry” with “diagonal” — they are different concepts.
  • Thinking the letter F has line symmetry — it does NOT.
  • Forgetting that rotational symmetry must be of less than 360°.
  • Counting order 1 as a meaningful rotational symmetry — in practice we ignore the trivial 360° case.
  • Confusing “order” (count) with “angle” (degrees).
  • Thinking all natural objects are perfectly symmetric — they are usually only approximately so.
  • Confusing reflectional with rotational symmetry — S has rotational, E has reflectional.
  • Thinking the centre of rotation is always the geometric centre — not always (for asymmetric figures).

Extended chapter wrap-up — Chapter 9

Symmetry is one of the most beautiful and powerful ideas in all of mathematics and science. From the patterns of snowflakes to the structure of crystals, from the design of cathedrals to the patterns on butterfly wings, symmetry pervades the natural and human-made world. As the great German mathematician Hermann Weyl wrote: “Symmetry, as wide or as narrow as you may define its meaning, is one idea by which man through the ages has tried to comprehend and create order, beauty and perfection.”

What did we learn? First, the idea of a line of symmetry — a fold line that produces matching halves. Different shapes have different numbers of lines: square (4), rectangle (2), equilateral triangle (3), regular pentagon (5), regular hexagon (6), circle (infinite). Second, the idea of rotational symmetry — a figure looking the same after a rotation of less than 360°. The order tells how many distinct positions look the same; the angle equals 360° divided by the order.

We learned to classify the 26 alphabet letters: A, M, T, U, V, W, Y have only vertical line symmetry; B, C, D, E, K have only horizontal; H, I, O, X have both; N, S, Z have only rotational; F, G, J, L, P, Q, R have none.

We celebrated India's deep symmetry traditions: rangoli at festivals, kolam at South Indian doorsteps, mandalas in spiritual art, yantras in meditation, and the precise geometric symmetries of temple architecture from Khajuraho to Konark to Madurai. The Ashoka Chakra on the Indian flag has 24-fold rotational symmetry.

Symmetry runs deep in nature too: bilateral symmetry in animal bodies, radial symmetry in flowers and starfish, 6-fold symmetry in snowflakes (due to water's molecular geometry), spiral symmetry in shells and galaxies, and fractal self-similarity in ferns and broccoli.

Looking ahead, symmetry becomes increasingly important in higher mathematics and physics. In algebra, the symmetries of an equation reveal its solutions (Galois theory). In physics, every conservation law (energy, momentum) corresponds to a symmetry of the laws of nature (Noether's theorem). In chemistry, molecular symmetry determines reactivity and spectra. The journey starts here, with the simple observation that a butterfly's left wing mirrors its right.

Suggested further activities

Activity 1. Collect 5 leaves from different plants. Identify the line(s) of symmetry of each.

Activity 2. Identify the rotational symmetry order of 5 flowers.

Activity 3. Design a rangoli on an 8×8 dot grid with 4-fold rotational symmetry.

Activity 4. Find 5 logos and classify them by symmetry type.

Activity 5. Photograph 3 symmetric buildings near you.

Activity 6. Cut a snowflake out of folded paper and observe its rotational symmetry.

Activity 7. Draw all 26 alphabet letters; classify each by line and rotational symmetry.

Activity 8. Research the Sri Yantra and describe its symmetry.

📋 Chapter 10 — What's Inside

📖 Topics Covered

  • 10.1 Bela’s Building of Fun (Introduction to Negative Numbers)
  • 10.2 The Token Model (Number Line with Both Sides of Zero)
  • 10.3 Integers in Other Places (Comparing & Ordering)
  • 10.4 Explorations with Integers (Addition & Subtraction)
  • 10.5 A Pinch of History

✍️ Figure it Out (Exercises)

  • Section 10.1 — Building/Lift Floors
  • Section 10.2 — Number Line
  • Section 10.3 — Addition
  • Section 10.4 — Subtraction
  • Section 10.5 — Temperature & Real-life

Chapter 10: The Other Side of Zero (Integers)

🎯 Why integers matter

Up to now your number world had only zero and counting numbers — 0, 1, 2, 3 …. But the real world demands more: a fridge can be at −5°C, a basement is at floor −2, a bank account can show −₹200, and a submarine can be at −150 m below sea level. To describe these “below-zero” situations we extend our number line to the left of zero and meet a beautiful new family: negative integers. Together with whole numbers they form the set of integers — one of the most important number systems in mathematics.

In-Depth Study — Chapter 10Each subtopic explained with diagrams, examples and real-life applications

§10.1 — The Need for Negative Numbers (In Depth)

Why do we need numbers smaller than zero? Because nature and daily life are full of situations where we must measure things in opposite directions.

SituationPositive directionNegative direction
TemperatureAbove 0°C (hot)Below 0°C (cold)
Bank accountMoney you have (credit)Money you owe (debt)
Lift / floorsFloors above groundBasements below ground
Sea levelMountain heightsOcean depths
TimeYears AD (after Christ)Years BC (before Christ)
Football / cricketRuns / goals scoredRuns / goals lost

Real example: the lowest place on land is the Dead Sea shore, at about −430 m (430 metres below sea level). The highest place is Mount Everest, at about +8848 m above sea level. The difference between these two points is 8848 − (−430) = 9278 m!

§10.2 — The Integer Number Line (In Depth)

To represent both positive and negative numbers we extend the number line in both directions from zero. The numbers to the right of zero are positive integers (+1, +2, +3, …), and the numbers to the left of zero are negative integers (−1, −2, −3, …). Zero itself is neither positive nor negative.

Reading the number line

  • A number to the right of another number is always greater. So −2 > −5, because −2 is to the right of −5.
  • A number to the left is always smaller.
  • Every positive integer is greater than every negative integer.
  • Zero is greater than every negative number and smaller than every positive number.

Worked example: arrange in order

Q. Arrange these integers from smallest to largest: 3, −7, 0, −2, 5, −1.

A. Imagine each number’s position on the line. From left to right: −7, −2, −1, 0, 3, 5.

§10.3 — Integers in Other Places — Comparing and Ordering Integers (In Depth)

For any two integers a and b:

  • If a lies to the right of b on the number line, then a > b.
  • If a lies to the left of b, then a < b.
  • If a and b are the same point, then a = b.

Key rules

  • Among positives, the one with the bigger absolute value is bigger: 7 > 3.
  • Among negatives, the one with the smaller absolute value is bigger: −3 > −7 (because −7 is “further below” zero).
  • Any positive is greater than any negative: +1 > −100.

Memory trick — debt analogy: A debt of ₹3 is “less bad” than a debt of ₹7, so −3 > −7. The bigger the debt, the smaller the number.

Practice

  1. Compare: −4 ___ −9. (Answer: −4 > −9.)
  2. Order from greatest to smallest: 0, −5, 3, −2, 1. (Answer: 3, 1, 0, −2, −5.)
  3. Which is bigger: −1 or 0? (Answer: 0.)

§10.4 — Explorations with Integers — Addition of Integers (In Depth)

Adding integers can be visualised as movement on the number line:

  • Adding a positive number means moving to the right.
  • Adding a negative number means moving to the left.

Rules

  1. Same sign: add the values and keep the common sign.
    e.g. (+3) + (+5) = +8;    (−3) + (−5) = −8.
  2. Different signs: subtract the smaller value from the larger and keep the sign of the larger.
    e.g. (+7) + (−3) = +4;    (−7) + (+3) = −4.
  3. Opposite equal: a + (−a) = 0. Numbers a and −a are called additive inverses.

Worked examples

  1. (−8) + (+3) = −5
  2. (+12) + (−7) = +5
  3. (−6) + (−4) = −10
  4. (+9) + (−9) = 0

§10.4 — Explorations with Integers — Subtraction of Integers (In Depth)

Subtracting an integer is the same as adding its additive inverse (its opposite). In symbols:

a − b = a + (−b)

Worked examples

  1. (+7) − (+3) = (+7) + (−3) = +4
  2. (+7) − (−3) = (+7) + (+3) = +10   (subtracting a negative is like adding!)
  3. (−5) − (+4) = (−5) + (−4) = −9
  4. (−5) − (−2) = (−5) + (+2) = −3

Story: If you have ₹5 in your pocket and someone takes away a debt of ₹3 (i.e. subtracts −3), then you are richer by ₹3. That’s why 5 − (−3) = 8.

Temperature problem

Q. At 6 a.m. the temperature was −3°C. By noon it had risen to +12°C. How much did the temperature rise?

A. Rise = final − initial = (+12) − (−3) = +15°C.

Beyond NCERT — Integers in Real Life (Extension Notes)

Lift in a tall building

A building has 12 floors above ground (1 to 12) and 4 basement floors below ground (−1 to −4). The ground floor is 0.

  • Going from basement 3 (−3) to floor 7: difference = 7 − (−3) = 10 floors up.
  • Going from floor 5 to basement 2: difference = −2 − 5 = −7 (7 floors down).

Bank account

If your balance is −₹200 (you owe ₹200) and you deposit ₹500, your new balance is (−200) + (+500) = +₹300.

Game scores

In a quiz, +5 for correct and −2 for wrong. If you got 6 correct and 4 wrong, total = 6×5 + 4×(−2) = 30 − 8 = +22 points.

Temperature differences

If Leh is at −15°C and Chennai is at +32°C on the same day, the difference is 32 − (−15) = 47°C.

§10.5 — A Pinch of History (In Depth)

Negative numbers may feel natural to us today, but mathematicians took a long time to accept them. NCERT highlights several beautiful milestones in this story:

  • Ancient China (around 200 BCE). In the famous text The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art, accountants used red rods for positive quantities and black rods for negative quantities while doing calculations on a counting board. This is the earliest known systematic use of negative numbers!
  • Kautilya’s Arthashastra (India, around 300 BCE). This treatise on economics and statecraft already distinguished clearly between income (positive) and expenditure / debt (negative) when keeping accounts.
  • Bakhshali Manuscript (India, possibly 3rd–7th century CE). Used a special symbol — a small +-like mark placed after a number — to indicate that the number was negative. This is one of the earliest written symbols for a negative number anywhere in the world.
  • Brahmagupta (628 CE, India). In his book Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta, Brahmagupta wrote down the complete arithmetic rules for positive numbers, negative numbers and zero — almost exactly as we use them today! He called positives dhana (“fortunes”) and negatives rina (“debts”).
  • Europe. Negative numbers were viewed with great suspicion in Europe for centuries — some called them “absurd” or “fictitious.” They were widely accepted only by the 17th–18th century.

Some of Brahmagupta’s rules (in his own words, modernised):

  • The sum of two positives is positive; of two negatives is negative.
  • The sum of a positive and a negative is their difference; if equal, it is zero.
  • A debt subtracted from zero becomes a fortune; a fortune subtracted from zero becomes a debt.
  • The product (or quotient) of two positives or two negatives is positive; of a positive and a negative is negative.

These are exactly the integer rules you are learning in Chapter 10!

The takeaway: negative numbers were born from real human needs — debts, losses, cold temperatures, below-ground depths. India played a leading role in turning them into proper mathematics.

🎯 Big picture of Chapter 10

  • Integers = …, −3, −2, −1, 0, +1, +2, +3, …
  • Negative numbers describe quantities below a reference point (zero).
  • On the number line, numbers grow as we move right and shrink as we move left.
  • Adding a negative = moving left; subtracting a negative = moving right.
  • Every integer has an additive inverse: a + (−a) = 0.

⚠️ Common mistakes

  • Thinking −7 > −3 because 7 > 3 — wrong! On the number line, −7 is further left, so −7 < −3.
  • Forgetting that subtracting a negative equals adding: 5 − (−3) = 8, not 2.
  • Saying zero is positive or negative — zero is neither.
  • Mixing up signs when adding: (−3) + (−5) = −8 (not −2 or +8).
Quick Reference — Chapter 10The original briefer summary follows

§10.1 — Introducing Negative Numbers

Until now, numbers started at 0 and went up: 0, 1, 2, 3, ... But what's on the OTHER side of zero?

Integers = ..., −3, −2, −1, 0, +1, +2, +3, ...

Real-life examples of negative numbers:

  • 🌡️ Temperature: −5°C means 5 degrees below zero (freezing!)
  • 🏢 Building floors: B1, B2 = basement floors below ground
  • 💰 Money: −₹500 means you OWE ₹500 (debt)
  • 🌊 Depth: −100 m means 100 metres below sea level

§10.4 — Explorations with Integers (Adding & Subtracting)

Think of it as a lift/elevator:

  • Adding positive = going UP → (+2) + (+3) = +5
  • Adding negative = going DOWN → (+2) + (−3) = −1
  • Subtracting = adding the INVERSE → (+2) − (+3) = (+2) + (−3) = −1

§10.3 — Integers in Other Places (Comparing & Real-world Integers)

On the number line, the number to the RIGHT is always bigger.

  • +3 > +1 (3 is right of 1) ✓
  • −1 > −5 (−1 is right of −5!) ✓
  • 0 > −100 (zero is right of ALL negatives!) ✓
  • Any positive > any negative ✓
Figure it Out — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved

📝 Quick Recap

  • Integers: ..., −3, −2, −1, 0, +1, +2, +3, ...
  • Positive: right of 0 | Negative: left of 0
  • Adding positive: move right | Adding negative: move left
  • Inverse: opposite sign, same distance from 0
  • Comparing: further right = bigger

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • −3 is NOT bigger than −1! Think: −3°C is COLDER than −1°C. On number line, −1 is to the right.
  • Subtracting a negative = adding! 5 − (−3) = 5 + 3 = 8
  • Zero is neither positive nor negative
  • (−2) × (−3) = +6 — two negatives multiply to give positive!

Figure it Out — Section 10.1 (Building/Lift)

Q1. You start from Floor +2 and press −3 in the lift. Where will you reach?

Solution:

(+2) + (−3) = −1

You reach Floor −1 (Toy Store — one floor below ground!)

Q2. Evaluate these expressions:

a. (+1)+(+4) b. (+4)+(+1) c. (+4)+(−3) d. (−1)+(+2) e. (−1)+(+1) f. 0+(+2) g. 0+(−2)

Solution:

(a) (+1)+(+4) = +5 (go up 4 from floor 1)

(b) (+4)+(+1) = +5 (same! addition order doesn't matter)

(c) (+4)+(−3) = +1 (go down 3 from floor 4)

(d) (−1)+(+2) = +1 (go up 2 from basement 1)

(e) (−1)+(+1) = 0 (go up 1 from basement → ground floor!)

(f) 0+(+2) = +2 (go up 2 from ground)

(g) 0+(−2) = −2 (go down 2 from ground)

Q3. Starting from different floors, find movements to reach Floor −5.

Solution:

  • (+1) + (−6) = −5 (from floor 1, go down 6)
  • (−2) + (−3) = −5 (from basement 2, go down 3 more)
  • 0 + (−5) = −5 (from ground, go down 5)
  • (+3) + (−8) = −5 (from floor 3, go down 8)
  • (−4) + (−1) = −5 (from basement 4, go down 1)

Q. Evaluate by combining button presses:

a. (+1)+(+4) b. (+4)+(+1) c. (+4)+(−3)+(−2) d. (−1)+(+2)+(−3)

Solution:

(a) +5

(b) +5

(c) (+4)+(−3)+(−2) = (+1)+(−2) = −1

(d) (−1)+(+2)+(−3) = (+1)+(−3) = −2

Q. Write the inverses of: +4, −4, −3, 0, +2, −1

Solution:

  • Inverse of +4 = −4
  • Inverse of −4 = +4
  • Inverse of −3 = +3
  • Inverse of 0 = 0 (zero is its own inverse!)
  • Inverse of +2 = −2
  • Inverse of −1 = +1

💡 The inverse of a number is the number you add to get ZERO. n + (−n) = 0 always!

Q. Place these integers in order from smallest to largest: +3, −5, 0, −2, +7, −7, +1

Solution:

−7 < −5 < −2 < 0 < +1 < +3 < +7

💡 All negatives come before zero, and the "more negative" a number is, the smaller it is!

Figure it Out — Section 10.1 (Building of Fun — Addition)

Q1. You start from Floor +2 and press −3 in the lift. Where will you reach? Write an expression.

Solution:

(+2) + (−3) = −1 (Toy Store)

Q2. Evaluate these expressions:

(a) (+1) + (+4) (b) (+4) + (+1) (c) (+4) + (−3) (d) (−1) + (+2) (e) (−1) + (+1) (f) 0 + (+2) (g) 0 + (−2)

Solution:

(a) +5 (b) +5 (c) +1 (d) +1 (e) 0 (f) +2 (g) −2

💡 Notice: (a) and (b) give the same answer! Addition is commutative: a + b = b + a

Q3. Starting from different floors, find movements to reach Floor −5. Write expressions.

Solution:

(+2) + (−7) = −5

(+1) + (−6) = −5

(−2) + (−3) = −5

(0) + (−5) = −5

(−3) + (−2) = −5

Q4. Evaluate by combining button presses:

(a) (+1) + (+4) (b) (+4) + (+1) (c) (+4) + (−3) + (−2) (d) (−1) + (+2) + (−3)

Solution:

(a) +5

(b) +5

(c) +4 − 3 − 2 = −1

(d) −1 + 2 − 3 = −2

Q5. Write the inverses (additive inverses) of: +4, −4, −3, 0, +2, −1

Solution:

Inverse of +4 = −4

Inverse of −4 = +4

Inverse of −3 = +3

Inverse of 0 = 0 (zero is its own inverse!)

Inverse of +2 = −2

Inverse of −1 = +1

Figure it Out — Section 10.1 (Comparing Integers)

Q1. Compare using < or >:

(a) −2 __ +5 (b) −5 __ +4 (c) −5 __ −3 (d) +6 __ −6 (e) 0 __ −4 (f) 0 __ +4

Solution:

(a) −2 < +5 (negatives always less than positives)

(b) −5 < +4

(c) −5 < −3 (more negative = smaller)

(d) +6 > −6

(e) 0 > −4 (0 is greater than all negatives)

(f) 0 < +4 (0 is less than all positives)

Q2. Compare: (a) −10 __ −12 (b) +17 __ −10 (c) 0 __ −20 (d) +9 __ −9 (e) −25 __ −7 (f) +15 __ −17

Solution:

(a) −10 > −12 (less negative = greater)

(b) +17 > −10

(c) 0 > −20

(d) +9 > −9

(e) −25 < −7

(f) +15 > −17

Figure it Out — Section 10.1 (Subtraction of Integers)

Q1. Complete these subtraction expressions:

(a) (+1) − (+4) (b) (0) − (+2) (c) (+4) − (+1) (d) (0) − (−2) (e) (+4) − (−3) (f) (−4) − (−3) (g) (−1) − (+2) (h) (−2) − (−2) (i) (−1) − (+1) (j) (+3) − (−3)

Solution:

(a) −3 (b) −2 (c) +3 (d) +2 (e) +7

(f) −1 (g) −3 (h) 0 (i) −2 (j) +6

💡 Subtracting a negative = Adding a positive! e.g., (+4) − (−3) = (+4) + (+3) = +7

Q2. Complete these expressions:

(a) (+40) + ___ = +200 (b) (+40) + ___ = −200 (c) (−50) + ___ = +200 (d) (−50) + ___ = −200 (e) (−200) − (−40) (f) (+200) − (+40) (g) (−200) − (+40)

Solution:

(a) +200 − 40 = +160

(b) −200 − 40 = −240

(c) +200 − (−50) = +200 + 50 = +250

(d) −200 − (−50) = −200 + 50 = −150

(e) (−200) − (−40) = −200 + 40 = −160

(f) (+200) − (+40) = +160

(g) (−200) − (+40) = −200 − 40 = −240

Figure it Out — Section 10.2 (Number Line & Tokens)

Q1. Mark 3 positive and 3 negative numbers on the number line.

Solution:

Examples: Positive numbers: 2, 5, 8. Negative numbers: −1, −3, −7

On number line: ...−7...−3...−1...0...2...5...8...

Q2. Is 2 > −3? Why? Is −2 < 3? Why?

Solution:

Yes, 2 > −3 because on the number line, 2 is to the RIGHT of −3. Numbers to the right are always greater.

Yes, −2 < 3 because 3 is to the right of −2 on the number line.

Q3. Evaluate: (i) −5 + 0 (ii) 7 + (−7) (iii) −10 + 20 (iv) 10 − 20 (v) 7 − (−7) (vi) −8 − (−10)

Solution:

(i) −5 + 0 = −5 (adding 0 changes nothing)

(ii) 7 + (−7) = 0 (a number + its inverse = 0)

(iii) −10 + 20 = 10

(iv) 10 − 20 = −10

(v) 7 − (−7) = 7 + 7 = 14

(vi) −8 − (−10) = −8 + 10 = 2

Q4. Use the number line to evaluate:

(a) −125 + (−30) (b) +105 − (−55) (c) +80 − (−150) (d) −99 − (−200)

Solution:

(a) −125 + (−30) = −125 − 30 = −155

(b) +105 − (−55) = 105 + 55 = 160

(c) +80 − (−150) = 80 + 150 = 230

(d) −99 − (−200) = −99 + 200 = 101

Q5 (Token method). Evaluate using tokens:

(a) (+6) + (+4) (b) (−3) + (−2) (c) (+5) + (−7) (d) (−2) + (+6)

Solution:

(a) 6 positive + 4 positive = +10

(b) 3 negative + 2 negative = −5

(c) 5 positive + 7 negative: cancel 5 pairs, left with 2 negative = −2

(d) 2 negative + 6 positive: cancel 2 pairs, left with 4 positive = +4

Q6. Evaluate differences using tokens:

(a) (+10) − (+7) (b) (−8) − (−4) (c) (−9) − (−4) (d) (+9) − (+12) (e) (−5) − (−7) (f) (−2) − (−6)

Solution:

(a) (+10) − (+7) = +3

(b) (−8) − (−4) = −8 + 4 = −4

(c) (−9) − (−4) = −9 + 4 = −5

(d) (+9) − (+12) = −3

(e) (−5) − (−7) = −5 + 7 = +2

(f) (−2) − (−6) = −2 + 6 = +4

Q7. Complete the subtractions:

(a) (−5) − (−7) (b) (+10) − (+13) (c) (−7) − (−9) (d) (+3) − (+8) (e) (−2) − (−7) (f) (+3) − (+15)

Solution:

(a) (−5) − (−7) = −5 + 7 = +2

(b) (+10) − (+13) = −3

(c) (−7) − (−9) = −7 + 9 = +2

(d) (+3) − (+8) = −5

(e) (−2) − (−7) = −2 + 7 = +5

(f) (+3) − (+15) = −12

Q8. Try to subtract: −3 − (+5). How many zero pairs needed? Result?

Solution:

−3 − (+5) = −3 − 5 = −8

Using tokens: Start with 3 negative tokens. To subtract 5 positive tokens (which we don’t have), add 5 zero pairs first. Then remove 5 positive tokens. Left with 3 + 5 = 8 negative tokens.

5 zero pairs needed.

Figure it Out — Section 10.3 (Bank Balance & Credits/Debits)

Q1. Start with ₹0. Credits: ₹30, ₹40, ₹50. Debits: ₹40, ₹50, ₹60. What is your balance?

Solution:

Balance = 0 + 30 + 40 + 50 − 40 − 50 − 60

= 120 − 150 = −₹30 (overdrawn!)

Q2. Start with ₹0. Debits of ₹1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128. Then a single credit of ₹256. What is your balance?

Solution:

Total debits = 1+2+4+8+16+32+64+128 = 255

Balance = −255 + 256 = ₹1

💡 Notice: 1+2+4+8+...+128 = 2&sup8; − 1 = 255. So 256 − 255 = 1!

Figure it Out — Section 10.3 (Geographical Heights)

Q1. From a geographical cross-section, fill in approximate heights:

A: Mountain peak, B: Below sea level, C: Small hill, D: Deep ocean, E: Hill, F: Shallow depth, G: Near sea level

Solution:

A ≈ +1500 m, B ≈ −500 m, C ≈ +300 m, D ≈ −1200 m

E ≈ +1200 m, F ≈ −200 m, G ≈ +100 m

Q2. Which is highest point? Lowest point? Write points in decreasing order of height.

Solution:

Highest: A (+1500 m), Lowest: D (−1200 m)

Decreasing order: A, E, C, G, F, B, D

Increasing order: D, B, F, G, C, E, A

Q3. What is the highest point above sea level on Earth? What is the lowest point on the ocean floor?

Solution:

Highest: Mount Everest, +8848 m above sea level

Lowest: Challenger Deep (Mariana Trench), ≈ −10,994 m below sea level

Figure it Out — Section 10.3 (Temperature)

Q. Match temperatures with appropriate times for Leh in November:

Temperatures: 14°C, 8°C, −2°C, −4°C
Times: 02:00 a.m., 11:00 a.m., 02:00 p.m., 11:00 p.m.

Solution:

  • 14°C → 02:00 p.m. (afternoon, warmest)
  • 8°C → 11:00 a.m. (late morning, warming up)
  • −2°C → 11:00 p.m. (night, cold)
  • −4°C → 02:00 a.m. (early morning, coldest)

Figure it Out — Section 10.4 (Border Sum Puzzles)

Q1. Complete the 3×3 grids to make the required border sums:

Grid A (border sum = +4), Grid B (border sum = −2), Grid C (border sum = −4)

Solution:

Grid A (border sum +4):

−10104
5 −5
9−105

Check: Top row: −10+10+4 = 4 ✔, Bottom: 9+(−10)+5 = 4 ✔

Grid B (border sum −2):

68−16
11 −5
−19−219

Figure it Out — Section 10.4 (Advanced Integer Problems)

Q1. Write all integers between the given pairs, in increasing order:

(a) 0 and −7   (b) −4 and 4   (c) −8 and −15   (d) −30 and −23

Solution:

(a) −6, −5, −4, −3, −2, −1

(b) −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3

(c) −14, −13, −12, −11, −10, −9

(d) −29, −28, −27, −26, −25, −24

Q2. Give three numbers such that their sum is −8.

Solution:

One combination: −5, 7, −10 (check: −5 + 7 + (−10) = −8 ✔)

Other possibilities: −3, −3, −2 or −10, 0, 2 or −1, −3, −4

Q3. Two dice with faces: −1, 2, −3, 4, −5, 6. Smallest sum = −10 ((−5)+(−5)). Largest = 12 (6+6). Find numbers between −10 and +12 that are NOT possible.

Solution:

Possible sums from rolling two such dice (each face: −1, 2, −3, 4, −5, 6):

All possible: −10, −8, −6, −4, −3, −2, −1, 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12

Numbers NOT possible: −9, −7, −5, 0, 2, 7, 9, 11

Q5. Find the years:

(a) From present year (2025), which year was 150 years ago?
(b) Which year was 2200 years ago? (Note: there was no year 0)
(c) What year is 320 years after 680 BCE?

Solution:

(a) 2025 − 150 = 1875 CE

(b) 2025 − 2200 = −175, but no year 0, so it’s 176 BCE

(c) 680 BCE + 320 years = 360 BCE (still BCE since 320 < 680)

Q6. Complete the sequences:

(a) (−40), (−34), (−28), (−22), ___, ___, ___
(b) 3, 4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 0, 7, ___, ___, ___
(c) ___, ___, 12, 6, 1, (−3), (−6), ___, ___, ___

Solution:

(a) Pattern: +6 each time. Next: −22+6 = −16, −10, −4

(b) Two sequences interleaved: 3,2,1,0,−1... and 4,5,6,7,8... Next: −1, 8, −2

(c) Differences: −6, −5, −4, −3, −3... Going back: 12+5=17, 17+6=23... Before: 27, 19, 12, 6, 1, −3, −6, −8, −9, −9

Q8. What is the result of:

(a) (positive) − (negative)   (b) (positive) + (negative)   (c) (negative) + (negative)
(d) (negative) − (negative)   (e) (negative) − (positive)   (f) (negative) + (positive)

Solution:

(a) (positive) − (negative) = positive + positive = Always positive

(b) (positive) + (negative) = Can be positive or negative (depends on which is bigger)

(c) (negative) + (negative) = Always negative

(d) (negative) − (negative) = negative + positive = Can be positive or negative

(e) (negative) − (positive) = negative + negative = Always negative

(f) (negative) + (positive) = Can be positive or negative

💭 Strategy Tip

For integer problems: (1) Draw a number line mentally, (2) "Adding negative" = moving LEFT, (3) "Subtracting negative" = moving RIGHT (double negative = positive!), (4) To compare: number further RIGHT on number line is always bigger.

🧠 Self-Check Quiz

  1. What is (−3) + (+7)?
  2. Which is bigger: −10 or −2?
  3. What is the inverse of −8?
  4. Evaluate: (+5) + (−5) = ?
  5. Temperature falls from 3°C by 7 degrees. New temperature?
  1. +4 → different signs, 7−3=4, keep bigger's sign (+)
  2. −2 → further right on number line
  3. +8 → flip the sign
  4. 0 → a number + its inverse = 0
  5. −4°C → 3 + (−7) = −4
Mastery Practice — Chapter 10Extra worked examples on integer arithmetic and real-life integer problems

Worked Example Set A — Comparing and ordering

  1. Q1. Arrange in ascending order: 4, −7, 0, −3, 5, −1.
    Solution. −7, −3, −1, 0, 4, 5.
  2. Q2. Arrange in descending order: −5, 2, −8, 0, 3, −1.
    Solution. 3, 2, 0, −1, −5, −8.
  3. Q3. Which is greater: −15 or −25?
    Solution. −15 (closer to zero).
  4. Q4. Insert the right sign (>, <, =): −3 ___ −7.
    Solution. > (−3 is to the right of −7).
  5. Q5. Five integers between −3 and 2 are ____.
    Solution. −2, −1, 0, 1 — only 4 integers exist strictly between them.

Worked Example Set B — Addition and subtraction

  1. Q1. (+9) + (−15) = ?
    Solution. Different signs: 15 − 9 = 6; bigger absolute is negative, so answer = −6.
  2. Q2. (−12) + (−8) = ?
    Solution. Same sign: 12 + 8 = 20; both negative ⇒ −20.
  3. Q3. (+13) − (−7) = ?
    Solution. = 13 + 7 = 20.
  4. Q4. (−10) − (−6) = ?
    Solution. = −10 + 6 = −4.
  5. Q5. (−7) − (+8) = ?
    Solution. = −7 + (−8) = −15.
  6. Q6. Evaluate (−3) + (+5) + (−8) + (+2).
    Solution. Positives: 5+2 = 7. Negatives: −3 − 8 = −11. Total: 7 + (−11) = −4.
  7. Q7. Find x: x + (−5) = −12.
    Solution. x = −12 + 5 = −7.
  8. Q8. Find x: (−9) − x = −3.
    Solution. x = −9 − (−3) = −9 + 3 = −6.

Worked Example Set C — Real-life integer problems

  1. Q1. The temperature in Shimla at 6 a.m. was −4°C. By noon it rose by 9°C. What is the noon temperature?
    Solution. −4 + 9 = +5°C.
  2. Q2. A submarine is at −120 m below sea level. It rises 45 m. What is its new position?
    Solution. −120 + 45 = −75 m (still below sea level).
  3. Q3. Ramesh’s bank balance was −₹500. He deposits ₹1200. New balance?
    Solution. −500 + 1200 = +₹700.
  4. Q4. A lift was at floor −3 (3rd basement). It goes up 7 floors. Where is it now?
    Solution. −3 + 7 = +4 (4th floor).
  5. Q5. In a quiz, +4 marks for correct and −2 for wrong. A student got 8 correct and 5 wrong. Total marks?
    Solution. 8 × 4 + 5 × (−2) = 32 − 10 = +22.
  6. Q6. The freezing point of water is 0°C and the boiling point is 100°C. What is the difference in temperature?
    Solution. 100 − 0 = 100°C.
  7. Q7. Mt. Everest is at +8848 m and Dead Sea shore is at −430 m. Difference in elevation?
    Solution. 8848 − (−430) = 9278 m.
  8. Q8. A trader gains ₹500 on Monday, loses ₹200 on Tuesday and gains ₹300 on Wednesday. Net result?
    Solution. +500 − 200 + 300 = +₹600 gain.

📝 Mini-quiz — Chapter 10

  1. The additive inverse of −13 is ____.
  2. (−6) + (+11) = ____.
  3. (+8) − (−5) = ____.
  4. Which is greater: 0 or −1? ____.
  5. The integer just to the left of 0 is ____.
  6. (−3) + (−3) + (−3) = ____.
Answers:
  1. +13
  2. +5
  3. +13
  4. 0
  5. −1
  6. −9

🎯 Chapter 10 — Key takeaways

  • Integers = …−3, −2, −1, 0, +1, +2, +3…
  • Negative numbers describe quantities below a reference (zero).
  • On the number line, right means greater, left means smaller.
  • Adding a negative = moving left; subtracting a negative = moving right.
  • a + (−a) = 0 always (additive inverse).
  • Real applications: temperature, sea level, money, lifts, games, history.

⭐ Class 6 — All Maths Formulas at One Place

📐 Chapter 1: Patterns

  • nth Triangular number = n(n+1)/2
  • nth Square number = n²
  • nth Cube number = n³
  • Sum of first n odd numbers = n²
  • Sum of first n even numbers = n(n+1)

📐 Chapter 2: Lines & Angles

  • Clock angle per hour = 30°
  • Acute < 90° < Obtuse < 180° (Straight) < Reflex < 360°
  • Angles on a straight line = 180°
  • Angles around a point = 360°

📐 Chapter 3: Number Play

  • Divisibility by 2: last digit even
  • Divisibility by 3: digit sum ÷ 3
  • Divisibility by 4: last 2 digits ÷ 4
  • Divisibility by 5: ends in 0 or 5
  • Divisibility by 6: ÷2 AND ÷3
  • Divisibility by 8: last 3 digits ÷ 8
  • Divisibility by 9: digit sum ÷ 9
  • Divisibility by 11: alternate digit-sum difference = 0 or ÷11
  • 2-digit − reverse = 9×(tens digit − units digit)

📐 Chapter 5: Prime Time

  • HCF = common primes × lowest powers
  • LCM = all primes × highest powers
  • HCF × LCM = Product of two numbers
  • Number of factors of p^a × q^b = (a+1)(b+1)
  • Co-prime: HCF = 1

📐 Chapter 6: Perimeter & Area

  • Rectangle perimeter = 2(l + w)
  • Rectangle area = l × w
  • Square perimeter = 4s
  • Square area = s²
  • Triangle area = ½ × base × height
  • Number of tiles = Total area ÷ Tile area
  • Side of square = √(Area) = Perimeter ÷ 4

📐 Chapter 7: Fractions

  • Mixed → Improper: (whole × den + num) / den
  • Improper → Mixed: divide (quotient, remainder)
  • Equivalent: multiply/divide both by same number
  • Simplify: divide both by HCF
  • Compare: cross-multiply (a/b vs c/d → a×d vs b×c)
  • Add/Subtract (diff den): LCM → convert → operate

📐 Chapter 8: Constructions

  • Diameter = 2 × Radius
  • 60° = radius stepped once on arc
  • 120° = radius stepped twice
  • Triangle inequality: a + b > c

📐 Chapter 9: Symmetry

  • Regular n-gon: n lines of symmetry
  • Rotational order of regular n-gon = n
  • Angle of rotation = 360° ÷ order

📐 Chapter 10: Integers

  • (+) + (+) = add, positive
  • (−) + (−) = add, negative
  • Different signs: subtract, sign of larger absolute value
  • a − b = a + (−b)
  • a − (−b) = a + b
  • Additive inverse of a = −a

📋 Chapter 1 — The Wonderful World of Science

📖 Topics Covered

  • 1.1 What is Science?
  • 1.2 Science is Everywhere
  • 1.3 What Will We Explore This Year?
  • 1.4 The Scientific Method
  • 1.5 Examples of Scientific Method in Daily Life
  • 1.6 Curiosity — The Heart of Science

✍️ Activities in This Chapter

  • Activity 1.1: Think of a problem you solved
  • Activity 1.2: Daily life scientific method
  • Activity 1.3: Ask "Why?" about something

Chapter 1: The Wonderful World of Science

🔬 1.1 — What is Science?

As human beings, we have always been curious about our surroundings. Right from childhood, we start exploring and asking questions about everything around us. This curiosity is the foundation of Science.

📝 Definition

Science is a way of thinking, observing and doing things to understand the world we live in and to uncover the secrets of the universe.

Think of science as a big adventure — we ask questions, explore the world, and try to understand how things work. For this, the most important thing is to have 'Curiosity', which is where the title of this textbook ("Curiosity") comes from.

Science is like a giant jigsaw puzzle. Every new discovery we make adds another piece to that puzzle. And the best part? There is no limit to what we can discover — every new piece of knowledge leads to more questions and more things to find out!

Sometimes, we find that a piece of this puzzle has been put in the wrong place and needs to be moved. New discoveries often change our understanding of the world. This is what makes science exciting — it keeps evolving!

🌍 1.2 — Science is Everywhere!

The most wonderful thing about science is that it is everywhere. From the depths of the ocean to the vastness of outer space, from what is cooking in the kitchen to what is happening on the playground — science is present in all aspects of our life.

Some of the most groundbreaking discoveries have come from unexpected places. A scientist named Newton discovered gravity just by watching an apple fall from a tree!

🤔 Think About This

  • Have you ever looked up at the night sky and wondered why the stars shine?
  • Have you watched a flower bloom and wondered how it knows when to open?
  • Have you seen a caterpillar transform into a beautiful butterfly and wondered how?

These are just a few of the many mysteries that science helps us unravel!

📚 1.3 — What Will We Explore This Year?

As you go through this book, you will encounter interesting ideas, do thought-provoking experiments, and see how what we discover is useful in our daily lives. And as we discover more, we start realising that all these ideas are connected.

Here are some questions that we will answer as we go through this book:

TopicQuestions We Will Answer
🌱 Living WorldHow do plants and animals survive in different regions? How do they grow?
🍽️ FoodWhat are different cuisines made of? What do we need to eat to stay healthy?
🧱 MaterialsWhat are things made of? How do we separate different materials?
🧲 MagnetsHow does the magnet keep the box closed? What can magnets attract?
💧 WaterWhy does water freeze into ice and boil into steam? Why does it rain?
🌡️ TemperatureHow do we understand hot and cold? How do we measure temperature?
📏 MotionHow do things move? How do we measure length and distance?
🌌 SpaceWhat is the Sun? Why do stars shine? What are planets?

🧪 1.4 — The Scientific Method (How Science Works)

Even though you may not have realised it, you have already been finding answers to many questions. Science is not just about memorising facts — it is about following a step-by-step process that helps us find answers.

📖 Story: The Pen That Stopped Writing

Suppose your pen stops writing. What would you do?

  1. You would ask: "Why did my pen stop writing?"
  2. You might guess: "Maybe the ink finished"
  3. You would test this by opening the pen and checking the refill
  4. If the refill is empty → your guess was correct! ✓
  5. But what if the ink was NOT finished? You would make another guess — "maybe the ink has dried up" — and test again!

This is exactly how science works! This process is called the Scientific Method.

The Steps Explained:

StepWhat You DoExample (Pen)
1. ObserveNotice something interesting or problematicMy pen stopped writing
2. QuestionAsk "why?" or "how?"Why did it stop?
3. Guess (Hypothesis)Think of a possible answerMaybe the ink is finished
4. Test (Experiment)Do something to check your guessOpen the pen, check the refill
5. Conclude (Analyse)Was your guess correct?Refill is empty → Yes, ink finished!

🚲 1.5 — More Examples of Scientific Method in Daily Life

We all use the scientific method in daily life, even without realising it! Scientists are people who follow this method professionally, but anyone who follows these steps is working like a scientist.

💡 Key Insight from the Textbook

"When we try to ask questions and find out answers, we are all, in a way, scientists!"

— Curiosity, NCERT Grade 6

🌟 1.6 — Curiosity: The Heart of Science

To learn science well, the first and foremost thing is to be curious and observe your surroundings keenly. When we are curious, we start posing questions, asking "How?" and "Why?"

Important Points to Remember:

  • Science is not just memorising — it's about thinking, questioning, and discovering
  • The scientific method can be used by anyone, anywhere, anytime
  • Science is a team effort — scientists work together and share discoveries
  • You won't find answers to all questions in one year — it's a journey of many years
  • The world is full of things waiting to be explored — just keep asking "Why?"

📝 Activities from This Chapter

Activity 1.1: A Problem You Solved

"Write about a similar problem that you tried to solve. What steps did you take?"

Sample Answer:

One day, my bicycle chain came off while riding.

  1. Observe: The bicycle suddenly stopped pedalling smoothly. The chain was hanging loose.
  2. Question: Why did the chain come off?
  3. Guess: Maybe the chain is too loose, or I shifted gears too fast.
  4. Test: I put the chain back on the gear teeth and pedalled slowly. It stayed on.
  5. Conclusion: I shifted gears too fast. Next time, I'll change gears slowly.

Activity 1.2: Scientific Method in Daily Life

"Describe a daily life situation where someone was following a scientific method."

Sample Answer:

A farmer notices his crops are not growing well.

  1. Observe: Crops look yellow and weak compared to neighbour's field.
  2. Question: Why are my crops not growing well?
  3. Guess: Maybe the soil doesn't have enough nutrients (fertiliser).
  4. Test: Add fertiliser to a small section and compare with unfertilised section after 2 weeks.
  5. Conclusion: The fertilised section grew better → the soil needed more nutrients.

Activity 1.3: Ask "Why?"

"If you have to ask 'Why?' about something, what would you ask about?"

Sample Questions (you can pick any!):

  • "Why is the sky blue?" → Because sunlight scatters through the atmosphere, and blue light scatters the most.
  • "Why do we yawn?" → Our body needs more oxygen when we're tired, or it might be a way to cool our brain.
  • "Why do birds fly in V-formation?" → It saves energy — each bird gets a lift from the bird in front.

Questions & Answers

Q1. What is Science?

Answer:

Science is a way of thinking, observing, and doing things to understand the world we live in and to uncover the secrets of the universe. It involves asking questions, exploring the world through observation and experiments, and trying to understand how things work. The most important quality for doing science is curiosity.

Q2. What is meant by the "Scientific Method"? Explain with an example.

Answer:

The Scientific Method is a step-by-step process used to find answers to questions. The steps are:

  1. Observation: Notice something interesting or puzzling
  2. Questioning: Ask "Why?" or "How?" about what you observed
  3. Hypothesis: Make an educated guess (possible answer)
  4. Experiment: Test your guess through experiments or observations
  5. Conclusion: Analyse results to check if your guess was correct

Example: A bicycle repair person finding a puncture —

  • Observes the tyre is flat
  • Questions: "Where is the air leaking from?"
  • Guesses: "Maybe there's a nail stuck"
  • Tests: Inflates the tube, puts it in water, looks for air bubbles
  • Concludes: Bubbles show the exact puncture spot → patches it!

Q3. "Science is everywhere." Explain this statement with examples.

Answer:

Science is present in every part of our life:

  • In the kitchen: Why does dal boil over? Why does milk curdle when we add lemon? (Chemistry)
  • In nature: How do seeds grow into trees? Why do birds migrate? (Biology)
  • In the sky: Why does it rain? Why do stars twinkle? (Physics/Astronomy)
  • At home: How does a fan rotate? Why do clothes dry in sunlight? (Physics)
  • In our body: Why do we get fever? How do wounds heal? (Biology)
  • On the road: Why does a tyre go flat? How does a car engine work? (Engineering)

From the depths of the ocean to outer space, from cooking to playing — science is indeed everywhere!

Q4. Why is curiosity important in science?

Answer:

Curiosity is the starting point and the heart of all science. It is important because:

  • When we are curious, we observe our surroundings keenly
  • It makes us ask questions — "Why?" and "How?"
  • These questions lead to investigation and experiments
  • Without curiosity, we would never question or explore anything
  • All great scientific discoveries started with someone being curious about something ordinary

As the textbook says: "To be a wise person, you must be a 'whys' person!"

Q5. "Anyone who follows the scientific method is working like a scientist." Do you agree? Give reasons.

Answer:

Yes, I completely agree. A scientist is simply someone who uses the scientific method to solve problems. This means:

  • A cook who figures out why dal spilled (too much water?) is working like a scientist
  • A bicycle mechanic finding a puncture by putting the tube in water is working like a scientist
  • An electrician testing whether the bulb or switch is faulty is working like a scientist
  • A student figuring out why their pen stopped writing is working like a scientist

Science is not limited to laboratories or people in white coats — it is a way of thinking that anyone can use in everyday life.

Q6. Why does the textbook compare science to a "jigsaw puzzle"?

Answer:

The textbook compares science to a jigsaw puzzle because:

  • Every new discovery adds another piece to the puzzle
  • There is no limit — the puzzle keeps growing as we discover more
  • Every new piece leads to more questions and more things to find out
  • Sometimes, pieces are in the wrong place — new discoveries often change our earlier understanding
  • Like a puzzle, all pieces of science are connected to each other

This makes science exciting — it is never complete and always evolving!

Q7. What are the important qualities needed to be good at science?

Answer:

The important qualities needed to be good at science are:

  1. Curiosity: Always wondering "why?" and "how?"
  2. Keen observation: Noticing things carefully
  3. Asking questions: Not being afraid to ask
  4. Patience: Science takes time; not all answers come immediately
  5. Teamwork: Working with others to find answers (science is rarely done alone)
  6. Open mind: Being ready to accept that your guess might be wrong
  7. Honesty: Recording observations truthfully, even if they don't match your guess

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Science = a way of thinking, observing, and doing things to understand the world
  • Science is EVERYWHERE — kitchen, playground, nature, space, our body
  • The Scientific Method has 5 steps: Observe → Question → Guess → Test → Conclude
  • Everyone who follows the scientific method is working like a scientist
  • CURIOSITY is the most important quality — "to be wise, be a 'whys' person!"
  • Science is like a jigsaw puzzle — every discovery adds a piece, and it never ends
  • Science is a team effort — share, discuss, and discover together!
🔬 In-Depth Study — Chapter 1Every topic explained in detail with examples, history, IKS, activities and worked problems

📚 §1.1 — What is Science? (Deep Dive)

You have read that science is a way of thinking. Let us go much deeper. The word "science" comes from the Latin word scientia, which simply means "knowledge". But science is not any kind of knowledge — it is a very special, careful, tested kind of knowledge that we build by following certain rules.

🌱 Three Pillars of Science

Whenever we do science, three things always work together:

reproducibility.

🇮🇳 Science in Ancient India — A Long Tradition

Science is not new to India. Our country has had scientists, mathematicians, astronomers and doctors for thousands of years. Here are a few examples:

Ancient Indian ScientistPeriodWhat They Did
Maharshi Kanad~6th century BCEProposed that all matter is made of tiny indivisible particles called "aṇu" (atoms) — long before modern atomic theory.
Sushruta~6th century BCE"Father of Indian surgery." Wrote Sushruta Samhita describing over 1,100 diseases and 300 surgical procedures, including rhinoplasty (nose surgery).
Charaka~3rd century BCEAuthor of Charaka Samhita, an early text of Ayurveda, dealing with diagnosis, nutrition, and treatment.
Āryabhaṭa476–550 CEBrilliant mathematician and astronomer. He showed that the Earth rotates on its axis, calculated π accurately, and explained eclipses scientifically.
Brahmagupta598–668 CEIntroduced rules for using zero and negative numbers — a revolution in mathematics.
Bhāskara II1114–1185 CEWrote Līlāvatī; understood the idea of infinitesimal calculus centuries before Newton and Leibniz.

💡 IKS Connection — The Spirit of Inquiry

In Sanskrit, the spirit of asking questions is captured in the word jijñāsā (जिज्ञासा) — "the desire to know." Ancient teachers said: "Yatra jijñāsā tatra jñānaṁ" — "Wherever there is the desire to know, there is knowledge." This is exactly the modern scientific attitude.

🤔 Common Misconceptions About Science

Common Belief (WRONG ❌)What is True (✅)
Science = only what is in textbooksScience is a way of thinking that anyone can do anywhere.
Scientists always get the right answerScientists are often wrong! They test, fail, retest. Wrong guesses are useful steps to truth.
Science needs a lab and white coatYou can do science with a leaf, a pebble, a thermometer, or just your eyes.
Science kills mystery and beautyScience actually increases wonder — a rainbow becomes even more amazing when you understand light!
Science is fixed and never changesScience is constantly updated as new evidence comes in. That is its biggest strength.

🌟 Story: How Newton Wondered About a Falling Apple

In the year 1666, a young Englishman named Isaac Newton was sitting in his garden in Woolsthorpe when he saw an apple fall from a tree. Instead of just eating the apple, he asked a question children rarely think to ask: "Why does the apple fall straight down? Why not sideways? Why not upwards?"

That single curious question led him to discover the law of gravity — the very same force that keeps planets going around the Sun. So the next time you see an apple fall, remember: a great discovery can begin with one good question.

📝 Activity 1.A — Be a Three-Day Curiosity Scientist

  1. Day 1: Carry a small notebook for the whole day. Whenever you see something you don't fully understand, write it down as a question — "Why …?" or "How …?" Try to collect at least 10 questions.
  2. Day 2: Go through your list. For each question, try to guess the answer (this is your hypothesis). Then discuss with friends or family.
  3. Day 3: Pick the three most interesting questions and try to find the actual answers from books, your teacher or the internet. Note what surprised you.

🌍 §1.2 — Science Is Everywhere (Deep Dive)

Let us now take a tour of one ordinary day to see how science is woven into every moment of your life.

🌅 Morning

  • You wake up because sunlight reaches your eyes and your brain processes the change in light. (Biology & Physics)
  • You brush with toothpaste that contains fluoride — a chemical that protects enamel. (Chemistry)
  • The bristles of your brush are made of nylon — a plastic invented in the 1930s. (Material Science)
  • You see your face in a mirror — a thin layer of silver behind glass reflects light. (Optics)

🍳 Breakfast

  • Your stove burns gas, a combustion reaction giving heat. (Chemistry)
  • Bread toasts brown — sugars react with proteins (Maillard reaction). (Food Chemistry)
  • Milk doesn't curdle because it's fresh — bacteria haven't acted on it yet. (Microbiology)
  • Your tea is hot because heat moved from the gas flame to the water. (Physics — heat transfer)

🚌 Going to School

  • The bus moves because its engine converts fuel energy into motion. (Physics)
  • You feel pushed back when the bus starts — that's inertia! (Newton's first law)
  • You see traffic signals — red light has the longest wavelength and is easiest to see from far away. (Optics)
  • The bus has tyres made of rubber for grip on the road. (Material Science)

📚 In the Classroom

  • Your fan rotates due to an electric motor — electricity flowing through wires makes a magnetic force. (Electromagnetism)
  • The chalk and board work because chalk particles stick to the rough surface. (Material Science)
  • Your pen writes because ink flows by capillary action through the ball. (Fluid Physics)
  • You hear the teacher because sound waves travel through air to your ears. (Acoustics)

🏏 Playing in the Evening

  • The ball bounces because of elastic properties of rubber. (Physics)
  • You sweat to cool down — water on skin evaporates and takes heat away. (Biology + Physics)
  • The Sun sets red because blue light scatters out, leaving red wavelengths. (Optics)

🌙 Night

  • You see stars twinkling because Earth's atmosphere bends starlight unevenly. (Astronomy)
  • The moon is bright not because it shines, but because it reflects the Sun's light. (Astronomy)
  • You sleep because your brain releases a hormone called melatonin. (Biology)

🤔 Big Question

Can you think of even one moment in a day when science is not involved? Discuss with your friends — most of us cannot find any!

🌐 Science Beyond Earth

Science doesn't stop at our planet. India's space agency ISRO has sent missions to the Moon (Chandrayaan-1, 2, 3) and Mars (Mangalyaan). In 2023, Chandrayaan-3 became the first ever to land near the lunar south pole — a great moment for Indian science. Even the satellites that bring you weather forecasts and TV channels are science at work!

📖 §1.3 — What Will We Explore This Year? (Deep Dive)

Your Class 6 science journey is a map. Let us look at this map chapter by chapter and see how each topic connects to the others.

🧭 How are these chapters connected?

Notice that every chapter touches at least two others. For example:

  • Chapter 3 (Food) connects to Chapter 2 (Living things) — plants and animals are our food sources.
