A device similar to this (floating compass) was used by Indian and Chinese sailors hundreds of years ago for navigation at sea. This was before the modern compass was invented!

⬅️ ➡️ 4.4 — Attraction and Repulsion Between Magnets

What happens when we bring two magnets close to each other?

Important Discovery:

  • Repulsion is the sure test for a magnet — attraction alone doesn't confirm something is a magnet (iron bars also get attracted)
  • But if something repels one pole of a known magnet → it MUST be a magnet!

Magnet Through Materials (Activity 4.7)

Magnetic force can pass through non-magnetic materials! Place wood, cardboard, plastic, or glass between a compass needle and a magnet — the needle still deflects! This means magnetic force can act at a distance through these materials.

🎮 4.5 — Fun With Magnets & Taking Care

Fun Activities:

ActivityHow It Works
Magnetic GarlandRing magnets floating on a pencil (like poles repel → they float!)
Maze PuzzleMove steel balls in a maze by moving a magnet below the cardboard
Pick Clip from WaterMove a magnet outside the glass to attract a steel clip inside water — without getting wet!
Magnet CarsTwo matchbox cars with like poles facing each other → they push each other away!

⚠️ How to Take Care of Magnets:

DON'T do thisWhy?
Don't heat a magnetIt loses its magnetism
Don't drop or hammer itIt loses its magnetism
Don't keep near phones/electronicsCan damage electronic devices

✅ How to Store Magnets:

  • Store in pairs with unlike poles on the same side
  • Keep a piece of wood between them
  • Place two pieces of soft iron across the ends

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are magnetic materials? Give five examples.

Answer:

Magnetic materials are materials that are attracted towards a magnet.

Examples: (1) Iron nails, (2) Steel scissors, (3) Nickel coins, (4) Cobalt, (5) Steel paper clips

These all contain iron, nickel, or cobalt, which are the three main magnetic metals.

Q2. What are the poles of a magnet? How can you locate them?

Answer:

The poles of a magnet are the two ends where the magnetic force is strongest. Every magnet has two poles — the North pole (N) and the South pole (S).

To locate them: Spread iron filings on paper and place the magnet on them. The iron filings stick most at the two ends — these are the poles. Very few filings stick in the middle.

Important: A single pole cannot exist. Even if you break a magnet into pieces, each piece will have both N and S poles.

Q3. How does a magnetic compass work? Why does it always point North-South?

Answer:

A magnetic compass has a small needle-shaped magnet that can rotate freely on a pivot inside a dial.

How it works:

  • The compass needle is a freely suspended magnet
  • A freely suspended magnet always comes to rest along the North-South direction
  • The end pointing North is the North pole; the end pointing South is the South pole

Why N-S? Because the Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet. The compass needle aligns with Earth's magnetic field, always pointing towards geographic North and South.

Q4. What happens when two magnets are brought close to each other?

Answer:

Poles Brought TogetherResult
N pole + S pole (unlike)Attract each other (pull together)
S pole + N pole (unlike)Attract each other
N pole + N pole (like)Repel each other (push apart)
S pole + S pole (like)Repel each other

Rule: Unlike poles attract, Like poles repel.

Note: Repulsion is the sure test for a magnet. An iron bar gets attracted to both poles but never repels, so attraction alone doesn't confirm a magnet.

Q5. How would you test whether a piece of metal is a magnet or just an iron bar?

Answer:

There are two reliable methods:

Method 1 (Repulsion test):

  • Bring one end of the metal piece near the North pole of a known magnet
  • If it repels → the metal is a magnet (because only a magnet can repel another magnet)
  • If it attracts → it could be either a magnet or just iron (not conclusive)

Method 2 (Free suspension test):

  • Suspend the metal piece freely with a thread
  • If it always rests in the North-South direction → it is a magnet
  • If it rests in any random direction → it is NOT a magnet

Q6. Fill in the blanks: (i) Unlike poles ___ each other. (ii) Materials attracted by a magnet are ___. (iii) Compass needle rests along ___. (iv) A magnet always has ___ poles.

Answer:

(i) Unlike poles attract each other, whereas like poles repel each other.

(ii) Materials attracted by a magnet are called magnetic materials.

(iii) The compass needle rests along the North-South direction.

(iv) A magnet always has two poles.

Q7. Can magnetic force pass through non-magnetic materials? Explain with an example.

Answer:

Yes! Magnetic force can pass through non-magnetic materials like wood, cardboard, plastic, and glass.

Experiment: Place a piece of cardboard between a compass needle and a bar magnet. The compass needle still deflects (moves) — this proves that the magnetic force passes through the cardboard.

Practical example: You can pick a steel paper clip out of water by moving a magnet on the outside of the glass — the magnetic force passes through the glass!

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Materials attracted by magnets = magnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt, steel)
  • Materials NOT attracted = non-magnetic materials (wood, plastic, glass, rubber)
  • Magnets have two poles (N and S) where attraction is strongest
  • A single pole cannot exist — poles always come in pairs
  • A freely suspended magnet points North-South (because Earth is a giant magnet)
  • A magnetic compass uses this property to find directions
  • Unlike poles attract, like poles repel
  • Repulsion is the sure test for identifying a magnet
  • Magnetic force can act through non-magnetic materials
  • Don't heat, drop, or hammer magnets — they lose magnetism
🔬 In-Depth Study — Chapter 4Every topic of Exploring Magnets explained with history, science, experiments, IKS, and worked examples

📜 §4.0 — A Brief History of Magnets

The story of magnets begins thousands of years ago. Around 600 BCE, a Greek shepherd named Magnes noticed that the iron tip of his stick stuck to certain black rocks on Mount Ida. Those rocks were called magnetite (or lodestone) — natural magnets — and they gave us the word "magnet".

In ancient India, magnetic rocks were known as chumbak. The famous Indian alchemy text Rasaratnasamuchchaya (~13th century) describes magnetic rocks and their medicinal uses. Around 100 BCE the Chinese discovered that a piece of lodestone, when allowed to swing freely, always pointed in the same direction — North–South. This was the birth of the compass.

By the 11th century, Chinese sailors were navigating across the Indian Ocean using magnetic compasses. They traded with India through the spice routes — and the technology spread quickly. Today, magnets are used in everything from your refrigerator door, to MRI scanners in hospitals, to the maglev (magnetic levitation) trains that float on air!

🕯️ The Five Big Discoveries About Magnets

  1. Magnets attract iron and a few other metals (~600 BCE, ancient Greeks).
  2. A freely suspended magnet always points North–South (~100 BCE, ancient China).
  3. Magnetic force acts through some materials (~13th century, William Gilbert).
  4. Earth itself is a giant magnet (1600 CE, William Gilbert in De Magnete).
  5. Electricity and magnetism are linked (1820 CE, Oersted's experiment).

🧲 §4.1 — Magnetic and Non-Magnetic Materials (Deep Dive)

A magnetic material is anything that is attracted by a magnet. A non-magnetic material is not.

📊 Magnetic vs Non-Magnetic — Detailed Examples

Magnetic (attracted) ✅Non-magnetic (not attracted) ❌
Iron nailPlastic ruler
Steel pinGlass marble
Iron filingsWood pencil
Nickel coinAluminium foil
Cobalt stripCopper wire
Old razor blade (steel)Rubber band
Refrigerator door (steel)Paper, cloth
School bell metal (some)Silver chain

The three main magnetic elements are Iron (Fe), Nickel (Ni), and Cobalt (Co) — together called the ferromagnetic trio. Most magnetic materials you see at home contain iron or steel (an alloy mainly of iron and carbon).

🤔 Why are some metals magnetic but not others?

Inside every material there are tiny invisible regions called domains, each behaving like a tiny magnet. In iron, nickel and cobalt, these domains can line up in the same direction when a magnet is brought near, producing a strong attraction. In materials like copper or gold, the domains don't line up easily, so the material is not magnetic.

🌟 Activity 4.A — Magnetic Hunt

  1. Take a small bar magnet and walk around your home.
  2. Touch it lightly to ten different objects.
  3. Make a table: "Object | Made of | Attracted? (Yes/No)".
  4. Notice: every object that gets attracted contains iron or steel.

🌧️ Activity 4.B — Magnet in Beach Sand

Wrap a strong magnet in a thin plastic bag. Drag it through dry sand. Pull it out — you will see tiny black grains stuck to the bag! Those are magnetite particles — natural magnetic dust!

🔴🔵 §4.2 — Poles of a Magnet (Deep Dive)

Every magnet has two ends where the pull is strongest. These ends are called poles. By convention they are named the North pole (N) and the South pole (S).

🤔 What happens if we break a magnet?

You might think: "If I cut the magnet in the middle, I can get just an N pole on one side and just an S pole on the other!" But nature has a surprise — every piece still has BOTH an N and an S pole. No matter how many times you break it, both poles always appear together. This is one of the deepest laws of magnetism: monopoles do not exist.

📏 Types of Magnets (Shapes)

  • Bar magnet — a simple rectangle. Best for experiments.
  • Horseshoe magnet — U-shape; very strong because both poles face the same way.
  • Cylindrical / Rod magnet — pole at each round face.
  • Ring magnet — round with a hole; used in speakers.
  • Disc magnet — small and flat; used in fridge magnets.
  • Needle magnet — long, thin; used in compasses.

🧪 Activity 4.C — Locate the Poles

Spread iron filings (or pins) on a sheet of paper. Carefully put a bar magnet on top. Lift the magnet — see where the most filings cling? Those points are the poles. The middle barely picks up any filings — confirming that magnetic pull is strongest at the ends.

🧭 §4.3 — Finding Directions (Magnetic Compass) (Deep Dive)

A compass is one of the most important inventions in human history. Without it, no one could have safely sailed across the Indian Ocean or the Atlantic.

🛠️ Parts of a Compass

  • Magnetic needle — a thin needle-shaped magnet, free to rotate.
  • Pivot — the sharp pin on which the needle balances.
  • Dial / Card — marked with the four cardinal directions: N, E, S, W.
  • Glass cover — protects the needle.

🧪 Activity 4.D — Build Your Own Compass

  1. Take a steel sewing needle. Rub it 40–50 times in one direction with one pole of a magnet (this is called induction magnetisation).
  2. Push the needle through a small piece of cork or thermocol.
  3. Float the cork in a bowl of water.
  4. The needle slowly turns and stops along the North–South line.
  5. Mark "N" on the cork end that points North.
  6. Congratulations — you have built a working compass!

🌐 Why Does a Compass Point North?

The Earth itself acts like a giant bar magnet. Its magnetic south pole is near the geographic North pole, so the N-pole of every compass needle is attracted to it and points roughly North.

🧭 The Four Cardinal Directions

  • North (N) — direction of the compass needle's North-pointing end.
  • South (S) — exactly opposite to North.
  • East (E) — direction of sunrise.
  • West (W) — direction of sunset.

📜 IKS — Indian Navigators Used Compasses

Indian sailors in Kerala, Tamil Nadu and Gujarat have used magnetic compasses for over a thousand years. The Tamil epic Manimekalai (~6th century CE) mentions sailors using a fish-shaped iron piece floating in oil to find direction at night. That iron fish was an early magnetic compass!

⬅️➡️ §4.4 — Attraction & Repulsion Between Magnets (Deep Dive)

Two magnets brought close to each other can either pull together (attract) or push apart (repel). The result depends on which poles face each other.

📜 The Golden Rule of Magnetism

Like poles repel; unlike poles attract.

  • N — N → repel
  • S — S → repel
  • N — S → attract
  • S — N → attract

🧪 Activity 4.E — Floating Magnets

Take three ring magnets and slide them on a vertical pencil so that the same poles face each other. You will see the magnets float in the air — they repel each other strongly enough to hold their own weight! This is the basic idea of maglev trains.

🧲 Why is Repulsion the "Sure Test" for a Magnet?

Both a magnet and an iron bar can attract a magnet (because iron itself becomes magnetised in the field). But only a magnet can repel another magnet. So if a metal piece repels a known magnet, you can be certain that the metal piece is itself a magnet.

⚡ Magnetic Force Through Materials

Magnetic force can act without contact — even through wood, paper, glass and plastic. This is why a fridge magnet sticks even with a thin sheet of paper between it and the door. But it cannot easily pass through thick iron, which absorbs the field.

🧪 Activity 4.F — Steel Clip in a Glass

  1. Put a small steel paper clip inside a glass of water.
  2. Without touching the water, move a magnet along the outside of the glass.
  3. The clip follows the magnet!
  4. Lift the clip out through the glass wall without getting wet.
  5. The magnetic force passed through glass and water — invisible but real.

🎮 §4.5 — Fun With Magnets & Care (Deep Dive)

🌟 Where Magnets Are Used in Real Life

PlaceHow a Magnet Is Used
Refrigerator doorMagnetic strip in the seal keeps the door tightly closed.
Electric bellElectromagnet pulls a hammer to strike the gong.
Television / SpeakerMagnet in the speaker vibrates a cone to make sound.
Electric motor (fan, mixer)Magnet and coil rotate to give motion.
Hospital MRI machineVery strong magnet helps see inside the body without surgery.
Maglev trainMagnets lift the train so it floats; no friction → very fast.
JunkyardsGiant electromagnets lift heavy iron scraps.
Magnetic locks & cardsHotel room doors, ATM cards, security badges.
Pencil case lidsSmall magnet keeps the lid closed.
Magnetic toys, mazes, fishing gamesFor learning & fun.

⚠️ How a Magnet Can Lose Its Magnetism

  • Strong heating — atoms vibrate too much; domains lose alignment.
  • Strong hammering or dropping — knocks domains out of order.
  • Keeping near strong opposite magnets for a long time.
  • Just leaving it lying around without proper storage.

✅ Correct Way to Store Magnets

  • Always keep two bar magnets in pairs with unlike poles together.
  • Put a small piece of wood between them to keep them in place.
  • Place small pieces of soft iron (called "keepers") across the ends.
  • Keep magnets away from mobile phones, watches, credit cards, computers, hard disks and ATM cards — strong magnets can damage these!

💡 Useful Magnetic Facts to Remember

  • The strongest natural magnet is magnetite (Fe₃O₄).
  • The strongest man-made magnet today is neodymium — small ones can lift more than 100 times their weight!
  • The Earth's magnetic field protects life by deflecting harmful solar radiation.
  • Birds and sea turtles use the Earth's field to navigate during migration.
  • Magnetism and electricity together form electromagnetism — the science that powers our entire modern world.
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 4

Q8. Why are coins not always attracted by magnets?

Answer:

Modern coins in India are made mostly of steel or nickel-coated steel, so they are attracted. Older copper, brass or aluminium coins are not attracted because they don't contain iron, nickel or cobalt. So whether a coin gets attracted depends on what metal it's made from.

Q9. Why does the iron tip of a stick get attracted to a lodestone, but the wooden part doesn't?

Answer:

Iron is one of the three ferromagnetic elements that get attracted by magnets. Wood is made of cellulose fibres (non-magnetic). So only the iron tip feels the magnetic force, exactly the discovery that started the story of magnets!

Q10. If you cut a bar magnet into three equal pieces, how many poles will there be in total?

Answer:

Each small piece will still be a complete magnet with both N and S poles. So 3 pieces × 2 poles = 6 poles in total.

Q11. The Earth behaves like a giant magnet. Where, geographically, are Earth's magnetic poles located?

Answer:

The Earth's magnetic south pole is near the geographic North pole, and its magnetic north pole is near the geographic South pole. This is why the N-pole of a compass needle points towards the geographic North — it is being attracted by Earth's magnetic south pole!

Q12. Why don't we use a magnet to find directions inside a moving car or train?

Answer:

Cars and trains contain large amounts of iron and steel, plus electronic devices that produce their own magnetic fields. These confuse the compass needle. We can use a compass while standing still in an open area away from metal objects.

Q13. Why are MRI scans considered very safe?

Answer:

MRI uses a very strong but harmless magnetic field along with radio waves to see inside the body. Unlike X-rays, it uses no radiation. The magnet only affects iron-containing molecules in the body (like haemoglobin). However, patients must remove all metal objects before entering an MRI room.

Q14. Give two examples each of magnetic and non-magnetic materials found in your school bag.

Answer:

  • Magnetic: steel scissors, metal pins, geometry box clips.
  • Non-magnetic: plastic ruler, eraser, wooden pencil, glass marble.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 4 (20 Questions)

  1. The three main magnetic elements are ___, ___ and ___. (iron, nickel, cobalt)
  2. A natural magnet is also called a ___. (lodestone)
  3. The mineral that is a natural magnet is ___. (magnetite)
  4. A freely suspended magnet points along the ___ direction. (North–South)
  5. The two ends of a magnet are called ___. (poles)
  6. Like poles ___ each other. (repel)
  7. Unlike poles ___ each other. (attract)
  8. The sure test for a magnet is ___. (repulsion)
  9. A magnet always has ___ poles. (two)
  10. A compass needle is a small ___. (magnet)
  11. The Earth itself behaves like a giant ___. (magnet)
  12. Strong heating makes a magnet lose its ___. (magnetism)
  13. Two bar magnets should be stored with ___ poles together. (unlike)
  14. The strongest man-made magnet uses the element ___. (neodymium)
  15. An MRI machine uses a powerful ___. (magnet)
  16. True or False: Magnetic force can pass through cardboard. (True)
  17. True or False: A horseshoe magnet has only one pole. (False)
  18. True or False: Birds use Earth's magnetic field to navigate. (True)
  19. True or False: Aluminium is a magnetic material. (False)
  20. True or False: The word "magnet" comes from the name of a Greek shepherd. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 4

TermMeaning
MagnetAn object that attracts iron, nickel and cobalt.
Magnetic materialMaterial attracted by a magnet.
Non-magnetic materialMaterial not attracted by a magnet.
PoleEnd of a magnet where pull is strongest.
AttractionPulling together (unlike poles).
RepulsionPushing apart (like poles).
CompassDevice with a magnetic needle to find direction.
LodestoneA natural magnet made of magnetite.
MagnetisationProcess by which a piece of iron becomes a magnet.
KeeperSmall piece of soft iron placed across the ends of bar magnets to preserve magnetism.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 4

  1. Magnet Hunt at Home — Find 10 objects with magnets in them (fridge door, TV speaker, etc.). Make a chart.
  2. Build a Compass — Use a needle, magnet, cork and bowl of water. Compare with a real compass.
  3. Magnetic Maze — Draw a paper maze; place a small iron ball on top; move it through the maze using a magnet underneath.
  4. Field Drawing — Place a bar magnet under a thin paper. Sprinkle iron filings on top and tap gently — the filings form beautiful curved lines showing the magnetic field.
  5. Magnetic Fishing Game — Make paper fish with paper clips attached. Tie a magnet to a string ("fishing rod"). Catch the fish — a fun way to demonstrate magnetic force.

📋 Chapter 5 — Measurement of Length and Motion

📖 Topics Covered

  • 5.1 How Do We Measure?
  • 5.2 Standard Units (SI System)
  • 5.3 Correct Way of Measuring Length
  • 5.4 Measuring a Curved Line
  • 5.5 Describing Position (Reference Point)
  • 5.6 Moving Things (Motion & Rest)
  • 5.7 Types of Motion

✍️ Activities

  • Activity 5.1: Measure objects with a scale
  • Activity 5.2: Identify objects in motion/rest
  • Activity 5.3: Linear motion (dropping an eraser)
  • Activity 5.4: Circular motion (whirling)
  • Activity 5.5: Oscillatory motion (pendulum)

Chapter 5: Measurement of Length and Motion

📖 Chapter Story

Deepa needs a new school uniform. The shopkeeper measures cloth with a metal rod. The tailor uses a flexible tape. Her mother says "increase by char angula (four finger-widths)." This makes Deepa curious — what's the correct way to measure?

📏 5.1 — How Do We Measure?

In ancient times, people measured lengths using body parts:

Body PartNameUsed For
Stretched thumb to little fingerHandspan (balisht)Measuring cloth, small objects
Elbow to fingertipCubit (haath)Measuring rooms, doors
Width of a fingerAngulaFine measurements (tailoring)
Length of a strideStep/PaceMeasuring fields, distances
Length of a footFootMeasuring land

⚠️ The Problem with Body-Part Measurements

Deepa and her friends measured the same table using handspans:

StudentMeasurement
AnishSlightly more than 13 handspans
Padma13 handspans
TasneemSlightly less than 13 handspans
Hardeep14 handspans

Problem: Everyone got different answers because their handspans are different sizes! This shows we need a standard unit that is the same for everyone.

📐 5.2 — Standard Units (SI System)

To avoid confusion, all countries adopted the International System of Units (SI Units).

🏛️ India's Measurement History

India has a rich history of measurement! Ancient units like angula, dhanusa, and yojana are mentioned in texts and used in architecture. Scales with ruled markings have been excavated from Harappan Civilisation sites!

✅ 5.3 — Correct Way of Measuring Length

Choosing the Right Measuring Tool:

What to MeasureBest ToolWhy
Length of pencil15-cm scaleSmall object, rigid
Height of a roomMetre scale / measuring tapeLarger length
Girth of a treeFlexible measuring tapeCurved surface
Distance between citiesKilometre stones / GPSVery large distance

5.4 — Measuring a Curved Line

For curved lines (like a winding road on a map), use a thread:

  1. Place a thread along the curve, matching every turn
  2. Straighten the thread
  3. Measure the straightened thread with a scale

Or use a flexible measuring tape that bends along the curve.

📍 5.5 — Describing Position (Reference Point)

To describe where something is, we need a reference point — a fixed point from which we measure distance.

Key Idea: Position is always described with respect to a reference point. Without a reference point, we cannot describe where something is or whether it's moving.

🚶 5.6 — Motion and Rest

An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point over time.

An object is said to be at rest if its position does NOT change with respect to a reference point over time.

💡 Interesting: Motion is Relative!

Deepa was on a bus. The passengers looked at rest (compared to the bus). But compared to buildings outside, the passengers were in motion! Whether something is "moving" depends on what reference point you choose!

🔄 5.7 — Types of Motion

Important Note: Periodic Motion

If an object repeats its path after a fixed interval of time, its motion is called periodic motion. Both circular and oscillatory motions are periodic in nature (they repeat again and again).

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why is measuring with body parts (handspan, cubit) not reliable? What is the solution?

Answer:

Measuring with body parts is not reliable because:

  • Different people have different-sized body parts (hands, feet, arms)
  • The same object will give different measurements when measured by different people
  • This creates confusion, especially in trade and science

Solution: Use standard units (SI units) like metre, centimetre, etc. These are the same for everyone, everywhere in the world. When all people use the same unit, measurements become consistent and reliable.

Q2. Express the following: (a) 5 km in metres (b) 25 m in cm (c) 150 cm in m

Answer:

(a) 5 km = 5 × 1000 = 5000 m

(b) 25 m = 25 × 100 = 2500 cm

(c) 150 cm = 150 ÷ 100 = 1.5 m

Remember: km → m: multiply by 1000 | m → cm: multiply by 100 | cm → mm: multiply by 10

Q3. What is a reference point? Why is it important for describing motion?

Answer:

A reference point is a fixed point (or object) from which we measure distances and positions.

It is important because:

  • Without a reference point, we cannot describe position — "5 km" means nothing unless we say "5 km from WHERE"
  • We cannot say if an object is in motion or at rest without a reference point
  • The same object can be at rest (with one reference) and in motion (with another reference)

Example: A passenger in a bus is at rest (with respect to the bus) but in motion (with respect to trees outside).

Q4. What are the three types of motion? Give two examples of each.

Answer:

TypeDefinitionExamples
Linear MotionObject moves along a straight line(1) Falling ball (2) Car on straight highway
Circular MotionObject moves along a circular path(1) Hands of a clock (2) Earth revolving around the Sun
Oscillatory MotionObject moves to and fro about a fixed position(1) Swing (jhula) (2) Pendulum of a wall clock

Note: Circular and oscillatory motions are both periodic (they repeat after a fixed interval).

Q5. Classify as Linear/Circular/Oscillatory: (a) Ceiling fan (b) Soldier marching (c) Swing (d) Hands of clock (e) Stone dropped from height

Answer:

(a) Ceiling fan — Circular motion (blades move in circles)

(b) Soldier marching — Linear motion (moves in a straight line)

(c) Swing — Oscillatory motion (moves to and fro)

(d) Hands of clock — Circular motion (rotate around center)

(e) Stone dropped — Linear motion (falls in a straight line)

Q6. How can you measure the length of a curved line?

Answer:

A curved line cannot be measured directly with a straight ruler. Instead:

Method 1 (Thread method):

  1. Place a thread carefully along the curve, following every bend
  2. Mark the starting and ending points on the thread
  3. Straighten the thread on a table
  4. Measure the length between the marks using a scale

Method 2: Use a flexible measuring tape that can bend along the curve (like a tailor's tape).

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Body parts are unreliable for measurement → we need standard units
  • SI unit of length = metre (m)
  • 1 km = 1000 m | 1 m = 100 cm | 1 cm = 10 mm
  • Place scale flat on object, eye directly above the reading
  • Curved lines: use a thread or flexible tape
  • Reference point = fixed point for measuring distance/position
  • Object is in motion if position changes w.r.t. reference point
  • Object is at rest if position doesn't change w.r.t. reference point
  • Motion is relative — depends on the reference point chosen
  • Linear = straight line | Circular = circle | Oscillatory = to & fro
  • Circular + Oscillatory = periodic motion (repeating)
🔬 In-Depth Study — Chapter 5Each topic of Measurement & Motion explained with history, examples, activities and worked problems

📜 §5.0 — A Brief History of Measurement

Humans have measured things for thousands of years — for trade, construction, farming, navigation and astronomy. The first measurements were made using body parts — the length of a finger, the span of a hand, the length of a foot, the length of a forearm (cubit).

Old unitOriginAbout how long?
AngulaWidth of a finger (Indian)≈ 2 cm
HastaLength of the hand (Indian)≈ 24 angulas ≈ 45 cm
CubitElbow to fingertip (Egyptian)≈ 46 cm
FootLength of a foot≈ 30 cm
PaceOne step≈ 75 cm
YardNose to fingertip of King Henry I≈ 90 cm

The problem? Every person's body is different! A cloth measured as "6 hand-spans" by a tall shopkeeper is much longer than 6 hand-spans of a small child. This caused unfairness and confusion in trade.

🌐 The Rise of Standard Units — The SI System

In 1790, after the French Revolution, scientists in France defined the metre as one ten-millionth of the distance from the equator to the North Pole. Later it was redefined using a metal bar, then a light wavelength, and today the metre is defined using the speed of light.

This system spread worldwide and became the SI System (Système International d'Unités). It is now used in almost every country, including India. The big advantage: everywhere on Earth, "1 metre" means exactly the same length.

🇮🇳 Standardisation in Ancient India

India had remarkably standard measurements thousands of years ago. The Harappan civilisation (~2500 BCE) used carefully cut cubical stone weights in a ratio of 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 — a decimal system! Their bricks were always in the same length-width-thickness ratio (4:2:1), which made them strong and easy to build with.

📏 §5.1 — How Do We Measure? (Deep Dive)

To measure a quantity means to compare it with a known fixed unit. Measurement has three essential parts:

  1. The quantity being measured (e.g. length of a pencil)
  2. The unit being used (e.g. centimetre)
  3. The number indicating how many units (e.g. 12)

So if we say "The pencil is 12 cm long", 12 is the number, cm is the unit, and length is the quantity. A measurement is meaningless without its unit!

📊 Quantities You Will Measure in Class 6

QuantitySI UnitCommon Tool
Length / Distancemetre (m)Ruler, measuring tape
Masskilogram (kg)Beam balance, weighing scale
Timesecond (s)Stopwatch, wall clock
Temperaturekelvin (K) — common: °CThermometer
Volumelitre (L)Measuring cylinder

🎯 Why Standard Units?

  • Everyone gets the same answer for the same object.
  • Allows fair trade — 1 kg of rice is 1 kg everywhere.
  • Scientists worldwide can share results.
  • Machines and engineering parts fit together perfectly.
  • Modern technology (computers, satellites, medicines) would be impossible without standard units.

📐 §5.2 — Standard Units (SI System) (Deep Dive)

The metre (m) is the SI unit of length. From the metre we get bigger and smaller units by multiplying or dividing by 10.

📊 Conversion Table

FromToMultiply by
kmm× 1000
mcm× 100
cmmm× 10
mmcm÷ 10
cmm÷ 100
mkm÷ 1000

🧮 Worked Examples — Unit Conversion

  1. Convert 4 m to cm: 4 × 100 = 400 cm.
  2. Convert 250 cm to m: 250 ÷ 100 = 2.5 m.
  3. Convert 3.6 km to m: 3.6 × 1000 = 3600 m.
  4. Convert 75 mm to cm: 75 ÷ 10 = 7.5 cm.
  5. Add 1 m + 25 cm + 50 mm: in cm: 100 + 25 + 5 = 130 cm = 1.30 m.

📏 Choosing the Right Unit

What you measureBest unit
Length of an antmillimetres (mm)
Length of a pencilcentimetres (cm)
Length of a classroommetres (m)
Distance between citieskilometres (km)
Thickness of a hairmicrometres (µm)

✅ §5.3 — Correct Way of Measuring (Deep Dive)

Even a good ruler can give wrong readings if used carelessly. Let us see the common errors and how to avoid them.

⚠️ Three Common Errors in Measurement

  1. Parallax error — when the eye is not directly above the reading. The reading changes if the eye moves sideways. Solution: always look straight down at the mark.
  2. Zero error — when the start of the ruler is broken or worn. Solution: begin from any clear whole number (like 1.0 cm) and subtract.
  3. Improper placement — when the ruler is held at an angle or not in contact with the object. Solution: place it flat against the object, exactly along its length.

📐 Step-by-Step — How to Measure a Pencil Correctly

  1. Place the pencil on a flat surface.
  2. Place the 0 mark of the ruler exactly at one end.
  3. Keep the ruler in close contact with the pencil along its length.
  4. Bring your eye directly above the other end of the pencil.
  5. Read the mark on the ruler — that is the length of the pencil.

🎯 Activity 5.A — Group Measurement

Ask 5 friends to measure the same pencil independently. Note their readings. Are they exactly the same? Why might there be small differences? Discuss errors and how to make readings agree.

🌀 §5.4 — Measuring Curved Lines

You cannot lay a stiff ruler along a curved path. Two ways to handle this:

  1. Thread method: place a thread along the curve, mark its ends, then straighten and measure with a ruler.
  2. Flexible tape: use a tailor's measuring tape that bends along the curve.

This is exactly how doctors measure your head circumference, or how tailors measure your waist!

📍🚶 §5.5–5.6 — Reference Points, Motion & Rest (Deep Dive)

"Where" is something? "Is it moving?" Both questions need a reference point — a fixed point we measure from.

🌳 What is a Reference Point?

A reference point is a fixed object or place from which we describe the position of other things. Examples:

  • School is 2 km from the bus stand (bus stand = reference point).
  • The book is on the table, 30 cm from the left edge (left edge = reference point).
  • Delhi is about 300 km from Agra (Agra = reference point).

🚶 Motion and Rest

  • An object is in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point as time passes.
  • An object is at rest if its position does not change with respect to a reference point as time passes.

💡 Motion is Relative!

Imagine you are sitting in a moving train. Compared to your seat, you are at rest. But compared to the trees outside, you are in motion! Whether something is "moving" depends entirely on the chosen reference point. This is one of the most important ideas in physics.

🌍 The Earth is Moving — And So Are We!

Even right now, while you sit and read, you are spinning with the Earth at about 1670 km/h (at the equator) and travelling around the Sun at about 1,07,000 km/h! But because everything around you moves with you, you don't feel it. That's relative motion at the cosmic scale!

🔄 §5.7 — Types of Motion (Deep Dive)

Motion comes in many flavours. Knowing the type helps us understand and predict the behaviour of objects.

📊 Five Important Types of Motion

TypePathExamples
Linear / RectilinearStraight lineCar on a straight road, stone falling vertically, train on a straight track
CircularCircleHands of a clock, blades of a fan, merry-go-round, Earth around the Sun (almost)
RotationalSpinning on an axisSpinning top, Earth rotating on its axis, wheel spinning
OscillatoryTo and froPendulum, swing, vibrating guitar string
RandomIrregular pathButterfly flying, bee in a garden, smoke particles in air

🔁 Periodic Motion — A Special Family

Any motion that repeats at regular intervals is called periodic motion. Examples: pendulum swing, fan blades, Earth's rotation, heart beats. Both circular and oscillatory motions are periodic.

🧪 Activity 5.B — Make a Pendulum

Tie a small stone to a long string and hang it from a hook. Pull the stone slightly to one side and release. It swings back and forth — that's oscillatory motion. Use a stopwatch to count how many complete swings it makes in 60 seconds. Try this with strings of different lengths and notice — longer string → slower swings. This is exactly what Galileo discovered in 1583 watching a chandelier in a church!

🧮 Worked Examples — Position and Motion

  1. Q: A car is at the 5 km mark at 10:00 AM and at the 25 km mark at 10:20 AM. How far did it travel?
    A: Distance = 25 − 5 = 20 km.
  2. Q: A boy moves from his home to school (1 km) and back. What is the total distance covered?
    A: 1 km + 1 km = 2 km.
  3. Q: Why does a flying bird seem to not move when you watch from a fast-moving car going in the same direction?
    A: Because the bird's position relative to the car barely changes — motion is relative.
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 5

Q1. Why is body-part measurement not reliable?

Answer:

Because everyone's body parts (finger width, hand span, foot length) are different sizes. The same object would have different "lengths" depending on who measures it. This causes confusion and unfairness in trade.

Q2. What is the SI unit of length? Why is "SI" important?

Answer:

The SI unit of length is the metre (m). SI (Système International) is important because it gives the world ONE standard system. Scientists, traders, engineers and ordinary people in every country use the same units — making communication and cooperation possible.

Q3. Convert: (a) 5.6 m to cm (b) 3 km to m (c) 4500 mm to m.

Answer:

  1. 5.6 × 100 = 560 cm
  2. 3 × 1000 = 3000 m
  3. 4500 ÷ 1000 = 4.5 m

Q4. What is a parallax error? How is it avoided?

Answer:

A parallax error occurs when the eye is not directly above the scale reading; the apparent reading changes when the eye moves sideways. Avoid it by keeping your eye directly above the mark being read.

Q5. What is a reference point? Why is it needed?

Answer:

A reference point is a fixed point from which we measure or describe the position of an object. Without it, statements like "the school is 2 km away" make no sense — 2 km from what?

Q6. Define motion and rest. Give two examples of each.

Answer:

Motion: object's position changes with time relative to a reference point. Examples — a running car, a flying bird.

Rest: object's position does not change. Examples — a parked car, a building on the ground.

Q7. Explain how motion is "relative".

Answer:

An object can be at rest in one reference frame and in motion in another. A passenger sitting in a moving train is at rest with respect to the seat but in motion with respect to a tree outside. So whether an object is moving or at rest depends on the chosen reference point.

Q8. Name and explain three types of motion with examples.

Answer:

  • Linear: along a straight line (car on highway).
  • Circular: along a circular path (fan blade, Earth around Sun).
  • Oscillatory: to and fro about a fixed point (pendulum, swing).

Q9. Why is the motion of a swing called oscillatory and periodic?

Answer:

A swing moves back and forth about a fixed point — that is oscillatory. The motion repeats itself at regular intervals — that makes it periodic.

Q10. How do you measure the length of a curved leaf?

Answer:

Place a piece of thread along the curve of the leaf, marking the start and end points. Then straighten the thread on a ruler and measure between the marks. Or use a flexible measuring tape.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 5 (20 Questions)

  1. The SI unit of length is ___. (metre)
  2. 1 m = ___ cm. (100)
  3. 1 km = ___ m. (1000)
  4. 1 cm = ___ mm. (10)
  5. Body parts as measurement units are unreliable because they ___. (vary from person to person)
  6. The error caused by improper eye position is ___ error. (parallax)
  7. The SI system was first formally introduced in ___ (country). (France)
  8. A pendulum shows ___ motion. (oscillatory)
  9. The hands of a clock show ___ motion. (circular)
  10. A car moving on a straight road shows ___ motion. (linear)
  11. Earth rotating on its own axis shows ___ motion. (rotational)
  12. To measure a curved line, we use a ___ or a flexible tape. (thread)
  13. A fixed point from which position is measured is a ___ point. (reference)
  14. Motion is ___ — it depends on the reference point chosen. (relative)
  15. Any motion that repeats at regular intervals is called ___. (periodic)
  16. True or False: A ruler with a broken zero cannot be used. (False — start from any whole mark and subtract)
  17. True or False: A passenger sitting in a moving train is in motion with respect to the seat. (False)
  18. True or False: The Earth moves around the Sun. (True)
  19. True or False: 5 mm = 0.5 cm. (True)
  20. True or False: Random motion is also called periodic. (False)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 5

TermMeaning
MeasurementComparing an unknown quantity with a known standard unit.
UnitA standard amount used for measurement.
SIThe international system of standard units.
MetreSI unit of length.
Parallax errorError due to wrong eye position.
Reference pointFixed point from which position is measured.
MotionChange in position with time.
RestNo change in position with time.
Linear motionMotion along a straight line.
Circular motionMotion along a circular path.
Rotational motionMotion of a body spinning about an axis.
Oscillatory motionTo-and-fro motion about a fixed point.
Random motionIrregular, unpredictable motion.
Periodic motionMotion that repeats at regular intervals.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 5

  1. Body-Unit Comparison — Measure a table using your hand-span; ask 3 friends to do the same. Compare. Discuss why standard units are better.
  2. Make a Pendulum — Test how string length affects the time period. Make a graph!
  3. Motion in My Neighborhood — Spot one example of each kind of motion (linear, circular, oscillatory, random) near your home. Take photos or draw.
  4. Conversion Chart — Make a colourful poster showing conversions between km, m, cm and mm with everyday examples.
  5. Curved Measurement — Use a thread to measure the perimeter of 5 leaves of different shapes. Tabulate.

📋 Chapter 6 — Materials Around Us

📖 Topics Covered

  • 6.1 Observing Objects Around Us
  • 6.2 How to Group Materials (Classification)
  • 6.3 Properties of Materials
  • 6.3.1 Appearance (Lustrous/Non-lustrous)
  • 6.3.2 Hardness and Softness
  • 6.3.3 Transparent, Translucent, Opaque
  • 6.3.4 Soluble and Insoluble in Water
  • 6.3.5 Objects that Float or Sink

✍️ Activities

  • Activity 6.1: List objects & their materials
  • Activity 6.2: Group objects by properties
  • Activity 6.4: Ball bounce experiment
  • Activity 6.7: Soluble/Insoluble in water
  • Activity 6.8: Float or Sink experiment

Chapter 6: Materials Around Us

📜 Sanskrit Wisdom

"The materials used to make a crucible are clay and iron." — Rasaratnasamuchchaya (ancient Indian chemistry text)

India's pottery dates back 7000-8000 years! Harappan civilization (2600-1900 BCE) developed wheel-turned pottery with beautiful painted designs.

👀 6.1 — Objects and Materials

Everything around us is made from one or more materials. A material is any substance used to create an object.

ObjectMaterial(s)Why that material?
NotebookPaperEasy to write on, lightweight, foldable
ChairWood / Plastic / MetalStrong, rigid, can bear weight
WindowGlassTransparent (lets light through)
Cooking potMetal (steel/aluminium)Conducts heat, strong, doesn't burn
RaincoatPlastic/PVCWaterproof, flexible
PenPlastic + Metal + InkMultiple materials for different parts!

Key Idea: We choose a material based on its properties and the purpose of the object.

📊 6.2 — Classification of Materials

Classification = arranging objects into groups based on common properties.

Just like a grocery store groups items (spices together, grains together), we can group materials by their properties.

The same object can be made from different materials (e.g., a tumbler can be glass, plastic, metal, or clay). And the same material can make different objects (e.g., plastic → bottle, chair, bag, toy).

🔍 6.3 — Properties of Materials

6.3.1 Lustre — Does It Shine?

Lustrous materials have a shiny surface — usually metals (gold, silver, copper, iron). But metals can lose lustre due to air and moisture — their freshly cut surface is shiny.

Non-lustrous materials don't shine naturally — wood, paper, rubber, cloth.

⚠️ "All that glitters is not gold!" — Some things look shiny due to polishing/coating but aren't metals.

6.3.2 Hardness — Can You Scratch It?

A material is hard if it cannot be easily compressed, scratched, or bent (stone, iron, diamond).

A material is soft if it can be compressed or scratched easily (cotton, sponge, rubber).

Test: If material A can scratch material B, then A is harder than B.

6.3.3 Transparent, Translucent, Opaque

TypeCan You See Through?Examples
TransparentYes — clearly!Clear glass, water, air, cellophane
TranslucentPartly — not clearlyFrosted glass, butter paper, thin cloth
OpaqueNo — not at allWood, metal, cardboard, stone, thick cloth

6.3.4 Soluble and Insoluble in Water

Soluble: Materials that dissolve (disappear) in water — sugar, salt, lemon juice, vinegar

Insoluble: Materials that DON'T dissolve — sand, chalk powder, sawdust, oil, stones

Test: Stir the material in water. If it disappears → soluble. If it settles/floats separately → insoluble.

6.3.5 Float or Sink?

Some objects float on water (wood, cork, plastic bottle) while others sink (stone, iron nail, coin). This depends on the material's density compared to water.

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why are cooking utensils made of metal and not paper or cloth?

Answer:

Cooking utensils are made of metal because:

  • Metals are good conductors of heat — they transfer heat from stove to food efficiently
  • Metals are hard and strong — they don't break easily
  • Metals have high melting points — they don't burn on the stove
  • Metals are insoluble in water and don't react with most food items

Paper and cloth would burn on the stove, and they cannot hold water (paper dissolves, cloth leaks). That's why they're unsuitable for cooking.

Q2. Classify the following as transparent, translucent, or opaque: Glass window, wooden door, butter paper, clean water, cardboard, frosted glass

Answer:

MaterialClassificationReason
Glass windowTransparentCan see through clearly
Wooden doorOpaqueCannot see through at all
Butter paperTranslucentLight passes but objects not seen clearly
Clean waterTransparentCan see objects through it clearly
CardboardOpaqueNo light passes through
Frosted glassTranslucentLight passes partially, image blurred

Q3. Which of the following are soluble in water: salt, sand, sugar, chalk powder, lemon juice, sawdust?

Answer:

Soluble in water: Salt, Sugar, Lemon juice (they dissolve and disappear in water when stirred)

Insoluble in water: Sand, Chalk powder, Sawdust (they do NOT dissolve — sand settles at bottom, sawdust floats on top, chalk makes water milky but doesn't dissolve)

Q4. What is the difference between lustrous and non-lustrous materials? Give examples.

Answer:

LustrousNon-lustrous
Have a shiny surfaceHave a dull surface
Usually metalsUsually non-metals
Examples: Gold, silver, copper, iron, aluminiumExamples: Paper, wood, rubber, jute, clay

Note: Metals can lose their lustre over time due to air and moisture. A freshly cut surface of iron looks shiny, but it becomes dull (rusts) over time.

Q5. An object floats on water. Does this tell us about its material? Give examples.

Answer:

Yes! If an object floats on water, it means the material is less dense than water (lighter per unit volume).

Materials that float: Wood (log, twig), cork, wax, plastic (empty bottle), dry leaves, oil

Materials that sink: Iron, stone, glass, copper coin

This is why boats are made of wood or designed with hollow spaces — even though the metal hull is heavy, the air inside makes the overall density less than water!

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Everything is made of materials (paper, wood, metal, plastic, glass, etc.)
  • We choose materials based on their properties and the object's purpose
  • Classification = grouping materials based on common properties
  • Lustrous (shiny, metals) vs Non-lustrous (dull, non-metals)
  • Hard (iron, stone) vs Soft (sponge, cotton)
  • Transparent (see through) vs Translucent (partly) vs Opaque (can't see)
  • Soluble (dissolves in water) vs Insoluble (doesn't dissolve)
  • Float (lighter than water) vs Sink (heavier than water)
  • Same object → different materials possible; Same material → different objects possible
🔬 In-Depth Study — Chapter 6Objects, Materials & Properties — explored with experiments, examples and IKS connections

🪑 §6.1 — Objects & the Materials They Are Made Of (Deep Dive)

Look around you. A pen, a chair, a window, a fan, your shoes — every object is made of some material. The same object can be made of different materials, and the same material can be used to make many different objects!

📊 Same Object, Different Materials

ObjectPossible Materials
ChairWood, plastic, iron, cane, steel
SpoonSteel, silver, plastic, wood
BagCloth, plastic, leather, jute, paper
BowlGlass, plastic, steel, clay, brass
ToyPlastic, wood, cloth, rubber, metal

📊 Same Material, Many Objects

MaterialMany Possible Objects
PlasticBottle, comb, chair, bag, toys, ruler, pen
WoodTable, chair, door, pencil, spoon, toy
Iron / SteelKnife, scissors, almirah, vehicle parts, nails
GlassWindow, mirror, tumbler, bulb, bottle
CottonShirt, towel, bedsheet, dhoti, saree

🤔 Why So Many Choices?

Each material has unique properties — hardness, weight, cost, look, durability, conductivity. We pick the material that fits our need. A drinking glass is made of glass (clear, lets us see the drink) but a cup is often made of clay or steel (won't break easily). A frying pan is made of iron (handles heat) — never of plastic (would melt!).

🇮🇳 IKS Corner — Indian Materials Through History

  • Cotton was first cultivated in the Indus Valley around 5000 BCE — Indian cotton clothes were famous worldwide.
  • Wootz steel from ancient Tamil Nadu was so high-quality that Damascus swords made from it were legendary.
  • Indigo dye from the Indian plant gave the world its first blue dye for cloth.
  • Terracotta (baked clay) was used in Harappa to make pots, toys and tiles 4500 years ago.
  • The Iron Pillar of Delhi (≈1600 years old) has barely rusted — testament to ancient Indian metallurgy.

🗂️ §6.2 — Grouping/Classifying Materials (Deep Dive)

When we sort things into groups based on something they share, we are classifying them. Classification helps us:

  • Find what we need quickly (like books in a library)
  • See patterns and similarities
  • Decide which material is best for which purpose
  • Study huge numbers of things in an organised way

🎯 Activity 6.A — Classify Your Pencil Box

Empty out your pencil box. Sort everything into piles by:

  1. Material: wood, metal, plastic, rubber, paper
  2. Use: writing, erasing, sharpening, measuring
  3. Colour: red, blue, black, etc.

Notice — the same item ends up in different piles based on the rule of classification you choose!

📊 Common Bases of Classification

BasisPossible Groups
AppearanceLustrous / Non-lustrous
Touch / hardnessHard / Soft
Light passingTransparent / Translucent / Opaque
Behaviour in waterSoluble / Insoluble  |  Float / Sink
SourceNatural / Man-made
StateSolid / Liquid / Gas

✨ §6.3.1 — Lustre (Deep Dive)

Lustre is the shiny appearance of a surface when light falls on it. A lustrous material acts like a tiny mirror — it reflects light in a regular way.

📊 Lustrous vs Non-Lustrous

Lustrous (Shiny)Non-Lustrous (Dull)
Gold, silver, copper, aluminiumWood, paper, cardboard
Iron, steel (freshly cut)Clay, jute, rubber, sponge
Diamond, polished marbleCoal, ash, chalk

🤔 Do Metals Always Stay Shiny?

No! Iron rusts and turns brownish, silver becomes black over time, copper turns greenish. This happens because air and moisture react with the metal surface. That's why metal objects are polished or painted to keep them shiny.

🎯 Activity 6.B — Test for Lustre

Take any 5 objects around you. Hold them in sunlight. Tilt them slowly. The ones that flash and reflect light are lustrous. Now scratch each with a steel nail. Does a fresh shine appear? If yes — the inside is also lustrous (likely a metal).

💪 §6.3.2 — Hardness (Deep Dive)

A material is hard if it is difficult to scratch, dent, cut or compress. Soft materials can be easily scratched, bent or pressed.

📊 Hard vs Soft

HardSoft
Iron, steel, stone, glass, diamond, brickCotton, sponge, rubber, clay, chalk, wax

💎 The Hardest Natural Material — Diamond

Diamond is the hardest natural substance known to man. It can scratch every other material but cannot be scratched by anything except another diamond. That's why diamond-tipped drills are used to cut rocks, glass and even bones in surgery!

🎯 Activity 6.C — Hardness Test

Try scratching the following with your fingernail: chalk, wood, plastic ruler, iron nail, glass. Note which ones leave a mark and which don't. Now try scratching them with a coin. The hardness order you will discover:

Chalk < Wood < Plastic < Glass < Iron

👁️ §6.3.3 — Transparency (Deep Dive)

Materials are classified into three groups based on how much light passes through them.

📊 Comparison Table

PropertyTransparentTranslucentOpaque
Light passes through?Yes, completelyPartlyNo
Can you see clearly?Yes — sharp imageBlurred imageNo image at all
ExamplesClean glass, water, air, polytheneButter paper, oily paper, ground glass, thin clothWood, iron, stone, brick, cardboard
Where usedWindows, spectacles, telescopesBathroom windows, lamp shadesWalls, doors, curtains, umbrellas

🎯 Activity 6.D — Test for Transparency

Hold each material in front of a lit torch. Look at the torch through it.

  • If you see the torch bulb clearly → transparent.
  • If you see a glowing patch but no clear shape → translucent.
  • If you see nothing → opaque.

💧 §6.3.4 — Solubility (Deep Dive)

When a substance dissolves in water (or another liquid) and disappears completely, it is soluble. When it stays separate, it is insoluble.

📊 Common Soluble & Insoluble Substances

Soluble in WaterInsoluble in Water
Sugar, salt, glucose, washing soda, alumSand, sawdust, chalk powder, plastic bits
Lemon juice, vinegar, alcoholOil, kerosene, petrol (these float instead)

🎯 Activity 6.E — Solubility Test

Take 5 transparent glasses with water. Add a spoonful of each — sugar, salt, sand, chalk powder, oil — one in each glass. Stir well. Observe:

  • Sugar & salt → disappear → soluble.
  • Sand & chalk → settle at the bottom → insoluble.
  • Oil → forms a layer on top → does not mix.

🤔 Why Does Sugar Disappear in Water?

Sugar tastes sweet even after dissolving — so it's still there, just broken into tiny invisible particles that mix uniformly with water particles. Heat the water and it dissolves faster. Warm water can dissolve more sugar than cold water. That's why hot tea tastes sweeter for the same spoon of sugar!

🌊 Why Does Sea Water Taste Salty?

Sea water contains dissolved salt — millions of years of rivers carrying dissolved minerals into the ocean have made the sea salty. When sea water evaporates in shallow pans (like in Tamil Nadu, Gujarat saltpans), the salt is left behind.

⚖️ §6.3.5 — Float or Sink? (Deep Dive)

Drop a stone in water — it sinks. Drop a piece of wood — it floats. Why?

It depends on the density — how much mass is packed in a given volume. If a material is less dense than water, it floats. If more dense, it sinks.

📊 Float vs Sink Examples

Floats on WaterSinks in Water
Wood, cork, dry leaves, wax, plastic bottle (empty), oilStone, iron nail, glass marble, coin, sugar (initially)

🚢 How Does a Heavy Iron Ship Float?

An iron block sinks — but a giant iron ship floats! The secret is its shape. A ship's hull is hollow and shaped like a bowl, so it pushes aside a huge amount of water. The water that's pushed away pushes the ship up. As long as the ship's average density (steel + air inside) is less than water, it floats. This is called buoyancy.

🎯 Activity 6.F — Aluminium Foil Test

Take a small piece of aluminium foil. Crumple it into a tight ball → it sinks. Flatten it and shape it like a tiny boat → it floats! Same material, different shape → different behaviour. Now you know why ships are bowl-shaped!

📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 6

Q1. Define material. How is it different from an object?

Answer:

A material is the substance from which something is made (e.g., wood, plastic, iron). An object is the thing made from one or more materials (e.g., chair, spoon, pencil). Material = "what it is made of"; Object = "what it is".

Q2. Why is classification of materials important?

Answer:

Classification helps us study large numbers of materials easily, find similarities and differences, choose the right material for each job, and store and retrieve things efficiently.

Q3. Give two examples each of transparent, translucent and opaque materials.

Answer:

  • Transparent: Clear glass, clean water
  • Translucent: Butter paper, oily paper
  • Opaque: Wood, iron sheet

Q4. Why is glass used for windows but not for the walls of a house?

Answer:

Glass is transparent — so windows let in light and let us see outside. But walls need to be strong, opaque (for privacy), insulating and load-bearing. Glass would break easily and not provide privacy. So walls use brick/concrete (hard, opaque, strong).

Q5. Name 3 soluble and 3 insoluble substances in water.

Answer:

Soluble: sugar, salt, glucose. Insoluble: sand, chalk powder, sawdust.

Q6. Why does a wooden log float but an iron nail sink in water?

Answer:

Wood is less dense than water — for the same volume, wood is lighter than water, so it floats. Iron is denser than water — for the same volume, iron is heavier than water, so it sinks.

Q7. How does shape affect whether an object floats or sinks?

Answer:

The shape decides how much water the object displaces. A flat boat-shape displaces more water, so it floats. A tight ball of the same material displaces less, so it sinks. That is why ships float even though they are made of heavy iron.

Q8. Give one reason each:

(a) Spoon is made of steel, not plastic, for hot food.
(b) Doors are not made of glass.
(c) Carry bags are made of plastic, not paper, for liquids.

Answer:

  • (a) Steel can handle heat; plastic melts at low temperatures.
  • (b) Glass is brittle and breaks easily; doors need strong, opaque material for safety and privacy.
  • (c) Plastic is waterproof; paper absorbs water and tears easily.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 6 (20 Questions)

  1. Materials with a shiny surface are called ___. (lustrous)
  2. Glass is ___ to light. (transparent)
  3. Wood is ___ to light. (opaque)
  4. Butter paper is ___ to light. (translucent)
  5. Substances that dissolve in water are ___. (soluble)
  6. The hardest natural material is ___. (diamond)
  7. Cotton is a ___ material (hard/soft). (soft)
  8. Iron in water ___ (floats/sinks). (sinks)
  9. Wood in water ___ (floats/sinks). (floats)
  10. Oil and water do not ___ (mix/separate). (mix)
  11. Grouping similar things is called ___. (classification)
  12. Salt in water is an example of ___ substance. (soluble)
  13. Sand in water is an example of ___ substance. (insoluble)
  14. True or False: All metals are lustrous when freshly cut. (True)
  15. True or False: Ice is transparent. (False — translucent/opaque depending on thickness)
  16. True or False: All plastics are soft. (False — some plastics are very hard)
  17. True or False: An aluminium foil can be made to float by shaping it like a boat. (True)
  18. True or False: Warm water dissolves more sugar than cold water. (True)
  19. True or False: Air is transparent. (True)
  20. True or False: A diamond can scratch glass. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 6

TermMeaning
ObjectAnything that exists and can be seen/touched.
MaterialThe substance from which an object is made.
PropertyA characteristic of a material (e.g. hard, shiny).
ClassificationSorting items into groups based on common features.
LustreThe shiny appearance of a surface.
HardnessThe ability to resist scratching or compression.
TransparentLets light pass through completely.
TranslucentLets some light pass; image is blurred.
OpaqueDoes not let light pass through.
SolubleDissolves completely in a liquid.
InsolubleDoes not dissolve in a liquid.
FloatStay on the surface of a liquid.
SinkGo down into the liquid.
DensityMass packed into a given volume.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 6

  1. Material Hunt — Walk through your home; list 30 objects and the material(s) each is made of. Make a colourful chart.
  2. Solubility Lab — Test 10 substances (sugar, salt, sand, chalk, flour, oil, vinegar, alum, baking soda, washing powder) for solubility in water. Record observations.
  3. Float & Sink Boats — Make 3 boats from aluminium foil (different shapes). Test which floats best and which sinks. Photograph each.
  4. Lustre Gallery — Make a poster of 10 lustrous and 10 non-lustrous materials with pictures or actual samples taped to paper.
  5. Transparency Test — Test 10 household items in front of a torch. Classify as transparent / translucent / opaque. Tabulate.

📋 Chapter 7 — Temperature and its Measurement

📖 Topics Covered

  • 7.1 Hot or Cold? (Sense of Touch)
  • 7.2 Temperature — A Reliable Measure
  • 7.3 Measuring Temperature
  • 7.3.1 Clinical Thermometer (Digital)
  • 7.3.2 Laboratory Thermometer
  • 7.4 Transfer of Heat
  • 7.5 Wearing Woollen Clothes in Winter

✍️ Key Facts

  • Normal body temperature = 37°C
  • SI unit of temperature = Kelvin (K)
  • Common unit = Degree Celsius (°C)
  • Heat flows: Hot → Cold

Chapter 7: Temperature and its Measurement

💬 Quote

"Wrong measurements are worse than no measurements at all." — Anna Mani (Indian physicist & meteorologist)

🌡️ 7.1 — Hot or Cold?

We use our sense of touch to feel if something is hot or cold. But can we always rely on it?

Activity 7.1: The Three Containers Experiment

  1. Container A = warm water, B = tap water, C = ice-cold water
  2. Dip right hand in A (warm) and left hand in C (cold) for 1-2 minutes
  3. Now put BOTH hands in B (tap water)
  4. Result: Right hand feels B is COOL, Left hand feels B is WARM!

Conclusion: The same water (B) feels different to both hands! Our sense of touch is unreliable for measuring temperature. We need a thermometer!

🌡️ 7.2 — Temperature

Temperature = a reliable measure of how hot or cold a body is. A hotter body has higher temperature.

🔥 7.4 — Transfer of Heat

Heat always flows from a hotter body to a colder body until both reach the same temperature.

SituationHeat Flows FromHeat Flows To
Hot tea in a cupTea (hot)Surroundings (cooler) → tea cools down
Ice in warm waterWater (warm)Ice (cold) → ice melts
Person with feverBody (hot)Cold compress → body temperature decreases

Why Woollen Clothes Keep Us Warm?

Wool has tiny air pockets trapped between its fibres. Air is a poor conductor of heat. So the woollen layer prevents our body heat from escaping to the cold surroundings. The wool doesn't give heat — it traps our body heat!

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why can we not rely on our sense of touch to determine the temperature of an object?

Answer:

We cannot rely on our sense of touch because it gives relative (not absolute) results. In the three-container experiment, the same tap water feels cold to a hand that was in warm water, but feels warm to a hand that was in ice-cold water. Our sense of touch depends on what we touched before, making it unreliable. We need a thermometer for accurate measurement.

Q2. What is the normal body temperature? What instrument is used to measure it?

Answer:

The normal body temperature of a healthy person is 37°C (or 98.6°F). It is measured using a clinical thermometer (digital type). The thermometer is placed under the tongue or in the armpit. Note: Body temperature can vary slightly based on age, time of day, and activity level.

Q3. In which direction does heat flow? Give an example.

Answer:

Heat always flows from a hotter body to a colder body (from higher temperature to lower temperature).

Example: When we put ice in warm water, heat flows from the warm water to the cold ice. The ice gains heat and melts, while the water loses heat and becomes cooler. Eventually, both reach the same temperature.

Q4. Why do we wear woollen clothes in winter?

Answer:

Woollen clothes have air trapped between their fibres. Air is a poor conductor of heat (bad at transferring heat). This trapped air layer prevents our body heat from escaping into the cold surroundings. So woolens don't give us heat — they prevent our body heat from being lost, keeping us warm.

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Sense of touch is unreliable for judging temperature
  • Temperature = reliable measure of hotness/coldness
  • Clinical thermometer: 35-42°C, digital, for body temperature
  • Laboratory thermometer: -10 to 110°C, glass tube with liquid
  • Normal body temperature = 37°C
  • Scales: Celsius (°C), Fahrenheit (°F), Kelvin (K = SI unit)
  • Heat flows from hot → cold always
  • Wool traps air (poor conductor) → keeps body heat from escaping
🌡️ In-Depth Study — Chapter 7Temperature, Heat & Thermometers — explored with diagrams, history and experiments

🤲 §7.1 — Hot and Cold: Why Our Senses Fool Us (Deep Dive)

Touch a steel plate and a wooden plate that have been sitting at the same room temperature. The steel feels colder! But both are at the same temperature. What's going on?

Steel is a good conductor — it pulls heat away from your hand quickly, so your hand feels cold. Wood is a poor conductor — it doesn't take heat as fast, so it feels less cold. Touch measures how fast heat moves, not the actual temperature!

🎯 Activity 7.A — The Three-Bowls Experiment (Classic!)

  1. Take 3 bowls: A = hot water, B = lukewarm (room temperature) water, C = ice-cold water.
  2. Put your left hand in A and right hand in C for one minute.
  3. Now put BOTH hands into bowl B at the same time.
  4. Your left hand says, "It's cold!" Your right hand says, "It's warm!"

The same water gives two opposite feelings to two hands — proving touch is unreliable!

🌡️ Hot vs Cold — A Matter of Comparison

To Person ASame Object Feels…To Person B
Coming from desertRoom (25°C)Coming from snowfall
CoolWarm

So we need a tool — the thermometer — that doesn't depend on feeling.

📏 §7.2 — Temperature & Thermometers (Deep Dive)

Temperature is the numerical measure of how hot or cold something is. Higher number → hotter. The instrument that measures it is called a thermometer.

📊 Three Temperature Scales

ScaleFreezing point of waterBoiling point of waterUsed in
Celsius (°C)0°C100°CMost countries including India (science)
Fahrenheit (°F)32°F212°FUSA (everyday use)
Kelvin (K)273 K373 KSI unit, scientific work

Conversion: °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32   |   K = °C + 273

🩺 The Clinical Thermometer (Digital)

Earlier mercury thermometers were used, but mercury is highly toxic. If broken, it spilled and was dangerous. So today only digital thermometers are recommended. They show the reading on a small screen.

📋 How to Use a Clinical Thermometer

  1. Switch on the digital thermometer.
  2. Place the probe under the tongue (or in armpit).
  3. Keep mouth closed and wait until you hear a beep.
  4. Note the reading.
  5. Wipe with an alcohol swab to disinfect.

🧪 The Laboratory Thermometer

Used in school labs. It has a glass tube with a coloured liquid (usually alcohol with red dye). Range is typically −10°C to 110°C. It cannot measure body temperature (range is too wide and bulb is bigger). It's used for measuring temperatures of water, soil, room, chemicals etc.

🌡️ Body Temperatures of Animals — A Comparison

AnimalBody Temperature
Human37.0°C
Cow / Buffalo38.5°C
Dog38.5°C
Chicken / Birds40-42°C
Elephant36.0°C
Frog / Lizardmatches surroundings (cold-blooded)

🔥 §7.3 — Heat & Its Flow (Deep Dive)

Heat is a form of energy. When a hot body and a cold body are placed together, heat always flows from hot → cold. The flow continues until both reach the same temperature (called thermal equilibrium).

🎯 Activity 7.B — Watch Heat Flow

Take a glass of hot tea and drop an ice cube in. Observe: the ice melts (gains heat) and the tea cools (loses heat). Both eventually become warm.

📊 Three Ways Heat Travels

MethodHowExample
ConductionThrough solids, heat moves from particle to particleIron handle of frying pan becoming hot
ConvectionIn liquids and gases, hot fluid rises and cold sinks — making currentsBoiling water, sea breeze
RadiationHeat travels as waves through empty spaceSun's heat reaching Earth, warmth from a campfire

🧥 Why Wool Keeps Us Warm

Wool isn't warm by itself — your body produces heat. Wool simply traps tiny pockets of air between its curly fibres. Air is a poor conductor of heat, so it slows down the loss of body heat to the cold outside. The thicker the wool, the more air, the warmer you feel!

🧊 Why Metals Feel Cold and Wood Feels Warm

Metals are good conductors — they quickly take heat away from your hand, making it feel cold. Wood is a poor conductor — it doesn't pull heat fast, so it feels warmer. Both are at the same room temperature!

📊 Good vs Poor Conductors

Good Conductors of HeatPoor Conductors of Heat (Insulators)
Iron, copper, aluminium, silver, goldWood, plastic, rubber, glass, cotton, wool, air
Used in: cooking pots, soldering ironUsed in: handles of pots, hot-water flasks, woollens
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 7

Q1. Why does a steel chair feel colder than a plastic chair, though both are at the same room temperature?

Answer:

Steel is a good conductor; it pulls heat from your skin quickly, making your skin feel cold. Plastic is a poor conductor; very little heat flows out, so your skin doesn't feel much change. Touch measures heat flow, not the actual temperature.

Q2. Compare the clinical and laboratory thermometers.

Answer:

FeatureClinicalLaboratory
Range35°C to 42°C−10°C to 110°C
UseHuman body temperatureLab/water/soil/chemicals
TypeMostly digital todayGlass with coloured liquid
Smallest division0.1°C1°C

Q3. What is the SI unit of temperature? Convert 27°C to Kelvin.

Answer:

SI unit of temperature = Kelvin (K). Conversion: K = °C + 273 → 27 + 273 = 300 K.

Q4. Name and define the three modes of heat transfer.

Answer:

  • Conduction: heat flows through a solid from hot end to cold end (e.g. iron rod).
  • Convection: heat flows through a liquid/gas by hot parts rising and cold parts sinking (e.g. boiling water).
  • Radiation: heat travels as waves through empty space (e.g. Sun's heat).

Q5. Why is the handle of a cooking pan made of wood or plastic?

Answer:

Wood and plastic are poor conductors of heat. They prevent the heat from the hot pan from reaching our hand, so we can hold the pan safely without burning.

Q6. Why don't we wear woollen clothes in summer?

Answer:

In summer the outside temperature is already very high. We need to lose body heat to feel cool. Woollen clothes trap heat (good in winter, bad in summer). So we wear thin cotton clothes in summer, which allow heat and sweat to escape easily.

Q7. Why is mercury no longer used in clinical thermometers?

Answer:

Mercury is highly toxic. If the thermometer breaks, mercury spills out and can poison anyone who touches or breathes its vapour. Digital thermometers are safer, more accurate and easier to read.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 7 (18 Questions)

  1. The instrument used to measure temperature is ___. (thermometer)
  2. SI unit of temperature is ___. (kelvin)
  3. Most common scale for temperature in India is ___. (Celsius)
  4. Normal human body temperature is ___. (37°C)
  5. Water freezes at ___ °C. (0)
  6. Water boils at ___ °C. (100)
  7. Clinical thermometer range is ___ to ___ °C. (35-42)
  8. Heat flows from ___ to ___. (hot, cold)
  9. Iron is a ___ conductor of heat. (good)
  10. Wood is a ___ conductor of heat. (poor)
  11. Sun's heat reaches us by ___. (radiation)
  12. Heat travelling through a metal rod is ___. (conduction)
  13. Hot water rises and cold sinks — this is ___. (convection)
  14. Wool keeps us warm because it traps ___. (air)
  15. True or False: Touch is reliable for temperature. (False)
  16. True or False: Mercury thermometers are recommended today. (False)
  17. True or False: 27°C = 300 K. (True)
  18. True or False: We feel cold from a steel plate because steel is at low temperature. (False — steel only conducts heat away faster)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 7

TermMeaning
TemperatureNumerical measure of how hot or cold something is.
HeatA form of energy that flows from hot to cold.
ThermometerInstrument used to measure temperature.
Clinical thermometerMeasures body temperature (35-42°C).
Laboratory thermometerMeasures broader temperatures (-10 to 110°C).
CelsiusCommon temperature scale; water freezes 0°C, boils 100°C.
KelvinSI unit of temperature (K = °C + 273).
ConductionHeat transfer through solids.
ConvectionHeat transfer through liquids/gases by currents.
RadiationHeat transfer through waves (no medium needed).
ConductorMaterial that lets heat pass through easily (metals).
InsulatorMaterial that resists heat flow (wood, wool, plastic).
Thermal equilibriumState when two bodies in contact reach same temperature.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 7

  1. Three-Bowls Demo — Perform the classic three-bowls experiment for your family. Record everyone's responses.
  2. Daily Temperature Log — Use a lab thermometer to record outdoor temperature at 8 AM, 1 PM and 6 PM for 7 days. Make a graph.
  3. Conductor or Insulator? — Test 10 household materials (spoon, ruler, towel, etc.) by feeling how cold they are. Classify and tabulate.
  4. Make a Thermos Model — Wrap a glass of hot water with cotton wool and another with foil. Measure temperatures every 10 min. Which holds heat longer?
  5. Family Body Temperatures — With permission, measure body temperatures of 5 family members. Compare and note variations.

📋 Chapter 8 — A Journey through States of Water

📖 Topics Covered

  • 8.1 Investigating Water's Disappearing Act
  • 8.2 Evaporation
  • 8.3 Condensation
  • 8.4 The Water Cycle
  • Three States: Solid, Liquid, Gas

✍️ Key Concepts

  • Ice ↔ Water ↔ Water Vapour
  • Melting, Freezing, Evaporation, Condensation
  • Water cycle in nature

Chapter 8: A Journey through States of Water

📜 Tamil Wisdom (Thirukkural)

"If it does not rain well, even the mighty ocean will be drained."

💧 Three States of Water

Water exists in three states:

☀️ 8.1 — Evaporation (Water Disappearing!)

Evaporation = liquid water turning into water vapour (gas) at ANY temperature. It happens from the surface of water.

Where Do We See Evaporation?

  • Puddles drying up after rain
  • Wet clothes drying on a clothesline
  • Water in utensils drying up
  • Water level in open containers decreasing over time

Factors that Speed Up Evaporation:

FactorEffectExample
Higher temperatureFaster evaporationClothes dry faster on a hot day
More surface areaFaster evaporationSpread-out water dries faster than water in a deep glass
Wind/breezeFaster evaporationClothes dry faster on a windy day
Less humidityFaster evaporationClothes dry slower on a rainy/humid day

💨 8.2 — Condensation

Condensation = water vapour (gas) turning back into liquid water when it cools down.

Where Do We See Condensation?

  • Water droplets on the outside of a cold glass of water
  • Fog/mist on a cold morning
  • Water drops on the lid of a boiling pot
  • Dew drops on grass in the early morning
  • Mirror fogging up when you breathe on it

🔄 8.3 — The Water Cycle

The water cycle is a continuous process — water keeps cycling between Earth's surface and the atmosphere. No new water is created; the same water is recycled!

Questions & Answers

Q1. What is evaporation? How is it different from boiling?

Answer:

EvaporationBoiling
Happens at any temperatureHappens only at 100°C (boiling point)
Slow processFast, vigorous process (bubbles)
Happens only at the surfaceHappens throughout the liquid
Example: Puddle dryingExample: Water boiling in a pot

Q2. Explain the water cycle with a diagram.

Answer:

The water cycle is the continuous circulation of water in nature:

  1. Evaporation: Sun heats water in oceans/rivers/lakes → water vapour rises
  2. Condensation: Vapour rises, cools down at height → forms tiny water droplets → clouds
  3. Precipitation: When clouds get heavy → water falls as rain, snow, or hail
  4. Collection: Rain water flows into rivers, lakes → eventually back to the ocean. Some seeps underground.

The cycle repeats endlessly — the same water has been cycling for millions of years!

Q3. Why do wet clothes dry faster on a hot, windy day than on a cold, humid day?

Answer:

On a hot, windy day, evaporation is faster because:

  • High temperature gives water molecules more energy to escape
  • Wind blows away the water vapour near the clothes, making room for more evaporation

On a cold, humid day, evaporation is slower because:

  • Low temperature means less energy for evaporation
  • High humidity means the air already has lots of moisture, so it can't absorb more easily

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Water exists in 3 states: Solid (ice), Liquid (water), Gas (water vapour)
  • Melting = ice → water (at 0°C) | Freezing = water → ice
  • Evaporation = water → vapour (at any temp, from surface)
  • Condensation = vapour → water (when cooled)
  • Evaporation faster with: more heat, wind, more surface area, less humidity
  • Water cycle = evaporation → condensation → precipitation → collection → repeat
  • Water is constantly recycled in nature — it's never lost!
💧 In-Depth Study — Chapter 8States of Water, Phase Changes & the Water Cycle — diagrams, experiments & environment

🧊💧☁️ §8.1 — The Three States of Water (Deep Dive)

Water is the only substance commonly found in all three states (solid, liquid, gas) on Earth at the same time! Ice in the polar caps, water in oceans, and vapour in the air.

📊 Compare the Three States

PropertySolid (Ice)Liquid (Water)Gas (Vapour)
ShapeFixedTakes container's shapeFills all available space
VolumeFixedFixedVariable
Can it flow?NoYesYes
ParticlesClose, vibrate in placeClose, slide past each otherFar apart, fly everywhere
DensityLower than water (floats!)HighestLowest

🤔 Why Does Ice Float on Water?

Unusual fact: most substances become denser when frozen and sink. Water is special — ice is less dense than water (its particles form a more open hexagonal structure). That's why ice floats. Without this property, oceans would freeze from the bottom up, killing all aquatic life!

🔄 §8.2 — Changes of State (Deep Dive)

Water can change from one state to another by gaining or losing heat. Let's understand each transition.

📊 Five Phase Changes — All in One Table

ChangeFrom → ToHeat gained / lost?Everyday Example
MeltingSolid → LiquidgainedIce cube turning into water
FreezingLiquid → SolidlostWater turning to ice in freezer
EvaporationLiquid → Gas (slow, from surface)gainedWet clothes drying
BoilingLiquid → Gas (fast, throughout)gainedBoiling water in a kettle
CondensationGas → LiquidlostDew on grass, water on cold glass

🎯 Activity 8.A — Watch Condensation

Take a glass and fill it with chilled water and ice. After 2 minutes, look at the outside surface of the glass. You'll see water droplets forming! This water didn't come from inside — it was already in the air as invisible water vapour. When it touched the cold glass, it cooled and condensed into visible drops. This proves air contains water vapour!

🎯 Activity 8.B — Make Evaporation Faster

Take 4 saucers, put equal amounts of water in each:

  1. Place one in the sun.
  2. Place one in the shade.
  3. Place one in the shade with a fan blowing on it.
  4. Place one in the shade, but cover with a transparent lid.

After 1 hour, observe which one has the least water left. (Answer: Sun + Wind dry fastest; covered one barely loses any.)

🤔 Difference Between Evaporation and Boiling

EvaporationBoiling
Happens at ANY temperatureHappens at boiling point (100°C for water)
Only at the surfaceThroughout the liquid
Slow, silentFast, with bubbling
Example: wet clothes dryingExample: kettle boiling

🌊 §8.3 — The Water Cycle (Deep Dive)
