The water cycle (or hydrological cycle) is the endless journey of water through the Earth's atmosphere, surface and underground. The same water has been cycling for billions of years — the water you drink may have once been a dinosaur's drink!

📋 The Four Main Steps

  1. Evaporation — Sun's heat turns water from oceans, rivers, lakes, soil and plants (transpiration) into water vapour.
  2. Condensation — Vapour rises, cools at higher altitudes, and condenses into tiny droplets forming clouds.
  3. Precipitation — When droplets grow heavy, they fall as rain, snow or hail.
  4. Collection / Run-off — Water flows over land into rivers and back to oceans. Some seeps into the ground as groundwater.

🌿 Transpiration — The Plants' Contribution

Plants also release water vapour through their leaves — this is called transpiration. A single large tree can release hundreds of litres of water into the air per day! That's why forests have higher humidity and rainfall.

🌧️ Why is Rainfall Uneven?

Places near oceans (Mumbai, Kerala) get heavy rain. Places far inland (Rajasthan) or behind mountain barriers get less rain. India's monsoon winds carry moisture from the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal — that's why most of India's rainfall happens between June and September.

🇮🇳 IKS Corner — Ancient Water Wisdom

  • Stepwells (Baolis) in Gujarat and Rajasthan — like Rani ki Vav and Chand Baori — captured monsoon water for year-round use.
  • Tanks (Tank irrigation) in Tamil Nadu — connected through chains to ensure no rainwater was wasted.
  • Johads in Rajasthan — small earthen check-dams to recharge groundwater.
  • Zings in Ladakh — collected glacial meltwater for farming in cold deserts.

💚 §8.4 — Water Conservation: Why & How

Of all the water on Earth, only about 2.5% is fresh — and just a tiny fraction of that is easily available for drinking. Yet we waste billions of litres every day. Every drop matters!

📊 Earth's Water Distribution

SourcePercentage
Oceans (saline)97.5%
Glaciers & ice caps1.7%
Groundwater0.7%
Rivers, lakes, atmosphere0.1%

🚰 Ways to Save Water at Home

  1. Close the tap while brushing teeth or soaping hands.
  2. Take short showers (bucket bath uses less than shower).
  3. Repair leaky taps — a single dripping tap wastes 4000+ litres per year!
  4. Use washing machine only with full load.
  5. Use water from washing vegetables to water plants.
  6. Collect rainwater from rooftops.
  7. Mulch garden plants to reduce evaporation.
  8. Re-use RO reject water for cleaning/mopping.
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 8

Q1. Why does ice float on water though most solids sink in their liquid forms?

Answer:

When water freezes, its molecules arrange in an open hexagonal pattern, making ice less dense than liquid water. Because ice is lighter for the same volume, it floats. Most other solids are denser than their liquids and sink.

Q2. Distinguish between evaporation and boiling.

Answer:

Evaporation occurs at any temperature, only at the surface, and is slow and silent. Boiling occurs only at the boiling point (100°C for water), happens throughout the liquid, and produces vigorous bubbling.

Q3. Why does dew form on grass in early morning?

Answer:

During the night, the ground and grass cool down a lot. The water vapour in the air touches these cold surfaces, loses heat, and condenses into tiny water droplets — dew. As the sun rises, the dew evaporates and disappears.

Q4. Name 4 factors that affect the rate of evaporation.

Answer:

  1. Temperature — higher temperature = faster evaporation.
  2. Surface area — larger exposed surface = faster evaporation.
  3. Wind speed — faster wind = faster evaporation.
  4. Humidity — lower humidity = faster evaporation.

Q5. Why do we sweat? How does sweating cool the body?

Answer:

When body temperature rises, sweat glands release water onto the skin. This water absorbs heat from the body and evaporates. Evaporation needs heat — that heat comes from your body — so the body cools down. This is nature's air-conditioner!

Q6. Describe the water cycle in your own words.

Answer:

The Sun heats water in oceans, rivers and lakes — water evaporates and rises into the sky. High up, it cools and condenses into clouds. When clouds become heavy, water falls as rain, snow or hail. The fallen water flows into rivers and back to the oceans (or seeps underground). This endless cycle repeats forever, keeping water available on Earth.

Q7. Suggest 3 ways to conserve water in school.

Answer:

  1. Install push-taps in washrooms so water automatically stops.
  2. Collect rainwater from the rooftop into a tank for cleaning toilets and gardens.
  3. Repair any leaking taps and pipes immediately.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 8 (20 Questions)

  1. Solid form of water is ___. (ice)
  2. Gaseous form of water is ___. (water vapour)
  3. Solid → liquid is called ___. (melting)
  4. Liquid → solid is called ___. (freezing)
  5. Liquid → gas (from surface) is called ___. (evaporation)
  6. Gas → liquid is called ___. (condensation)
  7. Water freezes at ___ °C. (0)
  8. Water boils at ___ °C. (100)
  9. Water vapour released by plants is called ___. (transpiration)
  10. Tiny water droplets on grass in the morning are called ___. (dew)
  11. Rain, snow and hail are forms of ___. (precipitation)
  12. The continuous circulation of water in nature is called ___. (water cycle)
  13. Wet clothes dry faster on a ___ day. (hot/windy)
  14. Sweat cooling our body is an example of ___. (evaporation)
  15. True or False: Ice floats on water. (True)
  16. True or False: Boiling happens only at the surface. (False — throughout)
  17. True or False: Air contains water vapour. (True)
  18. True or False: Evaporation does not need heat. (False)
  19. True or False: Glaciers are made of liquid water. (False — solid ice)
  20. True or False: A leaky tap can waste thousands of litres a year. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 8

TermMeaning
State of matterSolid, liquid or gas — the three physical forms of matter.
MeltingSolid changing to liquid on gaining heat.
FreezingLiquid changing to solid on losing heat.
EvaporationLiquid slowly changing to gas at the surface.
BoilingLiquid rapidly changing to gas at boiling point.
CondensationGas changing back to liquid on cooling.
PrecipitationWater falling from clouds (rain, snow, hail).
TranspirationLoss of water from plant leaves as vapour.
DewWater droplets formed on cool surfaces overnight.
HumidityAmount of water vapour in the air.
Water cycleEndless circulation of water through the atmosphere, land and oceans.
Run-offRain water that flows over land into rivers.
GroundwaterWater that has seeped into the ground.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 8

  1. Mini Water Cycle — Take a transparent jar, pour warm water, cover with cling film. Place ice cubes on top. Observe drops forming inside — your own mini water cycle!
  2. Evaporation Race — Put 50 ml water in (a) wide bowl (b) tall narrow glass. Place both in sunlight. After 3 hours, measure remaining water. Tabulate.
  3. Rainwater Harvesting Model — Make a small model showing rooftop water collection into a tank using cardboard, plastic pipes and a bottle.
  4. Water Audit at Home — Estimate weekly water use (bathing, cooking, washing, gardening). Identify 5 ways to reduce usage.
  5. Dew Watch — Place a dry cloth or steel plate outside overnight. Observe in the morning if it's wet. Repeat on 5 nights and note which nights produced more dew.

📋 Chapter 9 — Methods of Separation in Everyday Life

📖 Topics Covered

  • 9.1 Why Do We Separate Mixtures?
  • 9.2 Hand-picking
  • 9.3 Winnowing
  • 9.4 Sieving
  • 9.5 Sedimentation & Decantation
  • 9.6 Filtration
  • 9.7 Evaporation
  • 9.8 Churning (Centrifugation)

✍️ Key Principle

  • Separation uses DIFFERENCES in properties (size, weight, solubility)

Chapter 9: Methods of Separation in Everyday Life

📜 Kabir Says

"Just like winnowing makes the husk blow away while retaining the grains, similarly, sages let the virtues stay while discarding the vices." — Kabir

🔀 Why Do We Need to Separate?

  • To remove unwanted substances (stones from rice, dirt from water)
  • To obtain useful components (salt from seawater, butter from curd)
  • To remove harmful substances (mud from drinking water)

📊 Methods of Separation

📋 Detailed Comparison Table

MethodSeparates What?Property UsedDaily Life Example
Hand-pickingVisible unwanted piecesSize, colour, shapeStones from dal, rotten grains
WinnowingLight husk from heavy grainWeight difference + windAfter threshing wheat/rice
SievingDifferent-sized particlesParticle sizeSand from gravel (construction)
Sedimentation + DecantationHeavy solid from waterWeight (gravity)Muddy water → let mud settle → pour clear water
FiltrationInsoluble solid from liquidParticle size vs filter poresTea strainer, water filter, coffee filter
EvaporationDissolved solid from liquidDifferent boiling pointsGetting salt from sea water
ChurningLighter substance from liquidDensity differenceMaking butter from curd/cream

Questions & Answers

Q1. What is winnowing? When is it used?

Answer:

Winnowing is a method of separating lighter components (like husk/chaff) from heavier components (like grains) using wind.

How: The mixture is dropped from a height. Wind blows the lighter husk away to a separate pile, while heavier grains fall straight down.

When used: After threshing (beating) of wheat, rice, or other crops to separate the grain from the chaff.

Q2. How would you separate a mixture of sand and salt?

Answer:

This requires two methods in sequence:

  1. Dissolving + Filtration: Add water to the mixture. Salt dissolves in water, sand does not. Filter the mixture — sand stays on filter paper, salt water (solution) passes through.
  2. Evaporation: Heat the salt water. Water evaporates, leaving behind solid salt crystals.

Result: Sand on filter paper + Salt crystals after evaporation — both separated!

Q3. How is salt obtained from seawater?

Answer:

Salt is obtained from seawater by evaporation:

  1. Seawater is collected in large, shallow pits (salt pans) near the coast
  2. The Sun's heat slowly evaporates the water over many days
  3. As water evaporates, salt crystals are left behind
  4. The salt is collected, cleaned, and packed

This is practiced in coastal areas like Gujarat (Rann of Kutch) and Tamil Nadu.

Q4. What is filtration? How is it different from sieving?

Answer:

FiltrationSieving
Separates insoluble solid from liquidSeparates solids of different sizes
Uses filter paper/cloth with very tiny poresUses sieve with larger holes
Example: Tea leaves from tea, mud from waterExample: Stones from sand, bran from flour
Liquid passes, solid stays on filterSmall particles pass, large stay on sieve

📌 Chapter Summary

  • We separate mixtures to remove unwanted parts or get useful components
  • Method depends on properties of the mixture (size, weight, solubility)
  • Hand-picking: visible large pieces (stones from dal)
  • Winnowing: light vs heavy using wind (husk from grain)
  • Sieving: different sizes through mesh (flour from bran)
  • Sedimentation + Decantation: heavy settles, pour clear liquid
  • Filtration: insoluble solid from liquid (filter paper/cloth)
  • Evaporation: dissolved solid from liquid (salt from seawater)
  • Churning: lighter substance floats up (butter from curd)
⚗️ In-Depth Study — Chapter 9Methods of Separation — each method with diagrams, examples and home experiments

🤔 §9.1 — Why Do We Separate Mixtures? (Deep Dive)

Look around. A bowl of rice may have small stones. Sand mixed in salt. Tea leaves in your tea. Iron filings in soil. We almost never get a pure substance directly from nature — we get mixtures. To use them, we must separate the useful part from the rest.

📊 Three Big Reasons for Separation

ReasonExample
Remove harmful / useless partsRemoving stones from rice, dust from grains
Obtain a useful componentGetting salt from seawater, butter from milk, sugar from sugarcane
Separate parts that are useful in different waysSeparating cream from milk, oil from groundnut, gold from rock

💡 What Decides the Method?

The method we choose depends on the properties of the components:

  • Different sizes? → Sieving
  • Different weights / one is heavy? → Winnowing, sedimentation
  • One is soluble, one is not? → Filtration, evaporation
  • One is magnetic? → Magnetic separation
  • One can be picked by hand (large, visible)? → Hand-picking

✋ §9.2 — Hand-Picking (Deep Dive)

The simplest method. Used when the unwanted part is large enough to see and few in number.

📊 Examples

MixturePicked Out
Rice with stonesStones
Dal with husk / stones / bad seedsHusk, stones
Vegetables with stale leavesBad/yellow leaves
Coins of different denominationsBy visual sorting

Limitation: Can't be used when particles are very small or look similar.

🌬️ §9.3 — Winnowing (Deep Dive)

Used when one component is much lighter than the other and the mixture is being dropped from a height in the wind. The wind blows the lighter part away.

📊 Examples

MixtureWhat is removed by wind
Grain + husk after threshingLight husk blown away; heavy grain falls down
Sand + sawdustLight sawdust blown away

This is an ancient practice — Indian farmers have been winnowing wheat, rice and pulses for thousands of years using flat baskets (soop) lifted in the wind.

🪣 §9.4 — Sieving (Deep Dive)

Used when the components are solids of different sizes. A sieve has many small holes — small particles pass through, large ones stay above.

📊 Examples

  • Sieving flour to remove bran or lumps
  • Sieving sand and pebbles at construction sites
  • Tea strainer (a kind of sieve)
  • Big sieve in flour mills

The size of holes is chosen as per the particle size we want to retain.

🧱 §9.5 — Sedimentation, Decantation & Loading (Deep Dive)

When solid particles are insoluble and heavier than the liquid, we let them sink to the bottom.

  • Sedimentation: Heavy solids settle at the bottom (e.g. mud in muddy water).
  • Decantation: The clear liquid above is carefully poured off.
  • Loading: Adding alum (fitkari) makes small mud particles stick together and sink faster.

🎯 Activity 9.A — Settle & Pour

Stir mud in a glass of water. Wait 10 minutes. The mud settles, water becomes clear above. Now gently tilt and pour the clear water into another glass — that's decantation!

IKS: Adding a piece of alum (fitkari) speeds up sedimentation — used in Indian villages for thousands of years to clean drinking water.

🧻 §9.6 — Filtration (Deep Dive)

Used when insoluble solid particles are mixed with a liquid. The mixture is poured through a filter — paper, cloth or sand. The liquid passes through (filtrate), the solid is held back (residue).

📊 Examples

MixtureFilter Used
Tea + tea leavesTea strainer
Muddy waterCloth / sand filter
Lemon juice with seedsSieve / strainer
Lab: chalk powder in waterFilter paper in funnel

Filtration vs Sieving: Filtration separates solid from liquid using very tiny holes; sieving separates large solids from small solids using bigger holes.

☀️ §9.7 — Evaporation (Deep Dive)

When a solid is dissolved in a liquid, sieving or filtration won't work — the solid particles are too small. We use evaporation: heat the mixture so the liquid escapes as vapour, leaving the solid behind.

🧂 Salt from Seawater — A 5000-Year-Old Industry

  1. Seawater is allowed into large shallow pans on the coast (salt pans).
  2. Sun and wind slowly evaporate the water over many days.
  3. White salt crystals are left behind on the floor of the pan.
  4. Salt is collected, washed, refined and packed.

Major Indian salt-producing areas: Rann of Kutch (Gujarat), Marakanam (Tamil Nadu), Andhra coast.

🎯 Activity 9.B — Tiny Salt Pan

Dissolve a teaspoon of salt in a small bowl of warm water. Place it on a sunny windowsill for 2 days. Watch the water disappear and white salt crystals form. You just made salt the same way our ancestors did!

🥛 §9.8 — Churning, Threshing & Magnetic Separation (Deep Dive)

🥛 Churning

Used to separate a lighter substance from a heavier liquid by rapid stirring. Example: butter from curd. In a churner (or mixer), the lighter fat globules collide, join together, and float up as butter.

🌾 Threshing

The first step after harvest — beating or trampling stalks to knock off the grain. Done by hand-beating, by oxen treading, or by threshing machines in modern farms.

🧲 Magnetic Separation

When one component is iron (or any magnetic material), we can pick it out with a magnet. Used in:

  • Removing iron pieces from grain in flour mills
  • Separating iron junk from waste in scrap yards (giant electromagnets!)
  • Recovering iron filings from soil

🌑 Sublimation (Bonus)

Some substances (like camphor, naphthalene, dry ice) change directly from solid to gas without becoming liquid. We can use this to separate them from other solids by heating gently and collecting the vapour.

🔁 §9.9 — Combination of Methods (Deep Dive)

Real-life mixtures often need more than one method! Example: muddy salt water (salt + mud + water):

  1. Sedimentation + Decantation → settle the mud, pour off clear salt water.
  2. Filtration → remove any remaining fine mud particles using a filter.
  3. Evaporation → boil off the water to get pure salt crystals.

📊 Match the Mixture to the Method

MixtureBest Method(s)
Iron filings + sandMagnetic separation
Sand + sugarDissolve in water → filter → evaporate filtrate
Salt + chalk powder + waterFilter (chalk stays) → evaporate filtrate (salt remains)
Rice + small stonesHand-picking
Wheat + chaffWinnowing
Tea liquor + tea leavesFiltration (strainer)
Butter from curdChurning
Camphor + sandSublimation
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 9

Q1. Why do we need to separate substances?

Answer:

To remove harmful or useless parts (stones in rice), to obtain a useful component (salt from seawater), and to separate substances that are useful in different ways (cream from milk).

Q2. How is winnowing different from sieving?

Answer:

Winnowing uses wind to blow away lighter particles (husk from grain). Sieving uses a mesh to allow small particles to pass while larger ones stay. Winnowing needs a weight difference; sieving needs a size difference.

Q3. Explain how clean drinking water can be obtained from muddy water at home.

Answer:

  1. Let the muddy water stand for some time → mud settles (sedimentation).
  2. Pour the clear water carefully into another vessel (decantation).
  3. Pass this through a clean cloth or filter (filtration) to remove fine particles.
  4. Boil the water for 10 minutes to kill germs.

Q4. Why is filtration preferred over decantation for separating chalk from water?

Answer:

Chalk particles are very fine and don't settle quickly. Decantation would leave behind many chalk particles in the water. Filter paper traps even tiny particles, giving truly clear water.

Q5. How is salt obtained from seawater?

Answer:

Seawater is collected in shallow salt pans on the coast. Sun and wind evaporate the water over several days, leaving white salt crystals behind, which are then collected and refined.

Q6. Why can't sugar dissolved in water be separated by filtration?

Answer:

Once dissolved, sugar breaks into tiny invisible particles that mix completely with water. They are far smaller than the holes of filter paper, so they pass through with the water. We must use evaporation to recover the sugar.

Q7. Suggest a method to separate (a) iron pins from sand (b) butter from curd (c) wheat grains from chaff.

Answer:

  • (a) Magnetic separation — iron pins stick to a magnet.
  • (b) Churning — butter floats up while heavier liquid (buttermilk) remains.
  • (c) Winnowing — wind blows away the light chaff.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 9 (20 Questions)

  1. Removing stones from rice by looking at them is called ___. (hand-picking)
  2. Separation using wind is called ___. (winnowing)
  3. The instrument used in sieving is called ___. (sieve)
  4. Settling of heavy particles in liquid is ___. (sedimentation)
  5. Pouring off clear liquid above settled solids is ___. (decantation)
  6. Adding alum to speed up settling is called ___. (loading)
  7. Liquid passing through filter paper is called ___. (filtrate)
  8. Solid left on filter paper is called ___. (residue)
  9. Separating dissolved salt from water uses ___. (evaporation)
  10. Method to get butter from curd is ___. (churning)
  11. Beating stalks to knock off grain is ___. (threshing)
  12. Magnetic separation works when one part is ___. (magnetic / iron)
  13. Camphor changes from solid directly to gas — this is called ___. (sublimation)
  14. Major Indian state for sea-salt production: ___. (Gujarat / Tamil Nadu)
  15. True or False: Sieving separates solids from liquids. (False — solids of different sizes)
  16. True or False: Filtration can separate sugar dissolved in water. (False)
  17. True or False: A mixture may need more than one method to fully separate. (True)
  18. True or False: Filter paper has very tiny holes. (True)
  19. True or False: Alum (fitkari) is used to purify drinking water. (True)
  20. True or False: Churning works because butter is heavier than buttermilk. (False — butter is lighter)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 9

TermMeaning
MixtureTwo or more substances mixed but not chemically combined.
Hand-pickingRemoving unwanted particles by hand.
WinnowingSeparating lighter and heavier components using wind.
SievingSeparating solids of different sizes using a mesh.
SedimentationSettling of heavier insoluble solids in a liquid.
DecantationPouring off the upper clear liquid after sedimentation.
LoadingAdding alum to make small particles clump and sink faster.
FiltrationSeparating insoluble solid from liquid using filter.
FiltrateThe clear liquid that passes through the filter.
ResidueThe solid left on the filter.
EvaporationConverting liquid to vapour to leave behind dissolved solid.
ChurningStirring rapidly to separate lighter fat from milk/curd.
ThreshingBeating stalks to remove grain.
Magnetic separationUsing a magnet to pick out iron pieces.
SublimationSolid changing directly to gas without becoming liquid.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 9

  1. Multi-Step Separation — Make a mixture of salt + sand + iron filings. Separate all three using magnetic separation, dissolving + filtering + evaporating. Photograph each step.
  2. Make Salt at Home — Evaporate salt water on a saucer. Examine the crystals with a magnifying glass.
  3. Sand Filter — Build a simple water filter using a plastic bottle, layers of cotton, sand and gravel. Pour muddy water and observe.
  4. Kitchen Separation Survey — Watch your family cook for one day; list 10 places where separation methods are used (sieving flour, straining tea, hand-picking dal, etc.).
  5. Alum Demo — Take 2 glasses of muddy water; add alum to one. Compare how fast the mud settles.

📋 Chapter 10 — Living Creatures: Exploring their Characteristics

📖 Topics Covered

  • 10.1 Living vs Non-living Things
  • 10.2 Characteristics of Living Things
  • Movement, Growth, Respiration
  • Response to Stimuli, Reproduction
  • Excretion, Need for Food & Water
  • 10.3 Life Span

✍️ Key Question

  • How do we decide if something is LIVING?
  • All living things share certain characteristics

Chapter 10: Living Creatures — Exploring their Characteristics

🌱 Living vs Non-Living Things

How do we know if something is living or non-living? A snail inside its shell doesn't move — is it alive? YES! Living things share certain characteristics that non-living things don't have.

📊 Comparison: Living vs Non-Living

PropertyLiving ThingsNon-Living Things
Movement✅ Move on their own❌ Only if pushed/pulled
Growth✅ Grow from within❌ Don't grow (a stone stays same size)
Respiration✅ Breathe (O₂ in, CO₂ out)❌ Don't breathe
Food✅ Need food for energy❌ Don't need food
Reproduction✅ Produce young ones❌ Cannot reproduce
Response✅ React to surroundings❌ Don't respond
Excretion✅ Remove waste❌ No waste produced
Death✅ Have a life span❌ Don't die (can be broken)

Questions & Answers

Q1. List the characteristics of living things.

Answer:

The main characteristics of living things are:

  1. Movement — ability to move (animals move from place to place; plants show movements like growing towards light)
  2. Growth — increase in size over time
  3. Respiration — breathing (taking in oxygen, releasing carbon dioxide) to get energy
  4. Need for food — all living things need nutrition for energy and growth
  5. Response to stimuli — reacting to changes in the environment (heat, light, touch)
  6. Reproduction — producing new individuals of the same kind
  7. Excretion — removing waste products from the body
  8. Definite life span — all living things eventually die

Q2. A car moves, needs fuel, and produces waste (exhaust). Is it living? Why or why not?

Answer:

No, a car is NOT living. Even though it shows some characteristics similar to living things:

  • It moves — but only when driven by a person (not on its own will)
  • It needs fuel — but doesn't eat or digest; fuel is burned mechanically
  • It produces exhaust — but this is combustion, not biological excretion

A car does NOT: grow, reproduce, respond to stimuli on its own, breathe, or have a life span (it can be repaired indefinitely). For something to be classified as living, it must show ALL the characteristics of life.

Q3. Do plants also move and respond to stimuli? Give examples.

Answer:

Yes! Plants show movement and respond to stimuli, though differently from animals:

Movement:

  • Roots grow downward (towards gravity/water)
  • Stems grow upward (towards light)
  • Sunflower turns to face the sun throughout the day

Response to stimuli:

  • Touch-me-not (Mimosa) — leaves fold when touched
  • Venus flytrap — closes when an insect lands on it
  • Plants grow towards light (phototropism)

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Living things have: movement, growth, respiration, need for food, response to stimuli, reproduction, excretion, definite life span
  • Non-living things lack these characteristics
  • All living things need food, water, air, and shelter
  • Plants also show characteristics of life (movement, response, growth, reproduction)
  • To be classified as living, ALL characteristics must be present
🦋 In-Depth Study — Chapter 10Living vs Non-Living & the Seven Characteristics of Life — examples, activities & comparisons

🌱 §10.1 — The 7 Signs of Life (Deep Dive)

How do we know if something is alive? Scientists use seven key characteristics. If even one is clearly missing, the thing is non-living. Let's look at each one carefully.

📊 The Seven Characteristics

SignMeaningExample
1. MovementLiving things move on their ownBirds fly, fish swim, plants bend toward light
2. GrowthIncrease in size from insideSeedling → tree; baby → adult
3. RespirationTake in O₂ & release CO₂ (release energy from food)You breathing, fish using gills
4. NutritionNeed food for energyAnimals eat; plants make food
5. Response to StimuliReact to changes in environmentEye closing in bright light; touch-me-not closing
6. ExcretionRemove waste from bodySweat, urine, plants lose old leaves
7. ReproductionProduce offspring of own kindHen lays eggs; plant produces seeds

📅 Plus: Life Span

Every living thing also has a definite life span — it's born, grows, ages and finally dies. A car, however well-maintained, doesn't "die" — it just stops working and can be repaired.

🏃 §10.1 (a) — Movement & Locomotion (Deep Dive)

Locomotion = movement of the whole body from one place to another. Movement = any change in position, even if only part of the body moves.

📊 How Different Organisms Move

OrganismBody Part / Way of Moving
FishFins, tail
BirdWings (flight)
SnakeSlithering using body muscles
EarthwormMuscles + tiny bristles (setae)
SnailSingle muscular foot
HumanLegs (walking, running)
PlantRoots grow down; shoots grow up (slow movement)

Plants don't run — but their roots, stems and leaves do move slowly in response to light, water and gravity. A sunflower turning to face the sun is a clear example!

👀 §10.1 (b) — Response to Stimuli (Deep Dive)

A stimulus is any change in the surroundings that causes a response. All living things react.

📊 Stimulus & Response Examples

StimulusResponse (Living)
Strong sunlightPupil of eye becomes smaller
A loud soundYou turn your head
Hot panYou pull your hand back
Touch on leafTouch-me-not (Mimosa) closes its leaves
Light from windowPlant grows toward the light
Smell of foodMouth waters
Insect lands on leafVenus flytrap closes around it!

🎯 Activity 10.A — Test for Stimulus

Tap a sleeping cat gently. It will move its tail, twitch its ears, then wake up. Try the same on a wooden chair — nothing happens. The cat responds (alive); the chair doesn't (non-living).

💧 §10.1 (c) — Excretion (Deep Dive)

All life processes produce waste products that are harmful if kept inside. Removing these wastes is called excretion.

📊 Excretion in Different Organisms

OrganismWaste RemovedHow
HumansUrine, sweat, CO₂Kidneys, sweat glands, lungs
FishAmmoniaGills (released into water)
PlantsGum, resin, old leaves, water vapourBark, leaf-fall, transpiration
InsectsSolid uric acidThrough special tubes (Malpighian)

Even though plants don't have a "toilet", they remove waste by shedding old leaves, releasing oxygen during the day, and storing wastes in bark or specific cells.

🌾 §10.2 — What Do Living Things Need? (Deep Dive)

To stay alive, every living organism needs four basic things:

🍎 Food

  • Plants make their own food using sunlight, water and CO₂ (photosynthesis).
  • Animals eat plants or other animals.
  • Decomposers (fungi, bacteria) feed on dead matter.

💧 Water

About 70% of our body is water. Water is needed to dissolve nutrients, transport them, remove wastes, and regulate temperature. Plants need water for photosynthesis and to keep cells turgid.

🌬️ Air (Oxygen)

Almost all living things need oxygen to release energy from food (respiration). Aquatic animals get oxygen dissolved in water; land animals breathe atmospheric oxygen.

🏠 Shelter / Habitat

A habitat is a place where an organism lives — fish in water, bears in forests, camels in deserts. Each habitat provides the right temperature, moisture, food and protection.

🇮🇳 IKS — Vedic View of Life

Ancient Indian texts (Atharva Veda, Charaka Samhita) classified beings as jangama (moving — animals) and sthavara (stationary — plants). The concept of prana (life-breath) recognised respiration as the essence of life, long before modern biology.

🔍 §10.3 — Living vs Non-Living: Tricky Cases (Deep Dive)

📊 Quick Reference Comparison

CharacteristicLivingNon-Living
MovementOn their ownOnly if pushed/driven
GrowthFrom inside (cells multiply)Only by adding more from outside
RespirationYes (intake of O₂)No
NutritionNeed food/sunlightDon't eat
ReproductionProduce young onesNo
Stimulus responseYesNo
ExcretionYes (waste removal)No (some chemical changes maybe)
Life spanBorn, age, dieIndefinite (until destroyed)

🚗 Common Confusions — Are These Alive?

  • A car? No — moves only when driven, can't reproduce, no metabolism.
  • A river? No — water moves but it doesn't grow, eat or reproduce.
  • Fire? No — grows and consumes "food" but doesn't reproduce or respond.
  • A virus? Special case — alive inside a host, inactive outside (not in syllabus, but interesting!).
  • A seed? Alive but dormant — comes back to active life when given water and warmth.

🎯 Activity 10.B — Living or Not?

Make a 2-column chart. List 15 things from your surroundings (cow, plant, computer, car, rock, ant, mango, table, sun, bicycle, etc.). Decide: living or non-living? Defend each answer using the 7 signs.

📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 10

Q1. List the seven characteristics of life.

Answer:

Movement, growth, respiration, nutrition (need for food), response to stimuli, excretion, and reproduction. (Definite life span is sometimes added as the 8th.)

Q2. Differentiate between growth in living and non-living things.

Answer:

Living things grow from inside as cells multiply and add new tissues. Non-living things (like a snowball or a sand mound) can grow only by adding matter from outside — they don't have an internal growth mechanism.

Q3. Why is reproduction important for living things?

Answer:

Every individual eventually dies. Without reproduction, a species would disappear. Reproduction produces new individuals of the same kind, ensuring the species continues to exist.

Q4. Are plants living? Justify with three examples.

Answer:

  1. Plants grow from seed to fully formed tree (growth).
  2. They respond to stimuli — touch-me-not closes leaves; sunflower follows the sun.
  3. They reproduce — produce seeds that grow into new plants.

So yes — plants show all signs of life and are living.

Q5. Why is a car not a living thing even though it moves and uses fuel?

Answer:

A car moves only when a driver controls it; it does not move on its own will. It uses fuel, but fuel is mechanically burned — not digested. It does not grow, reproduce, respire, excrete, or respond to stimuli on its own. So it is non-living.

Q6. What is excretion? How do plants excrete?

Answer:

Excretion is the removal of harmful waste produced inside the body. Plants excrete by:

  • Shedding old leaves (with stored waste)
  • Releasing oxygen during photosynthesis
  • Storing waste in bark or specific cells
  • Losing water vapour through stomata (transpiration)

Q7. Name 4 basic needs of living things.

Answer:

Food, water, air (oxygen), and shelter (habitat).

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 10 (20 Questions)

  1. The seven signs of life include movement, growth, respiration, nutrition, response to stimuli, excretion, and ___. (reproduction)
  2. Movement of the whole body from one place to another is ___. (locomotion)
  3. Closing of touch-me-not's leaves on touch is a ___ to a stimulus. (response)
  4. Plants make their own food in a process called ___. (photosynthesis)
  5. Removal of harmful waste is called ___. (excretion)
  6. Living things release ___ during respiration. (carbon dioxide)
  7. Plants release water vapour through leaves — called ___. (transpiration)
  8. Fish breathe through ___. (gills)
  9. Worm's tiny bristles for movement are called ___. (setae)
  10. The place where an organism lives is its ___. (habitat)
  11. About ___ % of the human body is water. (70)
  12. Plant that closes when touched: ___. (touch-me-not / Mimosa)
  13. True or False: A car is a living thing because it moves and uses fuel. (False)
  14. True or False: Plants do not respond to stimuli. (False)
  15. True or False: All living things need oxygen. (True for most)
  16. True or False: Growth in non-living things happens from inside. (False)
  17. True or False: A seed is alive even when dormant. (True)
  18. True or False: Snail moves using one muscular foot. (True)
  19. True or False: Sunflower turning towards the sun is response to stimulus. (True)
  20. True or False: Bears live in deserts. (False — forests/mountains)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 10

TermMeaning
OrganismAny living being — plant, animal or microbe.
MovementChange in position by an organism.
LocomotionMovement of the whole body from one place to another.
GrowthIncrease in size or number of cells.
RespirationProcess of releasing energy from food using oxygen.
NutritionTaking in food for growth and energy.
StimulusAny change in the surroundings causing response.
ResponseReaction of an organism to a stimulus.
ExcretionRemoval of waste from the body.
ReproductionProduction of new individuals of the same kind.
HabitatThe place where an organism lives.
Life spanTotal time from birth to death of an organism.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 10

  1. Living vs Non-Living Album — Take photos / draw pictures of 20 things around you; classify them. For each, write one reason.
  2. Growth Journal — Plant a bean seed. Measure and photograph its growth daily for 3 weeks. Make a chart.
  3. Stimulus & Response Diary — Note 10 stimulus-response examples you experience in a day (loud horn → turning head, etc.).
  4. Habitat Project — Pick any animal. Draw its habitat and list 5 things that make it suitable for the animal.
  5. Mimosa Hunt — Look for touch-me-not plants in your area. Touch them gently and observe how quickly leaves close and how long they take to reopen.

📋 Chapter 11 — Nature's Treasures

📖 Topics Covered

  • 11.1 Natural Resources (Air, Water, Soil)
  • 11.2 Importance of Air
  • 11.3 Importance of Water
  • 11.4 Importance of Soil
  • 11.5 Forests and Biodiversity
  • 11.6 Conservation of Resources

✍️ Key Ideas

  • Nature provides everything we need
  • Resources are limited — conserve them!
  • 3 R's: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle

Chapter 11: Nature's Treasures

🌍 Natural Resources

Everything useful that we get from nature is called a natural resource. The main treasures of nature are:

☀️ Sunlight — The Ultimate Energy Source

The Sun is the primary source of energy for life on Earth. Plants use sunlight to make food (photosynthesis). All other living things depend on plants directly or indirectly.

♻️ Conservation — The 3 R's

RMeaningExamples
ReduceUse lessTurn off lights/fans, shorter showers, less plastic
ReuseUse againReuse bottles, bags, containers, old clothes as rags
RecycleConvert waste to newPaper recycling, plastic recycling, compost from waste

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are natural resources? Why should we conserve them?

Answer:

Natural resources are useful materials we get from nature — air, water, soil, sunlight, minerals, forests, wildlife.

We must conserve them because:

  • Many resources are limited (non-renewable) and will run out if overused
  • Pollution damages these resources (dirty air, contaminated water)
  • Future generations also need these resources
  • Destroying nature harms all living beings who depend on it

Q2. How do forests help us? Why is deforestation harmful?

Answer:

Forests help us by:

  • Producing oxygen and absorbing carbon dioxide
  • Providing habitat for animals, birds, insects
  • Preventing soil erosion (roots hold soil)
  • Bringing rainfall (trees release water vapour)
  • Giving us wood, fruits, medicines, rubber

Deforestation is harmful because: it leads to soil erosion, floods, less rainfall, loss of wildlife, and increases carbon dioxide (global warming).

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Natural resources: Air, water, soil, sunlight, forests, minerals
  • Air gives oxygen for breathing; polluted by vehicles/factories
  • Water for drinking, farming, cleaning; only 3% is freshwater!
  • Soil grows food; threatened by erosion and chemicals
  • Forests give oxygen, habitat, prevent erosion, bring rain
  • Sunlight = ultimate energy source for all life
  • Conserve using 3 R's: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
🌍 In-Depth Study — Chapter 11Nature's Treasures — Air, Water, Soil, Forests, Minerals, Sun & their Conservation

🎁 §11.1 — Natural Resources: Earth's Gifts (Deep Dive)

Anything we get from nature that is useful to us is a natural resource. Resources can be classified in two ways:

📊 Renewable vs Non-Renewable

Renewable (refill themselves)Non-Renewable (limited, take ages to form)
Sunlight, wind, water, airCoal, petroleum, natural gas
Forests, wildlife (if used carefully)Iron, copper, gold, diamonds
Tidal energy, geothermalFossil fuels — used in seconds, formed over millions of years

We must conserve all resources, but especially non-renewable ones — once gone, they're gone forever.

🌬️ §11.2 — Air (Deep Dive)

Air is an invisible mixture of gases that surrounds the Earth in a layer called the atmosphere.

📊 What's in the Air?

Gas%Use
Nitrogen (N₂)78%Plants need it (after conversion); makes air "dilute"
Oxygen (O₂)21%Breathing, burning of fuels
Carbon dioxide (CO₂)0.04%Plants use for photosynthesis
Argon & other gases~1%Various uses (e.g. argon in bulbs)
Water vapourVariableWater cycle, weather

🌪️ Uses of Air

  • For breathing by all animals (including humans)
  • For burning fuel — fire needs oxygen
  • For plants — photosynthesis uses CO₂
  • For flight — birds, planes, kites
  • For winds — wind energy, weather, sailing
  • For sound — sound needs air to travel

💨 Air Pollution

Air becomes polluted when harmful substances enter it:

  • Smoke from vehicles, factories, burning of garbage
  • Dust from construction and mining
  • Chemicals from sprays, pesticides
  • Burning of crop residues (stubble burning)

Effects: breathing problems, lung diseases, smog, acid rain. Delhi's air quality index often reaches dangerous levels in winter due to all these factors combined.

💧 §11.3 — Water (Deep Dive)

Water covers about 71% of Earth's surface, but only 2.5% of it is freshwater, and most of that is locked in glaciers or deep underground. The water we can easily use is less than 1%!

📊 Uses of Water

SectorUse
DomesticDrinking, cooking, bathing, cleaning
AgricultureIrrigation of crops (uses ~70% of fresh water!)
IndustryCooling machines, manufacturing, paper, textiles
PowerHydroelectricity, cooling thermal plants
TransportShips, boats use waterways
RecreationSwimming, boating, fountains

🚱 Water Pollution

  • Sewage and garbage dumped into rivers
  • Factory waste/chemicals released into water bodies
  • Pesticides and fertilisers washed from farms
  • Oil spills in oceans
  • Religious offerings, plastic waste

💚 How to Save Water

  1. Close taps when not in use
  2. Repair leaks immediately
  3. Take bucket baths instead of long showers
  4. Reuse RO water for cleaning
  5. Harvest rainwater on rooftops
  6. Use mulch in gardens to reduce evaporation
  7. Choose efficient washing machines

🌱 §11.4 — Soil (Deep Dive)

Soil is the loose top layer of Earth where plants grow. It is a mixture of weathered rock particles, decayed organic matter (humus), air and water.

📊 Types of Soil in India

Soil TypeFound InBest For
Alluvial soilNorthern plains (Ganga, Yamuna)Wheat, rice, sugarcane
Black soilMaharashtra, Gujarat, MPCotton, soybean
Red soilKarnataka, Tamil Nadu, OdishaMillets, groundnut
Laterite soilKerala, Karnataka coastTea, coffee, rubber
Desert soilRajasthan, GujaratDrought-resistant crops (bajra)

⛏️ Soil Erosion — A Silent Disaster

When the top fertile layer of soil is washed away by water or blown by wind, it's called soil erosion. Causes:

  • Cutting down forests
  • Overgrazing by cattle
  • Heavy rains on bare slopes
  • Wrong farming practices (ploughing along slopes)

It takes hundreds of years to form just 1 cm of fertile soil — but erosion can destroy it in a single storm.

🌿 Preventing Soil Erosion

  • Plant trees (afforestation)
  • Build terraced fields on slopes
  • Use cover crops
  • Build small check-dams
  • Avoid overgrazing

🌳 §11.5 — Forests & Wildlife (Deep Dive)

Forests are large areas covered with trees, plants, animals, birds and microbes. They are called the "lungs of the Earth" because they produce oxygen and absorb CO₂.

📊 Gifts of the Forest

Direct ProductsEcological Services
Wood, timber, paperProduce oxygen
Fruits, nuts, honey, gum, rubberAbsorb CO₂ (fight climate change)
Medicines (e.g., quinine from chinchona)Maintain rainfall & humidity
Bamboo, canePrevent soil erosion
Habitat for wildlifeRecharge groundwater
Tribal livelihoodReduce flooding

🪓 Deforestation: A Growing Threat

Cutting down forests for farms, factories, mines, roads, cities is called deforestation. Consequences:

  • Loss of wildlife habitats — many species extinct
  • Soil erosion and floods
  • Reduced rainfall
  • Rise in CO₂ → global warming
  • Loss of medicinal plants

🌳 Famous Indian Movements for Forests

  • Chipko Movement (1973, Uttarakhand) — Villagers, led by Sunderlal Bahuguna and Gaura Devi, hugged trees to stop loggers.
  • Appiko Movement (1983, Karnataka) — Inspired by Chipko, villagers protected Western Ghats forests.
  • Silent Valley Movement (Kerala) — Saved a tropical rainforest from a dam project.

⛏️ §11.6 — Minerals & ☀️ Solar Energy (Deep Dive)

⛏️ Minerals

Minerals are naturally occurring substances found in the Earth's crust — iron, copper, aluminium, gold, silver, coal, salt, limestone. They are extracted by mining. Once used, most cannot be replaced (non-renewable). India is rich in iron ore (Jharkhand, Odisha), coal (Jharkhand, West Bengal), bauxite, mica, manganese.

☀️ The Sun — The Ultimate Resource

Almost all energy on Earth comes from the Sun — either directly (solar panels, plants) or indirectly (wind, water cycle, coal/oil which formed from ancient plants).

  • Plants use sunlight for photosynthesis
  • Solar panels make electricity
  • Solar water heaters give hot water
  • Sun drives the water cycle (evaporation)
  • The wind is caused by uneven heating of Earth by the Sun

India launched the International Solar Alliance (ISA) to promote solar energy worldwide.

♻️ §11.7 — Conservation of Resources (Deep Dive)

To protect resources for ourselves and future generations, we follow the 3R's:

📊 The 3 R's in Action

RWhat to doExamples
ReduceUse less of a resourceSwitch off fans/lights when not needed; cycle instead of car
ReuseUse an item againReuse plastic bottles as planters; old clothes as mops
RecycleProcess waste into new thingsPaper, glass, aluminium and plastic can be recycled into new products

🌟 Bonus R's

  • Refuse — say no to single-use plastics
  • Repair — fix things instead of throwing them away
  • Rethink — change habits to be more sustainable

🇮🇳 IKS Corner — Traditional Conservation in India

  • Sacred groves — small forests around temples preserved for generations
  • Bishnoi community (Rajasthan) — protect blackbucks and khejri trees as religious duty
  • Stepwells, tanks, johads — ancient water harvesting
  • Eating with hands & using leaf plates — zero-waste, compostable
  • Rural composting of kitchen waste — feeds the soil naturally
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 11

Q1. Differentiate renewable and non-renewable resources with examples.

Answer:

Renewable resources are naturally replaced quickly (sunlight, wind, water, forests). Non-renewable resources take millions of years to form and can run out (coal, petroleum, minerals).

Q2. Name the gases in air and their percentages.

Answer:

Nitrogen 78%, Oxygen 21%, Carbon dioxide 0.04%, Argon and other gases ~0.96%, plus variable water vapour.

Q3. What is soil erosion? How can we prevent it?

Answer:

Soil erosion is the loss of fertile top soil by wind or running water. Prevent it by planting trees, terrace farming on slopes, using cover crops, building check-dams, and stopping overgrazing.

Q4. Why are forests called the "lungs of the Earth"?

Answer:

Forests absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen during photosynthesis — just like lungs take in oxygen and release CO₂ in the reverse. They keep the air breathable and balance the gases in the atmosphere.

Q5. Explain the 3 R's with one example each.

Answer:

  • Reduce: Switching off lights when leaving the room — less electricity used.
  • Reuse: Using an empty jam bottle to store sugar.
  • Recycle: Sending old newspapers to recycling units that turn them into new paper.

Q6. Mention 4 ways water gets polluted.

Answer:

  1. Sewage and garbage dumped into rivers.
  2. Factory waste releasing chemicals.
  3. Pesticides and fertilisers washing in from farms.
  4. Oil spills from ships in oceans.

Q7. What was the Chipko Movement?

Answer:

The Chipko Movement (1973, Uttarakhand) was a peaceful protest where villagers — led by Sunderlal Bahuguna and Gaura Devi — hugged trees to stop loggers from cutting them down. It became a famous symbol of forest conservation in India.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 11 (20 Questions)

  1. Resources from nature are called ___ resources. (natural)
  2. Sunlight, wind and water are examples of ___ resources. (renewable)
  3. Coal and petroleum are examples of ___ resources. (non-renewable)
  4. The most abundant gas in air is ___. (nitrogen)
  5. The gas needed for breathing is ___. (oxygen)
  6. Only about ___ % of Earth's water is fresh. (2.5)
  7. The loose top layer of Earth where plants grow is ___. (soil)
  8. The soil best for cotton is ___ soil. (black)
  9. Loss of top fertile soil is called soil ___. (erosion)
  10. Forests are called ___ of the Earth. (lungs)
  11. Famous Indian forest-saving movement: ___. (Chipko)
  12. Cutting down forests is called ___. (deforestation)
  13. 3 R's = ___, ___, ___. (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle)
  14. The Sun powers the water ___. (cycle)
  15. True or False: Trees cause floods. (False — they prevent floods)
  16. True or False: Bishnois protect blackbucks. (True)
  17. True or False: India is rich in iron ore. (True)
  18. True or False: We can drink directly from any river. (False)
  19. True or False: Solar energy is non-renewable. (False)
  20. True or False: Recycling paper saves trees. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 11

TermMeaning
Natural resourceUseful material obtained from nature.
RenewableResource that nature replaces easily (sun, wind, water).
Non-renewableResource that takes very long to form (coal, oil, minerals).
AtmosphereLayer of gases surrounding Earth.
PollutionHarmful change in air/water/soil due to wastes.
Soil erosionLoss of fertile top soil by wind or water.
DeforestationCutting down of forests.
AfforestationPlanting trees on barren land.
3 R'sReduce, Reuse, Recycle.
ConservationWise use and protection of resources.
ChipkoFamous Indian movement to save trees by hugging them.
Sacred groveForest preserved by community for religious reasons.
HydroelectricityElectricity made using flowing water.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 11

  1. Resource Map of India — Mark on an outline map: coal areas, iron ore areas, major rivers, forests, salt-producing regions.
  2. Save-Water Pledge — Make a poster with 10 ways your family will save water this month.
  3. Plant a Sapling — Plant a tree on your birthday and photograph its growth each month for one year.
  4. Recycle Drive — Collect old newspapers, plastic and metal at home for one month; weigh and send to a recycling unit. Calculate the trees/oil saved.
  5. Soil Comparison — Collect soil from 4 different places (garden, roadside, riverbank, pot). Compare colour, smell, water-holding capacity.
  6. Air-Quality Tracker — Note your city's AQI for 7 days. Make a graph. Discuss reasons for high or low values.

📋 Chapter 12 — Beyond Earth

📖 Topics Covered

  • 12.1 The Night Sky — Stars
  • 12.2 The Moon — Earth's Natural Satellite
  • 12.3 The Solar System (8 Planets)
  • 12.4 Constellations (Star Patterns)
  • 12.5 India's Space Programme (ISRO)

✍️ Key Facts

  • Sun = nearest star (150 million km)
  • 8 Planets in our solar system
  • Moon phases: 29.5 day cycle
  • Pole Star: always indicates North

Chapter 12: Beyond Earth

📖 Chapter Story

Yangdol and Dorjay live in Nubra, Ladakh — where the night sky is crystal clear with thousands of stars visible. With almost no air or light pollution, they see ancient caravans used these stars for navigation through the mountain passes.

⭐ 12.1 — Stars and the Sun

Stars are huge balls of hot, glowing gases (mainly hydrogen and helium). They produce their own light and heat through nuclear reactions. The Sun is our nearest star.

FactDetail
What is the Sun?A medium-sized star (appears large because it's closest)
Distance from EarthAbout 150 million km (light takes 8 minutes to reach us)
Why do stars twinkle?Their light passes through moving layers of Earth's atmosphere
Pole Star (Dhruv Tara)Always appears in the North; appears stationary; helps find direction
Why can't we see stars during day?Sun's bright light overpowers the faint starlight

🌙 12.2 — The Moon

The Moon is Earth's natural satellite — it revolves around Earth. It does NOT produce its own light; we see it because it reflects sunlight.

Key Facts about the Moon:

  • Takes about 27.3 days to revolve around Earth
  • The same side always faces Earth (we never see the "far side" from Earth)
  • Has no atmosphere, no water, no life
  • Has craters from asteroid impacts
  • Full moon = Purnima, New moon = Amavasya
  • Neil Armstrong was the first human to walk on the Moon (1969)

🪐 12.3 — The Solar System

PlanetPositionKey Fact
Mercury1st (closest)Smallest, no atmosphere, extreme temperatures
Venus2ndHottest planet (thick CO₂ atmosphere traps heat), rotates backward
Earth3rdOnly planet with life! Has water, oxygen atmosphere
Mars4thRed planet (iron oxide), has 2 moons, polar ice caps
Jupiter5thLargest planet, Great Red Spot (giant storm), 95+ moons
Saturn6thBeautiful rings of ice and rock, many moons including Titan
Uranus7thRotates on its side (tilted 98°), ice giant, blue-green
Neptune8th (farthest)Very cold, strongest winds in solar system, blue colour

Other Objects in the Solar System:

  • Asteroids: Rocky objects orbiting between Mars and Jupiter (asteroid belt)
  • Comets: Icy objects with long tails (e.g., Halley's comet, returns every 76 years)
  • Meteors: "Shooting stars" — small rocks burning up in Earth's atmosphere

⭐ 12.4 — Constellations

Constellations are patterns of stars as seen from Earth. Ancient people named them after animals, objects, and mythological figures.

ConstellationIndian NameWhat It Looks LikeUse
Ursa Major (Great Bear)Saptarishi7 stars in a dipper/spoon shapeHelps locate Pole Star
Orion (The Hunter)Mriga (Deer)3 stars in a line (belt)Visible in winter sky
Leo (Lion)SimhaLooks like a lion sittingVisible in spring

Finding the Pole Star: Extend the two "pointer stars" of Saptarishi (the edge of the "dipper") about 5 times → you reach the Pole Star (Dhruv Tara), which always indicates North.

🇮🇳 12.5 — India's Space Programme (ISRO)

MissionYearAchievement
Aryabhata1975India's first satellite launched into space
Chandrayaan-12008Discovered water molecules on the Moon's surface
Mangalyaan (MOM)2014Mars orbiter — India succeeded on first attempt! (only 4th country)
Chandrayaan-32023Soft landing on Moon's South Pole — world's FIRST to achieve this!
Aditya-L12023India's first solar observatory mission

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why does Earth support life while other planets do not?

Answer:

Earth supports life because of these special conditions:

  • Right distance from Sun → moderate temperature (not too hot like Venus, not too cold like Mars)
  • Atmosphere with oxygen for breathing and ozone layer for UV protection
  • Liquid water on the surface — essential for all life forms
  • Suitable gravity — strong enough to hold the atmosphere

No other planet in our solar system has all these conditions together.

Q2. Why do we see different phases of the Moon?

Answer:

The Moon doesn't produce its own light — it reflects sunlight. As the Moon revolves around Earth, the sunlit portion visible to us changes:

  • New Moon (Amavasya): Moon between Sun and Earth → sunlit side faces away → Moon invisible
  • Full Moon (Purnima): Earth between Sun and Moon → full sunlit face visible to us
  • Between these: We see crescent, quarter, gibbous shapes (waxing = growing, waning = shrinking)

This cycle repeats every 29.5 days (one lunar month).

Q3. Name the planets in order from the Sun. Give the mnemonic.

Answer:

In order from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune

Mnemonic: My Very Educated Mother Just Showed Us Nebulas

Largest: Jupiter | Smallest: Mercury | Hottest: Venus | Only with life: Earth

Q4. What are constellations? How can you locate the Pole Star?

Answer:

Constellations are recognizable patterns formed by groups of stars as seen from Earth. Examples: Ursa Major (Saptarishi), Orion (Mriga), Leo (Simha).

To locate the Pole Star:

  1. Find the Saptarishi (Ursa Major/Big Dipper) constellation — 7 bright stars in a spoon shape
  2. Look at the two stars forming the "front edge" of the dipper (pointer stars)
  3. Extend an imaginary line through these two stars upward, about 5 times the distance between them
  4. You'll reach a bright star — that's the Pole Star (Dhruv Tara)
  5. The Pole Star always indicates the North direction

📌 Chapter Summary

  • Stars = hot glowing gas balls producing their own light; Sun = nearest star
  • Stars twinkle due to Earth's atmosphere; Pole Star indicates North
  • Moon = Earth's satellite; reflects sunlight; phases repeat every 29.5 days
  • Solar System = Sun + 8 planets + moons + asteroids + comets
  • Planet order: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune
  • Earth is unique: right temperature + water + oxygen atmosphere = life!
  • Constellations: Star patterns (Saptarishi helps find Pole Star)
  • ISRO: Chandrayaan-3 (2023) landed on Moon's South Pole — world first!
🌌 In-Depth Study — Chapter 12Beyond Earth — Stars, Moon, Planets, Constellations & India's Space Missions

🌌 §12.1 — Looking at the Night Sky (Deep Dive)

On a clear, dark night — away from city lights — you can see thousands of stars, sometimes the Moon, planets like Venus or Jupiter, occasionally a shooting star, and if you're lucky, the faint band of the Milky Way.

📊 Things You Can See in the Night Sky

ObjectWhat it isLight source
StarsHot balls of gas (mainly hydrogen) producing their own lightOwn light
PlanetsSolid/gas bodies orbiting the SunReflected sunlight
MoonEarth's natural satelliteReflected sunlight
CometsIcy bodies with long tailsReflected sunlight
Meteors (shooting stars)Tiny rock pieces burning in atmosphereGlow from heat
Satellites (artificial)Man-made spacecraftReflected sunlight

✨ Why Do Stars Twinkle?

Stars are extremely far away — they look like tiny points of light. As starlight passes through layers of moving air in Earth's atmosphere, it gets bent in different directions, making the light flicker — that's twinkling. Planets are closer and look like tiny discs, so the flickering averages out and they don't twinkle.

🌟 The Pole Star (Dhruv Tara)

One special star in the northern sky — the Pole Star — doesn't seem to move. All other stars appear to circle around it! This is because the Pole Star sits almost exactly above Earth's North Pole. Sailors and travellers have used it for navigation for centuries.

☀️ §12.2 — The Sun: Our Local Star (Deep Dive)

The Sun is just an average star — but it looks huge and bright because it's so close (only 15 crore km!). The next nearest star is over 40 trillion km away!

📊 Key Facts About the Sun

PropertyValue
Distance from Earth~150 million km (1 AU)
Surface temperature~6000°C
Core temperature~15 million °C
Diameter~1.4 million km (about 109 Earths in a row!)
Age~4.6 billion years
Made mostly ofHydrogen (74%) and helium (24%)
Light takesAbout 8 minutes 20 seconds to reach Earth

⚠️ Never look directly at the Sun — especially through binoculars or a telescope. The Sun's light is so intense that it can permanently damage your eyes. Use special solar viewers or pinhole projectors to safely view the Sun or eclipses.

🌙 §12.3 — The Moon & Its Phases (Deep Dive)

The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite — it orbits Earth every 27.3 days. It doesn't produce its own light; we see it because it reflects sunlight.

📊 Key Moon Facts

PropertyValue
Distance from Earth~384,400 km
Diameter~3,475 km (about ¼ of Earth)
Orbit around Earth27.3 days
Phase cycle (new moon → new moon)29.5 days (one month!)
Has atmosphere?No (almost none)
Has water?Yes — ice in dark polar craters (confirmed by Chandrayaan-1)

🌑🌒🌓🌔🌕🌖🌗🌘 The 8 Phases of the Moon

📋 Indian Names for Phases

  • Amavasya — New Moon (no Moon visible)
  • Shukla Paksha — Waxing fortnight (Moon growing)
  • Purnima — Full Moon
  • Krishna Paksha — Waning fortnight (Moon shrinking)

🤔 Why Do We See Different Shapes?

The Moon is always a sphere — half of it is always lit by the Sun. But as the Moon orbits Earth, we see different portions of the lit half. When the lit half faces us fully → Full Moon. When the lit half faces away → New Moon (we see no Moon).

🪐 §12.4 — The Solar System (Deep Dive)

The Sun and everything orbiting it — 8 planets, their moons, asteroids, comets, dwarf planets — together form the Solar System.

📊 The 8 Planets — Order from the Sun

#PlanetTypeSpecial FeatureMoons
1Mercury (बुध)RockySmallest planet, no atmosphere0
2Venus (शुक्र)RockyHottest planet (~465°C!); rotates backwards; "Morning/Evening Star"0
3Earth (पृथ्वी)RockyOnly planet with known life1 (Moon)
4Mars (मंगल)Rocky"Red Planet" — iron oxide gives reddish colour2 (Phobos, Deimos)
5Jupiter (बृहस्पति)Gas GiantLargest planet; "Great Red Spot" — a giant storm95+
6Saturn (शनि)Gas GiantFamous rings of ice & rock146+
7Uranus (अरुण)Ice GiantRotates on its side (97°)27
8Neptune (वरुण)Ice GiantWindiest planet (winds up to 2000 km/h!)14

🌟 Memory Mnemonic

My Very Educated Mother Just Showed Us Nebulae

(Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune)

🤔 Why is Earth Special?

  • Right distance from the Sun → not too hot, not too cold (~15°C average)
  • Has liquid water
  • Has oxygen-rich atmosphere
  • Has a magnetic field protecting from harmful solar radiation
  • Has just enough gravity to hold onto its atmosphere

All this together → the only place we know where life exists!

☄️ Other Solar System Members

  • Asteroids — small rocky bodies, mostly between Mars and Jupiter (Asteroid Belt)
  • Comets — icy bodies with long glowing tails when near the Sun (Halley's Comet, etc.)
  • Dwarf planets — like Pluto (was the 9th planet until 2006!)
  • Meteors — small space rocks that burn up in atmosphere (shooting stars)
  • Meteorites — meteors that survive and hit the ground

⭐ §12.5 — Constellations (Deep Dive)

A constellation is a group of stars that form a recognisable pattern in the sky. There are 88 official constellations. Indian astronomy has had its own classification for thousands of years.

📊 Famous Constellations & Their Indian Names

Western NameIndian / Sanskrit NameAppearanceBest Seen
Ursa Major (Big Dipper)Saptarishi (Seven Sages)Spoon / dipper shape with 7 bright starsMarch – May
Ursa Minor (Little Dipper)—Smaller spoon; contains Pole StarYear-round (north)
Orion (The Hunter)Mriga (Deer) / KalpurushHunter shape with 3 belt starsDecember – March
Leo (The Lion)SimhaLike a sitting lionFebruary – June
Scorpius (Scorpion)VrishchikaCurved scorpion with bright AntaresMay – August
Cassiopeia—W-shape (or M-shape)October – February

🧭 How to Find the Pole Star (Step-by-Step)

  1. Find Saptarishi (Ursa Major) — a "spoon" or "frying pan" of 7 bright stars.
  2. Look at the two stars at the front edge of the spoon's "bowl" — these are the pointer stars.
  3. Draw an imaginary line through them, going up.
  4. Extend that line about 5 times the distance between the two stars.
  5. You'll land on a moderately bright star — that's the Pole Star (Dhruv Tara).
  6. It always points North. Used by sailors and travellers for ages.

🇮🇳 Indian Astronomy — Nakshatras

Ancient Indian astronomers divided the sky into 27 Nakshatras (lunar mansions) — each named after a star or star group. The Moon takes about one day to cross each Nakshatra (27 days × 1 = ≈ 27.3-day orbit). These were used in calendars, agriculture and festivals.

Names like Rohini, Ashwini, Bharani, Pushya, Magha, Chitra, Swati, Vishakha come from this system. Indian astronomers Aryabhata (5th century CE) and Bhaskara II (12th century CE) made remarkable contributions to astronomy and mathematics.

🚀 §12.6 — India's Space Programme (Deep Dive)

ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation), established in 1969, is India's space agency. From a humble start carrying rocket parts on bicycles in Thumba (Kerala), ISRO is now among the top space agencies in the world!

📊 Landmark Indian Space Missions

YearMissionAchievement
1975AryabhataIndia's first satellite (named after the great mathematician)
1980SLV-3 / RohiniIndia's first launch into orbit using own rocket
1984Rakesh Sharma in spaceFirst Indian in space (aboard Soviet spacecraft)
2008Chandrayaan-1Found water on Moon — a world first!
2013Mangalyaan (MOM)India became 1st country to reach Mars in 1st attempt & cheapest Mars mission
2017PSLV-C37Launched 104 satellites in one go — world record!
2019Chandrayaan-2Orbiter still active; lander crashed
2023Chandrayaan-3First country to soft-land on Moon's South Pole; Pragyan rover did experiments
2023Aditya-L1India's first solar mission, observing the Sun from L1 point
UpcomingGaganyaanIndia's first human spaceflight mission (planned)

🌟 Famous Indians in Space & Astronomy

  • Aryabhata (476-550 CE) — proposed Earth's rotation, calculated π, gave the concept of zero
  • Bhaskara II (12th c.) — gave methods for planetary positions
  • Vikram Sarabhai — "Father of Indian Space Programme"
  • APJ Abdul Kalam — "Missile Man of India", led SLV-3 project
  • Rakesh Sharma — First Indian in space (1984); famously said "Saare Jahan Se Achchha" when asked how India looked from space
  • Kalpana Chawla & Sunita Williams — Indian-origin women astronauts at NASA
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 12

Q1. Differentiate between a star and a planet.

Answer:

StarPlanet
Produces its own lightReflects sunlight
Very hot (thousands of °C)Much cooler
Made mostly of hydrogen/helium gasMade of rocks/metals or gases
Twinkles in the skyDoesn't twinkle
Example: Sun, Pole StarExample: Earth, Mars, Jupiter

Q2. Why does the Moon appear in different shapes on different days?

Answer:

Half of the Moon is always lit by the Sun, but as the Moon orbits Earth, we see different amounts of the lit half. When the entire lit side faces us, we see the Full Moon; when the unlit side faces us, we see no Moon (New Moon). In between, we see crescents and gibbous shapes. The cycle takes about 29.5 days.

Q3. Name the planets of our Solar System in order from the Sun.

Answer:

Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune. (Mnemonic: My Very Educated Mother Just Showed Us Nebulae.)

Q4. Why is Earth the only planet with life?

Answer:

Earth is at the right distance from the Sun (temperature suitable for liquid water), has an oxygen-rich atmosphere, has a magnetic field protecting from harmful solar radiation, and has enough gravity to hold onto air and water. All these conditions together allow life to exist.

Q5. What is a constellation? Give three examples.

Answer:

A constellation is a group of stars forming a recognisable pattern in the sky. Examples: Ursa Major (Saptarishi), Orion (Mriga), Leo (Simha).

Q6. How can you locate the Pole Star using Ursa Major?

Answer:

Find the Saptarishi (Ursa Major) constellation — 7 stars in a spoon shape. Locate the two stars at the front of the spoon (pointer stars). Imagine a line through them upward and extend it about 5 times their distance. You will reach the moderately bright Pole Star, which always points North.

Q7. List three major achievements of ISRO.

Answer:

  1. Chandrayaan-1 (2008): Discovered water on the Moon.
  2. Mangalyaan (2013): First country to reach Mars in the first attempt.
  3. Chandrayaan-3 (2023): First country to soft-land on the Moon's South Pole.

Q8. What are asteroids, comets and meteors?

Answer:

  • Asteroids: Small rocky bodies that orbit the Sun, mostly between Mars and Jupiter (asteroid belt).
  • Comets: Icy bodies that develop long glowing tails when they come close to the Sun.
  • Meteors: Small rock pieces that burn up entering Earth's atmosphere — seen as shooting stars.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 12 (22 Questions)

  1. Stars are made mostly of ___ and helium. (hydrogen)
  2. The nearest star to Earth is ___. (Sun)
  3. Light from the Sun takes about ___ minutes to reach Earth. (8)
  4. The Moon takes about ___ days to complete one orbit of Earth. (27.3)
  5. The full Moon to next full Moon cycle is ___ days. (29.5)
  6. The Pole Star indicates the ___ direction. (north)
  7. The Indian name for Ursa Major is ___. (Saptarishi)
  8. The Indian name for Orion is ___. (Mriga)
  9. The hottest planet in our Solar System is ___. (Venus)
  10. The largest planet in our Solar System is ___. (Jupiter)
  11. The smallest planet is ___. (Mercury)
  12. The red planet is ___. (Mars)
  13. Saturn is famous for its ___. (rings)
  14. India's first satellite was ___. (Aryabhata)
  15. India's mission to the Moon's South Pole was ___. (Chandrayaan-3)
  16. India's first mission to Mars was ___. (Mangalyaan)
  17. "Father of Indian Space Programme" is ___. (Vikram Sarabhai)
  18. True or False: Planets twinkle like stars. (False)
  19. True or False: The Moon has its own light. (False — reflects Sun)
  20. True or False: Mercury has many moons. (False — none)
  21. True or False: Asteroids are mostly between Mars and Jupiter. (True)
  22. True or False: Chandrayaan-1 found water on the Moon. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 12

TermMeaning
Celestial bodyAny natural object in the sky (star, planet, moon, etc.).
StarHot ball of gas producing its own light.
PlanetBody orbiting a star, reflecting its light.
SatelliteBody orbiting a planet (Moon is Earth's natural satellite).
Solar SystemThe Sun and all bodies that orbit it.
OrbitThe path a body follows around another body.
ConstellationA recognised pattern of stars.
Pole StarStar above Earth's North Pole that doesn't appear to move.
AsteroidSmall rocky body orbiting the Sun.
CometIcy body with a long tail when near the Sun.
MeteorRock that burns up in Earth's atmosphere (shooting star).
MeteoriteMeteor that survives and reaches the ground.
PhaseShape of the Moon as seen from Earth.
NakshatraOne of 27 lunar mansions in Indian astronomy.
ISROIndian Space Research Organisation.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 12

  1. Moon Diary — Observe and draw the Moon every clear night for a full month. Note the date and phase. See the full cycle!
  2. Find the Pole Star — On a clear night, locate Saptarishi and find the Pole Star using the pointer method. Take a parent along.
  3. Solar System Model — Make a hanging mobile of the 8 planets (use thermocol balls, paint them realistically).
  4. Constellation Hunt — Use a free app (e.g. Stellarium) or printable star chart to identify 5 constellations from your terrace.
  5. ISRO Timeline Poster — Create a colourful timeline of India's space achievements from 1975 to 2024.
  6. Planet Comparison Chart — Make a chart comparing diameter, distance from Sun, number of moons and one special fact for each planet.
  7. Letter to an Astronaut — Write a letter to Rakesh Sharma or any astronaut, asking what space looked like and how India looked from there.

📋 Chapter 1 — Introduction: Why Social Science? & Locating Places on the Earth

📖 Part A: Introduction

  • What is Social Science?
  • The 5 Themes of This Textbook
  • Theme A: India & the World (Geography)
  • Theme B: Tapestry of the Past (History)
  • Theme C: Cultural Heritage
  • Theme D: Governance & Democracy
  • Theme E: Economic Life

📖 Part B: Locating Places

  • What is a Map?
  • Components of a Map (Scale, Direction, Symbols)
  • Globe vs Flat Map
  • Coordinates System
  • Latitudes (Parallels)
  • Longitudes (Meridians)
  • Prime Meridian & Greenwich
  • Time Zones & IST
  • International Date Line

Part A: Introduction — Why Social Science?

📚 What is Social Science?

Social Science helps us understand people, societies, and how the world works. It studies human beings — how they live, work, govern themselves, and relate to each other and to the Earth.

Why Study Social Science?

  • Understand our society: How people live, work, celebrate, and solve problems together
  • Become responsible citizens: Know your rights and duties in a democracy
  • Learn from the past: History teaches us what worked and what didn't
  • Appreciate diversity: India's many languages, religions, and cultures are its strength
  • Understand the economy: How goods and services reach us, why some people are poor

Part B: Locating Places on the Earth

📜 Quote from Āryabhaṭa (about 500 CE)

"The globe of the Earth stands in space, made up of water, earth, fire and air and is spherical. It is surrounded by all creatures, terrestrial as well as aquatic."

🗺️ What is a Map?

A map is a drawing or representation of the Earth's surface (or part of it) on a flat surface like paper. Maps help us find places, measure distances, and understand the layout of an area.

Three Main Components of a Map:

ComponentWhat It DoesExample
ScaleShows the ratio between map distance and real distance1 cm on map = 500 m on ground
DirectionShows which way is North (usually top of map)North arrow (↑), compass rose
SymbolsSmall pictures/marks representing real featuresBlue = water, green = forest, ✚ = hospital

🌐 Globe vs Flat Map

The Earth is nearly spherical (slightly flattened at the poles). Representing a sphere on a flat surface always causes some distortion — like trying to flatten an orange peel!

FeatureGlobe 🌍Flat Map 🗺️
ShapeSpherical (like Earth)Flat (paper/screen)
AccuracyMore accurate (same shape as Earth)Some distortion always present
ShowsWhole Earth at onceCan show any area (world, country, city)
DetailLess detail (too small)Can show lots of detail
PortableBulky, hard to carryEasy to fold and carry
Best forUnderstanding Earth's shape, rotationFinding routes, measuring distances

📐 Coordinates — Locating Any Place on Earth

Just like a seat in a cinema has a row number and seat number, every place on Earth has two coordinates:

  • Latitude (how far north or south of the Equator)
  • Longitude (how far east or west of the Prime Meridian)

Together, latitude and longitude can pinpoint any location on Earth!

🌐 Latitudes (Parallels)

Important Parallels of Latitude:

NameLatitudeSignificance
Equator0°Divides Earth into Northern & Southern hemispheres. Longest latitude.
Tropic of Cancer23.5° NSun is directly overhead here on June 21 (summer solstice). Passes through India!
Tropic of Capricorn23.5° SSun is directly overhead here on Dec 22 (winter solstice)
Arctic Circle66.5° NRegion of midnight sun and polar nights
Antarctic Circle66.5° SRegion around South Pole with extreme cold
North Pole90° NTopmost point of Earth's axis
South Pole90° SBottommost point of Earth's axis

Heat Zones of the Earth:

ZoneLocationClimate
Torrid Zone (Hot)Between Tropic of Cancer & Tropic of CapricornReceives most direct sunlight → hot all year
Temperate Zone (Moderate)Between Tropics and Arctic/Antarctic circlesModerate temperatures, 4 distinct seasons
Frigid Zone (Cold)Between Arctic/Antarctic circles and PolesVery cold, ice, snow, polar nights

🌐 Longitudes (Meridians)

Important Facts about Longitudes:

  • All meridians are equal in length (unlike parallels of latitude)
  • They are not parallel — they meet at the poles and are farthest apart at the Equator
  • There are 360 meridians (180°E + 180°W)
  • The Prime Meridian (0°) divides Earth into Eastern and Western Hemispheres
  • India's ancient astronomers used the meridian of Ujjayinī (Ujjain) as their prime meridian!

⏰ Time Zones — How Longitude Determines Time

The Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours. That means it rotates 15° every hour.

🧮 Key Formula:

Time difference = Longitude difference ÷ 15 (in hours)

Moving EAST → time is AHEAD (add hours)
Moving WEST → time is BEHIND (subtract hours)

Indian Standard Time (IST):

FeatureDetail
Standard Meridian of India82°30' E (82.5° East)
Passes throughMirzapur, Uttar Pradesh
IST ahead of GMT by5 hours 30 minutes
Why single time zone?India spans only ~29° of longitude (68°E to 97°E) — one time zone is practical

Example:

If it is 12:00 noon at Greenwich (GMT), what time is it in India?

IST = GMT + 5:30 = 5:30 PM in India

Why does the Sun set earlier in Assam than Gujarat?

Assam is at about 94°E and Gujarat at about 70°E. The difference is ~24°. Since 15° = 1 hour, the time difference is about 1 hour 36 minutes. The Sun rises and sets earlier in the EAST. But since India uses one time zone (IST), clocks show the same time in both places — even though sunlight differs!

📅 International Date Line (IDL)

The IDL is an imaginary line at approximately 180° longitude (in the middle of the Pacific Ocean).

  • Crossing IDL going EAST → subtract one day (Monday becomes Sunday)
  • Crossing IDL going WEST → add one day (Sunday becomes Monday)
  • It zigzags to avoid cutting through countries

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are the three main components of a map? Explain each.

Answer:

The three main components of a map are:

  1. Scale: The ratio between the distance on the map and the actual distance on the ground. Example: 1 cm = 500 m means 1 centimetre on the map represents 500 metres in reality.
  2. Direction: Maps usually have a north arrow showing which direction is North. From this, we can determine South, East, West, and intermediate directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
  3. Symbols: These are small pictures or marks used to represent real features like rivers (blue lines), forests (green areas), roads, railways, hospitals, schools, etc. They are explained in the map's legend (key).

Q2. What is the difference between a globe and a flat map?

Answer:

GlobeFlat Map
Spherical model of EarthEarth's surface on flat paper
More accurate (same shape as Earth)Always has some distortion
Shows whole Earth at onceCan show any area in detail
Difficult to carryEasy to carry and fold
Cannot show small areas in detailCan show even a city in detail

A globe is better for understanding Earth's shape, rotation, and coordinates. A flat map is better for everyday use like finding routes.

Q3. What are latitudes and longitudes? How are they different?

Answer:

Latitudes (Parallels)Longitudes (Meridians)
Horizontal lines running east-westVertical lines running north-south
Parallel to each otherMeet at the poles
Measure distance from Equator (N/S)Measure distance from Prime Meridian (E/W)
Range: 0° to 90° N/SRange: 0° to 180° E/W
Equator is the longestAll meridians are equal length
Determine climate/heat zonesDetermine time zones

Q4. What is IST? Why does India have only one time zone?

Answer:

IST (Indian Standard Time) is the time followed throughout India. It is based on the standard meridian of 82°30' E longitude which passes through Mirzapur, Uttar Pradesh.

IST is 5 hours 30 minutes ahead of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).

India has only one time zone because its total longitude span is about 29° (68°E to 97°E), which equals about 2 hours of time difference — small enough to use a single standard time for convenience.

Q5. What is the International Date Line? What happens when you cross it?

Answer:

The International Date Line (IDL) is an imaginary line at approximately 180° longitude, running through the Pacific Ocean. It marks where one calendar day ends and the next begins.

  • If you cross the IDL travelling eastward, you go back one day (Monday → Sunday)
  • If you cross the IDL travelling westward, you go forward one day (Sunday → Monday)

The line zigzags in some places to avoid cutting a country into two different days.

Q6. If it is 10 AM at Greenwich (GMT), what is the time in India?

Answer:

IST = GMT + 5 hours 30 minutes

So if GMT = 10:00 AM, then IST = 10:00 + 5:30 = 3:30 PM

Q7. Name the important parallels of latitude and their significance.

Answer:

  1. Equator (0°) — Divides Earth into Northern and Southern hemispheres; longest latitude
  2. Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N) — Northern boundary of Torrid Zone; sun directly overhead on June 21; passes through India
  3. Tropic of Capricorn (23.5°S) — Southern boundary of Torrid Zone; sun directly overhead on December 22
  4. Arctic Circle (66.5°N) — Start of the north Frigid Zone; experiences midnight sun
  5. Antarctic Circle (66.5°S) — Start of the south Frigid Zone; extreme cold
🌐 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 1Why Social Science + Locating Places on Earth — explained with maps, examples and Indian context

📚 Part A — What is Social Science? (Deep Dive)
