Social Science is the study of people, society and how human beings live together. Unlike natural sciences (which study atoms, plants, planets), social sciences study us — our families, languages, rulers, jobs, beliefs and the places we live.

📊 The Main Branches of Social Science

BranchStudiesKey Question
GeographyEarth's surface, places, people-environment relationsWhere? Why there?
HistoryEvents of the past — rulers, wars, civilisationsWhat happened? Why?
Civics / Political ScienceGovernment, rights, laws, citizenshipHow do we govern ourselves?
EconomicsHow people earn, spend, share resourcesHow are needs satisfied?
Sociology / CultureCustoms, languages, beliefs, traditionsHow do we live together?

🎯 Why Do We Study Social Science?

  • To know who we are — our identity, heritage and country
  • To learn from past mistakes and successes
  • To be a responsible citizen — vote wisely, respect laws, defend rights
  • To understand diversity — different languages, religions, food, dress
  • To understand the economy — why prices rise, why farmers protest, how jobs are created
  • To solve real-world problems — pollution, poverty, inequality, climate change

🇮🇳 IKS — India's Long Tradition of Social Thought

Long before "social science" was a school subject, India had thinkers studying society:

  • Kautilya / Chanakya (4th century BCE) wrote the Arthashastra — on economics, administration and statecraft.
  • Manu, Yajnavalkya — wrote on civil & criminal law.
  • Megasthenes (Greek ambassador to Chandragupta) wrote Indica — an early sociological work on Indian society.
  • Al-Biruni (11th century) wrote Tahqiq al-Hind — on Indian languages, religion, science and customs.

🗺️ Part B — What is a Map? (Deep Dive)

A map is a flat picture of a place — your room, your school, your city, your country, or the whole world. It is drawn from a bird's-eye view (as if looking down from above).

📊 Types of Maps

TypeShowsExample
Physical MapMountains, rivers, deserts, plains, oceansPhysical map of India
Political MapCountries, states, capitals, bordersWorld political map
Thematic MapOne specific theme — rainfall, population, roadsRainfall map of India
Sketch MapRough drawing, not to scale (e.g. directions to school)Hand-drawn map to your house

📋 The 5 Essential Components of a Map (DRSSL)

  1. Direction — usually North on top (shown by an arrow / compass rose)
  2. Ratio (Scale) — relationship between map distance and actual distance
  3. Symbols — small pictures or colours that represent real things (blue = water)
  4. Scale bar / Legend — explains symbols and units
  5. Labels / Title — tells what area is shown

🌐 Globe vs Flat Map (Deep Dive)

A globe is a small ball-shaped model of the Earth — it shows shapes and sizes correctly. A flat map is made by "peeling" the globe — it can show details but distorts sizes near the poles.

📊 Globe vs Map — Compared

FeatureGlobeFlat Map
ShapeSpherical (3-D)Flat (2-D)
AccuracyVery accurate shapes and sizesSome distortion
PortabilityHard to carryEasy to fold
DetailLimited (small size)Can show lots of detail
UseUnderstanding Earth as a wholeTravel, study, navigation

📐 Latitudes & Longitudes (Deep Dive)

To find any place on Earth, we use a grid of imaginary lines: latitudes (horizontal, like rungs of a ladder) and longitudes (vertical, like slices of an orange). Together they form a coordinate system.

📏 Latitudes (Parallels)

  • Run east-west, parallel to each other
  • Measured in degrees north or south of the Equator (0°)
  • Equator = 0° (the longest parallel)
  • North Pole = 90°N | South Pole = 90°S
  • Important latitudes: Equator (0°), Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N), Tropic of Capricorn (23.5°S), Arctic Circle (66.5°N), Antarctic Circle (66.5°S)

📐 Longitudes (Meridians)

  • Run north-south, from pole to pole
  • All meridians are equal in length (unlike latitudes)
  • The Prime Meridian (0°) passes through Greenwich (London, UK)
  • Earth is divided into 180° East and 180° West of the Prime Meridian
  • The line at 180° is roughly the International Date Line

🌡️ Heat Zones of the Earth (Based on Latitudes)

ZoneBetweenClimateExamples
Torrid (Tropical) ZoneTropic of Cancer ↔ Tropic of CapricornHot all yearIndia, Brazil, Central Africa
North Temperate ZoneTropic of Cancer ↔ Arctic CircleModerate; 4 seasonsUSA, China, Europe
South Temperate ZoneTropic of Capricorn ↔ Antarctic CircleModerate; 4 seasonsArgentina, S. Africa, S. Australia
North Frigid ZoneArctic Circle ↔ North PoleExtremely coldGreenland, N. Russia
South Frigid ZoneAntarctic Circle ↔ South PoleExtremely coldAntarctica

🕒 Time Zones, GMT & IST (Deep Dive)

Since Earth rotates from west to east, the Sun rises earlier in eastern places. Different parts of the world have different local times. To handle this, the world is divided into 24 time zones, each 15° of longitude wide (because 360° ÷ 24 = 15°).

📊 Key Time Concepts

ConceptMeaning
GMT (Greenwich Mean Time)Standard time at the Prime Meridian (0°)
UTCModern replacement for GMT (Coordinated Universal Time)
IST (Indian Standard Time)GMT + 5 hours 30 minutes (based on 82.5°E)
Local timeSun-based time for any particular meridian
Date Line (180°)Crossing east → back 1 day; crossing west → forward 1 day

🇮🇳 Why India Has Only One Time Zone

India spans almost 30° of longitude — easily enough for 2 time zones. But to keep the whole country in sync (railways, business, government, broadcasting), India chose one Standard Meridian at 82.5°E (passing through Mirzapur, UP). The same time applies from Gujarat to Arunachal Pradesh.

🧮 Worked Examples — Time Conversion

  1. It is 6 AM in London (GMT). What is the time in India (IST)?
    6:00 + 5:30 = 11:30 AM IST
  2. It is 9 PM in India. What time is it in New York (GMT - 5)?
    21:00 − 5:30 (to GMT) − 5:00 (to NY) = 10:30 AM same day
  3. The sun rises first in Arunachal Pradesh or Gujarat?
    Arunachal Pradesh — it lies further east, so sees sunrise about 2 hours earlier.

🎯 Activities — Chapter 1

  1. Activity 1.A — Sketch Your School Route: Draw a simple sketch map from your home to your school. Show roads, shops, a temple/landmark and a compass direction. Don't worry about scale.
  2. Activity 1.B — Find Your Coordinates: Look up the latitude and longitude of your town/city/village using a smartphone map. Record it.
  3. Activity 1.C — Spin the Globe: Spin a globe with your eyes closed. Place a finger on it. Wherever your finger lands, write the country, continent, and which hemisphere it lies in.
  4. Activity 1.D — Time-Zone Phone Call: If you (in India) want to call a cousin in New York at 9 AM their time, at what time will you call from India?
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 1

Q1. What is the difference between a map and a globe?

Answer:

A globe is a 3-D, spherical model of the Earth that shows shapes and sizes accurately but is hard to carry. A map is a 2-D, flat drawing of the Earth (or part of it) — easy to carry and detailed, but with some distortion in size/shape.

Q2. Name the 5 essential components of a map.

Answer:

Title, Direction, Scale, Symbols (with a Legend), and a clear set of Labels.

Q3. What is the equator? Why is it called the longest parallel?

Answer:

The equator is the imaginary circle drawn around the middle of the Earth, at 0° latitude, dividing it into the Northern and Southern hemispheres. It is the longest parallel because Earth's circumference is greatest at the middle and shrinks toward the poles.

Q4. What is the Prime Meridian? Where does it pass through?

Answer:

The Prime Meridian is the longitude marked 0°. It passes through Greenwich (near London, UK) and divides the Earth into the Eastern and Western hemispheres.

Q5. Why does India have only one time zone?

Answer:

For uniformity in railways, business, broadcasting and government activities, India uses one Indian Standard Time (IST) based on the meridian at 82.5°E that passes through Mirzapur (Uttar Pradesh). IST is 5 hours 30 minutes ahead of GMT.

Q6. List the three heat zones of the Earth and one country in each.

Answer:

  • Torrid Zone — hot, e.g. India.
  • Temperate Zone — moderate, e.g. France (North) or Argentina (South).
  • Frigid Zone — extremely cold, e.g. Greenland (North) or Antarctica (South).

Q7. Why was Greenwich chosen for the Prime Meridian?

Answer:

In 1884, 25 nations met in Washington and chose Greenwich (where the Royal Observatory was located) as the Prime Meridian for global navigation, because Britain was then the leading maritime power and many sea charts already used Greenwich as their reference.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 1 (20 Questions)

  1. Social Science is the study of ___. (people / society)
  2. Geography studies ___. (Earth's surface)
  3. History studies ___. (the past)
  4. A flat drawing of the Earth is a ___. (map)
  5. A 3-D model of the Earth is a ___. (globe)
  6. Lines drawn east-west on the globe are ___. (latitudes)
  7. Lines drawn north-south are ___. (longitudes)
  8. Latitude of the equator is ___ °. (0)
  9. The Prime Meridian is at ___ °. (0)
  10. The Prime Meridian passes through ___. (Greenwich)
  11. India's standard meridian is at ___ °E. (82.5)
  12. IST = GMT + ___ hours ___ minutes. (5, 30)
  13. Tropic of Cancer is at ___ °N. (23.5)
  14. Tropic of Capricorn is at ___ °S. (23.5)
  15. The Earth is divided into ___ time zones. (24)
  16. True or False: All longitudes are of equal length. (True)
  17. True or False: All latitudes are of equal length. (False)
  18. True or False: India lies in the Torrid Zone. (True / partly)
  19. True or False: Antarctica lies in the Frigid Zone. (True)
  20. True or False: Aryabhata said the Earth is spherical. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 1

TermMeaning
Social ScienceStudy of human society and relationships.
GeographyStudy of Earth's surface and life on it.
HistoryStudy of the past.
CivicsStudy of citizenship and government.
EconomicsStudy of how people use resources.
MapFlat representation of the Earth or part of it.
GlobeSpherical model of the Earth.
ScaleRatio of map distance to actual distance.
SymbolSign on a map representing a real feature.
LegendKey explaining symbols on a map.
LatitudeEast-west line; measures distance from equator.
LongitudeNorth-south line; measures distance from Prime Meridian.
EquatorLatitude 0° — divides Earth into N & S hemispheres.
Prime MeridianLongitude 0° — passes through Greenwich.
ISTIndian Standard Time — GMT + 5 h 30 m.
Date Line180° meridian where the date changes.
HemisphereHalf of the Earth (north/south or east/west).

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 1

  1. Map of My Locality — Draw a map of your locality with title, direction, scale and symbols (school, post office, park, hospital).
  2. Latitude–Longitude Hunt — Find the coordinates of 10 famous places (Delhi, Mumbai, New York, Sydney, Tokyo, etc.) and mark them on a world outline.
  3. Time-Zone Wheel — Make a circular wheel showing 6 cities of the world and their current times.
  4. Heat-Zone Collage — Cut pictures of climates and animals; paste them under correct heat zones.
  5. Compass Walk — Take a real compass to your school ground; identify N, E, S, W and locate landmarks in each direction.

📋 Chapter 2 — Oceans and Continents

📖 Topics Covered

  • Earth — The Blue Planet
  • Distribution of Water & Land
  • The 5 Oceans in Detail
  • Ocean Life & Importance
  • The 7 Continents in Detail
  • Islands
  • India's Coastline & Islands

✍️ Key Facts

  • 71% Water, 29% Land
  • Pacific = largest ocean (1/3 of Earth)
  • Asia = largest continent
  • Australia = smallest continent
  • Greenland = largest island
  • Indian Ocean named after India!

Chapter 2: Oceans and Continents

📜 Quote from Jules Verne (1870)

"The ocean is everything. It covers seven-tenths of the terrestrial globe. Its breath is pure and healthy... The globe began with the ocean, so to speak, and who knows if it will not end with it."

🌊 Earth — The Blue Planet

When seen from space, Earth appears blue because about 71% of its surface is covered with water. Only 29% is land. Early astronauts lovingly called it the "Blue Planet".

Distribution of Water and Land:

  • The Southern Hemisphere has more water than the Northern Hemisphere
  • The Northern Hemisphere has more land than the Southern Hemisphere
  • Ocean water is salty (unfit for drinking)
  • Freshwater (rivers, lakes, glaciers, groundwater) is only a tiny fraction of total water

🌊 The Five Oceans (Largest to Smallest)

Special Focus: The Indian Ocean

FeatureDetails
Named afterIndia — the only ocean named after a country!
Bounded byAsia (north), Africa (west), Australia (east), Southern Ocean (south)
India's seasArabian Sea (west coast) and Bay of Bengal (east coast)
Trade routesAncient trade routes connecting India with Arabia, Africa, Southeast Asia
MonsoonsIndian Ocean monsoon winds bring rain to India
Tsunami 2004Devastating tsunami killed 2+ lakh people in 14 countries (caused by earthquake near Indonesia)

🌍 The Seven Continents

Detailed Continent Comparison:

ContinentHemispheresKey FeatureMajor Countries/Region
AsiaNorthern + EasternLargest & most populatedIndia, China, Japan, Saudi Arabia
AfricaBoth N & SHottest; Sahara Desert; Nile RiverEgypt, Kenya, South Africa, Nigeria
North AmericaNorthern + WesternGreat Lakes; Grand CanyonUSA, Canada, Mexico
South AmericaSouthern + WesternAmazon rainforest; AndesBrazil, Argentina, Peru
AntarcticaSouthernColdest; no countries; only researchNo permanent people
EuropeNorthern + EasternSmallest by area after AustraliaUK, France, Germany, Italy
Australia/OceaniaSouthern + EasternIsland continent; unique wildlifeAustralia, New Zealand

🏝️ Islands

An island is a piece of land completely surrounded by water on all sides. (Continents are also surrounded by water, but they are too large to be called islands.)

Important Islands:

IslandLocationSignificance
GreenlandNorth Atlantic (near Canada)World's LARGEST island
Andaman & NicobarBay of BengalIndia's islands (572 islands)
LakshadweepArabian SeaIndia's coral islands (36 islands)
Sri LankaIndian Ocean (south of India)India's closest island neighbour
MadagascarIndian Ocean (east of Africa)4th largest island, unique wildlife

🌊 Why Are Oceans Important?

  • Climate regulation: Oceans absorb heat and regulate Earth's temperature; without them, Earth would be a desert
  • Oxygen production: Ocean plants (phytoplankton) produce more than 50% of Earth's oxygen — oceans are "the planet's lungs"!
  • Water cycle: Evaporation from oceans creates clouds → rainfall → rivers → back to ocean
  • Food: Fishing provides food for billions of people (fish, prawns, seaweed)
  • Trade routes: Ships carry 80% of world's goods across oceans
  • Monsoons: India's monsoon rains come from moisture picked up over the Indian Ocean
  • Marine life: Oceans host incredible biodiversity — coral reefs, whales, dolphins, turtles, fish

⚠️ Threats to Oceans

Oceans face serious threats from: plastic pollution, oil spills, overfishing, coral bleaching (due to warming), and rising sea levels (due to climate change). We must protect our oceans!

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why is Earth called the "Blue Planet"?

Answer:

Earth is called the "Blue Planet" because about 71% of its surface is covered with water (oceans, seas, rivers, lakes). When seen from outer space, this vast expanse of water gives Earth a distinctive blue colour. Early astronauts who saw Earth from space lovingly gave it this name. The remaining 29% is land (continents and islands).

Q2. Name the five oceans in order of size. Which is the largest and smallest?

Answer:

The five oceans from largest to smallest are:

  1. Pacific Ocean — largest (covers 1/3 of Earth), deepest (Mariana Trench: 11,034 m)
  2. Atlantic Ocean — 2nd largest, 'S'-shaped, busiest for shipping/trade
  3. Indian Ocean — 3rd largest, warmest ocean, named after India
  4. Southern Ocean — 4th largest, surrounds Antarctica, very cold with icebergs
  5. Arctic Ocean — smallest and shallowest, around the North Pole, mostly frozen

Q3. Why is the Indian Ocean special for India?

Answer:

The Indian Ocean is special for India because:

  • It is the only ocean named after a country — India!
  • India is located on its northern shore, with the Arabian Sea on the west coast and the Bay of Bengal on the east coast
  • It has been an important trade route for thousands of years, connecting India with Arabia, Africa, and Southeast Asia
  • The monsoon winds from the Indian Ocean bring vital rainfall to India
  • India's islands — Andaman & Nicobar (Bay of Bengal) and Lakshadweep (Arabian Sea) — are in this ocean

Q4. Name the seven continents. Which is the largest and smallest?

Answer:

The seven continents from largest to smallest are:

  1. Asia (largest, most populated — includes India, China)
  2. Africa (2nd largest, hottest — Sahara Desert, Nile River)
  3. North America (3rd — USA, Canada, Mexico)
  4. South America (4th — Amazon rainforest, Andes mountains)
  5. Antarctica (5th — coldest, no permanent population, all ice)
  6. Europe (6th — many developed countries)
  7. Australia/Oceania (smallest — island continent, unique wildlife)

Q5. What is the difference between an island and a continent?

Answer:

IslandContinent
Smaller piece of landVery large landmass
Surrounded by waterAlso surrounded by water
Example: Greenland, Sri LankaExample: Asia, Africa
Thousands exist worldwideOnly 7 exist

The key difference is size. Australia is the smallest continent but is still much larger than Greenland, the largest island.

Q6. Why are oceans important for life on Earth?

Answer:

Oceans are vital for life on Earth because:

  1. Climate regulation: They absorb heat and keep Earth's temperature moderate
  2. Oxygen: Ocean plants produce over 50% of Earth's oxygen
  3. Water cycle: Evaporation from oceans → clouds → rain → rivers → agriculture
  4. Food: Fish and seafood feed billions of people
  5. Trade: Ships carry 80% of global goods across oceans
  6. Monsoons: Moisture from the Indian Ocean brings rain to India for farming

Without oceans, there would be no rainfall, no moderate climate, and very little life on land!

🌊 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 2Oceans and Continents — explored in depth with maps, facts and global examples

🌍 The 7 Continents (Deep Dive)

A continent is a very large, continuous landmass on Earth. There are seven continents. Let us look at each in detail.

📊 Continents at a Glance

#ContinentLargest countryFamous LandformNote
1AsiaRussia (also in Europe)Himalayas, Gobi DesertLargest, most populated; India is here
2AfricaAlgeriaSahara Desert, Mt Kilimanjaro, R. NileCradle of human life; "the Dark Continent"
3North AmericaCanadaRocky Mts, Grand Canyon, Niagara FallsHas 3 large countries: Canada, USA, Mexico
4South AmericaBrazilAmazon Forest, Andes, Atacama DesertHome of football and tango!
5AntarcticaNo countriesIce sheets, glaciers, no permanent populationColdest; reserved for science
6EuropeRussia (part)Alps, River DanubeMany small developed countries
7Australia/OceaniaAustraliaGreat Barrier Reef, Outback DesertSmallest continent; called "island continent"

🌏 Hemispheres & Continents

  • Northern Hemisphere: Most of Asia, Europe, North America, half of Africa
  • Southern Hemisphere: Most of South America, Africa (lower half), Australia, Antarctica
  • India lies in the Northern and Eastern hemispheres

🌊 The 5 Oceans (Deep Dive)

Oceans are huge bodies of salt water that cover about 71% of the Earth. They are interconnected and form one massive "world ocean", but we divide it into 5 oceans for convenience.

📊 Oceans at a Glance

#OceanLocationFamous Features
1PacificBetween Asia/Australia & AmericasLargest; deepest point — Mariana Trench (~11 km)
2AtlanticBetween Americas & Europe/Africa"S"-shaped; busiest trade route
3IndianSouth of Asia, east of AfricaOnly ocean named after a country (India); brings monsoon
4Southern (Antarctic)Around AntarcticaColdest; strong currents
5ArcticAround North PoleSmallest, mostly frozen

🌊 Why is the Indian Ocean Special for India?

  • India has the longest coastline on the Indian Ocean (~7517 km)
  • The Indian Ocean's warm waters fuel the monsoon winds that bring rain
  • Major Indian ports — Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Visakhapatnam — open into it
  • India's navy plays a key role in keeping these waters safe
  • Ancient trade routes (Roman, Arab, Chinese) all passed through the Indian Ocean

🌊 Why is Ocean Water Salty?

Rain water flowing over rocks dissolves minerals and carries them to the sea. The sea water evaporates (leaving salt behind), but more salt is constantly added. Over millions of years, oceans became salty. Average salinity is about 35 g per litre.

🇮🇳 IKS Corner — India's Ocean Heritage

  • Lothal (Gujarat) — World's oldest known dockyard, built by the Indus Valley people ~4500 years ago.
  • Chola Navy (10th c.) — Conquered parts of Southeast Asia and Sri Lanka.
  • Sangam-age maritime trade — Tamil ports traded with Rome, Egypt and the Persian Gulf.
  • "Sagarmala" Project — Modern initiative to develop India's ports and coastal economy.

🎯 Activities — Chapter 2

  1. Activity 2.A — Memory Game: List the 7 continents in order of size. Then list the 5 oceans. Time yourself!
  2. Activity 2.B — Map Tracing: Trace a world map. Colour and label each continent and ocean.
  3. Activity 2.C — Coastline Quiz: Name 5 Indian cities on the Indian Ocean coast.
  4. Activity 2.D — Ocean Animals: List 3 animals each from the Arctic Ocean (e.g. polar bears nearby) and Indian Ocean (e.g. dolphins).
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 2

Q1. What is a continent? Name the 7 continents in order of size.

Answer:

A continent is a very large, continuous landmass. In order of size (largest to smallest): Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe, Australia/Oceania.

Q2. Name the 5 oceans and the largest among them.

Answer:

Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern (Antarctic) and Arctic. The Pacific is the largest and deepest.

Q3. Why is the Indian Ocean named after India?

Answer:

Because India has the longest coastline on this ocean, the largest population around it, and a long history of maritime trade through it. It is the only ocean named after a country.

Q4. Why is no country allowed to settle in Antarctica?

Answer:

Under the Antarctic Treaty (1959), signed by many countries (including India), Antarctica is to be used only for peaceful, scientific purposes. No mining, no military activity and no permanent population is allowed. India has its own research stations there — Maitri and Bharati.

Q5. Distinguish between an island and a continent.

Answer:

Both are landmasses surrounded by water. A continent is very large (millions of square km); an island is much smaller. Australia is the smallest continent and still bigger than the largest island, Greenland.

Q6. Why is ocean water salty?

Answer:

Rivers carry tiny amounts of dissolved minerals (mainly salts) from land to the sea. The sea water keeps evaporating but the salt stays behind. Over millions of years, the salt has accumulated, making the oceans salty.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 2 (18 Questions)

  1. A very large continuous landmass is called a ___. (continent)
  2. The number of continents on Earth is ___. (7)
  3. The largest continent by area is ___. (Asia)
  4. The smallest continent is ___. (Australia/Oceania)
  5. The continent with no permanent population is ___. (Antarctica)
  6. The number of oceans is ___. (5)
  7. The largest ocean is the ___. (Pacific)
  8. The deepest point in the ocean is the ___ Trench. (Mariana)
  9. The only ocean named after a country is the ___. (Indian)
  10. The smallest, frozen ocean is the ___. (Arctic)
  11. Ocean around Antarctica is the ___ Ocean. (Southern)
  12. India's longest coastline state is ___. (Gujarat)
  13. India's research station in Antarctica is named ___. (Maitri / Bharati)
  14. True or False: Africa is bigger than North America. (True)
  15. True or False: Europe is the smallest continent. (False — Australia is)
  16. True or False: India lies in the Eastern hemisphere. (True)
  17. True or False: All oceans are connected. (True)
  18. True or False: Sahara desert is in South America. (False — Africa)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 2

TermMeaning
ContinentA very large continuous landmass.
OceanA vast body of salt water.
IslandA small piece of land surrounded by water.
PeninsulaLand almost surrounded by water (e.g. India).
StraitNarrow water passage joining two seas.
Gulf / BayPart of a sea/ocean cutting into the land.
CoastlineBorder between land and sea.
MonsoonSeasonal winds, especially over the Indian Ocean.
Antarctic Treaty1959 agreement to keep Antarctica peaceful and scientific.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 2

  1. World Map Poster — Make a coloured map showing all 7 continents and 5 oceans with labels.
  2. Continent Profile Cards — Make a card per continent: area, population, famous landmark, capital city of largest country.
  3. Ocean Diary — Read about one ocean for a week. Write 5 fun facts you didn't know.
  4. Sagarmala Booklet — Research India's port-development project and make a small booklet.
  5. Antarctica Story — Imagine you are a scientist at Bharati station. Write a 1-page diary entry.

📋 Chapter 3 — Landforms and Life

📖 Topics Covered

  • What are Landforms?
  • Mountains (types, features, life)
  • Mountain Ranges of the World
  • Mountain Animals & Plants
  • Valleys & Avalanches
  • Plateaus (features, minerals, examples)
  • Plains (formation, farming, population)
  • India's Major Landforms

✍️ Key Concepts

  • Three major landforms: Mountains, Plateaus, Plains
  • Landforms affect climate, occupation, lifestyle
  • Plains are most populated (fertile soil)
  • Plateaus are mineral-rich
  • Mountains are sources of rivers

Chapter 3: Landforms and Life

📜 From the Atharva Veda (Bhūmi Sūkta)

"Free from the burden of human beings, may the Earth with many heights, slopes and great plains, bearing plants endowed with varied powers, spread out for us and show us her riches! The Earth is my mother and I am her child."

🏔️ What are Landforms?

The surface of the Earth is not flat everywhere. It has various natural features called landforms — mountains, hills, plateaus, plains, valleys, deserts, etc. The three major landforms are:

🏔️ Mountains in Detail

Mountains are very high landforms with steep slopes and pointed or rounded peaks. They rise at least 600 metres above the surrounding land.

Types of Mountains:

TypeHow FormedFeaturesExample
Young Fold MountainsEarth's plates pushing against each otherTall, sharp peaks, still growing!Himalayas, Alps, Andes
Old Fold MountainsFormed long ago, worn down by erosionShorter, rounded topsAravalli (India), Appalachians (USA)
Volcanic MountainsLava piling up from eruptionsCone-shapedMt. Fuji (Japan), Mt. Kilimanjaro
Block MountainsLand pushed up between faultsFlat top, steep sidesVindhyas, Satpuras (India)

Famous Mountain Ranges:

Mountain RangeContinentHighest PeakHeight
HimalayasAsiaMt. Everest8,849 m (world's tallest!)
AndesSouth AmericaMt. Aconcagua6,961 m (longest range)
AlpsEuropeMont Blanc4,808 m
RockiesNorth AmericaMt. Elbert4,401 m
Western GhatsIndiaAnamudi2,695 m
Eastern GhatsIndiaMahendragiri1,501 m

Life in the Mountains:

  • Climate: Cold (temperature decreases with altitude), heavy snowfall at high altitudes
  • Farming: Difficult due to steep slopes; terrace farming (step-like fields cut into slopes)
  • Crops: Tea, coffee, spices, fruits (apples, oranges), potatoes
  • Animals: Snow leopard, ibex, Himalayan tahr, yak, golden eagle, mountain hare
  • People: Sparse population; occupations include herding, forestry, tourism
  • Tourism: Hill stations (Shimla, Manali, Darjeeling), trekking, skiing

Importance of Mountains:

  • Source of rivers (melting snow/glaciers feed rivers like Ganga, Yamuna)
  • Block cold winds, affect climate and rainfall patterns
  • Rich in forests → timber, medicines, biodiversity
  • Store water as glaciers (natural reservoirs)
  • Sacred in many cultures (Mt. Kailash, Mt. Everest/Chomolungma)

📜 Sacred Mountains

Many cultures worship mountains as sacred. Tibetans call Mt. Everest "Chomolungma" (Mother Goddess of the World). Nepalis call it "Sagarmatha" (Goddess of the Sky). Mt. Kailash in Tibet is sacred to Hindus, Buddhists, Jains, and Bon followers.

🏜️ Plateaus in Detail

A plateau is an elevated area of land with a flat top and steep sides. It stands higher than the surrounding plains but is not as pointed as a mountain.

Features of Plateaus:

  • Flat or gently rolling top surface
  • Steep slopes/cliffs on sides
  • Moderate elevation (higher than plains, lower than mountains)
  • Rich in minerals — many mines are located on plateaus
  • Waterfalls form where rivers flow off plateau edges

Famous Plateaus and Their Resources:

PlateauLocationKey Resources/Features
Deccan PlateauIndia (most of south India)Black soil (good for cotton), iron, manganese
Chhota Nagpur PlateauIndia (Jharkhand)Iron ore, coal, manganese — "Ruhr of India"
African PlateauAfricaGold, diamonds, copper
Tibetan PlateauAsia (Tibet/China)Highest plateau in world — "Roof of the World"
Western Australian PlateauAustraliaGold, iron ore

Life on Plateaus:

  • Farming: Moderate; lava plateaus have fertile black soil (cotton, sugarcane)
  • Mining: Major occupation — coal, iron, diamonds, gold
  • Population: Moderate (less than plains, more than mountains)
  • Climate: Generally moderate; can be dry (rain shadow effect)

🌾 Plains in Detail

Plains are flat, low-lying areas usually formed by rivers depositing soil (alluvium) over millions of years. They are the most fertile and densely populated areas on Earth.

How Plains Form:

Features of Plains:

FeatureDetail
SurfaceFlat or gently sloping
SoilVery fertile (alluvial — deposited by rivers)
FarmingExcellent — rice, wheat, sugarcane, cotton, jute
WaterRivers flow through; easy irrigation
TransportEasy to build roads, railways, airports
PopulationVery dense — most people live on plains
CitiesMost major cities are on plains (Delhi, Kolkata, Lucknow)

The Indo-Gangetic Plain (India's Great Plain):

  • Stretches from Punjab to West Bengal — about 2,400 km long
  • Formed by rivers: Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra and their tributaries
  • One of the most fertile regions in the world
  • Crops: Rice, wheat, sugarcane, maize, cotton, jute, barley
  • Most populated region of India
  • Major cities: Delhi, Lucknow, Patna, Kolkata, Varanasi, Prayagraj

📊 Complete Comparison: Mountains vs Plateaus vs Plains

FeatureMountains 🏔️Plateaus 🏜️Plains 🌾
HeightVery high (>600m above surroundings)Moderately elevated, flat topLow-lying, near sea level
SurfaceSteep slopes, peaked/rounded topsFlat top, steep sidesFlat or gently sloping
SoilThin, rockyRocky; sometimes fertile (lava)Very thick, fertile (alluvial)
ClimateCold, windy, heavy snowfallModerate; sometimes dryModerate to hot
WaterRivers originate (glaciers/snow)Waterfalls at edgesRivers flow through, easy irrigation
FarmingDifficult (terrace farming)ModerateExcellent (main food-producing areas)
ResourcesTimber, hydel power, tourismMinerals (iron, coal, gold, diamonds)Agriculture, cities, transport
PopulationSparseModerateVery dense
India exampleHimalayas, Western GhatsDeccan Plateau, Chhota NagpurIndo-Gangetic Plain
TransportDifficult (tunnels, winding roads)ModerateEasy (flat → roads, railways)

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are the three major landforms? Describe each with examples.

Answer:

  1. Mountains: Very high landforms with steep slopes and peaks. They rise at least 600m above surrounding land. Examples: Himalayas (Asia), Alps (Europe), Andes (South America). The highest mountain is Mt. Everest (8,849 m).
  2. Plateaus: Elevated areas with a flat top and steep sides. They are moderately high and rich in minerals. Examples: Deccan Plateau (India), Tibetan Plateau ("Roof of the World"), African Plateau (gold, diamonds).
  3. Plains: Flat, low-lying areas formed by river deposits. They have the most fertile soil and highest population. Examples: Indo-Gangetic Plain (India), Great Plains (USA), Nile Plain (Egypt).

Q2. Why are plains the most densely populated areas?

Answer:

Plains are the most densely populated because:

  • Fertile soil: Alluvial soil deposited by rivers is very fertile → excellent for agriculture (rice, wheat, sugarcane)
  • Flat land: Easy to build houses, roads, railways, airports, and factories
  • Water supply: Rivers flow through plains providing water for drinking, irrigation, and industry
  • Moderate climate: Neither too cold (like mountains) nor too dry (like some plateaus)
  • Easy transportation: Flat terrain allows easy movement of people and goods
  • Job opportunities: Factories, markets, offices are concentrated on plains

Q3. How are mountains important for us? List at least 5 ways.

Answer:

  1. Source of rivers: Snow and glaciers melt to form rivers (Ganga from Gangotri glacier)
  2. Rainfall: Mountains block monsoon winds forcing them to rise and cause rain
  3. Climate shield: Himalayas block cold Central Asian winds from entering India
  4. Forests & biodiversity: Mountain forests provide timber, medicines, and habitat for wildlife
  5. Tourism: Hill stations, trekking, skiing attract tourists (Shimla, Manali, Darjeeling)
  6. Defence: Mountains act as natural barriers protecting countries
  7. Water storage: Glaciers store water like natural reservoirs, releasing it slowly

Q4. What is the difference between young and old mountains?

Answer:

Young MountainsOld Mountains
Formed recently (in geological time)Formed very long ago
Tall, sharp, pointed peaksShort, rounded tops
Still growing! (tectonic activity)Worn down by erosion over time
Steep slopesGentle slopes
Example: Himalayas, Alps, AndesExample: Aravallis (India), Appalachians (USA)

Q5. Why are plateaus rich in minerals? Name some minerals found on Indian plateaus.

Answer:

Plateaus are rich in minerals because they are made of old, hard rocks that contain mineral deposits formed millions of years ago. Mining on plateaus is easier because mineral veins are close to the surface.

Minerals found on Indian plateaus:

  • Chhota Nagpur Plateau (Jharkhand): Iron ore, coal, manganese, mica — called "Ruhr of India"
  • Deccan Plateau: Iron ore, manganese, limestone, bauxite
  • Karnataka Plateau: Gold (Kolar Gold Fields), iron ore

Q6. Describe the Indo-Gangetic Plain and its importance.

Answer:

The Indo-Gangetic Plain is a vast alluvial plain stretching about 2,400 km from Punjab in the west to West Bengal in the east. It is formed by the deposits of rivers Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra and their tributaries.

Importance:

  • Most fertile region of India — thick alluvial soil supports multiple crops
  • Food bowl: Produces rice, wheat, sugarcane, cotton, jute, maize
  • Most populated: States like UP, Bihar, West Bengal have highest population density
  • Major cities: Delhi, Lucknow, Varanasi, Patna, Kolkata are all on this plain
  • Easy transport: Flat land allows dense road and railway networks
  • River transport: Rivers like Ganga are used for transportation
⛰️ In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 3Landforms and Life — mountains, plateaus, plains, deserts, coasts & the people who live there

🌍 The Earth's Surface — Major Landforms (Deep Dive)

The Earth's surface is not flat — it has different shapes: mountains, hills, plateaus, plains, valleys, deserts and coastal areas. These are called landforms. Each landform supports a different kind of life and economy.

📊 Comparing Landforms

LandformHeight/LookClimateMain ActivitiesIndian Example
MountainsVery high, steep, peaksCool/coldTourism, herding, hydroelectricityHimalayas, Aravallis
HillsSmall, roundedModerateTea, coffee, tourismNilgiris, Western Ghats
PlateausFlat-topped, raisedVariableMining of minerals, dryland farmingDeccan, Chhota Nagpur
PlainsFlat, low-lyingHot summers, cool wintersAgriculture, dense settlementIndo-Gangetic Plain
DesertsSandy / rocky, dryVery hot or very coldAnimal rearing, oasis farmingThar (Rajasthan)
Coastal PlainsFlat, near seaHumidFishing, trade, tourism, portsKonkan, Coromandel

🏔️ Mountains & Life (Deep Dive)

📊 Young vs Old Mountains

YoungOld
Sharp, tall peaks — still growingRounded, worn down by erosion
Himalayas (Asia), Alps (Europe), Andes (S America), Rockies (N America)Aravallis (India — oldest!), Appalachians (USA), Urals (Russia)

🇮🇳 The Himalayas — India's Crown

  • Stretch across 5 countries: India, Pakistan, Nepal, China, Bhutan
  • Contain Mount Everest (8848 m, world's highest) and 9 of the world's top 10 highest peaks
  • Source of major rivers: Ganga, Yamuna, Brahmaputra, Indus
  • Block cold winds from Central Asia — protect India from severe winter
  • Force monsoon winds to rise → rain → fertile plains
  • India's "Third Pole" — contains huge glaciers, frozen freshwater reserves

👥 Life in the Mountains

  • People build terraced farms on slopes (rice, potatoes, apples)
  • Wool industries — sheep adapt well to cold
  • Tourism & pilgrimage (Kedarnath, Vaishno Devi, Manali)
  • Houses are sloping-roof to slide off snow
  • Hydroelectric power from fast-flowing mountain rivers

🟫 Plateaus & Life (Deep Dive)

Plateaus are flat-topped raised lands. They are usually rich in minerals and form the backbone of many industries.

📊 Major Plateaus of India

PlateauRegionFamous For
Deccan PlateauCentral & South IndiaCotton, sugarcane, iron ore, bauxite
Chhota Nagpur PlateauJharkhand, Odisha, WBCoal, iron, mica — India's "Ruhr"
Malwa PlateauMadhya PradeshCotton, soybean
Karnataka PlateauSouth IndiaGold (Kolar fields), software (Bengaluru)

💎 Famous World Plateaus

  • Tibetan Plateau — "Roof of the World" (highest)
  • African Plateau — covers most of Africa
  • Brazilian Plateau — South America

🌾 Plains, Deserts & Coasts (Deep Dive)

🌾 Plains — Cradles of Civilisation

Most ancient civilisations grew on plains because they had water, fertile soil and were easy to cultivate. Indo-Gangetic plain is the most fertile and densely populated plain on Earth.

  • The Nile valley birthed Egyptian civilisation
  • The Tigris-Euphrates plain birthed Mesopotamia
  • The Indus-Saraswati plain birthed Harappan civilisation
  • The Hwang-Ho plain birthed Chinese civilisation

🏜️ Deserts — Land of Sand and Survival

Hot DesertsCold Deserts
Sahara (Africa) — world's largest hot desertGobi (Mongolia)
Thar (India/Pakistan)Ladakh, India
Arabian, Kalahari (Africa)Antarctica (world's largest cold desert!)

Life in the Thar Desert: camels for transport ("ship of the desert"), loose cotton clothes, mud houses with thick walls, oasis farming, festivals like the famous Pushkar Camel Fair.

🏖️ Coastal Plains & Islands

India has two coasts:

  • Western coast (Konkan + Malabar): Mumbai, Goa, Mangalore, Kochi — narrow plain with hills behind
  • Eastern coast (Coromandel + Northern Circars): Chennai, Visakhapatnam, Puri — wider plain with major deltas (Ganga, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri)

India has two island groups: Lakshadweep (Arabian Sea, coral) and Andaman & Nicobar (Bay of Bengal, volcanic).

🇮🇳 IKS & Activities — Chapter 3

🇮🇳 Indian Wisdom about Landforms

  • The Saptarishi peaks in the Himalayas are linked with sages of Indian mythology.
  • The Aravalli Range is one of the oldest in the world — Mount Abu's Dilwara temples are there.
  • Vedic literature mentions rivers like Saraswati, Sindhu, Ganga — showing how plains shaped Indian life.
  • Camels (Thar) and yaks (Ladakh) are special adaptations to India's deserts.

🎯 Activities

  1. Activity 3.A — Landform Diary: Write a one-day diary for a child in (a) Himalayas, (b) Thar Desert, (c) Coastal Kerala.
  2. Activity 3.B — Map Marking: On an outline of India, mark Himalayas, Deccan Plateau, Thar Desert, Indo-Gangetic Plain, Eastern & Western Ghats.
  3. Activity 3.C — Match the Crop: Match: tea–hills, rice–plains, cotton–plateau, dates–oasis.
  4. Activity 3.D — Mountain Music: Find one folk song or dance from a hill state and learn its tune.
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 3

Q1. List the 5 major landforms found on Earth.

Answer:

Mountains, hills, plateaus, plains and deserts (plus coastal plains/islands as additional landforms).

Q2. Why are the Aravallis called the oldest mountains?

Answer:

They formed hundreds of millions of years ago. Over time, wind and water have eroded their tall peaks into rounded, low hills — typical signs of very old mountains.

Q3. How do the Himalayas help India?

Answer:

They block cold winds, force monsoon clouds to give rain, are a source of major rivers, prevent invasions from the north, and provide forests, minerals and tourism.

Q4. Why are most ancient civilisations found on plains?

Answer:

Plains have flat fertile land, easy access to water (rivers), and allow easy movement of people and goods. These conditions are perfect for farming and large settlements, which gave rise to early civilisations like Harappa, Egypt and Mesopotamia.

Q5. How do people survive in the Thar Desert?

Answer:

They wear loose, light cotton clothes; live in mud houses with thick walls (keep cool); rely on camels for transport; depend on rainwater storage and oases for water; rear sheep, goats and camels for living; and grow crops like bajra and millets that need little water.

Q6. Why is the Deccan Plateau important?

Answer:

The Deccan Plateau has rich black soil ideal for cotton, vast mineral deposits (iron ore, bauxite), several big rivers (Krishna, Godavari) and many famous cities like Hyderabad and Pune. It is central to India's mineral and industrial economy.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 3 (18 Questions)

  1. The highest mountain in the world is ___. (Mount Everest)
  2. The world's longest mountain range is the ___. (Andes)
  3. The oldest mountain range in India is ___. (Aravalli)
  4. The "Roof of the World" is the ___ Plateau. (Tibetan)
  5. India's "Ruhr" (mineral plateau) is the ___ Plateau. (Chhota Nagpur)
  6. The most fertile plain in India is the ___ Plain. (Indo-Gangetic)
  7. The largest hot desert is the ___. (Sahara)
  8. India's main hot desert is the ___. (Thar)
  9. Camel is the "ship of the ___". (desert)
  10. Largest cold desert (continent) is ___. (Antarctica)
  11. India's western coast is called ___ coast. (Konkan/Malabar)
  12. Andaman and Nicobar lie in the ___ Sea. (Bay of Bengal)
  13. Lakshadweep islands lie in the ___ Sea. (Arabian)
  14. True or False: Hills are smaller than mountains. (True)
  15. True or False: Plateaus are usually rich in minerals. (True)
  16. True or False: Coastal areas have many ports. (True)
  17. True or False: Most people live on plateaus, not plains. (False)
  18. True or False: Tea grows best on hill slopes. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 3

TermMeaning
LandformNatural shape of the Earth's surface.
MountainVery high natural elevation of land.
HillSmaller, lower than a mountain.
PlateauFlat-topped raised land.
PlainFlat or gently rolling low-lying land.
DesertRegion of very low rainfall.
OasisFertile spot in a desert with water source.
Coastal plainFlat land along the sea.
DeltaTriangular land at a river's mouth.
Alluvial soilFertile soil deposited by rivers.
Terraced farmingFarming on stepped slopes of hills.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 3

  1. Landform Model — Build a clay or thermocol model showing mountains, plateau, plain, river and coast.
  2. Photo Album — Collect photos (newspaper/internet) of 5 famous Indian landforms; write 3 lines about each.
  3. Adventure Diary — Imagine you trekked the Himalayas / sailed Konkan / crossed Thar. Write a 1-page adventure story.
  4. Mineral Map — On India's outline map, mark places where iron, coal, gold, bauxite, mica are mined.
  5. Crop & Landform Chart — Match 10 crops to the landform they grow best in.

📋 Chapter 4 — Timeline and Sources of History

📖 Topics Covered

  • What is History?
  • How Do We Learn About the Past?
  • Measuring Time: CE & BCE
  • Centuries & Millennia
  • Timeline as a Tool
  • Sources of History
  • Archaeological Sources
  • Literary Sources
  • Oral Sources
  • The Beginnings of Human History
  • Early Humans: Life & Tools

✍️ Key Concepts

  • CE = Common Era (after year 1)
  • BCE = Before Common Era
  • No "Year Zero" in calendar!
  • Homo sapiens: ~3 lakh years old
  • History sources: Archaeological + Literary + Oral

Chapter 4: Timeline and Sources of History

📜 Quote

"History is an unending dialogue between the present and the past... between the society of today and the society of yesterday. We can fully understand the present only in the light of the past." — E.H. Carr

📚 What is History?

History is the study of the human past — the events, civilisations, people, and changes that have shaped our world. It tells us:

  • How people lived thousands of years ago
  • How societies developed and changed
  • What inventions and discoveries were made
  • Why certain events happened (wars, migrations, trade)
  • What we can learn from the past to build a better future

⏰ Measuring Historical Time: CE and BCE

TermFull FormMeaningExample
CECommon EraYears after year 1 (counting forward)2024 CE = current year
BCEBefore Common EraYears before year 1 (counting backward)2600 BCE = Harappan era
Century100 yearsA period of 100 years21st century = 2001–2100 CE
Millennium1000 yearsA period of 1000 years3rd millennium = 2001–3000 CE
Decade10 yearsA period of 10 years2020s = 2020–2029

⚠️ Important: No Year Zero!

In the Gregorian calendar, there is no year zero. Year 1 BCE is immediately followed by year 1 CE. This means:

  • 1st century CE = years 1 to 100
  • 1st century BCE = years 100 BCE to 1 BCE
  • To find the century: Divide the year by 100 and add 1. (e.g., 2024 ÷ 100 = 20, + 1 = 21st century)

How to Calculate Centuries:

YearCenturyHow?
2024 CE21st century CE20 + 1 = 21st
1857 CE19th century CE18 + 1 = 19th
500 CE5th century CE (Aryabhata's time)5th
2600 BCE26th century BCE (Harappan)26th
563 BCE6th century BCE (Buddha)5 + 1 = 6th

📜 Sources of History — How Do We Know About the Past?

Historians use different types of evidence to piece together what happened in the past. These are called sources of history.

Archaeological Sources — Detailed Examples:

Source TypeWhat It Tells UsExample
FossilsAbout early humans and animalsDinosaur bones, early human skulls
ToolsHow people worked and huntedStone axes, arrowheads, pottery wheels
PotteryFood habits, art, tradePainted Grey Ware, Harappan pottery
CoinsKings, kingdoms, trade, economyGupta gold coins, Mauryan punch-marked coins
Buildings/RuinsArchitecture, city planning, religionMohenjo-daro ruins, Sanchi Stupa
InscriptionsLaws, achievements of kingsAshoka's rock edicts, Allahabad Pillar
Cave paintingsDaily life, animals, huntingBhimbetka caves (Madhya Pradesh)

🧑‍🤝‍🧑 The Beginnings of Human History

Modern humans (Homo sapiens) have existed for about 3,00,000 (three lakh) years! But written records are only about 5,000 years old. Everything before writing is called pre-history.

How Early Humans Lived:

PeriodHow They LivedTools UsedFood
Early Stone Age
(~3 lakh – 10,000 BCE)		Nomads (moved place to place), lived in caves/rock sheltersRough stone tools, hand axesHunted animals, gathered fruits/roots
Middle Stone Age
(~10,000 – 6,000 BCE)		Started settling near riversSmaller, sharper tools (microliths)Fishing added to hunting/gathering
New Stone Age
(~6,000 – 4,000 BCE)		Permanent settlements, farming begins!Polished stone tools, potteryGrew crops (wheat, barley), reared animals
Metal Age
(~4,000 BCE onwards)		Cities, civilisations, tradeCopper, bronze, then iron toolsOrganised farming, stored food

Key Changes Over Time:

  • Discovery of fire — cooking food, warmth, protection from animals
  • Making tools — from rough stones to polished, then metal
  • Language — communication led to cooperation and community
  • Farming (Neolithic Revolution) — biggest change! People settled in one place, grew food, stored surplus
  • Wheel — transport, pottery making
  • Writing — record-keeping, laws, literature (marks the end of pre-history)

Questions & Answers

Q1. What is the difference between BCE and CE? Why is there no Year Zero?

Answer:

CE (Common Era) refers to all years after year 1 (counting forward from 1). BCE (Before Common Era) refers to all years before year 1 (counting backward). Example: 2024 CE is now; 2600 BCE is when Harappans lived.

There is no Year Zero because when the calendar was created (by a monk in 525 CE), the concept of zero was not widely used in Europe. So the calendar jumps directly from 1 BCE to 1 CE.

Q2. What are the three types of sources of history? Give examples of each.

Answer:

  1. Archaeological sources (physical remains): Fossils, stone tools, pottery, coins, ruins of buildings (like Mohenjo-daro), inscriptions on pillars (like Ashoka's edicts), cave paintings (Bhimbetka), seals
  2. Literary sources (written records): Vedas, Ramayana, Mahabharata, Arthashastra by Kautilya, travelogues of foreign visitors (Megasthenes, Fa-Hien, Hiuen Tsang), copper plate grants
  3. Oral sources (word of mouth): Folk tales and legends, songs and ballads, tribal traditions, family histories passed from grandparents to grandchildren. Note: The Vedas were originally oral!

Q3. How did early humans live? What major changes happened over time?

Answer:

Early humans (Homo sapiens, ~3 lakh years ago) were nomads — they moved from place to place, lived in caves and rock shelters, and survived by hunting animals and gathering fruits/roots.

Major changes over time:

  1. Fire: Cooking food, warmth, protection from wild animals
  2. Better tools: From rough stones → polished stones → copper → bronze → iron
  3. Language: Enabled cooperation and community building
  4. Farming (most important!): About 10,000 years ago, humans learned to grow crops. This meant they could settle permanently, store food, and build villages and cities.
  5. Writing: About 5,000 years ago — enabled record-keeping and marks the start of "history"

Q4. What is a timeline? How do we calculate centuries?

Answer:

A timeline is a visual tool that shows events in chronological order (from past to present) on a horizontal or vertical line. It helps us understand the sequence and time gaps between events.

Calculating centuries:

  • Divide the year by 100 and add 1
  • 2024 CE → 20 + 1 = 21st century CE
  • 1947 CE → 19 + 1 = 20th century CE
  • For BCE: 2600 BCE → 26th century BCE
  • One century = 100 years; One millennium = 1000 years; One decade = 10 years

Q5. Why is farming called the most important change in human history?

Answer:

The discovery of farming (about 10,000 years ago) is called the Neolithic Revolution and is the most important change because:

  • Humans could settle permanently (no more nomadic wandering)
  • Food surplus — extra food could be stored for bad times
  • Led to villages and cities (civilisation began)
  • People could do other jobs (pottery, weaving, trading) since not everyone needed to hunt
  • Led to writing (to keep records of stored grain and trade)
  • Eventually led to great civilisations like Harappan, Egyptian, Mesopotamian
📜 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 4Timeline & Sources of History — how historians piece together the past

⏳ Timelines & Dating (Deep Dive)

History is the story of the past. To make sense of it, we need to organise events in chronological order (oldest first, newest last). A picture that shows events in order is a timeline.

📊 Words to Measure Time

TermLengthExample
Year12025
Decade10 years1990s
Century100 years21st century = 2001-2100
Millennium1000 years3rd millennium = 2001-3000
Era / EpochA long period defined by characterVedic Era, Mughal Era

📏 BCE / CE / AD / BC — What Do They Mean?

  • BCE — Before the Common Era (older term: BC = Before Christ)
  • CE — Common Era (older term: AD = Anno Domini, Latin for "in the year of the Lord")
  • The line between BCE and CE is at the year 1 (no year 0).
  • BCE years count backwards — 500 BCE is older than 100 BCE!

🧮 Worked Examples — Calculating Time

  1. From 600 BCE to 1947 CE → 600 + 1947 = 2547 years.
  2. From 2024 CE to 1500 CE → 2024 − 1500 = 524 years.
  3. The 21st century CE covers the years 2001 to 2100.
  4. Harappan civilisation (~2600 BCE) belongs to the 26th century BCE.

🔍 Sources of History (Deep Dive)

How do we know what happened thousands of years ago? Historians use sources — clues left behind. They are of two main types:

📊 Two Main Types of Sources

TypeDescriptionExamples
ArchaeologicalThings we can see or dig upCoins, tools, pottery, buildings, jewellery, skeletons, inscriptions, seals
LiteraryThings written downBooks, manuscripts, scriptures, royal records, foreign accounts, palm-leaf books

🏺 Archaeological Sources

  • Inscriptions — engraved messages on stone, copper, pillars (e.g. Ashoka's edicts).
  • Coins — show rulers' faces, dates, religion, trade reach.
  • Pottery — reveals food habits, art, dating layers.
  • Tools & weapons — tell us about technology level.
  • Monuments — palaces, temples, mosques, forts.
  • Seals — like the Harappan seals (proves writing existed).
  • Skeletons — diet, health, age, diseases.

📚 Literary Sources

  • Vedas, Upanishads, Puranas — sacred Indian texts.
  • Ramayana, Mahabharata — epics that contain historical hints.
  • Arthashastra by Kautilya — administration of Mauryan empire.
  • Travelogues — Megasthenes (Indica), Fa-Hien, Hieun Tsang, Ibn Battuta, Marco Polo, Al-Biruni.
  • Court chronicles — Ain-i-Akbari, Akbarnama.
  • Sangam literature — Tamil poetry of 1st-3rd century CE.

🇮🇳 IKS — India's Greatest Records

  • Rigveda (~1500 BCE) — world's oldest surviving religious text.
  • Ashokan Edicts (3rd c BCE) — first stone messages of dharma carved by Emperor Ashoka.
  • Indus Valley seals — script still undeciphered!
  • Iron Pillar of Delhi (~400 CE) — barely rusted in 1600 years; shows ancient metallurgy.
  • Konark Sun Temple (13th c) — its architecture itself is a history book.

🔬 How Do We Date Old Objects?

  • Carbon-14 dating — used on organic remains (wood, bone, charcoal) up to 50,000 years old.
  • Stratigraphy — older items lie in deeper layers of earth.
  • Style and inscription study — comparing with known examples.

🪨 Pre-History → History (Deep Dive)

The story of humans is divided into ages based on tools and food:

📊 The Ages of Pre-History

AgeTimeKey Features
Old Stone Age (Palaeolithic)~25,00,000 – 10,000 BCERough stone tools; hunter-gatherers; lived in caves
Middle Stone Age (Mesolithic)~10,000 – 8,000 BCESmaller tools (microliths); first domestication of dog
New Stone Age (Neolithic)~8,000 – 3,000 BCEPolished tools; farming, pottery, villages
Chalcolithic / Bronze Age~3,000 – 1,000 BCECopper & bronze tools; Harappan civilisation
Iron Age~1,000 BCE – 0Iron tools; expansion of agriculture; later Vedic age

Pre-history = period before writing (we know it only by archaeology).
History = period after writing (we have written records).

📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 4

Q1. What does BCE/CE mean? Why are these used?

Answer:

BCE = Before the Common Era, CE = Common Era. These are religion-neutral replacements for the older BC/AD. The dividing year is 1; BCE counts backwards, CE counts forwards.

Q2. Differentiate archaeological and literary sources.

Answer:

Archaeological sources are physical remains (coins, pots, ruins, tools) that we dig up or see. Literary sources are written records (books, inscriptions, manuscripts). Together, they help historians reconstruct the past.

Q3. Why is the Rigveda an important historical source?

Answer:

The Rigveda (~1500 BCE) is the oldest surviving religious text of mankind. It gives us detailed information about early Indian society, language, gods, geography (mentions rivers Sindhu, Saraswati), animals, plants and customs.

Q4. What is the difference between pre-history and history?

Answer:

Pre-history is the time before the invention of writing — we know it only from archaeological remains. History is the time after writing began (~5000 years ago) — we have written records as well.

Q5. Name 3 foreign travellers who wrote about India.

Answer:

  • Megasthenes (Greek) — wrote Indica during Chandragupta Maurya's time.
  • Fa-Hien (Chinese) — visited during Gupta empire (5th c).
  • Ibn Battuta (Moroccan) — visited during Sultanate (14th c).

Q6. How can a coin be a source of history?

Answer:

A coin tells us many things: the ruler's name and face, the year, the language of the empire, the metal used (gold/silver/copper shows wealth), the symbols/religious motifs, and where the coin travelled (extent of trade).

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 4 (18 Questions)

  1. One century = ___ years. (100)
  2. One millennium = ___ years. (1000)
  3. BCE means ___. (Before the Common Era)
  4. CE means ___. (Common Era)
  5. 2025 belongs to the ___ st century. (21)
  6. Harappan civilisation is dated ~ ___ BCE. (2600)
  7. The world's oldest religious text is the ___. (Rigveda)
  8. Stone messages carved by Emperor Ashoka are called ___. (Edicts)
  9. Megasthenes wrote the book ___. (Indica)
  10. Kautilya wrote ___. (Arthashastra)
  11. Dating using radioactive carbon is called ___ dating. (Carbon-14)
  12. Period before writing is called ___. (pre-history)
  13. Tools of the Old Stone Age were made of ___. (stone)
  14. The age that brought farming is ___. (Neolithic)
  15. True or False: Coins are literary sources. (False — archaeological)
  16. True or False: 500 BCE is older than 200 BCE. (True)
  17. True or False: Travelogues are literary sources. (True)
  18. True or False: Pottery can help date a site. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 4

TermMeaning
HistoryThe study of the human past based on written records.
Pre-historyPeriod before writing was invented.
SourceMaterial giving information about the past.
ArchaeologyStudy of the past through physical remains.
InscriptionWriting carved on stone, metal or other hard surface.
EdictAn official order, especially Ashokan rock orders.
ManuscriptHand-written text on palm leaves, bark or paper.
ChronologyOrder of events in time.
CenturyA period of 100 years.
MillenniumA period of 1000 years.
BCE / CECommon dating system (replacing BC/AD).
Carbon-14 datingScientific method to date organic remains.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 4

  1. My Family Timeline — Make a timeline of major events in your family from grandparents to you.
  2. Coin Collection — Collect 5 old Indian coins (or copies). Note their year, picture, metal.
  3. Source Hunt — Pick any 1 monument in your city. List archaeological and literary sources about it.
  4. Make an Edict — On a flat stone or piece of cardboard, "carve" your own dharma message like Ashoka.
  5. Travelogue Re-creation — Imagine you are Hieun Tsang visiting India. Write a 1-page diary.

📋 Chapter 5 — India, That Is Bharat

📖 Topics Covered

  • India's Many Names Through History
  • Bhārata — Origin of the Name
  • India — From the River Sindhu/Indus
  • Hindustan — Persian Name
  • Jambudvīpa — Ancient Name
  • The Constitution: "India, that is Bharat"
  • India's Geographic Location
  • The Indian Subcontinent
  • Physical Features of India
  • Major Rivers

✍️ Key Facts

  • 7th largest country in the world
  • 28 States + 8 Union Territories
  • Latitude: 8°N to 37°N
  • Longitude: 68°E to 97°E
  • Tropic of Cancer passes through India
  • Surrounded by water on 3 sides (peninsula)

Chapter 5: India, That Is Bharat

📜 Quote from Jawaharlal Nehru

"In India at a very early time the spiritual and cultural unity was made complete and became the very stuff of the life of all this great surge of humanity between the Himalayas and the two seas."

🇮🇳 India's Many Names Through History

Our country has been known by different names at different times. Each name has a fascinating origin!

Origin of Each Name:

NameOriginWho Used It
Bhārata / BhāratvarshaFrom legendary King Bharata (ancestor of the Pandavas & Kauravas in Mahabharata)Ancient Indians, Vishnu Purana, Constitution (Hindi)
IndiaFrom the river Sindhu (Indus). Greeks couldn't pronounce 'S', called it "Indos" → "India"Greeks, Romans, Europeans, Constitution (English)
HindustanPersian: "Hindu" (their pronunciation of Sindhu) + "stan" (land) = "Land beyond the Indus"Persians, Mughals, common use today
Jambudvīpa"Island/Land of the Jambu (rose apple) tree" — an ancient name for the Indian subcontinentJain texts, Buddhist texts, Emperor Ashoka
TianzhuChinese word derived from "Sindhu"; understood as "heavenly master" — showing respect for India as land of the BuddhaAncient Chinese travellers

📜 From the Vishnu Purana:

"Uttaram yat samudrasya, Himādreścaiva dakṣhiṇam; Varsham tad Bhāratam nāma, Bhāratī yatra santatiḥ"

Translation: "The land that lies north of the ocean and south of the snowy mountains (Himalayas) is called Bhārata, and the people who live there are called Bhāratī."

🗺️ India's Geographic Location & Physical Features

FeatureDetail
LocationNorthern Hemisphere, South Asia
Area3.28 million sq km (7th largest country)
Latitude8°4'N to 37°6'N
Longitude68°7'E to 97°25'E
Tropic of CancerPasses through the middle of India (8 states)
Coastline~7,516 km (peninsula — water on 3 sides)
Land borders~15,200 km (6 neighbouring countries)
States & UTs28 States + 8 Union Territories

India is Surrounded By:

  • North: Himalayas (separates from China/Tibet)
  • South: Indian Ocean (where Arabian Sea & Bay of Bengal meet)
  • West: Arabian Sea; Pakistan, Afghanistan beyond
  • East: Bay of Bengal; Myanmar, Bangladesh beyond
  • Land neighbours: Pakistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Myanmar
  • Sea neighbours: Sri Lanka (south), Maldives (southwest)

Six Major Physical Divisions of India:

DivisionLocationFeatures
1. The HimalayasNorthYoung fold mountains; highest peaks; source of Ganga, Yamuna, Brahmaputra
2. Northern PlainsBelow HimalayasIndo-Gangetic Plain; most fertile & populated; rice, wheat
3. Thar DesertRajasthan (west)Sandy, hot, dry; sparse population; camels
4. Peninsular PlateauCentral & SouthDeccan Plateau; mineral-rich; Western & Eastern Ghats on sides
5. Coastal PlainsAlong east & west coastsNarrow strips; fishing, coconut, rice; ports
6. IslandsArabian Sea & Bay of BengalLakshadweep (coral) & Andaman-Nicobar

Major Rivers of India:

RiverOriginFlows IntoSpecial
GangaGangotri glacier (Himalayas)Bay of BengalHoliest river; longest in India (2,525 km)
YamunaYamunotri glacierMeets Ganga at PrayagrajTributary of Ganga; Delhi is on its bank
BrahmaputraTibet (Mansarovar)Bay of BengalOne of the largest rivers in the world
GodavariNashik (Maharashtra)Bay of BengalLongest Peninsular river; "Dakshin Ganga"
KrishnaMahabaleshwarBay of Bengal2nd longest Peninsular river
NarmadaAmarkantak (MP)Arabian SeaFlows WEST (unusual!)
KaveriCoorg (Karnataka)Bay of Bengal"Ganga of the South"

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why is India also called Bharat? What does the Constitution say?

Answer:

India is called Bharat after the legendary King Bharata, who is mentioned in the Mahabharata and Vishnu Purana. The Vishnu Purana describes Bharat as "the land north of the ocean and south of the Himalayas." The Indian Constitution (Article 1) states: "India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States." This means both "India" and "Bharat" are the official names of our country.

Q2. How did the name "India" originate?

Answer:

The name "India" comes from the river Sindhu (known in English as the Indus river). When the ancient Greeks came to know about this land, they couldn't pronounce the 'S' sound properly and called the river "Indos." The land beyond the Indus they called "India." The Persians similarly called it "Hindu" (their pronunciation of Sindhu) and the land "Hindustan" (land of the Hindus/land beyond the Indus).

Q3. Name the 6 major physical divisions of India.

Answer:

  1. The Himalayas: Young fold mountains in the north; source of major rivers
  2. The Northern Plains (Indo-Gangetic): Flat, fertile, most populated area
  3. The Thar Desert: Sandy desert in Rajasthan (west)
  4. The Peninsular Plateau: Deccan Plateau in the centre and south; mineral-rich
  5. The Coastal Plains: Narrow strips along the Arabian Sea (west) and Bay of Bengal (east)
  6. The Islands: Lakshadweep (Arabian Sea) and Andaman & Nicobar (Bay of Bengal)

Q4. Name India's neighbouring countries. Which bodies of water surround India?

Answer:

Land neighbours (6): Pakistan (west), China (north), Nepal (north), Bhutan (northeast), Bangladesh (east), Myanmar (east)

Sea neighbours (2): Sri Lanka (south, separated by Palk Strait), Maldives (southwest)

Water bodies:

  • Arabian Sea — west coast
  • Bay of Bengal — east coast
  • Indian Ocean — south (where the two meet)

Q5. Name any 5 major rivers of India and where they originate.

Answer:

  1. Ganga — originates from Gangotri glacier (Uttarakhand); flows into Bay of Bengal
  2. Yamuna — from Yamunotri glacier; meets Ganga at Prayagraj (Sangam)
  3. Brahmaputra — from near Mansarovar (Tibet); flows through Assam into Bay of Bengal
  4. Godavari — from Nashik (Maharashtra); longest peninsular river (called "Dakshin Ganga")
  5. Narmada — from Amarkantak (Madhya Pradesh); flows westward into Arabian Sea
🇮🇳 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 5India, That Is Bharat — the names, location, geography & identity of our country

📝 The Many Names of Our Country (Deep Dive)

Article 1 of the Indian Constitution says: "India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States." Our country has been known by many beautiful names across thousands of years.

📊 Names of India

NameOriginMeaning
Bharat / BhārataVedic literature; named after King BharataLand of King Bharata
HindustanPersian — Hindu (land beyond Sindhu/Indus river) + stan (land)Land of the Hindus / Land of the Sindhu
IndiaGreek (Indikē) → Latin → English; from "Indus" riverLand of the Indus
JambudvipaAncient Indian textsIsland of the Jambu (rose-apple) tree
AryavartaUsed in classical Sanskrit textsLand of the noble (Arya) people

🗺️ India's Location & Size

FeatureValue
Total area≈ 32.87 lakh km² (7th largest country)
Latitude range8°4′N to 37°6′N
Longitude range68°7′E to 97°25′E
Tropic of CancerPasses through 8 Indian states
North–South extent~ 3214 km
East–West extent~ 2933 km
Coastline~ 7517 km
Land boundary~ 15,200 km
Population~ 142 crore (1st in the world)
States & UTs28 states + 8 Union Territories

🌐 India's Neighbours (Deep Dive)

📊 India's Neighbours

CountryDirectionNotes
PakistanWest / NorthwestShares longest western border; River Indus flows here
AfghanistanNorthwest (via PoK)Shares short border
ChinaNorthBorder runs through the Himalayas
NepalNorthHome of Mt Everest; cultural ties
BhutanNortheastTiny Himalayan kingdom
BangladeshEastCreated in 1971; surrounded mostly by India
MyanmarEast / SoutheastShares border in northeast India
Sri LankaSouth (sea)Separated by Palk Strait & Gulf of Mannar
MaldivesSouthwest (sea)Group of small islands in Indian Ocean

🗻 Physical Divisions of India (Deep Dive)

India has every kind of landform — mountains, deserts, plateaus, plains, coasts and islands! Geographers divide India into 6 physical regions.

📊 The 6 Physical Divisions

RegionLocationImportant Features
1. Himalayan MountainsNorthMt Everest, sources of Ganga & Brahmaputra
2. Northern PlainsBelow HimalayasMost fertile; densely populated
3. Indian Desert (Thar)West (Rajasthan)Sand dunes, camels, very low rainfall
4. Peninsular PlateauCentral & SouthDeccan, Chhota Nagpur; mineral-rich
5. Coastal PlainsEast & West coastsPorts, fishing, paddy cultivation
6. IslandsIndian OceanLakshadweep (Arabian) & Andaman-Nicobar (Bengal)

🏞️ Rivers & National Symbols of India

🏞️ India's Major River Systems

RiverSourceFlows Into
Indus (Sindhu)Mansarovar, TibetArabian Sea (via Pakistan)
GangaGangotri GlacierBay of Bengal
YamunaYamunotri GlacierJoins Ganga at Prayagraj
BrahmaputraTibet (Tsangpo)Bay of Bengal (via Bangladesh)
GodavariTrimbak, Nashik (MH)Bay of Bengal
KrishnaMahabaleshwar (MH)Bay of Bengal
KaveriTalakaveri (Karnataka)Bay of Bengal
NarmadaAmarkantak (MP)Arabian Sea
TapiMultai (MP)Arabian Sea

🏛️ India's National Symbols

SymbolItem
National FlagTiranga (saffron-white-green with Ashoka Chakra)
National EmblemLion Capital of Sarnath
National Anthem"Jana Gana Mana" by Rabindranath Tagore
National Song"Vande Mataram" by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee
National BirdPeacock

📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 5

Q1. Mention 3 names of India and their meanings.

Answer:

  • Bharat — Land of King Bharata; used in the Constitution.
  • Hindustan — Persian "land of the people across the Sindhu river".
  • India — English form from the Greek "Indikē", from River Indus.

Q2. Why is India called a sub-continent?

Answer:

India is so vast and geographically distinct (with the Himalayas to the north and oceans on three sides) that it forms a region of its own — almost like a "small continent". Along with neighbours (Pakistan, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka), it is called the Indian Subcontinent.

Q3. How many states and union territories does India have today?

Answer:

28 states and 8 Union Territories.

Q4. Name 3 rivers that flow east and 2 that flow west.

Answer:

East-flowing (into Bay of Bengal): Ganga, Godavari, Krishna.
West-flowing (into Arabian Sea): Narmada, Tapi.

Q5. List 5 national symbols of India.

Answer:

  • National Flag — Tiranga
  • National Anthem — Jana Gana Mana
  • National Animal — Tiger
  • National Bird — Peacock
  • National Flower — Lotus

Q6. Why does the Tropic of Cancer divide India into nearly two halves?

Answer:

Because the Tropic of Cancer (23.5° N) passes through 8 Indian states (Gujarat, Rajasthan, MP, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, WB, Tripura, Mizoram) — roughly through the middle of India. The part to the north lies in the temperate zone, the part to the south lies in the tropical zone.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 5 (18 Questions)

  1. The Constitution names our country ___, that is ___. (India, Bharat)
  2. India is the ___ th largest country by area. (7)
  3. India has ___ states. (28)
  4. India has ___ Union Territories. (8)
  5. India lies entirely in the ___ hemisphere. (Northern)
  6. The latitude that almost cuts India in half is the ___. (Tropic of Cancer)
  7. The longest coastline state of India is ___. (Gujarat)
  8. National anthem of India: ___. (Jana Gana Mana)
  9. National animal: ___. (Tiger)
  10. National bird: ___. (Peacock)
  11. National flower: ___. (Lotus)
  12. India's eastern sea is ___. (Bay of Bengal)
  13. India's western sea is ___. (Arabian Sea)
  14. India's island group in Bay of Bengal: ___. (Andaman & Nicobar)
  15. True or False: Sri Lanka is a land neighbour of India. (False — sea)
  16. True or False: India shares the longest border with Bangladesh. (True among land neighbours)
  17. True or False: Indus river flows in India only. (False — also Pakistan)
  18. True or False: India is the most populous country. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 5

TermMeaning
ConstitutionSupreme law book of a country.
StateFederal unit of India with its own government.
Union TerritoryRegion directly governed by the central government.
HemisphereHalf of the Earth (N/S/E/W).
Sub-continentLarge self-contained region of a continent.
National symbolOfficially adopted symbol representing the nation.
TricolourIndian national flag with 3 colours.
CapitalMain city where the government sits.
SubcontinentIndia + neighbours considered as one region.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 5

  1. Outline Map Project — On an outline of India, mark all 28 states with their capitals.
  2. National Symbols Chart — Make a poster of all national symbols with colourful drawings.
  3. State Quiz — Find one famous food, dance and festival from any 5 states.
  4. Neighbour Notebook — Make a 1-page profile of each of India's neighbours: flag, capital, currency, language.
  5. Tiranga Story — Research the history of the Indian flag (Pingali Venkayya). Write a short essay.

📋 Chapter 6 — The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

📖 Topics Covered

  • What is a Civilisation?
  • The Harappan/Indus-Sarasvatī Civilisation
  • Major Cities & Their Locations
  • Remarkable Town Planning
  • The Great Bath (Mohenjo-daro)
  • Water Management (Dholavira)
  • Agriculture & Rural Life
  • Trade & Crafts
  • Seals & the Undeciphered Script
  • Art & Culture
  • Decline of the Civilisation

✍️ Key Facts

  • Period: ~2600–1900 BCE
  • One of world's first 3 civilisations
  • ~1,500 sites discovered so far
  • World's first urban drainage system
  • Script still undeciphered!
  • Peaceful — no weapons/army found
  • Dockyard at Lothal (world's first?)

Chapter 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

📜 About the Harappan Civilisation

"The most ancient civilisation of India, known variously as the Harappan, Indus or Indus-Sarasvatī Civilisation, was indeed remarkable in many ways... It showed how a well-balanced community lives."

🏛️ What is a Civilisation?

A civilisation is an advanced stage of human society characterised by:

  • Settled agriculture — growing food, storing surplus
  • Cities — large planned settlements with public buildings
  • Writing system — for communication and record-keeping
  • Trade — exchange of goods between cities and regions
  • Specialised crafts — pottery, metallurgy, weaving, jewellery
  • Social organisation — rulers, workers, artisans, traders
  • Art & religion — sculptures, seals, rituals

World's Three Earliest Civilisations:

CivilisationRiverRegion (Today)Period
MesopotamianTigris & EuphratesIraq~3500 BCE
EgyptianNileEgypt~3100 BCE
Harappan/IndianIndus & SarasvatīIndia & Pakistan~2600 BCE

Note: All early civilisations began near rivers — for water, fertile soil, and transport!

🏙️ The Harappan (Indus-Sarasvatī) Civilisation

🏗️ Remarkable Town Planning

The Harappan cities had astonishingly advanced urban planning — better than many cities even 3,000 years later!

FeatureDescriptionWhy Remarkable
Grid LayoutStreets crossed at right angles, creating neat rectangular blocksShows advanced planning BEFORE construction
Two SectionsCitadel (upper, fortified) + Lower Town (residential)Separate areas for public buildings and homes
Baked Brick HousesMulti-room houses with courtyards, bathrooms, and wellsStandardised brick sizes across ALL cities!
Covered DrainsEvery house had a drain → connected to street drains → covered!World's FIRST underground drainage system
Great BathLarge tank (12m × 7m × 2.4m) at Mohenjo-daro; waterproofed with bitumenProbably for religious/ritual bathing
GranariesLarge buildings for storing grainShows organised food storage & distribution
Water ManagementDholavira had 16 reservoirs! Largest = 73 metres longAdvanced water harvesting in a dry region

👨‍🌾 Life in the Harappan Civilisation

Agriculture & Food:

  • Crops grown: Wheat, barley, rice, cotton, peas, sesame, mustard
  • First to grow cotton! (Greeks later called it "sindon" — from Sindh)
  • Animals reared: Cattle, sheep, goats, buffaloes; used bullock carts
  • Hundreds of small villages supported the big cities with food

Trade & Crafts:

  • Internal trade: Between Harappan cities (standardised weights & measures)
  • International trade: With Mesopotamia (Iraq) — beads, ivory, cotton, gold
  • Dockyard at Lothal (Gujarat): Possibly the world's first port/dockyard for ships!
  • Crafts: Pottery (painted), jewellery (gold, shell bangles), bead-making, terracotta figurines
  • Standardised weights: Used a system based on multiples of 16 (not 10!)

Seals & Writing:

  • Made of steatite (soft stone, hardened by heating)
  • Showed animal figures (unicorn-bull, elephant, tiger, rhinoceros)
  • Had script above the animals (~400 different signs)
  • Script is still undeciphered — one of history's greatest mysteries!
  • Seals were probably used to mark ownership of goods (like stamps)

Art & Culture:

  • "Dancing Girl" — a famous bronze figurine from Mohenjo-daro (shows skill in metallurgy)
  • "Priest-King" — a stone bust with decorated robe and headband
  • Terracotta toys — carts, animals, dice (children played games!)
  • Swastika symbol — found on seals (ancient auspicious symbol)

📉 Decline of the Harappan Civilisation (~1900 BCE)

Around 1900 BCE, the great cities were gradually abandoned. No evidence of war or invasion was found! Possible reasons:

  • Climate change: Region became drier
  • River Sarasvatī dried up: The main river shifted course or disappeared
  • Floods: The Indus river may have changed course, causing flooding
  • Decline of trade: With Mesopotamia

The civilisation was remarkably peaceful — no weapons of war, no evidence of an army. It declined gradually, not suddenly.

Questions & Answers

Q1. What were the remarkable features of Harappan town planning?

Answer:

The Harappan town planning was remarkably advanced:

  • Grid pattern: Streets crossed at right angles forming rectangular blocks
  • Two parts: Citadel (upper, fortified — public buildings) and Lower Town (residential)
  • Baked brick houses: Multiple rooms, courtyards, private wells, and bathrooms
  • Covered drainage: Every house connected to underground street drains (world's first!)
  • Great Bath: Large public bathing pool at Mohenjo-daro (12m × 7m), waterproofed
  • Granaries: Large buildings for storing surplus grain
  • Standardised bricks: Same size ratio (4:2:1) used across all cities, showing central planning

Q2. What do we know about the trade of Harappan people?

Answer:

  • Internal trade: Between Harappan cities using standardised weights and measures
  • Trade with Mesopotamia (Iraq): Harappan seals found in Mesopotamian sites!
  • Goods exported: Cotton, beads, ivory, precious stones
  • Goods imported: Gold, silver, copper, tin
  • Dockyard at Lothal (Gujarat): Used for loading and unloading ships — possibly world's first port
  • Seals: Used like stamps/labels to mark goods (like shipping labels today)
  • Standardised weights: Based on multiples of 16, ensuring fair trade

Q3. Why did the Harappan Civilisation decline?

Answer:

The Harappan Civilisation declined around 1900 BCE. Possible reasons include:

  1. Climate change: The region became drier, reducing agricultural output
  2. River drying up: The Sarasvatī river may have changed course or dried up, leaving cities without water
  3. Floods: The Indus may have flooded repeatedly, damaging cities
  4. Trade disruption: Decline in trade with Mesopotamia

Importantly, there is no evidence of war or invasion. The Harappans were a peaceful people — no army or weapons have been found. The decline was gradual, not sudden.

Q4. Why is the Harappan script important? What is special about it?

Answer:

The Harappan script is one of history's greatest mysteries because:

  • It contains about 400 different signs/symbols
  • Found on seals, pottery, and copper tablets
  • It has never been deciphered — no one can read it despite 100 years of attempts!
  • It is the key to understanding their language, religion, and administration
  • Unlike Egyptian hieroglyphs or Mesopotamian cuneiform, no bilingual text (Rosetta Stone equivalent) has been found

If the script is ever decoded, it would revolutionise our understanding of ancient India.

🏺 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 6The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation — Harappan/Indus-Saraswati Civilisation

🏺 The Indus–Sarasvatī (Harappan) Civilisation (Deep Dive)

About 5000 years ago, in the plains watered by the Indus, Sarasvatī, Ravi and other rivers, one of the world's earliest urban civilisations flourished. Its first site was discovered at Harappa (1921) in present-day Pakistan — hence "Harappan Civilisation". It was discovered by Indian archaeologist Daya Ram Sahni.

📊 Quick Facts

ItemDetail
Time period~ 2600 BCE to ~ 1900 BCE
