  2. Activities done without pay are called ___ activities. (non-economic)
  3. Farming belongs to the ___ sector. (primary)
  4. Manufacturing belongs to the ___ sector. (secondary)
  5. Banking belongs to the ___ sector. (tertiary)
  6. Equal respect for all kinds of work is called dignity of ___. (labour)
  7. The Gita's path of selfless work is called ___ Yoga. (Karma)
  8. "Work is worship" was said by ___. (Swami Vivekananda)
  9. The spinning wheel symbol used by Gandhi is the ___. (charkha)
  10. The law banning child labour in India is the Child Labour ___ Act. (Prohibition)
  11. RTE stands for ___. (Right to Education)
  12. Domestic work by women is mostly ___. (unpaid)
  13. True or False: All work has dignity. (True)
  14. True or False: Domestic helpers are formal sector workers. (False)
  15. True or False: 90% of Indian workers are in informal sector. (True)
  16. True or False: Volunteering is non-economic activity. (True)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 13

TermMeaning
Economic activityWork done for money.
Non-economic activityWork done without monetary reward.
Primary sectorUses natural resources directly.
Secondary sectorManufactures & processes goods.
Tertiary sectorProvides services.
Dignity of labourRespect for all kinds of honest work.
Karma YogaPath of selfless action (Gita).
SevāSelfless service.
Child labourEmployment of children below legal age.
Informal sectorWorkers without contracts or benefits.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 13

  1. Workers Around You — List 15 workers seen in a single day; classify into primary/secondary/tertiary.
  2. Mom's Work Diary — For one day, note all tasks your mother does and how many hours; reflect on her contribution.
  3. Sevā Day — Spend one Sunday doing 3 acts of sevā without expecting anything; write your feelings.
  4. Gandhi & Work — Find Gandhi's quotes on dignity of labour and make a poster.
  5. Stop Child Labour — Design a slogan-poster campaign for the school against child labour.

📋 Chapter 14 — Economic Activities Around Us

📖 Topics Covered

  • Classification of Economic Activities
  • Primary Sector (Agriculture, Mining, etc.)
  • Secondary Sector (Manufacturing)
  • Tertiary Sector (Services)
  • How All 3 Sectors Work Together
  • Cooperatives (AMUL story)

✍️ Key Facts

  • 3 sectors: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary
  • Primary = directly from nature
  • Secondary = manufacturing/processing
  • Tertiary = services (banking, transport)
  • AMUL = famous milk cooperative
  • All 3 sectors are interdependent

Chapter 14: Economic Activities Around Us

📜 Quote from Kautilya's Arthashastra

"The root of prosperity is economic activity, the lack of it brings material distress. The absence of fruitful economic activity endangers both current prosperity and future growth."

📊 Three Economic Sectors

All economic activities can be classified into three sectors:

🌾 Primary Sector — From Nature

Activities that directly use natural resources to produce raw materials:

ActivityNatural Resource UsedProduct
AgricultureLand, water, sunlightWheat, rice, vegetables, fruits
MiningMinerals in earthCoal, iron ore, gold, diamonds
FishingWater bodies (sea, river)Fish, prawns, pearls
ForestryForestsTimber, rubber, honey, herbs
LivestockAnimalsMilk, eggs, wool, meat

Key feature: These activities produce raw materials that other sectors use.

🏭 Secondary Sector — Manufacturing

Activities that transform raw materials from the primary sector into finished goods:

  • Cotton (primary) → Textile factory → Clothes (secondary)
  • Iron ore (primary) → Steel plant → Steel (secondary)
  • Wheat (primary) → Flour mill → Flour/Bread (secondary)
  • Wood (primary) → Furniture factory → Tables, chairs (secondary)

Also includes: Construction (buildings, roads, bridges), electricity generation, water supply systems.

🏥 Tertiary Sector — Services

Activities that provide services to support the other two sectors and people:

  • Transportation: Moving goods from farms/factories to shops (trucks, trains, ships)
  • Banking: Providing loans to farmers and businesses, safeguarding savings
  • Healthcare: Hospitals, doctors, medicines
  • Education: Schools, colleges, online learning
  • Communication: Internet, phones, postal services
  • Trade: Shops, warehouses, e-commerce (selling goods to consumers)
  • IT/Software: Developing apps, maintaining websites

🥛 Cooperatives — The AMUL Story

A cooperative is a group of people who voluntarily come together to meet their economic needs collectively. Famous example:

AMUL (Anand Milk Union Limited):

  • Started in 1946 in Anand, Gujarat by dairy farmers
  • Farmers were being exploited by middlemen who bought milk cheaply and sold it at high prices
  • Under the leadership of Dr. Verghese Kurien (Father of White Revolution), farmers formed a cooperative
  • Farmers own the cooperative — decisions are made collectively
  • Milk is collected → processed → packaged → sold under AMUL brand
  • Farmers get fair prices directly — no middlemen!
  • Today AMUL is one of the world's largest dairy brands

Other Milk Cooperatives:

BrandState
NandiniKarnataka
Mother DairyDelhi-NCR
AavinTamil Nadu
VijayaAndhra Pradesh
SudhaBihar
VerkaPunjab

🔗 How All Three Sectors Work Together

No sector can work alone — they are interdependent:

Example: Making a Book

  1. Primary: Trees are grown and cut for wood pulp
  2. Secondary: Pulp is processed into paper; paper is printed into books
  3. Tertiary: Books are transported to warehouses → shipped to bookshops → sold to you

Without any one sector, the others cannot function effectively!

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are the three sectors of economic activity? Give examples of each.

Answer:

  1. Primary Sector: Activities using natural resources directly — agriculture, mining, fishing, forestry, livestock rearing
  2. Secondary Sector: Activities that transform raw materials into finished goods — manufacturing, construction, electricity production
  3. Tertiary Sector: Activities that provide services — transportation, banking, healthcare, education, communication, trade

Q2. How do the three sectors depend on each other? Give an example.

Answer:

The three sectors are interdependent. Example of making bread:

  • Primary: Farmer grows wheat (using land and water)
  • Secondary: Flour mill processes wheat into flour; bakery makes bread
  • Tertiary: Transport delivers bread to shops; shopkeeper sells it to consumers

Without any one sector, the chain breaks — no wheat means no flour, no transport means bread can't reach consumers.

Q3. What is a cooperative? How did AMUL help dairy farmers?

Answer:

A cooperative is a group of people who voluntarily come together to meet their economic needs collectively, owning and making decisions together.

AMUL helped dairy farmers in Gujarat by:

  • Eliminating middlemen who exploited farmers by paying very low prices
  • Collecting milk directly from farmers and processing it collectively
  • Giving farmers fair prices for their milk
  • Creating a world-famous brand that ensures steady demand
  • Empowering farmers to own and control their own cooperative
🏭 In-Depth Study — SST Chapter 14Economic Activities Around Us — Sectors, Markets & Cooperatives

🏭 Three Sectors of the Economy (Deep Dive)

An economic activity is any work that produces goods or services for money. All such activities are grouped into three main sectors.

SectorWhat it DoesIndia Examples
PrimaryUses natural resources directlyFarming, dairy, fishing in Kerala, mining in Jharkhand
SecondaryConverts raw material → finished goodsTextile mills (Surat), steel plants (Bhilai), car factories (Pune)
TertiaryProvides servicesBanking, IT (Bengaluru), schools, hospitals, transport

🛒 Needs, Wants & Markets (Deep Dive)

📊 Needs vs Wants

NeedsWants
Essential for lifeExtra desires
Food, water, shelter, clothing, education, healthcareToys, video games, branded shoes, ice cream
LimitedUnlimited

Resources are limited but wants are unlimited — so we must use money wisely, distinguish needs from wants, and save / share.

🏬 Types of Markets

  • Local market / weekly bazaar / haat — for daily needs.
  • Wholesale market — large quantities sold to shopkeepers (Mandis).
  • Shopping mall — many shops under one roof.
  • Online market — buying/selling on apps and websites.
  • Stock market — where shares of companies are traded.

🔄 The Chain from Producer to Consumer

Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer (you)

At every stage some profit is added — that is why retail prices are higher than farm prices.

🤝 Cooperatives — People-Powered Economy (Deep Dive)

A cooperative is a group of people who voluntarily come together to meet common economic goals, jointly own and democratically manage their enterprise.

🥛 The AMUL Story

  • Started in 1946 in Anand, Gujarat by Dr. Verghese Kurien.
  • Today: 36 lakh+ farmer-members; world's largest dairy cooperative.
  • Slogan: "The Taste of India".
  • Triggered the White Revolution (Operation Flood) — made India the world's largest milk producer.

📋 Other Famous Cooperatives

BrandStateProduct
AMULGujaratDairy
Mother DairyDelhi NCRDairy
NandiniKarnatakaDairy
AavinTamil NaduDairy
IFFCOPan-IndiaFertiliser
KRIBHCOPan-IndiaFertiliser
Lijjat PapadMaharashtraWomen's cooperative — papad

🇮🇳 IKS — Indian Economic Strengths

  • India is the world's #1 producer of milk and pulses.
  • Among top producers of rice, wheat, sugarcane, cotton, tea.
  • World's #1 in IT services exports.
  • 5th largest economy by GDP (and rising).
  • UPI is the world's biggest real-time digital payment system.
📝 Additional Questions & Answers — Chapter 14

Q1. Differentiate between a need and a want with examples.

Answer:

A need is essential for living (food, water, shelter, clothing, education). A want is something we'd like but can live without (toys, video games, branded clothes).

Q2. Classify these into 3 sectors: tailor, farmer, taxi driver, software engineer, fisherman, baker.

Answer:

  • Primary: Farmer, fisherman.
  • Secondary: Tailor, baker.
  • Tertiary: Taxi driver, software engineer.

Q3. Show the journey of milk from cow to your cup.

Answer:

  1. Primary: Farmer milks the cow.
  2. Village cooperative society collects milk.
  3. Secondary: Dairy plant pasteurises & packs milk.
  4. Tertiary: Trucks deliver milk; shop sells; you buy and drink.

Q4. Why is AMUL called a successful cooperative?

Answer:

Because it removed middlemen, gave farmers fair prices, made India the world's #1 milk producer, and is owned and run by 36+ lakh dairy farmer-members themselves.

Q5. Name 3 modern ways of buying things without going to a shop.

Answer:

Online shopping apps, doorstep grocery delivery, UPI digital payments at home, telephone orders.

📝 Mini-Quiz — Chapter 14 (18 Questions)

  1. Activities that produce goods/services for money are called ___ activities. (economic)
  2. Farming is in the ___ sector. (primary)
  3. Making cars is in the ___ sector. (secondary)
  4. Banking is in the ___ sector. (tertiary)
  5. Things essential for life are called ___. (needs)
  6. Extra desires are called ___. (wants)
  7. A weekly village market is called a ___. (haat)
  8. A large wholesale farm market is called a ___. (mandi)
  9. AMUL is from the state of ___. (Gujarat)
  10. AMUL's founder is Dr. ___. (Verghese Kurien)
  11. The white revolution made India #1 in ___ production. (milk)
  12. IFFCO is a cooperative of ___. (fertilisers)
  13. Lijjat is a women's cooperative for ___. (papad)
  14. UPI stands for ___. (Unified Payments Interface)
  15. True or False: India is the world's #1 milk producer. (True)
  16. True or False: All sectors are independent of each other. (False — interdependent)
  17. True or False: Online shopping is a tertiary-sector service. (True)
  18. True or False: Cooperatives are owned by private investors. (False — by members)

📕 Keyword Glossary — Chapter 14

TermMeaning
Economic activityWork that earns money.
Primary sectorUses natural resources directly.
Secondary sectorManufactures goods.
Tertiary sectorProvides services.
NeedSomething essential for living.
WantExtra desire, not essential.
MarketPlace where goods are bought and sold.
Mandi / HaatWholesale / weekly market.
CooperativeMember-owned enterprise.
ConsumerPerson who buys for personal use.
ProducerPerson/firm that makes goods.
UPIIndian digital payment platform.

🛠️ Suggested Projects — Chapter 14

  1. Market Visit — Visit a local bazaar and list 10 items, their prices and who made them.
  2. Needs vs Wants Diary — For one week, record your purchases and mark each as need or want.
  3. Cooperative Cards — Make flashcards of 6 famous Indian cooperatives.
  4. Chain Chart — Trace the journey of bread / milk / pencil from raw material to your hand.
  5. UPI Awareness — Prepare a 1-page poster explaining how UPI works (with a parent's help).

📋 Unit 1 — What's Inside

📖 Texts

  • Story: A Bottle of Dew (by Sudha Murty)
  • Poem: The Raven and the Fox (by Jean de La Fontaine)

✍️ Skills Covered

  • Reading Comprehension
  • Homophones
  • Connecting Words (because, before, as soon as)
  • Alliteration
  • Rhyming Words

Unit 1: Fables and Folk Tales

Story: A Bottle of Dewby Sudha Murty — A tale about the magic of hard work

📖 Story Summary

Characters

CharacterRoleWhat They Represent
Rama NathaRich landlord's sonPeople who seek shortcuts
MadhumatiRama Natha's wifeHard work and practical wisdom
Sage MahipatiWise manA clever teacher who leads by example

Detailed Story

Part I: Rama Natha inherited large tracts of land from his father but never worked on them. He was obsessed with finding a magic potion that could turn objects into gold. People cheated him, taking his money. His wife Madhumati was worried they would become poor.

Part II: Sage Mahipati came to town and told Rama Natha he could make the potion — but Rama Natha had to collect 5 litres of morning dew from banana plant leaves, all by himself. This would take years since dew forms only in winter.

Part III: Rama Natha planted rows of banana trees and tended them carefully for 6 years. He collected dew every winter morning. Meanwhile, Madhumati sold the bananas at the market and earned good money. They built a huge banana plantation.

Part IV: When Rama Natha finally brought the dew to the sage, nothing happened when he sprinkled it on copper. He was furious! But Madhumati opened a box full of gold coins — earned from selling bananas over 6 years. The sage explained: "It was your hard work that created this wealth, not magic."

📝 Key Vocabulary (Word Meanings)

WordMeaningUse in Sentence
tractslarge areas (of land)The farmer owned large tracts of land.
potiona liquid with magical powersThe witch made a magic potion.
dewtiny water drops on leaves in the morningThe grass was wet with dew.
sagea very wise personThe sage gave good advice.
tendedlooked after / cared forShe tended her garden every day.
plantationa large farm where one crop is grownKerala has many tea plantations.
mutteredspoke in a very low voiceHe muttered something under his breath.
preciousvery valuableTime is precious — don't waste it.
wisdomdeep understanding; good judgementWith age comes wisdom.
sprinkledscattered drops of liquidShe sprinkled water on the plants.
Comprehension Questions & AnswersQuestions from the textbook with detailed answers

Let Us Think and Reflect

Q1. What did Rama Natha believe?

Answer:

Rama Natha believed that there existed a magic potion that could turn any object into gold. He spent all his time and money trying to find this potion, even though people often cheated him by falsely promising to tell him about it.

Q2. How did the sage help Rama Natha?

Answer:

The sage played a clever trick on Rama Natha. Instead of directly telling him that magic potions don't exist (which Rama Natha wouldn't have believed), the sage gave him a task that would force him to work hard — planting banana trees and collecting dew for years. This made Rama Natha develop the habit of hard work, and the banana plantation generated real wealth through honest labour.

Q3. What did the sage ask Rama Natha to do to make the magic potion?

Answer:

The sage asked Rama Natha to:

  1. Plant banana trees
  2. Water them regularly with his own hands
  3. In winter, collect the morning dew from the banana leaves
  4. Store the dew in a bottle until he had 5 litres
  5. Bring the 5 litres to the sage who would chant magic words

The important condition was that Rama Natha had to do everything himself.

Q4. Why did the sage ask Rama Natha to do everything himself?

Answer:

The sage wanted Rama Natha to experience the value of hard work personally. If servants did the work, Rama Natha would not learn the lesson. By doing everything himself — planting, watering, and collecting dew — he would develop discipline, patience, and a strong work ethic. The sage knew that once Rama Natha worked hard, wealth would follow naturally.

Q5. How could Rama Natha have a big banana plantation after six years?

Answer:

Over six years, Rama Natha:

  • Cleaned his large empty fields
  • Planted rows and rows of banana plants
  • Tended them carefully every single day
  • Kept adding more plants each year

Through consistent hard work and dedication over 6 years, what started as empty land became a huge banana plantation. This shows how small daily efforts compound into great results over time.

Q6. How did Rama Natha and Madhumati create wealth?

Answer:

They worked as a team:

  • Rama Natha looked after the banana plantation — planting, watering, and maintaining the trees
  • Madhumati gathered the banana crop, took it to the market, and got a good price for it

Over six years, the plantation grew larger and produced more fruit. The money from selling bananas accumulated into a box full of gold coins. Their wealth came from honest labour and teamwork, not from any magic.

Q7. True or False: (A) Madhumati was troubled about what her husband did. (B) Rama Natha was very happy digging his fields. (C) The sage wanted to show the right path to Rama Natha.

Answer:

(A) TRUE — She was "tired of this and also worried" because he was wasting money on the potion hunt.

(B) FALSE — Rama Natha never worked on his fields before the sage's task. He spent all his time chasing the magic potion.

(C) TRUE — The sage's entire plan was to guide Rama Natha towards the value of hard work without directly lecturing him.

Q8. Why was Rama Natha angry at the end?

Answer:

Rama Natha was angry because when he sprinkled the "magic potion" (the dew with sage's chanting) on a copper vessel, nothing happened — it did not turn into gold. He felt he had wasted six precious years of his life on a false promise. He accused the sage of cheating him. However, his anger turned to surprise when he saw the box of gold coins earned through the plantation.

📚 Grammar: Homophones

Homophones are words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings.

Word 1MeaningWord 2Meaning
dewwater drops on leavesdueexpected at a certain time
sonmale childsunthe star in the sky
Imyself (pronoun)eyeorgan of sight
onethe number 1wonpast tense of "win"
atepast tense of "eat"eightthe number 8
seeto look atseaa large body of water
yourbelonging to youyou'reshort for "you are"

Example sentences using homophones:

  • "I ate eight bananas for breakfast today."
  • "I can see the sea from my window."
  • "The son played in the sun."

📚 Grammar: Connecting Words

Connecting words (conjunctions) join ideas and show the relationship between them:

WordShowsExample
becausereason (why?)He was late because there was a traffic jam.
beforetime (earlier)Finish homework before the deadline.
as soon asimmediately whenThe children ran as soon as the bell rang.
asbecause / whileHe stayed inside as it was raining.
Poem: The Raven and the Foxby Jean de La Fontaine — A fable about flattery and pride

🎭 The Poem (Complete Text)

Stanza 1:
Mr Raven was perched upon a limb,
And Reynard the Fox looked up at him;
For the Raven held in his great big beak,
A morsel the Fox would go far to seek.

Stanza 2:
Said the Fox, in admiring tones: "My word!
Sir Raven, you are a handsome bird.
Such feathers! If you would only sing,
The birds of these woods would call you King."

Stanza 3:
The Raven, who did not see the joke,
Forgot that his voice was just a croak.
He opened his beak, in his foolish pride –
And down fell the morsel the Fox had eyed.

Stanza 4:
"Ha-ha!" laughed the Fox. "And now you know,
Ignore sweet words that make you glow.
Pride, my friend, is rather unwise;
I'm sure this teaching is quite a surprise."

📝 Poem — Word Meanings

WordMeaning
perchedsat (on a branch)
limbbranch of a tree
Reynardthe name of the fox
morsela small piece of food
admiringshowing praise and wonder
croakthe harsh sound a raven makes
pridefeeling that you are better than others
eyedlooked at with interest/desire
woodsa smaller area of forest
glowto feel happy and proud

Poem — Questions & Answers

Q1. What does the Raven have in his beak?

Answer:

The Raven has a morsel (a small piece of food) in his beak, which the Fox badly wants.

Q2. How does Reynard (the Fox) trick the Raven into opening his beak?

Answer:

The Fox uses flattery — he praises the Raven's appearance, calling him a "handsome bird" with beautiful feathers. Then he says that if the Raven would only sing, all the birds would call him King. This false praise makes the Raven open his beak to sing, and the food falls out.

Q3. Why does the Raven forget that his voice is just a croak?

Answer:

The Raven forgets because he is overcome by foolish pride. The Fox's praise makes him feel so good about himself that he genuinely believes he can sing beautifully. Flattery blinds him to reality — he "did not see the joke."

Q4. Why does Reynard say that pride is not wise?

Answer:

Reynard says pride is unwise because it makes us vulnerable to manipulation. When we are too proud, we believe any praise people give us — even false flattery. The Raven's pride made him lose his food. The lesson is: don't let sweet words go to your head.

Q5. What is the moral of this poem?

Answer:

The moral is: "Beware of people who praise you too much — they may have selfish motives." We should not let flattery make us foolish. Excessive pride leads to loss. Always think before acting on someone's sweet words.

✨ Literary Devices in the Poem

1. Rhyming Words

StanzaRhyming Pairs
Stanza 1limb — him; beak — seek
Stanza 2word — bird; sing — King
Stanza 3joke — croak; pride — eyed
Stanza 4know — glow; unwise — surprise

2. Alliteration

Alliteration = repetition of the same consonant sound at the beginning of nearby words.

  • "great big beak" — /b/ sound repeats
  • "sweet words ... surprise" — /s/ sound repeats

3. Personification

The animals are given human qualities — the Fox speaks, flatters, and laughs; the Raven feels pride.

💭 Exam Tips — Unit 1

  • Moral of "A Bottle of Dew": Hard work is the real magic — there are no shortcuts to success.
  • Moral of "The Raven and the Fox": Don't be fooled by flattery; excessive pride leads to loss.
  • Homophones: Remember — same SOUND, different SPELLING and MEANING.
  • Alliteration: Look for words starting with the SAME CONSONANT SOUND close together.

📋 Chapter 2 — The Unlikely Best Friends

📖 Contents

  • Story: The Elephant and the Dog
  • Poem: Friendship by Jill Wolf
  • Vocabulary & Word Meanings
  • Grammar: Verb Forms (Past Tenses)
  • Writing: Describing a Friend

✍️ Key Points

  • Theme: True friendship, unlikely bonds
  • Source: Jataka Tales (Buddhist stories)
  • Values: Loyalty, compassion, kindness
  • Grammar: Simple Past, Past Continuous, Past Perfect

Chapter 2: The Unlikely Best Friends

📖 Story Summary: The Elephant and the Dog

Part I — An Unusual Friendship Begins

Gajaraj, a royal elephant, lived in the best booth of the king's stables. Despite royal comforts, he was sad because he had no friends. The mahout (elephant trainer) was kind but not a friend.

One night, a stray dog wandered into the stable, attracted by the smell of food. Gajaraj pushed some food towards the dog. The dog wagged his tail in thanks, ate the food, and fell asleep. From that day, the two became inseparable friends.

They ate together, slept together, and played together. The elephant would swing the dog with his trunk, and the dog would slide down the elephant's back.

Part II — Separation & Reunion

One day, a farmer saw the dog and offered to buy him from the mahout. The mahout, not realising how close the friends were, accepted. The dog was taken away.

After the separation:

  • Gajaraj refused to eat — his favourite food remained untouched
  • The mahout was worried and reported to the king
  • The king asked his wise minister to investigate
  • The minister discovered the elephant had stopped eating after his dog friend was taken away
  • The king ordered the dog to be returned

The farmer was told to bring the dog back. When the rope was removed, the dog ran straight to the stable. Gajaraj picked him up with his trunk and gave him a joyous swing. Both friends ate together again, reunited and happy!

💡 Key Message

True friendship knows no boundaries — it can exist between the most unlikely pairs. The bond of friendship is so strong that life loses meaning without it.

Source: This story is from the Jataka Tales — ancient Buddhist stories about life values and wisdom. These tales have been translated into many languages worldwide.

📝 Vocabulary & Word Meanings

WordMeaning
MahoutAn elephant trainer/keeper
BoothA stable/shelter for an elephant
StrayA homeless/wandering animal
WincedShrunk back in pain or distress
ChoresDaily duties/tasks
YelpedMade a short sharp cry (of a dog)
InseparableUnable to be separated; always together
ComfortsThings that make life easy and pleasant
CrumbsSmall pieces of food
ReunitedBrought together again after separation

📜 Poem: Friendship — by Jill Wolf

May my friendships always be
The most important thing to me.
With special friends I feel I'm blessed,
So let me give my very best.

I want to do much more than share
The hopes and plans of friends who care;
I'll try all that a friend can do
To make their wishes come true.

Let me use my heart to see,
To realise what friends can be,
And make no judgements from afar,
But love my friends the way they are.

Poem Explanation:

  • Stanza 1: The poet values friendship above all. Having friends makes her feel blessed, so she wants to give her best.
  • Stanza 2: Friendship is more than just sharing — it means actively helping friends achieve their dreams.
  • Stanza 3: Use your heart (feelings) to understand friends, don't judge them, and accept them as they are.

Key Values in the Poem:

  • Gratitude: Feeling blessed to have friends
  • Giving: Offering your best, not just receiving
  • Empathy: Using your heart to understand
  • Acceptance: Loving friends as they are, without judgement

📐 Grammar: Verb Forms — Past Tenses

TenseFormUseExample
Simple Pastverb + ed (or irregular)Completed action in the pastThe farmer noticed that Buntee was sad.
Past Continuouswas/were + verb-ingAction that was ongoing in the pastThe farmer was tying Buntee with a rope.
Past Perfecthad + past participleAction completed before another past actionHe had tied Buntee before he left.

Practice:

"The farmer came back and noticed (simple past) that Buntee was sad. He had tied (past perfect) Buntee with a rope the day before. He asked (simple past) Buntee why he was sad. The farmer felt (simple past) that Buntee was missing (past continuous) Gajaraj and set him free."

Tips to Remember:

  • Simple Past: "Yesterday, I played" — done and finished
  • Past Continuous: "I was playing when..." — was ongoing
  • Past Perfect: "I had finished before he came" — earlier past action

Questions & Answers

Q1. Why was Gajaraj sad despite living in royal comforts?

Answer:

Gajaraj was sad because he had no friends. Although the king had ordered that he be well looked after, and the mahout served him food and gave him baths, the mahout was a caretaker, not a friend. Gajaraj was lonely despite all material comforts.

Q2. How did Gajaraj and the dog become friends?

Answer:

One night, a stray dog wandered into the stable attracted by the smell of food. Gajaraj kindly pushed some food towards the dog. The dog wagged his tail in thanks and ate. From that day, the dog kept coming back. They ate together, played together, and became inseparable friends. The elephant would swing the dog with his trunk.

Q3. What happened when the dog was taken away?

Answer:

When the dog was taken away by a farmer, Gajaraj refused to eat. His favourite food remained untouched. The mahout reported to the king, who sent his wise minister to investigate. The minister discovered that the elephant had stopped eating after his dog friend was taken. The king ordered the dog to be returned, and when the dog came back, both friends happily reunited.

Q4. What is the message of the poem "Friendship" by Jill Wolf?

Answer:

The poem teaches us that:

  • Friendships should be the most important thing in our lives
  • We should give our best to friends, not just receive
  • We should use our heart (feelings and empathy) to understand friends
  • We should not judge friends but accept and love them as they are
  • True friendship means actively helping friends achieve their dreams

Q5. What is the difference between Simple Past, Past Continuous, and Past Perfect?

Answer:

  • Simple Past (played/ate/went): A completed action in the past. "He noticed the dog."
  • Past Continuous (was playing/was eating): An action that was ongoing at a particular time in the past. "He was sleeping when the phone rang."
  • Past Perfect (had played/had eaten): An action completed BEFORE another past action. "He had left before I arrived."

📋 Chapter 3 — Nurturing Nature

📖 Contents

  • Story: Neem Baba (dialogue form)
  • Poem: What a Bird Thought
  • Reading: Spices that Heal Us
  • Grammar: Personification
  • Vocabulary: Nature & medicinal words

✍️ Key Points

  • Theme: Nature, trees, healing, growth
  • Personification: treating non-humans as persons
  • Neem tree: medicinal uses (skin, teeth, crops)
  • Bird poem: metaphor for growing up
  • Spices: turmeric, ginger, clove — healing

Chapter 3: Nurturing Nature

📖 Story: Neem Baba

(Written as a dialogue/conversation — an example of Personification)

Summary:

Amber, a schoolgirl, sits under a neem tree in her courtyard and has a conversation with the tree, whom she calls "Neem Baba" (Grandfather Neem).

Key Facts About the Neem Tree (from the story):

FeatureDetails
OriginBorn in India; found across the subcontinent
NamesSanskrit: Arishta ("curer of diseases"), Nimba; Hindi: Nimb; Others: Leemba, Nimori, Nimo
Name "Neem"Given by Iranians (Persian origin)
Medicinal usesBark cures cough; flowers cure eye problems (kajal); leaves for skin diseases; twigs for cleaning teeth
AgricultureNeem seed powder protects crops from pests (natural pesticide)
Mosquito controlNeem oil in stagnant water stops mosquito breeding (cost-effective!)
EnvironmentProvides shade, purifies air, prevents soil erosion

⚠️ Important Note

Traditional remedies should only be used by well-trained, experienced people. Always consult a doctor for medical issues!

📜 Poem: What a Bird Thought

I lived first in a little house,
And lived there very well;
I thought the world was small and round,
And made of pale blue shell.

I lived next in a little nest,
Nor needed any other;
I thought the world was made of straw,
And brooded by my mother.

One day I fluttered from the nest
To see what I could find.
I said, "The world is made of leaves;
I have been very blind."

At last I flew beyond the tree
And saw the sky so blue.
Now, how the world is really made
I cannot tell—can you?

Stanza-by-Stanza Explanation:

StanzaThe Bird's WorldMeaning
1Inside the egg — "pale blue shell"The bird thinks the whole world is its tiny egg
2In the nest — "made of straw"The bird's world expands to the nest; still small
3On the tree — "made of leaves"The bird explores but still has limited vision
4Beyond the tree — "sky so blue"The world is vast and unknowable; always more to discover!

Theme & Message:

  • As we grow, our understanding of the world keeps expanding
  • We should never assume we know everything — there's always more to learn
  • Growth = moving from a small, limited world to a bigger one
  • Metaphor for children growing up and discovering the wider world

Rhyming Words:

well/shell, other/mother, find/blind, blue/you

🌿 Reading: Spices That Heal Us

Many common kitchen spices have medicinal properties passed down through generations:

SpiceHealing Use
Turmeric (Haldi)Anti-inflammatory, wound healing, immunity booster; turmeric milk for cold/cough
Ginger (Adrak)Helps digestion, relieves nausea, cures cold & sore throat
Clove (Laung)Toothache relief, antibacterial
Black Pepper (Kali Mirch)Helps digestion, cough relief
Cinnamon (Dalchini)Blood sugar control, antioxidant

The narrator's grandmother would find cures for most weather-related illnesses in the kitchen — using these common spices!

📐 Grammar: Personification

Personification = giving human qualities to non-human things (animals, objects, nature).

ExampleWhat is personified?Human quality given
"The leaves whispered in the wind."LeavesWhispering (speaking softly)
"The flowers danced merrily in the breeze."FlowersDancing
"Neem Baba spoke to Amber."Neem treeSpeaking, having a name
"The sun smiled down on us."SunSmiling

The entire "Neem Baba" story uses personification — the tree speaks, remembers, and has feelings!

Questions & Answers

Q1. What are the medicinal uses of the neem tree?

Answer:

  • Bark: Cures cough and breathing problems
  • Flowers: Used to prepare kajal that cures eye itching
  • Leaves: Treats skin diseases, purifies blood
  • Twigs: Used as natural toothbrush (datun) for healthy teeth
  • Seeds: Neem seed powder acts as natural pesticide for crops
  • Oil: Applied in stagnant water to stop mosquito breeding

Q2. What is the main idea of each stanza in "What a Bird Thought"?

Answer:

  1. Stanza 1: The bird lived inside its egg and thought the world was small and round (like the shell).
  2. Stanza 2: The bird lived in a nest and thought the world was made of straw, with its mother brooding over it.
  3. Stanza 3: The bird fluttered out of the nest onto the tree and thought the world was made of leaves.
  4. Stanza 4: The bird flew beyond the tree and saw the vast blue sky — realising the world is too big to fully know.

Message: As we grow, our world keeps expanding. There is always more to discover!

Q3. What is personification? Give two examples from this chapter.

Answer:

Personification is a figure of speech in which human qualities are given to non-human things.

Examples from this chapter:

  1. "The neem tree spoke to Amber" — The tree is given the human ability to speak
  2. "The leaves whispered in the wind" — Leaves are given the human action of whispering

📋 Chapter 4 — Sports and Wellness

📖 Contents

  • Story: Change of Heart
  • Poem: The Winner
  • Reading: Benefits of Yoga
  • Grammar: Practice vs Practise
  • Values: Sportsmanship & Fair Play

✍️ Key Points

  • Theme: Sporting spirit, accepting defeat
  • Values: Honesty, enjoyment over winning
  • Poem: Joy of outdoor play
  • Yoga: Physical & mental benefits
  • Grammar: Practice (noun) vs Practise (verb)

Chapter 4: Sports and Wellness

📖 Story: Change of Heart

Characters:

  • Prabhat: A boy who always wants to win; cannot accept defeat; even cheats to win
  • Surya: A boy who enjoys playing regardless of winning or losing; always happy

Summary:

Beginning: Prabhat always wanted to win. He could not bear losing — not even at marbles. He felt terrible when he lost. If he sensed he would lose, he would quit the game midway or make excuses.

The Badminton Match: A new boy, Surya, joined Prabhat's school. Surya was cheerful, always smiling and cracking jokes. On Friday, they played badminton. Surya was excellent and kept winning points. But he was paying so little attention (joking around) that Prabhat managed to cheat by changing the scoreboard when Surya wasn't looking. Prabhat "won" but felt no real happiness.

Observation: Prabhat noticed something remarkable about Surya. The next day, Surya played basketball and was terrible at it — couldn't score for 10 minutes! Yet he was happy, smiling the whole time. Prabhat watched Surya for days and realised: Surya was great at some things, terrible at others, but enjoyed everything equally.

Change of Heart: Prabhat realised that enjoying the game is more important than winning. He understood that:

  • Cheating brings no real satisfaction
  • You can enjoy a game even if you lose
  • The process of playing matters more than the result
  • True sporting spirit means accepting both victory and defeat gracefully

💡 Key Message — Sporting Spirit

True sportsmanship means enjoying the game, playing fairly, accepting defeat gracefully, and celebrating others' victories without jealousy. Winning by cheating is not winning at all!

📜 Poem: The Winner

A poem about children playing outdoors — running, chasing, playing until evening.

Key Ideas:

  • Children play in a neighbour's field
  • They run so fast they can't catch their breath
  • They play from afternoon till evening (when sky turns dark)
  • Their feet get cold as night approaches
  • They finally stop when they can't see anymore
  • "Night wins!" — personification (night is shown as winning over day)

Literary Devices:

DeviceExample from PoemExplanation
Personification"Night wins!"Night is given the human quality of winning
Repetition"I can't even catch my breath"Emphasises being out of breath from running
Imagery"blue sky turns dark", "trees not clearly visible"Vivid description of evening approaching

🧘 Reading: Benefits of Yoga

Yoga is an ancient Indian practice now recognised worldwide (WHO mYoga app!).

Benefits of Yoga:

Physical BenefitsMental Benefits
Stretches & strengthens musclesCalms the mind
Increases flexibility & balanceReduces stress & anxiety
Improves physical fitnessImproves sleep quality
Reduces back painImproves concentration
Builds endurancePromotes emotional well-being

Yoga involves: Asanas (postures) + Pranayama (breathing techniques) + Meditation (calming the mind)

📐 Grammar: Practice vs Practise

WordTypeUseExample
PracticeNounThe thing/activity itself"Yoga practice is beneficial." / "I need more practice."
PractiseVerbThe act of doing it"I practise yoga daily." / "She practises singing."

Trick to remember: Practice = noun (like "advice"). Practise = verb (like "advise").

Questions & Answers

Q1. How did Prabhat's attitude towards winning affect his enjoyment of games?

Answer:

Prabhat's obsession with winning ruined his enjoyment of games. He felt terrible when he lost, so he would quit midway or make excuses rather than continue. He even cheated (changed the scoreboard) to win against Surya. But cheating brought him no real satisfaction — only guilt. His attitude meant he could never relax and enjoy playing.

Q2. How did Surya's attitude differ from Prabhat's?

Answer:

Surya's attitude was completely opposite to Prabhat's:

  • Surya enjoyed playing regardless of winning or losing
  • He was happy even when he was terrible at basketball (couldn't score for 10 minutes!)
  • He was always smiling, joking, and having fun during games
  • He was great at some things and bad at others, but enjoyed everything equally
  • Prabhat only felt good when winning; Surya felt good just by playing

Q3. Why is sporting spirit important?

Answer:

Sporting spirit is important because:

  • It teaches us to enjoy the process, not just the result
  • It helps us accept defeat gracefully and learn from it
  • It prevents cheating and dishonesty — fair play builds character
  • It promotes respect for opponents
  • It makes games enjoyable for everyone, not just the winner
  • It teaches life lessons — we can't win everything in life, and that's okay

Q4. What are the benefits of yoga?

Answer:

Physical benefits: Yoga stretches and strengthens muscles, increases flexibility and balance, improves physical fitness, and reduces body pains like lower back pain.

Mental benefits: Yoga involves breathing techniques and meditation that calm the mind, reduce stress and anxiety, improve sleep quality, and boost concentration.

📋 Chapter 5 — Culture and Tradition

📖 Contents

  • Reading: Hamara Bharat — Incredible India
  • Story: Kalakritiyon ka Bharat (Art Forms)
  • Poem: The Kites
  • Story: Ila Sachani — Embroidering Dreams
  • Grammar: Sequencing words
  • Writing: Describing art forms

✍️ Key Points

  • Theme: India's rich cultural diversity
  • Art forms: Dokra, coconut craft, Tanjore painting
  • Poem: Joy of kite-flying, imagination
  • Values: Determination despite challenges
  • Grammar: Sequencing (first, then, finally)
  • Programme: Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat

Chapter 5: Culture and Tradition

🇮🇳 Hamara Bharat — Incredible India

A passage describing India's greatness:

  • Known worldwide as a land of wise and heroic individuals
  • Rich and diverse culture has attracted travellers since ancient times
  • People live in unison — presenting unity in diversity to the world
  • Rich with rivers, lakes, high mountains, and green forests
  • Home to many plants and animals
  • Advice: Explore and appreciate India's beauty and heritage!

🎨 Kalakritiyon ka Bharat — Art Forms of India

Students from different states share art forms under the "Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat" programme:

StudentStateArt FormProcess
PriyaranjanChhattisgarh/OdishaDokra (metal casting)Make clay mould → Pour molten metal → Remove clay layer → Finish the figure
ChitraKeralaCoconut Shell CraftClean shell → Smooth with sandpaper → Give shape → Fix base → Polish with wood polish
BalamuraliTamil NaduTanjore PaintingDraw on board → Apply gold foil → Add gemstones → Paint with colours

Other Famous Indian Art Forms:

  • Madhubani painting — Bihar (natural colours on walls)
  • Warli art — Maharashtra (tribal art with geometric shapes)
  • Phulkari embroidery — Punjab (colourful thread work)
  • Pattachitra — Odisha (cloth-based scroll painting)

🪁 Poem: The Kites

A poem about a child watching kites fly and imagining riding one:

Key Ideas:

  • The child sees kites of many colours flying in the sky
  • They look like birds of different colours
  • The child imagines climbing a tree to reach a kite
  • The child dreams of riding the kite up in the sky
  • From above, they would hear songs of the wind
  • People below would look up in wonder at the child on the kite
  • The poem captures the joy and freedom of imagination

Kite Festivals in India:

Festival/OccasionRegion
Makar Sankranti / UttarayanGujarat (International Kite Festival)
Independence Day (15 August)Delhi and North India
Basant PanchamiPunjab, North India
VishuKerala

💪 Story: Ila Sachani — Embroidering Dreams with Her Feet

An inspiring story about determination and courage:

  • Ila Sachani — a woman who embroiders beautiful designs using her feet
  • Despite physical challenges, she excels at intricate embroidery work
  • Shows that disability is not inability
  • With determination and practice, people can achieve extraordinary things

Similar Inspiring Person:

Pranav M. Balasubramaniam from Palakkad, Kerala — born without both hands, he excels as an artist, singer, and para-sportsperson!

💡 Key Message

Physical challenges do not define a person's ability. With determination, hard work, and a positive attitude, anyone can achieve remarkable things.

📐 Grammar: Sequencing Words

When describing a process or sequence of events, we use sequencing words:

PositionWords to UseExample
StartFirst, To begin, InitiallyFirst, I get up and brush my teeth.
MiddleThen, Next, After that, MeanwhileThen, I take a bath and get dressed.
EndFinally, At last, In the endFinally, I pick up my bag and leave for school.

Example: How I Get Ready for School

First, I get up and go to take a bath. Then, I get dressed in my school uniform. After that, I eat my breakfast. Next, I pack my school bag. Finally, I say goodbye to my parents and leave for school.

Questions & Answers

Q1. What has Bharat always been known for?

Answer:

Bharat has been known worldwide as a land of wise and heroic individuals. Its rich and diverse culture has attracted numerous travellers since ancient times. The people live in unison, presenting unity in diversity to the world. It is also rich with rivers, lakes, high mountains, and green forests with diverse plants and animals.

Q2. Describe any one art form mentioned in the chapter.

Answer:

Dokra is a traditional metal casting art from Chhattisgarh/Odisha, shared by Priyaranjan:

  1. First, a clay mould is made in the desired shape
  2. Then, molten metal (usually brass) is poured into the mould
  3. After that, when the metal cools, the outer layer of clay is removed
  4. Finally, the metal figure is finished and polished as desired

Dokra art creates beautiful figurines, jewellery, and decorative items.

Q3. What does the child imagine in the poem "The Kites"?

Answer:

The child watches colourful kites flying in the sky and imagines:

  • Climbing a tree to reach a kite
  • Riding the kite up into the sky
  • Hearing songs of the wind while flying
  • Looking down at people who would gaze up in wonder

The poem captures the joy of imagination and the desire for freedom, like a kite soaring in the sky.

Q4. What message does the story of Ila Sachani give us?

Answer:

The story of Ila Sachani teaches us that:

  • Disability is not inability — physical challenges don't define a person's capabilities
  • With determination, hard work, and a positive attitude, anyone can achieve extraordinary things
  • Ila embroiders beautiful designs using her feet — proving that where there's a will, there's a way
  • We should never give up on our dreams, no matter what obstacles we face

📋 पाठ 1 — मातृभूमि (Motherland)

📖 इस पाठ में (In This Chapter)

  • कविता: मातृभूमि — सोहनलाल द्विवेदी
  • Poem: Matrubhoomi (Motherland) — Sohanlal Dwivedi
  • कठिन शब्दार्थ (Difficult Word Meanings)
  • भावार्थ (Explanation of each stanza)
  • प्रश्न-उत्तर (Questions & Answers)
  • वंदे मातरम् — अर्थ (Meaning)

✍️ याद रखें (Key Points)

  • विधा: कविता (Form: Poetry)
  • कवि: सोहनलाल द्विवेदी
  • विषय: देशभक्ति / भारत का गौरव
  • Theme: Patriotism / Glory of India
  • प्रमुख बिंदु: हिमालय, नदियाँ, महापुरुष
  • Key: Himalayas, Rivers, Great personalities

पाठ 1: मातृभूमि (Motherland)

✏️ कवि परिचय (About the Poet)

सोहनलाल द्विवेदी हिंदी के प्रसिद्ध देशभक्ति कवि थे। वे लगभग 125 साल पहले पैदा हुए थे। उस समय भारत पर अंग्रेज़ों का राज था। उन्होंने अपनी कविताओं से अंग्रेज़ों का विरोध किया।

Sohanlal Dwivedi was a famous Hindi patriotic poet born about 125 years ago. During British rule over India, he opposed the British through his poetry. His favourite subject was patriotism (देशभक्ति). Other famous poems: "बढ़े चलो, बढ़े चलो", "कोशिश करने वालों की हार नहीं होती".

📜 सम्पूर्ण कविता (Complete Poem)

छंद 1 (Stanza 1):

ऊँचा खड़ा हिमालय, आकाश चूमता है,
नीचे चरण तले झुक, नित सिंधु झूमता है।
गंगा यमुन त्रिवेणी, नदियाँ लहर रही हैं,
जगमग छटा निराली, पग पग छहर रही हैं।
वह पुण्य-भूमि मेरी, वह स्वर्ण-भूमि मेरी।
वह जन्मभूमि मेरी, वह मातृभूमि मेरी।

छंद 2 (Stanza 2):

झरने अनेक झरते, जिसकी पहाड़ियों में,
चिड़ियाँ चहक रही हैं, हो मस्त झाड़ियों में।
अमराइयाँ घनी हैं, कोयल पुकारती है,
बहती मलय पवन है, तन-मन सँवारती है।
वह धर्मभूमि मेरी, वह कर्मभूमि मेरी।
वह जन्मभूमि मेरी, वह मातृभूमि मेरी।

छंद 3 (Stanza 3):

जन्मे जहाँ थे रघुपति, जन्मी जहाँ थी सीता,
श्रीकृष्ण ने सुनाई, वंशी पुनीत गीता।
गौतम ने जन्म लेकर, जिसका सुयश बढ़ाया,
जग को दया सिखाई, जग को दिया दिखाया।
वह युद्ध-भूमि मेरी, वह बुद्ध-भूमि मेरी।
वह मातृभूमि मेरी, वह जन्मभूमि मेरी।

📖 कठिन शब्दार्थ — Word Meanings (Hindi + English)

शब्द (Word)हिंदी अर्थ (Hindi Meaning)English Meaning
हिमालयबर्फ का घर — भारत के उत्तर में सबसे ऊँचा पर्वतThe Himalayas — highest mountain in the north of India
आकाश चूमनाआसमान को छूना (इतना ऊँचा होना)Kissing the sky (being extremely tall)
चरणपैरFeet
सिंधुसमुद्र (ocean)The ocean / sea
नितहमेशा, रोज़Always, daily
त्रिवेणीतीन नदियों का संगम (गंगा + यमुना + सरस्वती)Confluence of three rivers (Ganga + Yamuna + Saraswati)
जगमगचमकती हुईShining, glittering
छटासुंदरता, शोभाBeauty, splendour
छहर रहीबिखर रही, फैल रहीSpreading, scattering
पुण्य-भूमिपवित्र भूमिSacred/holy land
स्वर्ण-भूमिसोने जैसी कीमती भूमिGolden land (precious land)
मातृभूमिमाँ समान भूमि, जन्मस्थानMotherland
अमराइयाँआम के पेड़ों के बगीचेMango groves/orchards
मलय पवनदक्षिण से आने वाली शीतल और सुगंधित हवाCool, fragrant breeze from the south (Malaya mountains)
रघुपतिभगवान राम (रघु वंश के स्वामी)Lord Rama (lord of Raghu dynasty)
वंशीबाँसुरीFlute
पुनीतपवित्रSacred, holy
गीताश्रीमद्भगवद्गीता (श्रीकृष्ण का उपदेश)The Bhagavad Gita (Lord Krishna's teachings)
गौतमगौतम बुद्धGautam Buddha
सुयशअच्छा यश / प्रसिद्धिGood fame, glory
दयाकरुणा, दूसरों पर तरसCompassion, kindness
दियारोशनी / ज्ञान का प्रकाशLamp / light of knowledge

📝 भावार्थ — Detailed Explanation (Hindi + English)

छंद 1 का भावार्थ (Stanza 1 Explanation):

हिंदी में: कवि कहते हैं कि ऊँचा हिमालय पर्वत खड़ा है जो आकाश को छू रहा है (इतना ऊँचा है)। नीचे उसके पैरों (तलहटी) में समुद्र झूम रहा है (लहरा रहा है)। गंगा, यमुना और त्रिवेणी (तीन नदियों का संगम) — ये नदियाँ बह रही हैं। हर जगह जगमगाती सुंदरता फैली हुई है। यह मेरी पवित्र भूमि है, यह मेरी सोने जैसी कीमती भूमि है। यह मेरी जन्मभूमि है, यह मेरी माँ समान भूमि (मातृभूमि) है।

In English: The poet says that the tall Himalayas stand touching the sky. Below, at its feet, the ocean sways. The Ganga, Yamuna and Triveni (confluence of three rivers) are flowing. Everywhere there is sparkling beauty. This is my sacred land, my golden land. This is my birthland, my motherland.

छंद 2 का भावार्थ (Stanza 2 Explanation):

हिंदी में: कवि कहते हैं कि इस देश की पहाड़ियों में अनेक झरने बहते हैं। झाड़ियों में चिड़ियाँ खुशी से चहक रही हैं। आम के बगीचे (अमराइयाँ) घने हैं और कोयल पुकार (कूक) रही है। दक्षिण से आने वाली शीतल सुगंधित हवा (मलय पवन) बह रही है जो तन और मन दोनों को सँवार रही है (ताज़ा कर रही है)। यह मेरी धर्मभूमि है, यह मेरी कर्मभूमि है।

In English: The poet says that many waterfalls cascade in this country's hills. Birds chirp happily in the bushes. The mango groves are dense and the cuckoo is calling. The cool, fragrant breeze from the south (Malay wind) blows, refreshing body and mind. This is my land of dharma (righteousness), my land of karma (action).

छंद 3 का भावार्थ (Stanza 3 Explanation):

हिंदी में: कवि कहते हैं कि इसी भूमि पर भगवान राम (रघुपति) का जन्म हुआ, इसी भूमि पर सीता जी का जन्म हुआ। यहीं श्रीकृष्ण ने बाँसुरी बजाई और पवित्र गीता सुनाई। गौतम बुद्ध ने यहाँ जन्म लेकर इस देश का यश (प्रसिद्धि) बढ़ाया। उन्होंने संसार को दया (करुणा) सिखाई और ज्ञान का प्रकाश (दिया) दिखाया। यह मेरी युद्ध-भूमि भी है और बुद्ध-भूमि भी।

In English: The poet says that Lord Rama (Raghupati) was born on this land, Sita was born here. Lord Krishna played his flute and taught the sacred Gita here. Gautam Buddha was born here and increased this country's fame. He taught the world compassion and showed the light of knowledge. This is my land of warriors AND my land of the Buddha.

🎨 कविता की विशेषताएँ (Special Features of the Poem)

🔤 भाषा-ज्ञान (Language Learning)

"भूमि" से बने शब्द (Words made with "Bhoomi"):

शब्दअर्थ (Hindi)Meaning (English)
जन्मभूमिजहाँ जन्म हुआBirthplace
मातृभूमिमाँ समान भूमि, अपना देशMotherland
कर्मभूमिजहाँ काम किया जाएLand of action/work
धर्मभूमिधर्म की भूमिLand of righteousness
युद्धभूमिजहाँ युद्ध होBattlefield
बुद्धभूमिबुद्ध की भूमिLand of the Buddha
तपोभूमिजहाँ तपस्या की जाएLand of penance
रणभूमिलड़ाई का मैदानBattlefield

"दया" और "दिया" — एक मात्रा का अंतर!

एक मात्रा बदलने से अर्थ बदल जाता है (One vowel mark changes the meaning):

शब्द 1अर्थशब्द 2अर्थ
दयाकरुणा (compassion)दियारोशनी/दीपक (lamp/light)
घड़ामिट्टी का बर्तन (pot)घड़ीसमय बताने वाली (clock)
कलबीता हुआ/आने वाला दिनकीलनुकीली चीज़ (nail)
सरसिर (head)सारनिचोड़ (essence)

प्रश्न-उत्तर (Questions & Answers)

प्रश्न 1: कवि ने भारत को कौन-कौन सी उपाधियाँ दी हैं?

Q1: What titles has the poet given to India?

उत्तर (Answer):

हिंदी में: कवि ने भारत को ये उपाधियाँ दी हैं:

  • पुण्य-भूमि — पवित्र भूमि (Sacred land)
  • स्वर्ण-भूमि — सोने जैसी कीमती भूमि (Golden/precious land)
  • जन्मभूमि — जहाँ जन्म हुआ (Birthplace)
  • मातृभूमि — माँ समान भूमि (Motherland)
  • धर्मभूमि — धर्म की भूमि (Land of righteousness)
  • कर्मभूमि — काम करने की भूमि (Land of action)
  • युद्ध-भूमि — वीरता की भूमि (Land of warriors)
  • बुद्ध-भूमि — ज्ञान की भूमि (Land of wisdom/Buddha)

In English: The poet has called India: Sacred land, Golden land, Birthland, Motherland, Land of righteousness, Land of action, Land of warriors, and Land of the Buddha.

प्रश्न 2: कविता में भारत की प्रकृति का वर्णन कैसे किया गया है?

Q2: How is India's nature described in the poem?

उत्तर (Answer):

हिंदी में: कवि ने भारत की प्रकृति का सुंदर वर्णन किया है:

  • हिमालय — ऊँचा खड़ा है, आकाश को छू रहा है
  • समुद्र — हिमालय के चरणों (नीचे) में झूम रहा है
  • नदियाँ — गंगा, यमुना, त्रिवेणी लहरा रही हैं
  • झरने — पहाड़ियों में अनेक झरने बह रहे हैं
  • पक्षी — चिड़ियाँ झाड़ियों में चहक रही हैं, कोयल पुकार रही है
  • अमराइयाँ — आम के घने बगीचे हैं
  • मलय पवन — शीतल सुगंधित हवा तन-मन सँवार रही है

In English: The poet describes: the Himalayas touching the sky, the ocean swaying below, rivers (Ganga, Yamuna, Triveni) flowing, waterfalls in the hills, birds chirping, cuckoo calling in dense mango groves, and cool fragrant breeze refreshing body and mind.

प्रश्न 3: कविता में किन-किन महापुरुषों का नाम आया है? उनके बारे में बताइए।

Q3: Which great personalities are mentioned in the poem? Tell about them.

उत्तर (Answer):

महापुरुषहिंदी में परिचयIn English
रघुपति (राम)भगवान राम — अयोध्या में जन्मे, मर्यादा पुरुषोत्तमLord Rama — born in Ayodhya, ideal man
सीताभगवान राम की पत्नी — आदर्श नारीSita — wife of Rama, ideal woman
श्रीकृष्णभगवान कृष्ण — बाँसुरी बजाने वाले, गीता का उपदेश दियाLord Krishna — flute player, taught the Bhagavad Gita
गौतम बुद्धबौद्ध धर्म के संस्थापक — दुनिया को दया और ज्ञान सिखायाFounder of Buddhism — taught compassion and wisdom to the world

प्रश्न 4: "जग को दया सिखाई, जग को दिया दिखाया" — इस पंक्ति का अर्थ समझाइए।

Q4: Explain the meaning of "Jag ko daya sikhai, jag ko diya dikhaya".

उत्तर (Answer):

हिंदी में: इस पंक्ति में गौतम बुद्ध की बात कही गई है। "दया सिखाई" का अर्थ है — बुद्ध ने दुनिया (जग) को करुणा और दया का पाठ पढ़ाया। "दिया दिखाया" का अर्थ है — उन्होंने ज्ञान का दीपक (प्रकाश) दिखाया, यानी अज्ञानता के अँधेरे को दूर किया।

In English: This line is about Gautam Buddha. "Taught compassion to the world" means Buddha taught kindness and mercy. "Showed the lamp to the world" means he showed the light of knowledge, removing the darkness of ignorance. Note the beautiful wordplay: दया (daya = compassion) and दिया (diya = lamp) — just one vowel mark different!

प्रश्न 5: कविता का मूल भाव (central idea) क्या है?

Q5: What is the central idea of the poem?

उत्तर (Answer):

हिंदी में: इस कविता का मूल भाव देशभक्ति (patriotism) है। कवि सोहनलाल द्विवेदी ने भारत की प्रकृति (हिमालय, नदियाँ, झरने), भारत के महापुरुषों (राम, कृष्ण, बुद्ध) और भारत की महानता का गौरवपूर्ण वर्णन किया है। कवि भारत को अपनी माँ की तरह मानते हैं और उसे पवित्र, सुंदर और गौरवशाली बताते हैं।

In English: The central idea is patriotism. The poet Sohanlal Dwivedi glorifies India by describing its nature (Himalayas, rivers, waterfalls), its great personalities (Rama, Krishna, Buddha), and its greatness. The poet considers India as his mother and calls it sacred, beautiful, and glorious.

📚 गहन अध्ययन — पाठ 1 (In-Depth Study — Chapter 1)कवि-परिचय, ऐतिहासिक संदर्भ, शब्द-संपदा, व्याकरण, अलंकार, गतिविधियाँ & अभ्यास

👤 कवि सोहनलाल द्विवेदी — विस्तृत परिचय (Detailed Biography)

हिंदी में:

  • जन्म: 22 फ़रवरी 1906, बिंदकी (फ़तेहपुर), उत्तर प्रदेश
  • मृत्यु: 1 मार्च 1988, कानपुर
  • पिता का नाम: पंडित बिंदा प्रसाद द्विवेदी
  • शिक्षा: स्नातकोत्तर (M.A.) — हिंदी & संस्कृत
  • व्यवसाय: कवि, पत्रकार, संपादक (पत्रिका "बालसखा")
  • उपाधि: "राष्ट्रकवि" (National Poet) — महात्मा गांधी द्वारा सम्मानित
  • प्रसिद्ध रचनाएँ: भैरवी, पूजागीत, चेतना, युगाधार, प्रभाती
  • बच्चों के लिए: "बच्चों के बापू", "शिशु भारती", "दूधबताशा"

In English:

  • Born: 22 February 1906 at Bindki (Fatehpur), Uttar Pradesh
  • Died: 1 March 1988 at Kanpur
  • Father: Pandit Binda Prasad Dwivedi
  • Education: Post-graduate (M.A.) in Hindi & Sanskrit
  • Profession: Poet, journalist, editor of children's magazine "Baalsakha"
  • Title: Honoured as "Rashtra-Kavi" (National Poet) by Mahatma Gandhi
  • Famous works: Bhairavi, Pooja-geet, Chetana, Yugadhaar, Prabhati
  • For children: "Bachchon ke Bapu" (Children's Gandhi), "Shishu Bharati", "Doodh-batasha"

📜 साहित्यिक यात्रा (Literary Journey)

हिंदी: सोहनलाल द्विवेदी जी ने महज़ 14 साल की उम्र में कविता लिखना शुरू कर दिया था। वे महात्मा गांधी के विचारों से बहुत प्रभावित थे और उन्होंने गांधी जी को अपनी कविताओं में बार-बार स्थान दिया। उनकी सबसे प्रसिद्ध पंक्ति है — "कोशिश करने वालों की कभी हार नहीं होती"।

English: Sohanlal Dwivedi started writing poems at just 14 years of age. He was deeply influenced by Mahatma Gandhi's ideas and gave Gandhiji a special place in many of his poems. His most famous line is — "Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahin hoti" ("Those who keep trying never lose").

🏆 पुरस्कार और सम्मान (Awards & Honours)

वर्षपुरस्कार/सम्मानदेने वाला (Awarded by)
1965"पद्मश्री" (Padma Shri)भारत सरकार (Government of India)
1970सोवियत लैंड नेहरू पुरस्कारUSSR Government
—"राष्ट्रकवि" की उपाधिमहात्मा गांधी
—"विद्यावाचस्पति"हिंदी साहित्य सम्मेलन

🇮🇳 ऐतिहासिक संदर्भ — Historical Context

हिंदी में: यह कविता तब लिखी गई जब भारत पर अंग्रेज़ों का राज था (1858 से 1947 तक)। अंग्रेज़ों के विरुद्ध स्वतंत्रता संग्राम चल रहा था। कवि अपनी कविताओं द्वारा भारतीयों के मन में देशप्रेम जगाना चाहते थे ताकि वे अंग्रेज़ों के अत्याचार के विरुद्ध एकजुट हो सकें। उस समय कई अन्य देशभक्ति कवि भी थे — सुभद्राकुमारी चौहान, रामधारी सिंह दिनकर, माखनलाल चतुर्वेदी आदि।

In English: This poem was written when India was under British rule (1858–1947). The Indian freedom struggle was at its peak. The poet wanted to awaken patriotism in Indian hearts so that they could unite against British oppression. Several other patriotic poets of that era include Subhadra Kumari Chauhan, Ramdhari Singh Dinkar, Makhanlal Chaturvedi.

⏳ Timeline of Indian Freedom Movement (in which this poem belongs)

🤝 उस युग के अन्य देशभक्ति कवि (Other Patriotic Poets of That Era)

कवि का नाम (Poet)प्रसिद्ध रचना (Famous Work)विषय (Theme)
सुभद्राकुमारी चौहान"झाँसी की रानी"रानी लक्ष्मीबाई की वीरता
रामधारी सिंह "दिनकर""रश्मिरथी", "कुरुक्षेत्र"क्रांति, वीरता, सामाजिक न्याय
माखनलाल चतुर्वेदी"पुष्प की अभिलाषा"देश पर बलिदान
जयशंकर प्रसाद"कामायनी", "हिमाद्रि तुंग शृंग से..."राष्ट्रगौरव, दर्शन
श्यामलाल गुप्त "पार्षद""विजयी विश्व तिरंगा प्यारा" (झंडा गीत)तिरंगे का गौरव
बंकिमचंद्र चटर्जी"वंदे मातरम्" (बंगला)माँ-भारत की वंदना

🔬 छंद-वार गहन विश्लेषण (Stanza-wise Deep Analysis)

📖 छंद 1 — पंक्ति-दर-पंक्ति विश्लेषण / Stanza 1 — Line-by-line Analysis

पंक्ति 1 / Line 1: "ऊँचा खड़ा हिमालय, आकाश चूमता है"

  • शब्द-विश्लेषण / Word analysis: हिंदी: "ऊँचा" = विशेषण, "खड़ा" = क्रिया (खड़े होने की मुद्रा), "हिमालय" = संज्ञा (पर्वत का नाम), "आकाश चूमना" = मुहावरा = बहुत ऊँचा होना
    English: "ūnchā" = adjective (tall), "kharā" = verb-form (standing), "Himālaya" = noun (mountain name), "ākāś chūmnā" = idiom = to be extremely tall
  • अलंकार / Figure of speech: हिंदी: मानवीकरण — हिमालय को मनुष्य की तरह "खड़ा" और "चूमता" दिखाया गया।
    English: Personification — the Himalayas are described as "standing" and "kissing", like a human being.
  • भाव / Feeling: हिंदी: हिमालय इतना ऊँचा है मानो आकाश को छू रहा हो।
    English: The Himalayas are so tall they seem to be touching the sky.

पंक्ति 2 / Line 2: "नीचे चरण तले झुक, नित सिंधु झूमता है"

  • शब्द-विश्लेषण / Word analysis: हिंदी: "चरण तले" = पैरों के नीचे, "नित" = हमेशा, "सिंधु" = समुद्र, "झूमता" = लहराता
    English: "charan tale" = beneath the feet, "nit" = always, "sindhu" = ocean, "jhoomta" = swaying with waves
  • अलंकार / Figure of speech: हिंदी: मानवीकरण — समुद्र को "झुकना" और "झूमना" दिखाया गया।
    English: Personification — the ocean is shown "bowing" and "swaying".
  • भाव / Feeling: हिंदी: समुद्र हिमालय के सामने झुककर हमेशा लहर लेता है — मानो आदर कर रहा हो।
    English: The ocean bows before the Himalayas and forever sways — as though paying respect.

पंक्तियाँ 3-4 / Lines 3–4: गंगा, यमुना, त्रिवेणी का वर्णन / Description of Ganga, Yamuna, Triveni

  • भौगोलिक संदर्भ / Geographical context: हिंदी: गंगा (गंगोत्री से बंगाल की खाड़ी), यमुना (यमुनोत्री से प्रयागराज), त्रिवेणी (प्रयागराज में गंगा+यमुना+सरस्वती का संगम)
    English: Ganga (Gangotri to Bay of Bengal); Yamuna (Yamunotri to Prayagraj); Triveni (Ganga + Yamuna + Saraswati confluence at Prayagraj).
  • अलंकार / Figure of speech: हिंदी: अनुप्रास (alliteration) — "नदियाँ", "लहर", "जगमग", "पग पग"
    English: Alliteration — "nadiyaan, lahar, jagmag, pag pag".
  • भाव / Feeling: हिंदी: भारत की नदियाँ बहुत सुंदर हैं और चारों ओर सुंदरता बिखेरती हैं।
    English: India's rivers are beautiful and scatter beauty all around.

पंक्तियाँ 5-6 / Lines 5–6 (अंतिम — टेक / Refrain):

  • हिंदी: छंद के अंत में "मेरी... मेरी..." की पुनरुक्ति से प्रेम-भाव गहरा होता है।
    English: The closing repetition of "meri… meri…" deepens the feeling of love.
  • हिंदी: "मेरी" शब्द 4 बार — अधिकार-भाव।
    English: The word "meri" appears 4 times — a sense of belonging.

📖 छंद 2 — पंक्ति-दर-पंक्ति विश्लेषण / Stanza 2 — Line-by-line Analysis

पंक्ति 1-2 / Lines 1–2: झरने और चिड़ियाँ / Waterfalls and birds

  • हिंदी: "झरने अनेक झरते" — झरना/झरने का यमक अलंकार-जैसी ध्वनि-समानता।
    English: "jharne anek jharte" — a Yamak-like sound repetition of jharnā/jharne.
  • हिंदी: "चिड़ियाँ चहक रही हैं" — श्रवण-चित्रण (sound imagery)।
    English: "chidiyaan chahak rahi hain" — sound imagery; we can almost hear the birds.

पंक्ति 3-4 / Lines 3–4: अमराइयाँ और कोयल / Mango groves and the cuckoo

  • हिंदी: "अमराइयाँ घनी हैं" — आम के बागों का दृश्य।
    English: "amraayian ghani hain" — a picture of dense mango groves.
  • हिंदी: "कोयल पुकारती है" — मानवीकरण; भारतीय गाँव का जीवंत चित्र।
    English: "koyal pukaarti hai" — personification; a vivid scene of an Indian village.

पंक्ति 5-6 / Lines 5–6: मलय पवन / The southern breeze

  • हिंदी: "मलय पवन" = दक्षिण भारत के मलय पर्वत से आने वाली शीतल हवा (चंदन के पेड़ों से सुगंधित)।
    English: "Malay pavan" = the cool southern breeze from the Malay hills, scented by sandalwood trees.
  • हिंदी: "तन-मन सँवारती है" — द्वंद्व समास (तन + मन); मानवीकरण।
    English: "tan-man sanwaarti hai" — a Dvandva compound (body + mind); also personification.

📖 छंद 3 — पंक्ति-दर-पंक्ति विश्लेषण / Stanza 3 — Line-by-line Analysis

पंक्ति 1-2 / Lines 1–2: राम और सीता / Rama and Sita

  • हिंदी: रघुपति = रघु वंश के स्वामी = श्री राम। राम का जन्म अयोध्या में हुआ।
    English: Raghupati = Lord of the Raghu clan = Lord Rama. He was born at Ayodhya.
  • हिंदी: सीता का जन्म मिथिला (जनकपुर) में हुआ; वे राजा जनक की पुत्री थीं।
    English: Sita was born at Mithila (Janakpur); she was the daughter of King Janaka.

पंक्ति 3-4 / Lines 3–4: श्रीकृष्ण और गीता / Krishna and the Gita

  • हिंदी: श्रीकृष्ण का जन्म मथुरा में हुआ; वे वंशी (बाँसुरी) बजाते थे।
    English: Lord Krishna was born at Mathura and played the flute (vanshi).
  • हिंदी: गीता = श्रीमद्भगवद्गीता; कुरुक्षेत्र युद्ध के समय अर्जुन को कृष्ण ने 18 अध्यायों का उपदेश दिया।
    English: The Gita = Bhagavad Gita; on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna delivered an 18-chapter discourse to Arjuna.

पंक्ति 5-6 / Lines 5–6: गौतम बुद्ध / Gautama Buddha

  • हिंदी: गौतम बुद्ध (असली नाम सिद्धार्थ) का जन्म लुम्बिनी में हुआ था (≈ 563 ईसा पूर्व)।
    English: Gautama Buddha (originally Siddhartha) was born at Lumbini (≈ 563 BCE).
  • हिंदी: उन्होंने बौद्ध धर्म चलाया जो आज भी जापान, चीन, थाईलैंड, श्रीलंका में फैला है।
    English: He founded Buddhism, which today spreads across Japan, China, Thailand, and Sri Lanka.
  • हिंदी: "दया सिखाई, दिया दिखाया" — श्लेष अलंकार-जैसा सौंदर्य — "दया" और "दिया" में सिर्फ़ एक मात्रा का अंतर!
    English: "daya sikhaayi, diya dikhaaya" — a Shlesha-like beauty — "daya" (mercy) and "diya" (lamp) differ by just one vowel mark!

पंक्ति 7 (टेक / Refrain): "वह युद्ध-भूमि मेरी, वह बुद्ध-भूमि मेरी"

  • हिंदी: यहाँ विरोधाभास अलंकार है — "युद्ध" और "बुद्ध" — दो विपरीत बातें एक साथ।
    English: This is the figure of antithesis — "yuddh (war)" and "buddh (Buddha/peace)" — two opposites placed together.
  • हिंदी: संदेश: भारत में जब आवश्यकता हो तो वीरों की भूमि है, और शांति के समय बुद्ध (ज्ञान) की भूमि है।
    English: Message: When needed, India is a land of warriors; in peaceful times, it is a land of the Buddha (of wisdom).

🎨 अलंकार — Figures of Speech in this Poem

अलंकार (Figure)अर्थ (Meaning)कविता से उदाहरण (Example)
मानवीकरण
(Personification)		निर्जीव वस्तु को मनुष्य की तरह दिखाना"हिमालय आकाश चूमता है"
"सिंधु झूमता है"
"कोयल पुकारती है"
अनुप्रास
(Alliteration)		एक ही अक्षर का बार-बार आना"पग पग छहर रही हैं"
"नदियाँ लहर रही हैं"
"चिड़ियाँ चहक रही हैं"
पुनरुक्ति
(Repetition)		एक शब्द का बार-बार दोहराया जाना"मेरी, मेरी, मेरी, मेरी" (हर छंद के अंत में)
विरोधाभास
(Antithesis)		दो विपरीत बातों का मेल"युद्ध-भूमि / बुद्ध-भूमि"
श्लेष
(Pun)		एक ध्वनि से दो अर्थ का खेल"दया सिखाई / दिया दिखाया"
(दया = mercy, दिया = lamp)
उपमा
(Simile)		एक चीज़ को दूसरी जैसा बताना"मातृभूमि" (माँ जैसी भूमि)
"स्वर्ण-भूमि" (सोने जैसी भूमि)
रूपक
(Metaphor)		एक चीज़ को सीधे दूसरी कहना"पुण्य-भूमि" (भूमि = पवित्रता का प्रतीक)

मूल शब्द / WordEnglish Meaningपर्यायवाची (कम-से-कम 4) / Hindi Synonyms

आकाशSkyनभ, गगन, अंबर, व्योम, अनंत, आसमान
सिंधुOcean / seaसमुद्र, सागर, उदधि, रत्नाकर, जलधि
नदीRiverसरिता, तटिनी, तरंगिणी, निर्झरिणी, आपगा
भूमिLand / earthधरा, धरती, ज़मीन, वसुधा, धरणी, पृथ्वी, उर्वी
माँMotherमाता, जननी, अम्मा, मातु, अंबा
रामLord Ramaरघुपति, रघुनंदन, सीतापति, रघुवीर, राघव
कृष्णLord Krishnaकेशव, माधव, गोविंद, मुरारी, मोहन, श्याम
बुद्धThe Buddhaतथागत, सिद्धार्थ, शाक्यमुनि, बोधिसत्व
पवनWind / breezeवायु, हवा, समीर, अनिल, मारुत
चिड़ियाBirdपक्षी, खग, विहग, पंछी, द्विज
दयाCompassion / mercyकरुणा, अनुकंपा, कृपा, सहानुभूति, ममता

2️⃣ विलोम शब्द / Antonyms

शब्द / WordEnglish Meaningविलोम / Opposite (Hindi)English Opposite
ऊँचाTall / highनीचाShort / low
आकाशSkyपाताल / धरतीUnderworld / earth
पवित्र (पुण्य)Sacred / holyअपवित्र (पाप)Impure / sin
जन्मBirthमृत्युDeath
स्वर्ण (सोना)Goldलोहा / काँसाIron / bronze
घनाDenseविरलSparse
शीतलCoolउष्ण (गर्म)Hot / warm
युद्धWarशांतिPeace
दयाMercy / kindnessक्रूरताCruelty
सुयशGood fameअपयशIll fame / disgrace
प्रकाश (दिया)Light / lampअंधकारDarkness
घनीDense (fem.)विरलSparse

3️⃣ अनेक शब्दों के लिए एक शब्द / One Word for Many

हिंदी विवरणEnglish Descriptionएक शब्द / One Word
जहाँ जन्म हुआ होWhere one was bornजन्मभूमि (Janmabhoomi / birthland)
जहाँ कर्म (कार्य) किया जाएWhere one's work is doneकर्मभूमि (Karmabhoomi / land of action)
तीन नदियों का संगमConfluence of three riversत्रिवेणी (Triveni)
आम के पेड़ों का बगीचाGrove of mango treesअमराई (Amrai / mango grove)
देश से प्रेम करने वालाOne who loves the countryदेशभक्त (Deshbhakt / patriot)
देश के लिए काम करने वालाOne who serves the countryदेशसेवक (Deshsevak / country-servant)
देश से प्रेम (भाव)Love of one's countryदेशप्रेम / देशभक्ति (Patriotism)
राष्ट्र का गीतSong of the nationराष्ट्रगीत (National Song)
राष्ट्र का गानAnthem of the nationराष्ट्रगान (National Anthem)
राष्ट्र का ध्वजFlag of the nationराष्ट्रध्वज (National Flag)

4️⃣ शब्द-युग्म / Compound Words (Samaas)

समस्त पद / Compoundसमास-विग्रह (Hindi split)English Split & Meaningसमास का प्रकार / Samaas Type
मातृभूमिमाता रूपी भूमिLand which is like a mother (Motherland)कर्मधारय / Karmadharya
जन्मभूमिजन्म की भूमिLand of birth (Birthland)संबंध तत्पुरुष / Sambandh Tatpurush
स्वर्ण-भूमिस्वर्ण के समान भूमिLand like gold (Golden land)कर्मधारय / Karmadharya
तन-मनतन और मनBody and mindद्वंद्व / Dvandva
राजा-रंकराजा और रंकKing and pauperद्वंद्व / Dvandva
गंगा-यमुनागंगा और यमुनाGanga and Yamunaद्वंद्व / Dvandva
देशभक्तिदेश की भक्तिDevotion to one's country (Patriotism)संबंध तत्पुरुष / Sambandh Tatpurush

📚 व्याकरण-कोना — Grammar Corner

1️⃣ संज्ञा (Nouns) कविता में

संज्ञा का प्रकारउदाहरणEnglish Type
व्यक्तिवाचकहिमालय, गंगा, यमुना, राम, सीता, कृष्ण, गौतमProper Nouns
जातिवाचकपर्वत, नदी, चिड़िया, पेड़, झरनाCommon Nouns
भाववाचकसुयश, दया, छटा, सुंदरताAbstract Nouns
समूहवाचकअमराई (आम के पेड़ों का समूह)Collective Nouns

2️⃣ सर्वनाम / Pronouns

हिंदी: कविता में "वह" (हर छंद में 3 बार) निश्चयवाचक सर्वनाम है — "वह पुण्य-भूमि मेरी"। "मेरी" अधिकारवाचक सर्वनाम है।

English: In the poem, "वह (vah)" appears thrice in each stanza as a demonstrative pronoun — "That is my sacred land." "मेरी (meri)" is a possessive pronoun meaning "my".

3️⃣ विशेषण (Adjectives)

विशेषणविशेष्य (जिसकी विशेषता)प्रकार
ऊँचाहिमालयगुणवाचक
नित (= हमेशा)सिंधु (झूमता है)कालवाचक
अनेकझरनेसंख्यावाचक
घनीअमराइयाँपरिमाणवाचक
पुनीतगीतागुणवाचक
निरालीछटागुणवाचक

4️⃣ क्रिया / Verbs (Kriya)

हिंदी: कविता में आए कुछ क्रिया-पद:

English: Some verbs that appear in the poem:

  • हिंदी: चूमता है, झूमता है, लहर रही हैं, छहर रही हैं (वर्तमान काल)
    English: kisses, sways, are flowing, are spreading (Present tense)
  • हिंदी: झरते, चहक रही हैं, पुकारती है, बहती है, सँवारती है
    English: cascade, are chirping, calls, flows, refreshes
  • हिंदी: जन्मे थे, जन्मी थी, सुनाई, बढ़ाया, सिखाई, दिखाया (भूत काल)
    English: were born (male), was born (female), narrated, increased, taught, showed (Past tense)

5️⃣ लिंग (Gender) — Hindi Specials

शब्दलिंगउदाहरण-वाक्य
हिमालयपुल्लिंगहिमालय खड़ा है।
सिंधुपुल्लिंगसिंधु झूमता है।
गंगास्त्रीलिंगगंगा लहर रही है।
यमुनास्त्रीलिंगयमुना बहती है।
चिड़ियास्त्रीलिंगचिड़ियाँ चहक रही हैं।
कोयलस्त्रीलिंगकोयल पुकारती है।
पवन (वायु)पुल्लिंगपवन बहता है।
झरनापुल्लिंगझरना बहता है।

6️⃣ वचन / Number (Vachan)

एकवचन / Singularबहुवचन / PluralEnglish Meaning
नदीनदियाँriver → rivers
चिड़ियाचिड़ियाँbird → birds
पहाड़ीपहाड़ियाँhill → hills
झाड़ीझाड़ियाँbush → bushes
अमराईअमराइयाँmango grove → mango groves
झरनाझरनेwaterfall → waterfalls
चरणचरण (दोनों वचन समान / same in both)foot / feet (same form)

🗺️ कविता में भारत का भूगोल (India's Geography in the Poem)

📋 कविता में आए भौगोलिक तत्व / Geographical Elements in Poem

तत्व / Elementहिंदी स्थान व विवरणEnglish Location & Descriptionहिंदी महत्त्वEnglish Importance
हिमालय / Himalayasभारत के उत्तर में, लगभग 2500 कि.मी. लंबाIn northern India, ~2500 km longदुनिया का सबसे ऊँचा पर्वत; एवरेस्ट (8848 m)World's highest mountain range; Mt. Everest (8848 m)
गंगा / Gangaगंगोत्री से बंगाल की खाड़ी (2525 कि.मी.)Gangotri to Bay of Bengal (2525 km)भारत की सबसे पवित्र नदी; "मोक्षदायिनी"India's most sacred river; "giver of liberation"
यमुना / Yamunaयमुनोत्री से प्रयागराज (1376 कि.मी.)Yamunotri to Prayagraj (1376 km)दिल्ली, मथुरा, आगरा यमुना के किनारेDelhi, Mathura, Agra sit on its banks
त्रिवेणी (संगम) / Triveni Sangamप्रयागराज (इलाहाबाद)Prayagraj (Allahabad)गंगा + यमुना + सरस्वती का मिलन; कुंभ मेलाConfluence of Ganga + Yamuna + Saraswati; Kumbh Mela site
सिंधु महासागर / Indian Oceanभारत के तीन ओर (पूर्व, पश्चिम, दक्षिण)On India's three sides (east, west, south)3 ओर समुद्र = प्रायद्वीप (peninsula)Ocean on 3 sides = peninsula shape
मलय पर्वत / Malaya Mountainsदक्षिण भारत (केरल/तमिलनाडु)South India (Kerala/Tamil Nadu)चंदन के पेड़ों के लिए प्रसिद्ध; सुगंधित हवाFamous for sandalwood; fragrant breeze

🙏 कविता के महापुरुष — विस्तार से (Great Personalities — In Detail)

👑 1. भगवान श्री राम (रघुपति) / Lord Rama (Raghupati)

  • जन्म / Born: अयोध्या (उत्तर प्रदेश), चैत्र शुक्ल नवमी (राम नवमी) / Ayodhya (Uttar Pradesh), on Chaitra Shukla Navami (Ram Navami)
  • माता-पिता / Parents: रानी कौशल्या और राजा दशरथ / Queen Kaushalya and King Dasharatha
  • पत्नी / Wife: सीता / Sita
  • भाई / Brothers: लक्ष्मण, भरत, शत्रुघ्न / Lakshmana, Bharata, Shatrughna
  • गुरु / Guru: ऋषि वसिष्ठ / Sage Vasishtha
  • प्रमुख घटनाएँ / Key events: 14 वर्ष का वनवास, सीता का हरण, रावण-वध, राम-राज्य / 14 years of exile, Sita's abduction, slaying Ravana, ideal Ram-Rajya rule
  • ग्रंथ / Epic: "रामायण" — ऋषि वाल्मीकि द्वारा रचित (24,000 श्लोक) / "Ramayana" — composed by Sage Valmiki (24,000 verses)
  • आदर्श / Ideal: "मर्यादा पुरुषोत्तम" — आदर्श पुत्र, भाई, पति, मित्र, राजा / "Maryada Purushottam" — ideal son, brother, husband, friend, king

👸 2. माता सीता / Mother Sita

  • जन्म / Born: मिथिला (आज का जनकपुर, नेपाल) / Mithila (today's Janakpur, Nepal)
  • पिता / Father: राजा जनक (मिथिला के राजा) / King Janaka of Mithila
  • पति / Husband: भगवान राम / Lord Rama
  • पुत्र / Sons: लव और कुश / Lava and Kusha
  • अन्य नाम / Other names: जानकी (जनक की पुत्री), वैदेही (विदेह राज्य की), मैथिली / Janaki (daughter of Janaka), Vaidehi (of Videha kingdom), Maithili
  • आदर्श / Ideal: त्याग, धैर्य, पतिव्रता धर्म की प्रतीक / Symbol of sacrifice, patience, and devotion

🪈 3. भगवान श्रीकृष्ण / Lord Krishna

  • जन्म / Born: मथुरा — भाद्रपद कृष्ण अष्टमी (जन्माष्टमी) / Mathura — on Bhadrapad Krishna Ashtami (Janmashtami)
  • माता-पिता / Parents: देवकी और वसुदेव / Devaki and Vasudeva
  • पालक माता-पिता / Foster parents: यशोदा और नंद बाबा / Yashoda and Nanda Baba
  • पत्नी / Wife: रुक्मिणी (मुख्य) / Rukmini (chief)
  • मित्र / Friends: अर्जुन, सुदामा / Arjuna, Sudama
  • प्रसिद्ध बातें / Famous for: बाँसुरी बजाना, गायें चराना, माखन चुराना, गोवर्धन पर्वत उठाना / Playing the flute, herding cows, stealing butter, lifting Govardhan Mountain
  • उपदेश / Teaching: महाभारत के समय कुरुक्षेत्र में अर्जुन को भगवद्गीता (18 अध्याय, 700 श्लोक) सुनाई / Imparted the Bhagavad Gita (18 chapters, 700 verses) to Arjuna at Kurukshetra during the Mahabharata war
  • गीता का संदेश / Gita's message: कर्म करो, फल की चिंता मत करो; धर्म के लिए लड़ो / Do your duty, don't worry about the fruits; fight for righteousness

☸️ 4. गौतम बुद्ध / Gautam Buddha

  • जन्म / Born: लुम्बिनी (नेपाल) — 563 ईसा पूर्व / Lumbini (Nepal) — 563 BCE
  • असली नाम / Real name: सिद्धार्थ गौतम / Siddhartha Gautama
  • पिता / Father: राजा शुद्धोदन (कपिलवस्तु के राजा) / King Shuddhodana of Kapilavastu
  • माता / Mother: रानी महामाया / Queen Mahamaya
  • पत्नी / Wife: यशोधरा / Yashodhara
  • पुत्र / Son: राहुल / Rahula
  • घटना / Event: 29 वर्ष की उम्र में बीमार, बूढ़ा, मृत व्यक्ति और संन्यासी देखकर महल छोड़ा / At 29, he saw a sick man, an old man, a dead man and a monk — and renounced the palace
  • ज्ञान / Enlightenment: बोधगया (बिहार) में बोधि वृक्ष के नीचे ज्ञान प्राप्त (निर्वाण) — इसलिए "बुद्ध" / Attained Enlightenment (Nirvana) under the Bodhi tree at Bodhgaya (Bihar) — hence "Buddha" (the enlightened one)
  • पहला उपदेश / First sermon: सारनाथ (वाराणसी) में / Delivered at Sarnath (Varanasi)
  • चार आर्य सत्य / Four Noble Truths: (1) दुःख है / Suffering exists (2) दुःख का कारण है / Suffering has a cause (3) दुःख से छुटकारा संभव है / Suffering can end (4) अष्टांगिक मार्ग से छुटकारा / The Eightfold Path leads to its end
  • संदेश / Message: अहिंसा, करुणा, सत्य, मध्यम मार्ग / Non-violence, compassion, truth, the Middle Path

🎵 "वंदे मातरम्" से समानता / Connection with "Vande Mataram"

हिंदी: "मातृभूमि" कविता का भाव "वंदे मातरम्" गीत से बहुत मिलता है — दोनों में भारत-माता की वंदना है।
English: The poem "Maatrubhoomi" carries the same emotion as "Vande Mataram" — both are hymns of praise to Mother India.

📜 वंदे मातरम् / Vande Mataram (मूल संस्कृत/बंगला; रचयिता — बंकिमचंद्र चटर्जी, 1875 / Original Sanskrit-Bengali; by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, 1875)

वंदे मातरम्!
सुजलाम् सुफलाम् मलयजशीतलाम्
शस्यश्यामलाम् मातरम्।
शुभ्रज्योत्स्ना पुलकितयामिनीम्
फुल्लकुसुमित द्रुमदलशोभिनीम्,
सुहासिनीं सुमधुर भाषिणीम्
सुखदां वरदां मातरम्॥

हिंदी अर्थ: "हे माँ, मैं तेरी वंदना करता हूँ। तू अच्छे जल वाली, अच्छे फलों वाली, मलय हवा से शीतल, धान-फसलों से हरी-भरी है। चाँदनी रात में चमकती, फूलों से भरे पेड़ों से सजी, हँसती हुई, मीठी बोलने वाली, सुख देने वाली माता को मेरा प्रणाम!"

English meaning: "Mother, I bow to you! You with sweet waters, rich fruits, cooled by the southern breeze, fields green with corn — Mother! Radiant with white moonlight, beautiful with flowering trees, smiling sweetly, soft-spoken, giver of bliss and boons — Mother, I salute you!"

🔍 दोनों कविताओं की तुलना / Comparison of the Two Poems

विशेषता / Feature"मातृभूमि" (द्विवेदी) / "Maatrubhoomi" (Dwivedi)"वंदे मातरम्" (बंकिमचंद्र) / "Vande Mataram" (Bankim Chandra)
भाषा / Languageहिंदी (खड़ी बोली) / Hindi (Khari Boli)संस्कृत + बंगला / Sanskrit + Bengali
विषय / Themeदेशभक्ति / Patriotismदेशभक्ति / Patriotism
प्रकृति / Nature imageryहिमालय, नदियाँ, झरने / Himalayas, rivers, waterfallsजल, फल, मलय, धान / waters, fruits, southern breeze, grain
महापुरुष / Great figuresराम, सीता, कृष्ण, बुद्ध / Ram, Sita, Krishna, Buddha(सीधा उल्लेख नहीं) / (no direct mention)
भाव / Mood"मेरी मातृभूमि" — गर्व / "My motherland" — pride"वंदे मातरम्" — प्रणाम / "I bow to thee, Mother" — reverence
उपयोग / Useस्कूल/प्रार्थनासभा में / In school/morning assemblyभारत का राष्ट्रीय गीत / India's National Song

🇮🇳 तथ्य / Fact: हिंदी: "वंदे मातरम्" को राष्ट्रीय गीत का दर्जा 24 जनवरी 1950 को संविधान सभा ने दिया। / English: "Vande Mataram" was officially adopted as India's National Song by the Constituent Assembly on 24 January 1950.

🎯 गतिविधियाँ / Activities

गतिविधि 1: कविता-पाठ / Activity 1: Recitation

हिंदी: तीनों छंदों को सही उच्चारण, उतार-चढ़ाव और भाव के साथ कक्षा में सुनाओ। टेक "वह जन्मभूमि मेरी, वह मातृभूमि मेरी" को ज़ोर देकर बोलो।
English: Recite all three stanzas in class with correct pronunciation, rhythm and feeling. Emphasise the refrain "That is my birthland, that is my motherland."

गतिविधि 2: चित्र बनाओ / Activity 2: Draw a Picture

हिंदी: एक A4 पेज पर बनाओ:
English: On an A4 sheet draw:

  • ऊँचा हिमालय (बर्फ़ से ढका) / Tall snow-capped Himalayas
  • नीचे गंगा-यमुना (नीली लहरें) / Below, Ganga-Yamuna (blue waves)
  • एक अमराई (कोयल बैठी) / A mango grove with a koel
  • एक झरना / A waterfall
  • रंग भरो और शीर्षक दो — "मेरी मातृभूमि" / Colour it and title it "My Motherland"

गतिविधि 3: मानचित्र अंकन / Activity 3: Map Marking

हिंदी: भारत के नक़्शे पर ये जगहें ढूँढो और चिह्नित करो:
English: On a map of India, locate and mark:

  • हिमालय पर्वत / Himalayas
  • गंगा नदी (गंगोत्री से बंगाल की खाड़ी) / Ganga (Gangotri to Bay of Bengal)
  • यमुना नदी / Yamuna
  • प्रयागराज (त्रिवेणी संगम) / Prayagraj (Triveni Sangam)
  • अयोध्या, मथुरा, बोधगया / Ayodhya, Mathura, Bodh Gaya
  • लुम्बिनी (नेपाल में) / Lumbini (in Nepal)

गतिविधि 4: समूह-चर्चा / Activity 4: Group Discussion

हिंदी: 4-5 के समूह में चर्चा करो:
English: In groups of 4–5, discuss:

  1. क्या आज भी हम कविता में बताए गए भारत को देख सकते हैं? / Can we still see the India described in the poem today?
  2. प्रदूषण से नदियाँ कैसे प्रभावित हो रही हैं? / How is pollution affecting our rivers?
  3. युद्ध-भूमि और बुद्ध-भूमि — दोनों होना क्यों ज़रूरी है? / Why is it important to be both a "land of warriors" and a "land of the Buddha"?

गतिविधि 5: छंद-रचना / Activity 5: Compose a Stanza

हिंदी: कविता की तर्ज़ पर अपना एक छंद लिखो — अपने राज्य/शहर के बारे में। उदाहरण:
English: Compose a stanza in the style of the poem — about your own state or city. Example:

"मेरा बेंगलुरु प्यारा, बागों से भरा-भरा,
कन्नड़ की मीठी बोली, हर रास्ते में चहकी।
चामुंडी का दर्शन, और कावेरी का जल,
मेरा यह गृह-प्रदेश, मेरा गर्व-स्थल।"

गतिविधि 6: कविता को संगीत में ढालो / Activity 6: Set the Poem to Music

हिंदी: कक्षा में कविता को किसी जाने-पहचाने राग (लोरी, राष्ट्रगीत) पर गाने का प्रयास करो। यह कविता का संदेश और गहरा करता है।
English: Try singing the poem to a familiar tune (a lullaby, or the National Song) in class. Music makes the message of the poem sink in more deeply.

गतिविधि 7: देशभक्ति कविताओं की डायरी / Activity 7: Patriotic Poetry Diary
